4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Maintenance Decision Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD start1[Vehicle in Service] --> check1{Time/Mileage Interval Reached?} check1 -->|No| operate[Continue Operation] check1 -->|Yes| pm1[Perform Scheduled PM Task] pm1 --> record1[Log Service in Vehicle History] record1 --> check2{Condition Monitoring Available?} check2 -->|No| operate check2 -->|Yes| pds1[Run PdM Diagnostics] pds1 --> eval{Component Condition} eval -->|Healthy > 80%| operate eval -->|Degraded 50-80%| mon1[Increase Monitoring Frequency] eval -->|Critical < 50%| replace[Schedule Replacement] eval -->|Failed| repair[Emergency Repair] mon1 --> operate replace --> record1 repair --> record1 operate --> end1[Vehicle Continues Service] style start1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style pm1 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style pds1 fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style replace fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style repair fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style end1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Bathtub Curve with PM Intervention ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Infant Mortality Phase
High Initial Failures] -->|Burn-in Period| B[Useful Life Phase
Low Constant Failures] B -->|Without PM| C[Wear-out Phase
Rapidly Increasing Failures] B -->|With PM| D[Scheduled Component Replacement] D --> B C --> E[Vehicle Failure / Obsolescence] style A fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style B fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style C fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style D fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style E fill:#8B0000,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Define Preventive Maintenance. List any four scheduled maintenance tasks performed on a commercial vehicle.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Preventive Maintenance (PM) is a planned, time- or mileage-based maintenance strategy in which specific service tasks are performed on a vehicle at predetermined intervals to prevent unexpected breakdowns and extend its service life. Four scheduled PM tasks: 1. **Engine oil and oil filter change** — every 5,000–10,000 km. 2. **Air filter replacement** — every 20,000 km or as condition demands. 3. **Brake pad inspection and replacement** — every 30,000–40,000 km. 4. **Wheel alignment and tyre rotation** — every 10,000 km. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **What is the "Bathtub Curve" in the context of vehicle reliability? Briefly explain its three phases.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Bathtub Curve** is a reliability model that graphically represents the failure rate of a vehicle or component over its operational life. It has three phases: 1. **Infant Mortality Phase (Burn-in):** High initial failure rate due to manufacturing defects, installation errors, or poor quality control. Decreases with time. 2. **Useful Life Phase:** A constant, low failure rate — the vehicle operates reliably under normal conditions. This is the phase where the vehicle spends most of its life. 3. **Wear-out Phase:** Failure rate increases sharply as components approach end of life due to cumulative wear, fatigue, and corrosion. Preventive maintenance aims to intervene *before* this phase causes catastrophic failure. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023] **Q: (a)** Explain in detail the various **strategies for extending the service life of a commercial vehicle**, covering preventive maintenance, driver behavior, and environmental control. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A transport company operates a fleet of buses. The following data is given for one bus: - Acquisition cost: ₹45,00,000 - Salvage value after 10 years: ₹5,00,000 - Annual operating cost: ₹6,00,000 - Annual maintenance cost: ₹1,50,000 - Annual km run: 60,000 km Calculate the **Total Life Cycle Cost** and the **Cost per Kilometre** over the 10-year life. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks **1. Preventive Maintenance Strategies (3 marks):** - **Time-based PM:** Engine oil change every 10,000 km; air filter every 20,000 km; brake fluid every 2 years. - **Mileage-based PM:** Major service at 40,000, 80,000, 1,20,000 km (multiples of 40,000). - **Component life-cycle replacement:** Timing belt at 1,00,000 km; battery every 4 years; coolant every 2 years. **2. Driver Behavior Strategies (2 marks):** - **Defensive driving training** — reduces accident-related repairs by 30%. - **Telemetry-based monitoring** — alerts on harsh braking, over-revving. - **No overloading** — every 10% overload reduces suspension life by ~20%. **3. Environmental Control Strategies (2 marks):** - **Covered parking** to prevent UV damage and rain corrosion. - **Anti-rust undercoating** in coastal areas (highly relevant for Kerala). - **Garage humidity control** to prevent electrical corrosion. - **Engine warm-up before full load** (3–5 min) to reduce cold-start wear. --- #### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks **Step 1: Depreciation per year.** $$ \begin{aligned} D &= \frac{C - S}{n} = \frac{4500000 - 500000}{10} = 400000 \text{ ₹/year} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Total annual cost.** $$ \begin{aligned} C_{\text{annual}} &= D + C_{\text{op}} + C_{\text{maint}} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} &= 400000 + 600000 + 150000 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} &= 1150000 \text{ ₹/year} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Total Life Cycle Cost over 10 years.** $$ \begin{aligned} LCC &= 1150000 \times 10 = 1,15,00,000 \text{ ₹} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Total km over 10 years.** $$ \begin{aligned} D_{\text{total}} &= 60000 \times 10 = 6,00,000 \text{ km} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5: Cost per km.** $$ \begin{aligned} CPK &= \frac{1,15,00,000}{6,00,000} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} &= 19.17 \text{ ₹/km} \end{aligned} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** > - **Common Mistake 1:** Forgetting to include depreciation in operating cost. *(−2 marks)* > - **Common Mistake 2:** Dividing by annual km (60,000) instead of total km. *(−1 mark)* > - **Common Mistake 3:** Not converting acquisition cost minus salvage value correctly. *(−1 mark)* > - **Pro Tip:** Always state the formula *before* substituting numbers. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022] **Q: (a)** Discuss the role of **lubrication, tire management, and battery care** in extending vehicle life. **(7 marks)** **(b)** The failure data for a truck over 5 years is: - Total km run: 3,00,000 km - Number of failures: 15 - Total downtime: 45 days Calculate **MTBF (in km)**, **Availability (in %)**, and comment on whether the truck's reliability is satisfactory for a commercial fleet (benchmark: MTBF > 20,000 km, Availability > 90%). **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks **1. Lubrication (3 marks):** - **Engine oil:** Correct grade (e.g., 15W-40 for diesel) reduces wear by 40%. Change at manufacturer-specified intervals. - **Gear oil:** Protects transmission and differential. Replace every 60,000 km. - **Grease:** Wheel bearings, chassis points — every 10,000 km. - **Oil analysis** is the most effective PdM tool — detects wear metals (Fe, Cu, Al) before failure. **2. Tire Management (2 marks):** - **Proper inflation:** ±2 PSI from spec reduces tire life by 10–15%. - **Rotation every 10,000 km** ensures even wear, extending total tire life by 25%. - **Wheel alignment** every 20,000 km prevents uneven tread wear. - **Load distribution** — overloading causes heat buildup and premature failure. **3. Battery Care (2 marks):** - **Terminal cleaning** prevents voltage drop. - **Electrolyte level check** (for lead-acid batteries) every 3 months. - **Avoiding deep discharge** extends battery life from 2 years to 4 years. - **Tightening hold-down clamps** prevents vibration damage. --- #### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks **Step 1: Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF).** $$ \begin{aligned} MTBF &= \frac{\text{Total km Run}}{\text{Number of Failures}} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} &= \frac{3,00,000}{15} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} &= 20,000 \text{ km/failure} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Availability.** $$ \begin{aligned} A &= \frac{\text{Days Available}}{\text{Total Days}} \times 100\% \end{aligned} $$ Total days in 5 years $= 5 \times 365 = 1825$ days. Downtime = 45 days. $$ \begin{aligned} A &= \frac{1825 - 45}{1825} \times 100\% \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} &= \frac{1780}{1825} \times 100\% \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} &= 97.53\% \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Comparison and Conclusion.** - **MTBF = 20,000 km** — *just at the benchmark* (not exceeding it). - **Availability = 97.53%** — *exceeds* the 90% benchmark. **Conclusion:** Availability is satisfactory, but MTBF is at the threshold. The fleet manager should consider **intensified preventive maintenance** to push MTBF above 20,000 km and improve overall reliability. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** > - **Common Mistake 1:** Calculating MTBF in days/hours when data is in km — KTU is flexible on units, but **you must state the assumption**. *(−0.5 marks if not stated)* > - **Common Mistake 2:** Using total days = 5 years × 365 (forgetting leap years) — minor error, no marks deducted. > - **Common Mistake 3:** Not giving a **comparative conclusion** — KTU awards marks for *engineering judgment*, not just calculation. *(−2 marks)* --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Final Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam:** - **Vehicle life** is measured in **km / hours**, not years. - **Preventive Maintenance (PM)** is the *backbone* of life extension — fixed-interval servicing. - **Predictive Maintenance (PdM)** is more efficient but needs sensors/instruments. - **The 5-R Framework:** Right Fuel · Routine Maintenance · Record Keeping · Restraint in Operation · Repair-before-failure. - **Bathtub Curve** has three phases: Infant mortality → Useful life → Wear-out. - **CPK formula:** $CPK = C_{\text{total}} / D$ (always include depreciation). - **MTBF formula:** $MTBF = T / N$ — benchmark > **20,000 km** for trucks. - **Availability formula:** $A = (D_{\text{avail}} / D_{\text{total}}) \times 100\%$ — benchmark > **90%**. - **Depreciation:** $(C - S) / n$ where $n$ is the useful life in years. - **Driver behavior** contributes 15–25% of total wear. - **Overloading** — every 10% overload → 20% suspension life reduction. - **Lubrication grade** correctness alone can reduce engine wear by **40%**. - **Tire rotation** every 10,000 km extends total tire life by **25%**. - **Anti-rust undercoating** is critical in coastal Kerala (humidity, salt). - **Always state units** in every formula — KTU awards partial marks for unit consistency. - **Engineering judgment** is required in conclusion — don't just calculate, *interpret*. - **Records** are a *mandatory* assumption in any KTU problem — assume a service logbook exists. > **End of Notes — Module 1, Topic: Strategies for Extending Vehicle Life**

topics.title: Disposal and Resale of Vehicles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT866 - VEHICLE TRANSPORT MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Disposal and Resale of Vehicles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Vehicle Disposal** is the systematic, planned process of removing a vehicle from active operational fleet service once it reaches the end of its economically useful life, followed by its transfer through resale, auction, scrapping, or trade-in channels. **Vehicle Resale** refers specifically to the commercial re-transfer of a used (pre-owned) vehicle from the current owner (fleet operator) to a secondary buyer in the open market, generating recovery value based on depreciated worth, market demand, and remaining service potential.

In the context of **Transport Fleet Management**, disposal and resale represent the terminal stage of the **Vehicle Life Cycle**, completing the closed-loop cycle of: *Acquisition → Operation → Maintenance → Disposition*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Disposal and resale form the critical "value recovery" phase. The decision to dispose is NOT arbitrary — it is a financially optimized decision based on the comparison between *Annual Operating Cost (AOC)* of retaining the old vehicle versus the *Equivalent Annual Cost (EAC)* of a replacement unit.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you own a **smartphone**. After 3 years:
- The battery health drops to 75%
- Repair costs are rising
- New models offer 3× better efficiency

You now have three choices:
1. **Resell** it on OLX/Quikr (Classified Marketplace) — recover some money based on condition.
2. **Trade-in** at the Apple/Samsung store — get an exchange discount on a new phone.
3. **Scrap/Recycle** it — sell the gold, copper, and rare-earth metals inside.

**Vehicle disposal works EXACTLY the same way**, just on a much larger financial scale. A truck or bus costing ₹25 Lakhs can recover ₹4–6 Lakhs through resale or ₹50,000–1 Lakh through scrap. The *timing* and *method* of disposal can mean a difference of **several lakhs of rupees** to a fleet operator.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Industry Metric:** The average commercial vehicle in India (a TATA LPT truck, for example) has an optimal economic life of **7–10 years** or **8,00,000 – 10,00,000 km**, whichever comes first. Beyond this, the **Annual Operating Cost (AOC)** rises sharply due to escalating maintenance.

## 1.3 Core Disposal Channels (The 5 Standard Routes)

| Channel | Description | Typical Recovery |
|---|---|---|
| **Direct Resale** | Sold to private buyer or dealer | 20–35% of original cost |
| **Auction** | Public/online auction (e.g., CARS24, Spinny for cars) | 15–30% of original cost |
| **Trade-in Exchange** | Exchanged with OEM for new vehicle | 18–32% of original cost |
| **Scrap/Salvage** | Sold to scrap dealers for parts & metal | 5–10% of original cost |
| **Inter-company Transfer** | Shifted to a less-demanding branch | Book value based |

> [!NOTE]
> The choice among these channels is governed by the **Disposal Decision Matrix**, which balances *vehicle condition, market demand, urgency of disposal, tax implications, and administrative cost*.

## 1.4 Physical & Financial Constants Used in Disposal Calculations

- **Standard Fleet Economic Life:** **7 to 10 years** for commercial vehicles.
- **Scrap Value (Salvage Value) Factor:** Typically **5% to 10%** of the original purchase price.
- **Resale Value Drop Pattern:** Steepest in **Year 1 (15–20% drop)**, then gradual decline.
- **Vehicle Age Categories:**
  - *New:* 0–2 years
  - *Used (Certified):* 2–5 years
  - *Old:* 5–8 years
  - *End-of-Life:* 8+ years
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework: The Disposition Decision

The decision to dispose a vehicle is fundamentally a **cost-minimization problem** in fleet economics. A fleet manager must continuously compare two alternatives:

**Option A — Retain the old vehicle:**
- Pay rising maintenance, fuel inefficiency penalties, and downtime costs.
- Continue depreciating the asset on books.

**Option B — Dispose and Replace:**
- Recover resale/scrap value.
- Incur capital expenditure on new vehicle.
- Benefit from lower AOC and higher reliability.

The optimal disposal point is reached when:

$$EAC_{old} \geq EAC_{new} + \text{Disposal Cost Premium}$$

Where $EAC$ is the *Equivalent Annual Cost* of operating the vehicle.

## 2.2 The Four Pillars of Disposal Strategy

### Pillar 1: **Timing of Disposal (Economic Life Determination)**
- Determined by *Minimum EAC* principle.
- Influenced by usage intensity (km/year), load profile, and maintenance history.
- **Rule of Thumb:** Dispose when **Annual Maintenance Cost > 40% of Annual Depreciation**.

### Pillar 2: **Valuation Method**
The resale value is calculated using one of three depreciation frameworks (covered in Section 3):
1. **Straight-Line Method (SLM)**
2. **Declining Balance Method (DBM) / Written Down Value (WDV)**
3. **Sum-of-Years-Digits Method (SYD)**

### Pillar 3: **Disposal Channel Selection**
- Choice between *Resale, Auction, Trade-in, Scrap* based on:
  - Vehicle age and condition
  - Market demand for that make/model
  - Speed of disposal required
  - Documentation complexity (RC transfer, NOC, fitness certificate)

### Pillar 4: **Regulatory & Documentation Compliance**
Under the **Motor Vehicles Act, 1988** (India) and **Central Motor Vehicles Rules, 1989**:
- **Form 29** — Notice of Transfer of Ownership.
- **Form 30** — Application for intimation of transfer.
- **NOC** (No Objection Certificate) from the financing bank if under loan.
- **Fitness Certificate** renewal for vehicles older than 15 years.
- **Pollution Under Control (PUC) Certificate** mandatory for resale.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Straight-Line Depreciation (Annual)** | $D_a = \dfrac{C - S}{N}$ | $C$ = Capital Cost (₹), $S$ = Salvage Value (₹), $N$ = Useful Life (years) | Uniform wear, simple fleets |
| 2 | **Book Value (SLM) at year $t$** | $BV_t = C - t \cdot D_a$ | $t$ = years elapsed | Resale asking price base |
| 3 | **Declining Balance Depreciation (Annual)** | $D_t = BV_{t-1} \times r$ | $r$ = depreciation rate (decimal) | High-tech vehicles, fast obsolescence |
| 4 | **Double Declining Balance Rate** | $r_{DDB} = \dfrac{2}{N}$ | $N$ = useful life in years | Standard for commercial vehicles in India |
| 5 | **Written Down Value (after $t$ years)** | $BV_t = C \times (1 - r)^t$ | $r$ = WDV rate | Income Tax Act compliance |
| 6 | **Sum-of-Years-Digits (SYD)** | $D_t = \dfrac{(N - t + 1)}{SYD} \times (C - S)$ | $SYD = \dfrac{N(N+1)}{2}$ | Accelerated depreciation front-loading |
| 7 | **Resale Value Ratio (RVR)** | $RVR = \dfrac{\text{Expected Resale Price}}{C}$ | Dimensionless (0–1) | Cross-fleet comparison metric |
| 8 | **Net Recovery from Disposal** | $NR = P_{sale} - C_{disposal} - T_{transfer}$ | $P_{sale}$ = Sale Price, $C_{disposal}$ = Disposal cost, $T_{transfer}$ = Tax/Transfer fees | True economic recovery |
| 9 | **Equivalent Annual Cost (EAC)** | $EAC = \dfrac{(C - S)}{N} + \text{Annual OpEx}$ | OpEx = Operating Expenses (₹/year) | Optimal replacement timing |
| 10 | **Cost Per Kilometer (CPK)** | $CPK = \dfrac{\text{Total Cost of Ownership}}{\text{Total km operated}}$ | ₹/km | Benchmark for disposal decision |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Questions on disposal most commonly test the *Straight-Line Method* and the *Declining Balance Method*. Always mention the **salvage value assumption** — examiners specifically look for this in 14-mark problems.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **commercial transport operations** (KSRTC, private fleet operators, logistics companies like Delhivery, BlueDart), the disposal decision is tied to:
- **GST implications** — Input Tax Credit (ITC) reversal on sale of capital goods.
- **Income Tax compliance** — Depreciation claimed under Section 32 of the Income Tax Act, 1961.
- **TCO (Total Cost of Ownership) optimization** — Reducing per-km cost across the fleet.
- **Emission compliance** — BS-VI vehicles with old BS-IV units face resale discounts of **20–30%** due to regulatory pressure.

> [!NOTE]
> A well-timed resale of a 5-year-old commercial vehicle in India typically recovers **35–45% of original cost**, while late disposal (8+ years) recovers only **15–20%**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation 1: Straight-Line Method (SLM) Resale Value

**Problem Setup:** A fleet operator purchases a delivery van for **₹15,00,000**. The expected useful life is **8 years**, and the estimated salvage value at end-of-life is **₹1,50,000**. Calculate the **resale value at the end of Year 5** using the Straight-Line Method.

### Step 1: Identify the Variables
- Capital Cost ($C$) = ₹15,00,000
- Salvage Value ($S$) = ₹1,50,000
- Useful Life ($N$) = 8 years
- Time elapsed ($t$) = 5 years

### Step 2: Calculate Annual Depreciation
The annual depreciation is the uniform reduction in value per year:

$$
D_a = \frac{C - S}{N}
$$

$$
D_a = \frac{15{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}50{,}000}{8}
$$

$$
D_a = \frac{13{,}50{,}000}{8}
$$

$$
D_a = 1{,}68{,}750 \text{ ₹/year}
$$

**Interpretation:** Every year, the van loses ₹1,68,750 of its book value uniformly, assuming equal wear.

### Step 3: Calculate Book Value at End of Year 5
The book value (the theoretical resale value under SLM) is the original cost minus the accumulated depreciation over 5 years:

$$
BV_5 = C - (t \times D_a)
$$

$$
BV_5 = 15{,}00{,}000 - (5 \times 1{,}68{,}750)
$$

$$
BV_5 = 15{,}00{,}000 - 8{,}43{,}750
$$

$$
BV_5 = 6{,}56{,}250 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Result:** The **theoretical resale value** of the van at the end of Year 5 is **₹6,56,250**.

### Step 4: Validate Against Market Reality
The *actual* market resale value may be lower (or higher) than the book value due to:
- Brand reputation (TATA, Ashok Leyland, Mahindra)
- Odometer reading
- Accident history
- Service record
- Regional demand

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Always state in your answer: *"The book value computed using SLM is a theoretical value; the actual market resale value may deviate based on the vehicle's physical condition, market demand, and brand perception."*

---

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation 2: Declining Balance Method (Written Down Value)

**Problem Setup:** A logistics company buys a refrigerated truck for **₹30,00,000**. Using the **Double Declining Balance Method** with a useful life of **10 years**, compute the depreciation and book value at the **end of Year 4**.

### Step 1: Calculate the Depreciation Rate ($r$)
For the Double Declining Balance (DDB) method, the rate is twice the straight-line rate:

$$
r = \frac{2}{N} = \frac{2}{10} = 0.20 = 20\%
$$

### Step 2: Build the Depreciation Schedule Year-by-Year

**Year 1:**
$$
D_1 = C \times r = 30{,}00{,}000 \times 0.20 = 6{,}00{,}000
$$
$$
BV_1 = 30{,}00{,}000 - 6{,}00{,}000 = 24{,}00{,}000
$$

**Year 2:**
$$
D_2 = BV_1 \times r = 24{,}00{,}000 \times 0.20 = 4{,}80{,}000
$$
$$
BV_2 = 24{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}80{,}000 = 19{,}20{,}000
$$

**Year 3:**
$$
D_3 = BV_2 \times r = 19{,}20{,}000 \times 0.20 = 3{,}84{,}000
$$
$$
BV_3 = 19{,}20{,}000 - 3{,}84{,}000 = 15{,}36{,}000
$$

**Year 4:**
$$
D_4 = BV_3 \times r = 15{,}36{,}000 \times 0.20 = 3{,}07{,}200
$$
$$
BV_4 = 15{,}36{,}000 - 3{,}07{,}200 = 12{,}28{,}800
$$

### Step 3: Tabulate the Final Schedule

| Year | Opening BV (₹) | Depreciation @ 20% (₹) | Closing BV (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 30,00,000 | 6,00,000 | 24,00,000 |
| 2 | 24,00,000 | 4,80,000 | 19,20,000 |
| 3 | 19,20,000 | 3,84,000 | 15,36,000 |
| 4 | 15,36,000 | 3,07,200 | **12,28,800** |

### Step 4: Derive the Closed-Form WDV Formula
The pattern above is a **geometric progression**. We can express the book value at the end of year $t$ as:

$$
BV_t = C \times (1 - r)^t
$$

**Verification at Year 4:**

$$
BV_4 = 30{,}00{,}000 \times (1 - 0.20)^4
$$

$$
BV_4 = 30{,}00{,}000 \times (0.80)^4
$$

$$
BV_4 = 30{,}00{,}000 \times 0.4096
$$

$$
BV_4 = 12{,}28{,}800 \text{ ₹} \quad \checkmark
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** The WDV method front-loads depreciation, recognizing that vehicles lose value faster in the early years. This matches real-world market behavior where a 1-year-old vehicle loses 15–20% of its value, but a 5-year-old vehicle depreciates more slowly in percentage terms.

---

## 3.3 Detailed Derivation 3: Sum-of-Years-Digits (SYD) Method

**Problem Setup:** A taxi company purchases a Maruti Eeco for **₹6,00,000**. Useful life = **5 years**, Salvage Value = **₹1,00,000**. Compute depreciation for **each year** using the SYD method.

### Step 1: Calculate the Sum of Years Digits (SYD)

$$
SYD = \frac{N(N+1)}{2} = \frac{5 \times 6}{2} = 15
$$

### Step 2: Compute Depreciable Base

$$
\text{Depreciable Base} = C - S = 6{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000 = 5{,}00{,}000
$$

### Step 3: Apply SYD Formula Year-by-Year

The general formula for year $t$:

$$
D_t = \frac{(N - t + 1)}{SYD} \times (C - S)
$$

**Year 1:**
$$
D_1 = \frac{5}{15} \times 5{,}00{,}000 = 1{,}66{,}667
$$

**Year 2:**
$$
D_2 = \frac{4}{15} \times 5{,}00{,}000 = 1{,}33{,}333
$$

**Year 3:**
$$
D_3 = \frac{3}{15} \times 5{,}00{,}000 = 1{,}00{,}000
$$

**Year 4:**
$$
D_4 = \frac{2}{15} \times 5{,}00{,}000 = 66{,}667
$$

**Year 5:**
$$
D_5 = \frac{1}{15} \times 5{,}00{,}000 = 33{,}333
$$

### Step 4: Verify the Schedule

| Year | Remaining Life Fraction | Depreciation (₹) | Accumulated Dep. (₹) | Book Value (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 5/15 | 1,66,667 | 1,66,667 | 4,33,333 |
| 2 | 4/15 | 1,33,333 | 3,00,000 | 3,00,000 |
| 3 | 3/15 | 1,00,000 | 4,00,000 | 2,00,000 |
| 4 | 2/15 | 66,667 | 4,66,667 | 1,33,333 |
| 5 | 1/15 | 33,333 | 5,00,000 | 1,00,000 |

> [!NOTE]
> The book value at end of Year 5 = ₹1,00,000, which correctly equals the salvage value $S$. ✓

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: Choosing the Disposal Method

| Vehicle Scenario | Recommended Depreciation Method | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| **Heavy commercial trucks** (TATA, AL) | WDV / DDB | High early wear, technology depreciation |
| **Office staff cars** (Sedans) | SLM | Predictable usage, company policy |
| **Construction equipment** | SYD | Front-loaded wear in harsh early years |
| **Electric vehicles / Modern tech** | WDV / DDB | Rapid obsolescence risk |
| **Government / PSU fleets** | SLM | Audit-friendly, transparent |
| **Taxi / Ride-share vehicles** | WDV | High km/year, front-loaded value loss |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Vehicle Life Cycle & Disposal Position

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["STAGE 1: ACQUISITION"] --> nodeB["STAGE 2: OPERATION"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["STAGE 3: MAINTENANCE"]
    nodeC --> nodeD{"DISPOSAL DECISION POINT"}
    nodeD --> nodeE["EAC of Old > EAC of New ?"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["YES: Proceed to Disposal"]
    nodeE --> nodeG["NO: Continue Operation"]
    nodeG --> nodeC
    nodeF --> nodeH["STAGE 4A: VALUATION"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["STAGE 4B: CHANNEL SELECTION"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["Direct Resale"]
    nodeI --> nodeK["Auction Sale"]
    nodeI --> nodeL["Trade-in to OEM"]
    nodeI --> nodeM["Scrap and Salvage"]
    nodeJ --> nodeN["RECOVERY OF CAPITAL"]
    nodeK --> nodeN
    nodeL --> nodeN
    nodeM --> nodeN
    nodeN --> nodeO["FLEET RENEWAL LOOP"]
    nodeO --> nodeA
```

## 4.2 Disposal Channel Selection Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA["Vehicle Marked for Disposal"] --> condA{"Vehicle Age?"}
    condA --> youngA["Less than 5 years"]
    condA --> midA["5 to 8 years"]
    condA --> oldA["More than 8 years"]
    youngA --> chanA["Resale or Trade-in"]
    midA --> condB{"Market Demand High?"}
    condB --> yesB["Auction Sale"]
    condB --> noB["Direct Resale to Dealer"]
    oldA --> chanC["Scrap or Salvage to Parts Dealer"]
    chanA --> docA["Documentation: Form 29, 30, NOC"]
    yesB --> docA
    noB --> docA
    chanC --> docA
    docA --> endA["Disposal Complete and Capital Recovered"]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Resale Value Computation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inp1["Original Cost C"] --> proc1["Apply Depreciation Method"]
    inp2["Useful Life N"] --> proc1
    inp3["Salvage Value S"] --> proc1
    inp4["Time Elapsed t"] --> proc1
    proc1 --> out1["Book Value BV"]
    out1 --> adj1["Adjust for Condition"]
    adj1 --> adj2["Adjust for Market Demand"]
    adj2 --> adj3["Adjust for Odometer"]
    adj3 --> adj4["Adjust for Brand and Model"]
    adj4 --> final["Final Resale Value Offered"]
```

## 4.4 Comparative Block Architecture: SLM vs WDV vs SYD

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start1["Depreciation Method Selection"] --> q1{"Type of Asset?"}
    q1 --> q1a["Uniform Wear Asset"] --> m1["STRAIGHT LINE METHOD"]
    q1 --> q1b["High Early Depreciation Asset"] --> m2["DECLINING BALANCE / WDV"]
    q1 --> q1c["Front-loaded Wear Pattern"] --> m3["SUM OF YEARS DIGITS"]
    m1 --> out1["Constant D per year"]
    m2 --> out2["Reducing Balance Geometric"]
    m3 --> out3["Arithmetic Series Front Loaded"]
    out1 --> app1["Used in SLM-based Resale"]
    out2 --> app2["Used in WDV-based Resale"]
    out3 --> app3["Used in SYD-based Resale"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safety Note:** All node IDs above are alphanumeric (e.g., `nodeA`, `condA`) to comply with Mermaid v10+ parser. No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`) are used as standalone IDs.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define vehicle disposal. List any four methods of vehicle disposal.**

**Model Answer:**

Vehicle disposal is the systematic process of retiring a vehicle from active fleet service once it reaches the end of its economically useful life and transferring it through resale, auction, trade-in, or scrap channels to recover residual value.

The four common methods of vehicle disposal are:
1. **Direct Resale** — Sold to a private buyer or used-car dealer.
2. **Auction Sale** — Sold through a public or online auction platform.
3. **Trade-in Exchange** — Exchanged with the OEM or dealer as part of purchasing a new vehicle.
4. **Scrap/Salvage** — Sold to scrap dealers for metal and reusable parts.

*(Word Count Target: ~70 words. Marks: 1 for definition + 2 for listing with brief explanation.)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**What is salvage value? How does it influence the resale value of a vehicle?**

**Model Answer:**

**Salvage Value (S)** is the estimated residual value of a vehicle at the end of its useful life, typically expressed as a percentage of the original purchase cost (commonly 5% to 10% for commercial vehicles in India).

**Influence on Resale Value:**
- A **higher assumed salvage value** results in **lower annual depreciation**, which keeps the book value higher throughout the vehicle's life.
- A **lower salvage value** increases the depreciation charge, reducing the book value and the resale price.
- Salvage value is used as the **floor price** in depreciation calculations — no matter how old the vehicle, its book value cannot fall below the salvage value.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]**

A transport company purchases a delivery truck for **₹20,00,000**. The estimated useful life is **8 years**, and the salvage value is **₹2,00,000**.

**(a)** Calculate the annual depreciation and the book value at the end of the **5th year** using the **Straight-Line Method**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the same truck is depreciated using the **Double Declining Balance Method**, calculate the book value at the end of **Year 4**. Compare the two methods and comment on which method gives a **higher resale value at Year 4**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Straight-Line Method:**

**Step 1: Annual Depreciation**

$$
D_a = \frac{C - S}{N} = \frac{20{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000}{8} = \frac{18{,}00{,}000}{8} = 2{,}25{,}000 \text{ ₹/year}
$$

**Step 2: Book Value at End of Year 5**

$$
BV_5 = C - (t \times D_a) = 20{,}00{,}000 - (5 \times 2{,}25{,}000)
$$

$$
BV_5 = 20{,}00{,}000 - 11{,}25{,}000 = 8{,}75{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Result:** Annual depreciation = **₹2,25,000**; Book Value at Year 5 = **₹8,75,000**.

---

**Part (b) — Double Declining Balance Method:**

**Step 1: DDB Rate**

$$
r = \frac{2}{N} = \frac{2}{8} = 0.25 = 25\%
$$

**Step 2: Year-wise Book Value**

| Year | Opening BV (₹) | Depreciation @ 25% (₹) | Closing BV (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 20,00,000 | 5,00,000 | 15,00,000 |
| 2 | 15,00,000 | 3,75,000 | 11,25,000 |
| 3 | 11,25,000 | 2,81,250 | 8,43,750 |
| 4 | 8,43,750 | 2,10,938 | **6,32,813** |

**Step 3: Closed-Form Verification**

$$
BV_4 = 20{,}00{,}000 \times (1 - 0.25)^4 = 20{,}00{,}000 \times (0.75)^4
$$

$$
BV_4 = 20{,}00{,}000 \times 0.31640625 = 6{,}32{,}813 \text{ ₹} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 4: Comparison at End of Year 4**

| Method | Book Value at Year 4 (₹) |
|---|---|
| Straight-Line (BV₅ for reference) | (8,75,000 at Year 5) |
| Double Declining Balance | **6,32,813** |

> **Comment:** The **Straight-Line Method** retains a higher book value (and therefore resale value) in the early years compared to the Double Declining Balance Method. The DDB method front-loads depreciation, recognizing that vehicles lose value faster initially. For a fleet aiming to **maximize early resale recovery**, the **SLM is more favorable**; for **tax-shield benefits** in early years, **DDB is preferred**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the **Sum-of-Years-Digits (SYD) method** of depreciation. A mini-bus is purchased for **₹12,00,000** with a useful life of **5 years** and a salvage value of **₹1,00,000**. Compute the **depreciation for each year** using the SYD method. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **factors affecting the resale value of a vehicle** in the Indian commercial transport market. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — SYD Method Explanation and Computation:**

The **Sum-of-Years-Digits (SYD) method** is an accelerated depreciation technique in which the depreciable base $(C - S)$ is allocated in fractions based on the remaining useful life each year. The fraction for year $t$ is $\frac{(N - t + 1)}{SYD}$, where $SYD = \frac{N(N+1)}{2}$.

This method assumes that an asset loses more value in the early years of its life and less value in later years.

**Step 1: Compute SYD**

$$
SYD = \frac{5 \times 6}{2} = 15
$$

**Step 2: Depreciable Base**

$$
C - S = 12{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000 = 11{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 3: Year-wise Depreciation**

| Year | Fraction | Depreciation (₹) | Book Value (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 5/15 | 3,66,667 | 8,33,333 |
| 2 | 4/15 | 2,93,333 | 5,40,000 |
| 3 | 3/15 | 2,20,000 | 3,20,000 |
| 4 | 2/15 | 1,46,667 | 1,73,333 |
| 5 | 1/15 | 73,333 | 1,00,000 |

> **Verification:** The book value at the end of Year 5 = ₹1,00,000 = Salvage Value. ✓

---

**Part (b) — Factors Affecting Resale Value of a Vehicle:**

1. **Vehicle Age and Odometer Reading** — Older vehicles and higher km readings reduce resale value. A commercial vehicle with over 8,00,000 km fetches 30–40% lower price.

2. **Brand and Model Reputation** — Brands like TATA, Ashok Leyland, Mahindra retain value better in the Indian market due to service network and parts availability.

3. **Maintenance and Service History** — A well-documented service logbook can increase resale value by 10–15%.

4. **Accident History and Body Condition** — Accident-damaged vehicles suffer 20–40% depreciation penalty.

5. **Fuel Type and Emission Norms** — BS-VI vehicles command premium; older BS-IV vehicles face reduced demand and discounts.

6. **Market Demand and Seasonality** — Demand for used commercial vehicles peaks during festive and agricultural seasons (October–December).

7. **Regulatory and Legal Status** — Pending loans, RC issues, fitness certificate validity, and pending challans reduce buyer interest.

8. **Technology and Features** — Modern features (ABS, airbags, GPS, telematics) improve resale attractiveness.

9. **Regional Preferences** — Some vehicle types (e.g., TATA Ace mini-trucks) are in high demand in specific states, affecting regional resale prices.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Salvage Value (S):** Students often compute depreciation using only $C$ and $N$, forgetting that the formula requires $(C - S)$. This leads to a 1–2 mark deduction.
> 2. **Wrong DDB Rate:** Use $r = 2/N$ for DDB. Some students mistakenly use $1/N$ (which is SLM rate).
> 3. **No Year-wise Table in DDB/SLM Problems:** Always present a tabulated schedule for full marks (3–4 marks in 14-mark questions).
> 4. **Comparing Methods Without Numerical Evidence:** In comparison sub-questions, you must show the numbers side-by-side. Stating "DDB is higher" without computation loses 2 marks.
> 5. **Ignoring Documentation:** For 7-mark theory questions on disposal, mentioning **Form 29, Form 30, and NOC** earns extra credit.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Vehicle disposal = retiring a vehicle from fleet service; Vehicle resale = commercial re-transfer to a secondary buyer.
- **5 Disposal Channels:** Direct Resale, Auction, Trade-in, Scrap/Salvage, Inter-company Transfer.
- **Standard Economic Life:** 7–10 years for commercial vehicles; **5–7 years** for staff cars in India.
- **Salvage Value Range:** 5% to 10% of original cost.
- **SLM Formula:** $D_a = (C - S)/N$; $BV_t = C - t \cdot D_a$.
- **DDB Formula:** $r = 2/N$; $BV_t = C \times (1 - r)^t$.
- **SYD Formula:** $D_t = \frac{(N - t + 1)}{SYD} \times (C - S)$ where $SYD = N(N+1)/2$.
- **EAC Principle:** Dispose when EAC of old vehicle ≥ EAC of new replacement.
- **Documentation:** Form 29 (Notice of Transfer), Form 30 (Intimation), NOC from financer, Fitness Certificate (for >15-year-old), PUC.
- **Income Tax Link:** Depreciation under **Section 32, Income Tax Act, 1961** (typically 15% WDV for commercial vehicles).
- **Key Comparative Insight:** SLM gives **higher early resale value**; DDB/SYD give **higher early tax shields**.
- **Resale Value Benchmarks (India):** 5-year-old commercial vehicle → 35–45% recovery; 8-year-old → 15–20%; scrap → 5–10%.
- **Real-world Influencers:** Brand, odometer, accident history, service record, emission norms (BS-VI premium), regional demand.
- **Decision Rule of Thumb:** Dispose when **Annual Maintenance Cost > 40% of Annual Depreciation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Tip:** Module 1 questions on disposal-resale almost always combine a **numerical problem (SLM or DDB)** with a **theory sub-question (factors or documentation)**. Practice both formats extensively. Allocate 8–9 minutes for the numerical part and 5–6 minutes for the theory part in a 14-mark, 30-minute slot.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fleet Operations - Fleet Scheduling and Routing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT866 - VEHICLE TRANSPORT MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fleet Operations - Fleet Scheduling and Routing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — FLEET OPERATIONS: FLEET SCHEDULING AND ROUTING

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fleet Scheduling** is the strategic and tactical process of assigning vehicles from an available fleet to a defined set of transport tasks (trips, jobs, or deliveries) across a planning horizon, while determining the **departure time, sequence of stops, and crew assignment** for each vehicle so that operational objectives (cost, service level, utilization) are optimized subject to capacity, time-window, and regulatory constraints.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fleet Routing** is the spatial component of fleet operations: the determination of the **physical path** (sequence of nodes/links) each vehicle traverses between an origin depot and a set of customer locations, such that the total distance (or time, or cost) traveled is minimized while satisfying demand, vehicle capacity, and service-time constraints. It is formally modeled as the **Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP)**.

Together, scheduling (the *when* and *which vehicle*) and routing (the *where* and *in what order*) form the twin pillars of **Fleet Operations Planning** in transport management.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you run a **bakery chain in Kochi with 30 customers** who order fresh bread every morning. You have **3 delivery vans** parked at your central depot. Each van can carry only a limited number of bread boxes (capacity), each customer has a specific time window during which they accept delivery (e.g., 7–9 AM), and you want to spend the **least amount on fuel, driver wages, and van wear-and-tear**.

- **Scheduling** answers: *"Which van goes out, with what load, and leaves the depot at what time?"*
- **Routing** answers: *"In what order should each van visit its assigned customers?"*

If you simply sent all vans out in random directions, you would burn fuel, miss time windows, and overload some vans. Fleet scheduling and routing is the science of avoiding exactly that chaos — turning **scattered customer demand into optimized, repeatable routes**.

### Standard Metrics in Fleet Operations (Bolded for Emphasis)

- **Total Distance Traveled (km)** — sum of all link distances on chosen routes.
- **Total Operating Cost (₹)** — combines fuel, driver wages, depreciation, and maintenance.
- **Vehicle Utilization (%)** — *((Loaded km + Loaded hours) / (Total km + Total hours)) × 100*.
- **On-Time Performance (%)** — share of deliveries made within the promised time window.
- **Deadhead (Empty) Mileage (%)** — proportion of distance driven without load.
- **Load Factor (%)** — *((Total payload carried) / (Total vehicle capacity used)) × 100*.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Emphasis:** In the 2024 Scheme, examiners frequently test the *distinction* between scheduling and routing. Remember: scheduling is **time + vehicle assignment**, routing is **spatial sequence**.

---

## 1.2 Why Fleet Scheduling and Routing Matter

A well-optimized fleet operation can:
1. Reduce fuel consumption by **10–25%**.
2. Improve vehicle utilization by **15–30%**.
3. Increase on-time delivery rates from **~70% to over 95%**.
4. Reduce the required fleet size by **one or more vehicles** through better loading.

In KTU board questions, expect a clear link to **logistics cost minimization** and **service-level maximization** as the dual objective.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Building Blocks of Fleet Scheduling and Routing

### 2.1.1 Inputs (Decision Data)

| **Input Parameter** | **Symbol** | **Unit** | **Meaning** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Number of customers | $n$ | count | Demand points to be served |
| Number of vehicles | $K$ | count | Fleet size available at depot |
| Vehicle capacity | $Q$ | kg / m³ / units | Maximum load a single vehicle can carry |
| Customer demand | $q_i$ | kg / units | Load required at node $i$ |
| Distance / cost matrix | $d_{ij}$ | km or ₹ | Cost to travel from node $i$ to node $j$ |
| Service time | $s_i$ | minutes | Time spent at customer $i$ |
| Time window at $i$ | $[e_i, \ell_i]$ | hours | Earliest and latest acceptable service start |
| Depot index | $0$ (or $n+1$) | — | Origin and end of all routes |

### 2.1.2 Decision Variables

- $x_{ij}^{k} \in \{0,1\}$: equals **1** if vehicle $k$ travels directly from node $i$ to node $j$, **0** otherwise.
- $y_i^{k} \in \{0,1\}$: equals **1** if customer $i$ is assigned to vehicle $k$.
- $t_i$: time at which service begins at node $i$.

### 2.1.3 The Core Objective

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Primary Objective of the Classical VRP:** Minimize the **total distance (or cost) traveled by the entire fleet**, subject to every customer being visited exactly once, every route starting and ending at the depot, and vehicle capacity never being violated.

### 2.1.4 Constraints (Logical Bullets)

- **Every customer is visited exactly once** — no double-serving, no skipping.
- **Every route starts and ends at the depot** — vehicles return to base.
- **Capacity constraint** — the sum of demands on any single route cannot exceed $Q$.
- **Time-window constraint** — service at node $i$ must begin in $[e_i, \ell_i]$.
- **Flow conservation** — what comes into a node must go out (vehicle continuity).
- **Subtour elimination** — the solution must be a valid set of complete tours, not fragments.

## 2.2 Classical Variants of the Routing Problem

| **Variant** | **Abbreviation** | **Extra Feature Beyond Basic VRP** |
|---|---|---|
| Capacitated VRP | **CVRP** | Vehicle capacity $Q$ constraint |
| VRP with Time Windows | **VRPTW** | Each customer has $[e_i, \ell_i]$ |
| VRP with Pickups and Deliveries | **VRPPD** | Goods move from origin to destination customers |
| Open VRP | **OVRP** | Vehicles need NOT return to the depot |
| Periodic VRP | **PVRP** | Visits are scheduled over multiple days |
| VRP with Backhauls | **VRPB** | Deliveries first, then pickups on return leg |
| Split Delivery VRP | **SDVRP** | A customer may be served by multiple vehicles |

## 2.3 The Scheduling Component

While routing handles *spatial sequence*, scheduling adds:

1. **Dispatch timing** — at what hour does vehicle $k$ leave the depot?
2. **Driver shift allocation** — which driver (with HOS limits) operates which vehicle?
3. **Trip chaining** — can vehicle $k$ perform multiple trips in a day?
4. **Multi-depot assignment** — for large fleets, which depot services which zone?

The mathematical linkage: once a route is fixed, the schedule is determined by adding service times $s_i$ and travel times $t_{ij}$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Table for Board Examinations** — memorize the structure and units.

| **Formula / Equation** | **Meaning** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|
| $Z = \min \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{i=0}^{n} \sum_{j=0}^{n} d_{ij} \, x_{ij}^{k}$ | Minimize total fleet travel cost | km or ₹ |
| $\sum_{k=1}^{K} y_i^{k} = 1 \quad \forall i \in \{1,\dots,n\}$ | Each customer served by exactly one vehicle | dimensionless |
| $\sum_{i=1}^{n} q_i \, y_i^{k} \leq Q \quad \forall k$ | Vehicle $k$ capacity limit | kg / units |
| $t_j \geq t_i + s_i + T_{ij} - M(1 - x_{ij}^{k})$ | Time continuity along route | hours |
| $e_i \leq t_i \leq \ell_i$ | Time-window feasibility at $i$ | hours |
| $\sum_{j=0}^{n} x_{ij}^{k} = \sum_{j=0}^{n} x_{ji}^{k} = y_i^{k}$ | Flow conservation at node $i$ | dimensionless |
| $\text{Utilization} = \dfrac{D_{\text{loaded}}}{D_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ | Vehicle utilization ratio | % |
| $\text{Load Factor} = \dfrac{\sum q_i}{K \cdot Q} \times 100$ | Effective use of fleet capacity | % |
| $C_{\text{total}} = C_{\text{fuel}} + C_{\text{driver}} + C_{\text{depr}} + C_{\text{maint}}$ | Total operating cost decomposition | ₹ |
| $\text{Deadhead \%} = \dfrac{D_{\text{empty}}}{D_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ | Empty-run proportion | % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pitfall Reminder:** In the markdown table above, vertical bars were deliberately replaced with `\vert`-style phrasing to keep table syntax safe. When writing absolute-value expressions on the board, prefer $\vert x \vert$ rendered with the `\vert` LaTeX command.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **E-commerce logistics** (Amazon, Flipkart): VRPTW with same-day delivery windows.
- **Public transit**: bus scheduling and crew rostering in city transport corporations (KSRTC).
- **Cold chain**: refrigerated routing with perishable time windows.
- **Solid waste collection**: minimizing route length while serving every neighborhood.
- **School bus routing**: VRP with capacity and student pickup time constraints.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme specifically highlights **public transport fleet scheduling** and **goods transport routing** as exam-relevant applications.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: From Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP) to the Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP)

We begin with the **Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP)** — the single-vehicle ancestor of the VRP — and progressively relax and generalize it to obtain the full VRP formulation.

### Step 1 — Formal Statement of the TSP

A salesman starts at depot node $0$, must visit every customer node $i \in \{1, 2, \dots, n\}$ **exactly once**, and return to node $0$. Find the tour with minimum total distance.

$$
Z_{\text{TSP}} = \min \sum_{i=0}^{n} \sum_{j=0, j \neq i}^{n} d_{ij} \, x_{ij}
$$

Subject to:
- $\sum_{j=0, j \neq i}^{n} x_{ij} = 1$ for every $i$ (leave each city once),
- $\sum_{i=0, i \neq j}^{n} x_{ij} = 1$ for every $j$ (enter each city once),
- subtour elimination constraints (MTZ or DFJ formulation).

### Step 2 — Introducing Vehicle Capacity $Q$

In the TSP, capacity is unlimited. In real fleets, vehicle $k$ can carry at most $Q$ units. Define:

$$
\sum_{i \in \text{route}(k)} q_i \;\leq\; Q
$$

This converts the **single-tour TSP** into the **Capacitated Vehicle Routing Problem (CVRP)**.

### Step 3 — Introducing Multiple Vehicles and Route Index $k$

Index each vehicle by $k \in \{1, 2, \dots, K\}$. The decision variable $x_{ij}^{k}$ now depends on vehicle identity.

$$
Z_{\text{CVRP}} = \min \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{i=0}^{n} \sum_{j=0, j \neq i}^{n} d_{ij} \, x_{ij}^{k}
$$

### Step 4 — Capacity Constraint Derivation

The total demand assigned to vehicle $k$ equals the sum of $q_i$ over customers $i$ served by $k$. The decision variable $y_i^{k}$ is linked to $x_{ij}^{k}$ via:

$$
y_i^{k} = \sum_{j=0}^{n} x_{ij}^{k}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n} q_i \, y_i^{k} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} q_i \sum_{j=0}^{n} x_{ij}^{k} \;\leq\; Q \quad \forall k \in \{1,\dots,K\}
$$

### Step 5 — Adding Time Windows (VRPTW)

Introduce a time variable $t_i$ for service start at node $i$. Travel time between $i$ and $j$ is $T_{ij}$. For each arc $(i,j)$ traversed by vehicle $k$:

$$
t_j \;\geq\; t_i + s_i + T_{ij} - M \left(1 - x_{ij}^{k}\right)
$$

Here $M$ is a large positive constant (Big-M method) that relaxes the constraint when $x_{ij}^{k} = 0$. Combined with the time-window bounds:

$$
e_i \;\leq\; t_i \;\leq\; \ell_i \quad \forall i \in \{1,\dots,n\}
$$

### Step 6 — Full VRPTW Objective Function (Complete Form)

$$
Z_{\text{VRPTW}} = \min \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{i=0}^{n} \sum_{j=0, j \neq i}^{n} \left[ c_{ij} \, x_{ij}^{k} + \alpha \cdot \max\!\left(0,\, t_i - \ell_i\right) + \beta \cdot \max\!\left(0,\, e_i - t_i\right) \right]
$$

where $c_{ij}$ is the arc cost (distance or ₹), and $\alpha, \beta$ are penalties for early or late service.

### Step 7 — Economic Translation: Total Operating Cost

For board questions that ask *"estimate the daily fleet operating cost"*, decompose cost per kilometer:

$$
C_{\text{per-km}} = C_{\text{fuel}} + C_{\text{driver}} + C_{\text{depr}} + C_{\text{maint}}
$$

| **Cost Component** | **Typical Formula** | **Typical Range** |
|---|---|---|
| Fuel | $\dfrac{\text{Fuel price (₹/L)}}{\text{Mileage (km/L)}}$ | ₹4–₹8 per km |
| Driver wages | $\dfrac{\text{Daily wage (₹)}}{\text{Loaded km per day}}$ | ₹2–₹5 per km |
| Depreciation | $\dfrac{\text{Vehicle cost}}{\text{Life km}}$ | ₹1–₹3 per km |
| Maintenance | empirical — usually ₹1–₹2 per km | ₹1–₹2 per km |

$$
C_{\text{daily fleet}} = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \left( D_k \cdot C_{\text{per-km}} \right) + \text{Fixed overhead}
$$

where $D_k$ is total km driven by vehicle $k$ in the planning day.

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Numerical Illustration:** Suppose 3 vehicles run **120, 95, and 140 km** respectively, and the per-km cost is **₹12**. Then
> $$C_{\text{daily}} = (120 + 95 + 140) \times 12 = 355 \times 12 = \text{₹}\,4{,}260$$
> Plus a fixed depot overhead of, say, ₹1,500 → **₹5,760/day total fleet cost**.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Solution Approaches

| **Method** | **Type** | **Solution Quality** | **Computation Time** | **Use Case** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Nearest Neighbor | Heuristic | Good (within 25%) | Very fast | Real-time dispatch |
| Clarke & Wright (Savings) | Heuristic | Good (within 10–15%) | Fast | Tactical planning |
| Sweep Algorithm | Heuristic | Moderate | Fast | Geographic clustering |
| 2-opt / 3-opt | Local Search | Good improvement | Moderate | Post-optimization |
| Tabu Search | Meta-heuristic | Very good | Slow | Strategic planning |
| Genetic Algorithm | Meta-heuristic | Near-optimal | Slow | Complex variants |
| Branch-and-Bound | Exact | Optimal | Very slow (NP-hard) | Small instances (n ≤ 25) |
| Integer Linear Programming (ILP) | Exact | Optimal | Very slow | Benchmark / research |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** The **Clarke and Wright Savings Algorithm** is the most commonly asked heuristic in KTU board papers. Memorize its savings formula:
> $$s_{ij} = d_{0i} + d_{0j} - d_{ij}$$
> The pair $(i,j)$ producing the largest positive savings is merged into a single route first.

## 3.3 Worked-Out Scheduling Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A depot serves 4 customers. Travel times (in minutes) are given below. Each customer needs 10 minutes of service. The driver works an **8-hour shift (480 minutes)**. Find the schedule for **one vehicle**.

| From \ To | Depot (0) | A | B | C | D |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Depot (0)** | 0 | 15 | 25 | 20 | 30 |
| **A** | 15 | 0 | 18 | 22 | 28 |
| **B** | 25 | 18 | 0 | 14 | 35 |
| **C** | 20 | 22 | 14 | 0 | 24 |
| **D** | 30 | 28 | 35 | 24 | 0 |

**Route chosen (by savings method):** Depot → A → B → C → D → Depot.

**Total travel time calculation:**
$$
T_{\text{travel}} = d_{0A} + d_{AB} + d_{BC} + d_{CD} + d_{D0} = 15 + 18 + 14 + 24 + 30 = 101 \text{ min}
$$

**Total service time:**
$$
T_{\text{service}} = 4 \times 10 = 40 \text{ min}
$$

**Total route duration:**
$$
T_{\text{total}} = 101 + 40 = 141 \text{ min}
$$

**Slack time within 8-hour shift:**
$$
T_{\text{slack}} = 480 - 141 = 339 \text{ min}
$$

This vehicle can therefore take a **second trip** in the same day — a clear demonstration of how routing and scheduling interact.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Fleet Scheduling and Routing Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputA[Customer Demand Data] --> proc1[Demand Aggregation Module]
    inputB[Vehicle Availability] --> proc1
    inputC[Time Window Constraints] --> proc1
    inputD[Distance Time Matrix] --> proc1
    proc1 --> proc2[Clustering by Geography]
    proc2 --> proc3[Initial Route Construction]
    proc3 --> proc4[Clarke Wright Savings]
    proc4 --> proc5[Capacity and Time Check]
    proc5 --> decision{Violation Found?}
    decision -->|Yes| proc6[Local Search 2 opt 3 opt]
    proc6 --> proc5
    decision -->|No| proc7[Schedule Generation]
    proc7 --> proc8[Driver and Shift Assignment]
    proc8 --> proc9[Dispatch Ready Route Plan]
    proc9 --> output[Final Route Schedule Output]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Customer demand, vehicle availability, time windows, and the travel matrix enter the system. They are aggregated, geographically clustered, then passed through the **Clarke–Wright savings algorithm** to build initial routes. A constraint-check loop iteratively improves the routes via **2-opt / 3-opt local search** until no violation remains. Finally, **driver-shift assignment** creates a dispatch-ready plan.

## 4.2 Schematic: Vehicle Routing Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    start([Customer Request Received]) --> checkCap{Vehicle Capacity OK?}
    checkCap -->|No| split[Split Delivery Across Vehicles]
    checkCap -->|Yes| checkTW{Within Time Window?}
    split --> checkTW
    checkTW -->|No| rejig[Re assign or Re schedule]
    checkTW -->|Yes| append[Add Customer to Route]
    rejig --> append
    append --> more{More Customers?}
    more -->|Yes| start
    more -->|No| finalize([Close Route Return to Depot])
```

## 4.3 Schematic: Savings Algorithm Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    s1[Initialize each customer as separate route] --> s2[Compute savings s_ij for every pair i,j]
    s2 --> s3[Sort savings in descending order]
    s3 --> s4{Pick highest positive savings}
    s4 --> s5{Combining violates capacity or time window?}
    s5 -->|Yes| s4
    s5 -->|No| s6[Merge routes of i and j]
    s6 --> s7{More savings pairs left?}
    s7 -->|Yes| s4
    s7 -->|No| s8[Output final merged routes]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Adaptation note:** Since the topic is operational rather than physical, the diagrams above are **functional block schematics** showing the *flow of decisions* rather than physical layouts. This follows the diagram-fallback protocol for non-physical engineering topics.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`
**Define the Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP). List any four real-world constraints that distinguish VRP from the classical Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP)** is the combinatorial optimization problem of designing optimal delivery or pickup routes from a central depot to a set of geographically dispersed customers using a finite fleet of vehicles, such that each customer is served exactly once and total route cost is minimized.

Four real-world constraints distinguishing VRP from TSP:
1. **Vehicle capacity limit** $Q$ — TSP assumes infinite capacity.
2. **Multiple vehicles** $K$ — TSP uses a single tour.
3. **Time windows $[e_i, \ell_i]$** — TSP ignores service timing.
4. **Multiple depots or pickup-delivery requirements** — TSP assumes pure return-to-origin delivery only.

*(Award 0.5 mark for definition, 2 marks for constraints, 0.5 mark for clarity.)*

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`
**Explain the term "deadhead mileage" in fleet operations. How does effective routing reduce it?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Deadhead mileage** is the distance a vehicle travels **without carrying any load** — typically the empty trip back to the depot or to the next pickup point. It is measured as a percentage of total mileage:

$$\text{Deadhead \%} = \frac{D_{\text{empty}}}{D_{\text{total}}} \times 100$$

Effective routing reduces deadhead mileage by:
- **Backhauling** — picking up return loads so the return trip is loaded.
- **Route consolidation** — fewer, fuller routes reduce empty repositioning.
- **Zone balancing** — assigning nearby customers to the same vehicle.

*(Award 1 mark for definition + formula, 2 marks for reduction strategies.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Formulate the **Capacitated Vehicle Routing Problem (CVRP)** as a mathematical programming model. Clearly state the sets, parameters, decision variables, objective function, and all constraints. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A logistics company operates **4 delivery vehicles** of capacity **50 units each** from a single depot. Five customers require delivery with demands **20, 15, 18, 22, 25** units. Using the **Clarke and Wright savings algorithm**, determine the initial route structure. The distance matrix from depot (D) is:
$D \to A = 10,\ D \to B = 15,\ D \to C = 12,\ D \to D\_cust = 18,\ D \to E = 20$ (km).
Pairwise distances: $A-B = 8,\ A-C = 12,\ A-D = 14,\ A-E = 22,\ B-C = 10,\ B-D = 9,\ B-E = 15,\ C-D = 7,\ C-E = 11,\ D-E = 6$.
Compute total distance of the merged routes. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for A(a) — CVRP Formulation (7 marks)

**Sets:**
- $C = \{1, 2, \dots, n\}$: customer nodes.
- $V = \{0\} \cup C$: all nodes including depot 0.
- $K = \{1, 2, \dots, m\}$: vehicles.

**Parameters:**
- $q_i$: demand at customer $i$.
- $Q$: vehicle capacity.
- $d_{ij}$: distance (cost) from $i$ to $j$.

**Decision Variables:**
- $x_{ij}^{k} \in \{0,1\}$: 1 if vehicle $k$ goes from $i$ to $j$.
- $y_i^{k} \in \{0,1\}$: 1 if customer $i$ is served by vehicle $k$.

**Objective Function:**

$$\min Z = \sum_{k \in K} \sum_{i \in V} \sum_{j \in V, j \neq i} d_{ij} \, x_{ij}^{k}$$

**Constraints:**

1. Each customer served once:
$$\sum_{k \in K} y_i^{k} = 1, \quad \forall i \in C$$

2. Capacity limit per vehicle:
$$\sum_{i \in C} q_i \, y_i^{k} \leq Q, \quad \forall k \in K$$

3. Flow conservation:
$$\sum_{j \in V} x_{ij}^{k} = \sum_{j \in V} x_{ji}^{k} = y_i^{k}, \quad \forall i \in C, \; \forall k \in K$$

4. Depot departure and return:
$$\sum_{j \in C} x_{0j}^{k} = 1, \quad \sum_{i \in C} x_{i0}^{k} = 1, \quad \forall k \in K$$

5. Subtour elimination (DFJ form):
$$\sum_{i \in S} \sum_{j \in S} x_{ij}^{k} \leq \vert S \vert - 1, \quad \forall S \subseteq C, \; \vert S \vert \geq 2, \; \forall k \in K$$

6. Binary restrictions:
$$x_{ij}^{k}, \; y_i^{k} \in \{0,1\}$$

*(Marks: 1 set, 1 parameters, 1 variables, 1 objective, 3 constraints.)*

---

#### Model Solution for A(b) — Clarke–Wright Savings (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Initial separate routes:** D-A, D-B, D-C, D-D\_cust, D-E (5 routes).

**Step 2 — Savings formula:**
$$s_{ij} = d_{0i} + d_{0j} - d_{ij}$$

**Step 3 — Compute all pair savings:**

| Pair $(i,j)$ | $d_{0i}$ | $d_{0j}$ | $d_{ij}$ | $s_{ij}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A,B | 10 | 15 | 8 | **17** |
| A,C | 10 | 12 | 12 | **10** |
| A,D | 10 | 18 | 14 | **14** |
| A,E | 10 | 20 | 22 | **8** |
| B,C | 15 | 12 | 10 | **17** |
| B,D | 15 | 18 | 9 | **24** |
| B,E | 15 | 20 | 15 | **20** |
| C,D | 12 | 18 | 7 | **23** |
| C,E | 12 | 20 | 11 | **21** |
| D,E | 18 | 20 | 6 | **32** |

**Step 4 — Sort descending:** D,E (32) → B,D (24) → C,D (23) → C,E (21) → B,E (20) → A,B (17) → B,C (17) → A,D (14) → A,C (10) → A,E (8).

**Step 5 — Capacity check while merging:**

- Merge D & E: combined demand = 22 + 25 = 47 ≤ 50 ✅. New route: **D-D\_cust-E-D**, distance = 18 + 6 + 20 = 44 km.
- Merge B with D\_cust-E route: 15 + 47 = 62 > 50 ❌ **Reject**.
- Try merging C with D-E route: 18 + 47 = 65 > 50 ❌ **Reject**.
- Merge C with E: 18 + 25 = 43 ≤ 50 ✅. New route: **D-D\_cust-C-E-D**, distance = 18 + 7 + 11 + 20 = 56 km. (Combine with D-E: combined demand 47 + 43 = 90 — only feasible as one route since 90 > 50, so we revert to separate routes or split.)

Let us redo with **strict per-route capacity**:

- Route 1: D → D\_cust → E → D. Demand = 47. Distance = 44 km.
- Route 2: D → B → C → D. Demand = 15 + 18 = 33 ≤ 50 ✅. Distance = 15 + 10 + 12 = 37 km.
- Route 3: D → A → D. Demand = 20. Distance = 10 + 10 = 20 km.

**Total distance:**
$$Z_{\text{total}} = 44 + 37 + 20 = 101 \text{ km}$$

**Vehicles used:** 3 out of 4.

*(Marks: 2 for savings table, 2 for capacity check, 1 for final route structure, 2 for total distance.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a)** Differentiate between **fleet scheduling** and **fleet routing** with a clear engineering example. Explain how **time-window constraints** affect vehicle dispatch decisions in a city bus transport system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **KSRTC-type city bus depot** operates **5 buses** on routes serving **6 bus stops** in addition to the depot. Each bus has a capacity of **60 passengers**. Average travel times between consecutive stops are given below. Service (dwell) time at each stop is **3 minutes**. The first shift starts at **6:00 AM** and ends at **10:00 AM** (240 minutes). Determine:
   (i) The dispatch time of each bus if a round-trip is to be completed within one shift.
   (ii) Total passenger-handling capacity of the fleet in the shift.
   (iii) Headway (time between successive bus departures). **(7 marks)**

Travel time matrix (minutes): D-S1: 8, S1-S2: 6, S2-S3: 5, S3-S4: 7, S4-S5: 6, S5-S6: 8, S6-D: 9.

---

#### Model Solution for B(a) (7 marks)

**Difference between scheduling and routing:**

| Aspect | Fleet Scheduling | Fleet Routing |
|---|---|---|
| Decides | *When* a vehicle departs and which driver/vehicle is assigned | *Which sequence of stops* the vehicle follows |
| Time dimension | Primary | Secondary |
| Space dimension | Secondary | Primary |
| Output | Dispatch timetable + crew assignment | Route map + ordered stop list |

**Engineering example:** A KSRTC depot in Thiruvananthapuram must send buses on Route 6 (Trivandrum → Kovalam). **Routing** decides the path (which via-points, e.g., Neyyattinkara or direct highway). **Scheduling** decides whether the 6:00 AM, 6:30 AM, and 7:00 AM departures are spaced evenly given passenger demand and traffic.

**Effect of time-window constraints on dispatch:**
- Buses must depart such that they arrive at each stop **within the passenger-arrival peak window** (e.g., office hours 8:30–9:30 AM).
- Tight time windows force **smaller headways** and **more vehicles**.
- A bus delayed beyond its window causes **passenger wait-time penalty**, often included in the objective as $\alpha \cdot \max(0, t_i - \ell_i)$.

*(Marks: 2 for difference table, 2 for example, 3 for time-window explanation.)*

---

#### Model Solution for B(b) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Compute one-way travel time:**
$$
T_{\text{one-way}} = 8 + 6 + 5 + 7 + 6 + 8 + 9 = 49 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 2 — Round-trip time including dwell:**
Number of stops served on one way = 6 (S1 to S6). Total dwell = $6 \times 3 = 18$ min.
$$
T_{\text{round-trip}} = 49 + 18 + 49 = 116 \text{ min}
$$

Wait — dwell applies both directions, so $T_{\text{rt}} = 49 \times 2 + 18 \times 2 = 98 + 36 = 134$ min. Let's adopt this:
$$
T_{\text{round-trip}} = 134 \text{ min}
$$

Actually, careful: dwell time of 3 min occurs at **every stop**, both directions. So total dwell = 6 stops × 2 directions × 3 min = 36 min.
$$
T_{\text{round-trip}} = 98 + 36 = 134 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 3 — Number of round-trips per bus per shift:**
$$
N_{\text{rt}} = \left\lfloor \frac{240}{134} \right\rfloor = 1 \text{ round-trip (with slack)}
$$

Each bus can therefore complete **1 full round-trip** and have slack $240 - 134 = 106$ min for a possible second partial trip.

**Step 4 — Dispatch times (i):**
With 5 buses completing 1 round-trip each, the simplest schedule is to dispatch all 5 buses at the **start of the shift** at staggered intervals.

**Step 5 — Total passenger capacity (ii):**
$$
\text{Capacity}_{\text{total}} = 5 \text{ buses} \times 60 \text{ passengers} \times 1 \text{ round-trip} = 300 \text{ passengers}
$$

If we assume a second partial trip is feasible in the slack:
$$
\text{Capacity}_{\text{total,max}} = 5 \times 60 \times 2 = 600 \text{ passengers}
$$

**Step 6 — Headway (iii):**
If all 5 buses depart at the **start of shift (6:00 AM)** in a tight schedule of 10 minutes:
$$
\text{Headway} = \frac{60 \text{ min}}{5 \text{ buses}} = 12 \text{ min}
$$

Or, more commonly, spread evenly across the shift:
$$
\text{Headway} = \frac{240}{5} = 48 \text{ min}
$$

A reasonable interpretation: headway = (shift duration) / (number of buses on route) = **48 minutes**.

*(Marks: 2 for time computation, 2 for dispatch schedule, 2 for capacity, 1 for headway.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Fleet Scheduling & Routing Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing scheduling with routing** — examiners explicitly award marks for distinguishing them. Do NOT use the terms interchangeably.
> 2. **Skipping the depot return constraint** — every route must end at node 0. Forgetting this loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Wrong capacity check order in Clarke–Wright** — students often merge without checking $Q$. The first valid merge is the *maximum* savings subject to capacity.
> 4. **Forgetting dwell/service time** in bus problems — the round-trip time = travel + service, not travel alone.
> 5. **Off-by-one errors in customer indexing** — depot is 0, customers are 1 to $n$. Mixing these breaks the model.
> 6. **Writing `|x|` inside markdown tables** — breaks the table render. Use `\vert x \vert` in LaTeX.
> 7. **Skipping units** in numerical answers — always state km, minutes, ₹, or passengers explicitly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Fleet Scheduling and Routing:**

- **Scheduling** = *which vehicle, which driver, when*; **Routing** = *where, in what order*.
- The **VRP** generalizes the **TSP** by adding capacity $Q$, multiple vehicles $K$, and optionally time windows $[e_i, \ell_i]$.
- **Objective:** minimize total distance / cost across all routes.
- **Core constraints:** each customer served exactly once, capacity respected, time windows respected, depot return, no subtours.
- **Savings formula (Clarke–Wright):** $s_{ij} = d_{0i} + d_{0j} - d_{ij}$.
- **Heuristic ordering (highest savings first):** merge if capacity not violated, otherwise skip.
- **Total operating cost** = fuel + driver + depreciation + maintenance (per-km × total km).
- **Vehicle utilization** = loaded km / total km × 100%.
- **Deadhead %** = empty km / total km × 100% — keep this LOW.
- **Round-trip time** for a bus route = 2 × (one-way travel) + dwell at every stop in both directions.
- **Headway** = shift duration / number of buses on the route.
- **Total passenger capacity** = buses × capacity per bus × number of trips.
- Always state **units** explicitly in numerical answers.
- The VRP is **NP-hard** — exact methods scale poorly; heuristics are used in practice.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Trip Planning and Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT866 - VEHICLE TRANSPORT MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Trip Planning and Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Trip Planning and Management** is the systematic engineering process of forecasting, organizing, scheduling, routing, and controlling vehicular movement between designated origin–destination (O-D) pairs to achieve optimal utilization of transport infrastructure while satisfying passenger/freight mobility demand. In the context of KTU's PEMUT866 syllabus, it encompasses the classical **Four-Step Transportation Planning Model (4STPM)**, supplemented by modern fleet-level **Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP)** solutions, **Travel Demand Forecasting**, and **Intelligent Transport System (ITS)** tools.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme – Module 2):** Trip planning is examined under the umbrella of *transportation systems management*, where students must demonstrate competency in **(i) trip generation modelling, (ii) trip distribution, (iii) modal split, and (iv) traffic assignment**, plus the **economic evaluation** of trip-based costs and **GIS-based route optimization**.

### Core Nomenclature
| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Trip** | A one-way movement from a single origin to a single destination for a single purpose. |
| **O-D Matrix** | A two-dimensional array quantifying the number of trips between all origin and destination zone pairs. |
| **TGM** | Trip Generation Model – predicts the number of trips produced/attracted per zone. |
| **Gravity Model** | Analogous to Newton's law of gravitation; estimates inter-zonal trip interchange. |
| **VRP** | Vehicle Routing Problem – combinatorial optimization for multi-vehicle route design. |
| **PHT** | Peak Hour Trip – trips occurring during the design peak hour (typically the **30th highest hour**). |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are organizing a **college fest** where **5,000 students** from **20 different departments** must travel from their hostels to **8 different event venues** spread across a sprawling campus. You have only **10 shuttle buses**.

- **Trip Planning** answers: *"How many students want to go from Department A to Venue X?"* → **Trip Generation & Distribution**
- It then asks: *"Will they walk, take a bus, or use a bicycle?"* → **Modal Split**
- Finally: *"Which exact road should each bus take, and in what order should it visit stops?"* → **Trip Assignment & Vehicle Routing**

In real engineering practice, instead of hostels and venues, we deal with **Traffic Analysis Zones (TAZs)** in a city, and the "shuttle buses" become cars, buses, trucks, and metro trains. The principle remains identical: **match demand with supply, minimize cost, and respect capacity constraints**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Every vehicle on the road is the *result* of a trip decision made earlier. Trip planning is therefore a **proactive (predictive)** discipline — it builds tomorrow's traffic by understanding today's choices.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

The following engineering constants and benchmark metrics must be memorized for KTU 2024 examinations:

- **Design Hour Factor (DHF):** Typically **0.10 to 0.15** of Average Annual Daily Traffic (AADT), corresponding to the **30th highest hourly volume**.
- **Peak Hour Factor (PHF):** $PHF = \dfrac{\text{Peak 15-min flow} \times 4}{\text{Total peak hour flow}}$ (range **0.70 – 1.00**).
- **Average Vehicle Occupancy (AVO):** **1.2 to 1.5 persons/vehicle** for urban passenger cars; **2.5 to 40** for buses.
- **Time Value of Travel:** Typically **30% to 50% of hourly wage rate** for work trips.
- **Planning Horizon:** Short-term = **5 years**, Medium-term = **10–20 years**, Long-term = **20–30 years**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** *"Plan for the 30th highest hour of the year, not the absolute peak."* This balances economy (avoiding over-design) with reliability (acceptable congestion only 29 hours/year).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four-Step Transportation Planning Model (4STPM)

The classical 4STPM is the **cornerstone** of any trip planning exercise. Each step transforms an input into a refined output, building up the complete picture of travel demand.

### Step 1 — Trip Generation
**Goal:** Predict the *total number of trips originating from* (production) and *terminating at* (attraction) each Traffic Analysis Zone (TAZ).

- **Trip Production (Pᵢ):** Home-based trips produced by zone *i* (linked to households, income, car ownership).
- **Trip Attraction (Aⱼ):** Trips attracted to zone *j* (linked to employment, retail floor area, school enrolment).

**Mathematical Foundation (Regression Form):**

$$
Y_i = a_0 + a_1 X_{1i} + a_2 X_{2i} + \dots + a_n X_{ni} + \varepsilon_i
$$

Where $Y_i$ is the dependent variable (trips produced/attracted), $X_{ki}$ are independent socio-economic variables, and $\varepsilon_i$ is the error term.

**Categories of Trips:**
1. **HBW** — Home-Based Work trips (commuter; peak-period dominant)
2. **HBNW** — Home-Based Non-Work trips (shopping, recreation)
3. **NHB** — Non-Home-Based trips (business, intermediate stops)

---

### Step 2 — Trip Distribution
**Goal:** Pair origins with destinations to form the **Origin-Destination (O-D) Matrix** $T_{ij}$.

**Three principal methods:**

#### (a) Uniform Growth Factor Method
$$
T_{ij}^{\text{new}} = T_{ij}^{\text{old}} \times E_i
$$

Where $E_i$ is the growth factor of zone *i*.

#### (b) Fratar Method (Iterative)
$$
T_{ij}^{\text{new}} = T_{ij}^{\text{old}} \times E_i \times F_j
$$

$$
F_j = \dfrac{A_j^{\text{new}}}{\displaystyle\sum_x T_{xj}^{\text{old}} \cdot E_x}
$$

The Fratar method converges quickly and accounts for *growth at both ends* of the trip.

#### (c) Gravity Model (Most Important for KTU)
Analogous to Newton's Law:

$$
T_{ij} = P_i \cdot \dfrac{A_j \cdot f(C_{ij})}{\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{n} A_k \cdot f(C_{ik})}
$$

Where $C_{ij}$ is the *generalized cost* (time, distance, fare) between zones *i* and *j*, and $f(C_{ij})$ is the **deterrence function** (typically inverse-power or exponential).

**Common Deterrence Functions:**

| Type | Formula | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| Inverse Power | $f(C_{ij}) = C_{ij}^{-b}$ | Long-distance, low-density |
| Exponential | $f(C_{ij}) = e^{-\beta C_{ij}}$ | Short-distance, urban |
| Tanner Function | $f(C_{ij}) = C_{ij}^{-b} \cdot e^{-\beta C_{ij}}$ | Combined effect |
| Gamma | $f(C_{ij}) = a \cdot C_{ij}^{-b} \cdot e^{-\beta C_{ij}}$ | Most flexible |

---

### Step 3 — Modal Split (Mode Choice)
**Goal:** Distribute trips among available transport modes (car, bus, train, walk, two-wheeler).

**Two modelling paradigms:**

1. **Trip-End (Aggregate) Models:** Applied *before* distribution, using zonal averages.
2. **Trip-Interchange (Disaggregate) Models:** Applied *after* distribution, using individual/household utility.

**The Logit Model (KTU Favourite):**

$$
P(m) = \dfrac{e^{U_m}}{\displaystyle\sum_{k} e^{U_k}}
$$

Where $P(m)$ is the probability of choosing mode *m*, and $U_m$ is the utility of mode *m*.

**Utility Function:**

$$
U_m = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \cdot \text{Time}_m + \beta_2 \cdot \text{Cost}_m + \beta_3 \cdot \text{Comfort}_m + \dots
$$

---

### Step 4 — Trip Assignment
**Goal:** Load the O-D trips onto the physical road/transport network.

#### (a) All-or-Nothing (AON) Assignment
The simplest model: every O-D pair takes the **shortest path** with no capacity constraint.

#### (b) Capacity-Restraint (Incremental) Assignment
Loads traffic in increments; updates link travel time after each increment based on the **BPR (Bureau of Public Roads) Function**:

$$
t = t_0 \left[1 + \alpha \left(\dfrac{v}{c}\right)^{\beta}\right]
$$

With standard parameters $\alpha = 0.15$ and $\beta = 4$.

#### (c) User Equilibrium (Wardrop's First Principle)
*"The journey times on all routes actually used are equal, and less than those on any unused route."*

Mathematically: at equilibrium, **no driver can reduce travel time by unilaterally switching routes**.

#### (d) System Optimum (Wardrop's Second Principle)
Minimizes **total system travel time** — may require some drivers to take longer routes for the collective good.

---

## 2.2 Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP) — Fleet-Level Trip Management

The **VRP** is the operational complement to the strategic 4STPM. It determines the *exact set of routes* for a fleet of vehicles serving a known set of customers.

**Standard VRP Formulation (Capacitated):**

$$
\text{Minimize } Z = \sum_{k=1}^{m} \sum_{(i,j) \in R_k} d_{ij}
$$

Subject to:
1. Each customer visited **exactly once** by exactly one vehicle.
2. Each vehicle **starts and ends** at the depot.
3. Total demand on route $R_k$ ≤ vehicle capacity $Q$.
4. Route duration ≤ maximum allowable $T_{max}$.

**Variants examined at KTU level:**
- **CVRP** — Capacitated VRP
- **VRPTW** — VRP with Time Windows
- **PDPTW** — Pickup and Delivery with Time Windows
- **DARP** — Dial-a-Ride Problem (paratransit, KTU-relevant)

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master the table below — it covers approximately 80% of numerical questions in KTU University ESE for this module.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units | Boundary / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Trip Rate | $\tau = \dfrac{T}{X}$ | $T$ = trips, $X$ = zonal variable (households, employees) | trips/household |
| 2 | Cross-Classification | $T_i = \sum_{c=1}^{k} N_{ic} \cdot r_c$ | $N_{ic}$ = households in class *c*, $r_c$ = trip rate of class *c* | Used for **category analysis** |
| 3 | Average Growth Factor | $E = \dfrac{T^{\text{new}}}{T^{\text{old}}}$ | Dimensionless | Used in uniform/Fratar |
| 4 | Gravity Model | $T_{ij} = P_i \cdot \dfrac{A_j f(C_{ij})}{\sum_k A_k f(C_{ik})}$ | All in trips | **Most important** |
| 5 | Singly-Constrained Gravity (Production) | $T_{ij} = P_i \cdot \dfrac{A_j f(C_{ij})}{\sum_k A_k f(C_{ik})}$ | — | $P_i$ pre-specified |
