| 6 | Doubly-Constrained Gravity | $T_{ij} = a_i P_i \cdot b_j A_j f(C_{ij})$ | $a_i, b_j$ = balancing factors | Iterative solution |
| 7 | BPR Travel Time | $t = t_0 \left[1 + 0.15 \left(\dfrac{v}{c}\right)^{4}\right]$ | $t_0$ = free-flow time, $v/c$ = vol/cap ratio | $\alpha=0.15,\beta=4$ |
| 8 | Logit Choice Probability | $P(m) = \dfrac{e^{U_m}}{\sum_k e^{U_k}}$ | $U$ = utility | $0 \le P \le 1$ |
| 9 | Logsum (Accessibility) | $LS = \ln \sum_k e^{U_k}$ | Numerically ≥ largest $U_k$ | Used in nested logit |
| 10 | Vehicle Utilization | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{Payload Carried}}{\text{Payload Capacity}}$ | $0 < \eta \le 1$ | $1.0$ = full load |
| 11 | Trip Cost | $C_{\text{trip}} = C_{\text{fixed}} + C_{\text{variable}} \cdot D$ | $D$ = distance (km) | Fuel + depreciation |
| 12 | Dead-Mileage Ratio | $DMR = \dfrac{D_{\text{empty}}}{D_{\text{total}}}$ | Dimensionless | $0 \le DMR < 1$ |
| 13 | Frequency (Transit) | $f = \dfrac{1}{h}$ | $h$ = headway (min) | $f$ in veh/hr |
| 14 | Route Factor (VRP) | $RF = \dfrac{D_{\text{actual}}}{D_{\text{straight-line}}}$ | $\ge 1$ | $1.0$ = ideal |
| 15 | O-D Matrix Balance | $\sum_j T_{ij} = P_i$, $\sum_i T_{ij} = A_j$ | Conservation law | Must be enforced |

> [!NOTE]
> **LaTeX Tip for Tables:** The absolute value or conditional symbol that would normally be `|x|` is written as `\vert x \vert` inside tables to avoid breaking the markdown pipe structure. KTU scripts accept both notations.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of Trip Planning

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Urban Transport** | Metro corridor feasibility, BRT network design, traffic impact analysis (TIA) for new townships. |
| **Freight & Logistics** | Last-mile delivery optimization (Amazon, Flipkart, FedEx routing engines). |
| **Paratransit** | Dial-a-Ride for elderly/disabled (KSRTC Ashwas, Smart City feeder services). |
| **Disaster Evacuation** | Reverse-direction contraflow planning, mass evacuation route assignment. |
| **Tourism** | KSRTC tour package design, Kerala house-boat route scheduling. |
| **Ride-Sharing** | Uber/Ola dynamic trip matching, surge pricing optimization. |
| **Highway Engineering** | Toll plaza placement, rest-area spacing, lane addition decisions. |
| **Smart Cities** | Real-time route guidance using Floating Car Data (FCD) and probe vehicles. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Perspective:** A 1% reduction in system travel time across a city of 2 million commuters saves approximately **₹50–100 crore/year** in aggregate time cost — trip planning is therefore *high-leverage public investment*.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Doubly-Constrained Gravity Model Balancing

The doubly-constrained gravity model is the **most analytically rigorous** trip distribution method. The derivation below shows exactly how the balancing factors $a_i$ and $b_j$ are computed iteratively.

### 3.1.1 Starting Equation

$$
T_{ij} = a_i \cdot P_i \cdot b_j \cdot A_j \cdot f(C_{ij})
$$

### 3.1.2 Row Constraint (Production Balance)

For each origin zone *i*, the total predicted productions must equal $P_i$:

$$
\sum_{j=1}^{n} T_{ij} = P_i
$$

Substitute the model equation:

$$
\sum_{j=1}^{n} a_i P_i b_j A_j f(C_{ij}) = P_i
$$

Since $a_i P_i$ is common, factor it out:

$$
a_i P_i \cdot \sum_{j=1}^{n} b_j A_j f(C_{ij}) = P_i
$$

Divide both sides by $P_i$:

$$
a_i \cdot \sum_{j=1}^{n} b_j A_j f(C_{ij}) = 1
$$

Solve for the row-balancing factor $a_i$:

$$
a_i = \dfrac{1}{\displaystyle\sum_{j=1}^{n} b_j A_j f(C_{ij})}
$$

### 3.1.3 Column Constraint (Attraction Balance)

Symmetrically, for each destination zone *j*:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n} T_{ij} = A_j
$$

Following the same algebraic steps:

$$
b_j = \dfrac{1}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i P_i f(C_{ij})}
$$

### 3.1.4 Iterative Algorithm (KTU Exam Favourite)

> **Algorithm (Furness Method)**
> 
> **Input:** Base $T_{ij}^{(0)}$ from singly-constrained run; convergence tolerance $\varepsilon = 0.001$.
> 
> **Step 1.** Set iteration counter $k \leftarrow 0$. Initialize $b_j^{(0)} = 1$ for all *j*.
> 
> **Step 2.** Compute $a_i^{(k)} = \dfrac{1}{\sum_j b_j^{(k)} A_j f(C_{ij})}$ for each *i*.
> 
> **Step 3.** Compute updated $T_{ij}^{(k+0.5)} = a_i^{(k)} P_i b_j^{(k)} A_j f(C_{ij})$.
> 
> **Step 4.** Compute $b_j^{(k+1)} = \dfrac{1}{\sum_i a_i^{(k)} P_i f(C_{ij})}$ for each *j*.
> 
> **Step 5.** Compute final $T_{ij}^{(k+1)} = a_i^{(k)} P_i b_j^{(k+1)} A_j f(C_{ij})$.
> 
> **Step 6.** Check $\max \left\vert \dfrac{T_{ij}^{(k+1)} - T_{ij}^{(k)}}{T_{ij}^{(k+1)}} \right\vert < \varepsilon$ for all *i,j*.
> 
> **Step 7.** If converged, stop. Else $k \leftarrow k+1$, go to Step 2.

### 3.1.5 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem (KTU-style):** A 3-zone system has the following data:

| Zone | Production $P_i$ | Attraction $A_j$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 400 | 300 |
| 2 | 300 | 500 |
| 3 | 500 | 400 |

Cost matrix (in minutes of travel time) and deterrence $f(C_{ij}) = C_{ij}^{-2}$:

| From \ To | 1 | 2 | 3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | — | $C=10 \Rightarrow f=0.0100$ | $C=15 \Rightarrow f=0.0044$ |
| **2** | $C=10 \Rightarrow f=0.0100$ | — | $C=20 \Rightarrow f=0.0025$ |
| **3** | $C=15 \Rightarrow f=0.0044$ | $C=20 \Rightarrow f=0.0025$ | — |

**Iteration 0:** Initialize $b_1^{(0)} = b_2^{(0)} = b_3^{(0)} = 1$.

Compute $a_i^{(0)}$ for each *i*:

For *i* = 1:
$$
a_1^{(0)} = \dfrac{1}{b_1 A_1 f(C_{11}) + b_2 A_2 f(C_{12}) + b_3 A_3 f(C_{13})}
$$

The term $f(C_{11})$ is the intrazonal deterrence, often set to 1.0 (no impedance within zone). However, with $b_1 = 1$ and $A_1 = 300$ and $f_{11} = 1$:

$$
a_1^{(0)} = \dfrac{1}{(1)(300)(1) + (1)(500)(0.0100) + (1)(400)(0.0044)}
$$

$$
= \dfrac{1}{300 + 5.0 + 1.78} = \dfrac{1}{306.78} = 0.003260
$$

For *i* = 2:
$$
a_2^{(0)} = \dfrac{1}{(1)(300)(0.0100) + (1)(500)(1) + (1)(400)(0.0025)}
$$

$$
= \dfrac{1}{3.0 + 500 + 1.0} = \dfrac{1}{504.0} = 0.001984
$$

For *i* = 3:
$$
a_3^{(0)} = \dfrac{1}{(1)(300)(0.0044) + (1)(500)(0.0025) + (1)(400)(1)}
$$

$$
= \dfrac{1}{1.32 + 1.25 + 400} = \dfrac{1}{402.57} = 0.002484
$$

**Step 3 — Intermediate $T_{ij}$:** Compute $T_{ij}^{(0.5)} = a_i^{(0)} P_i b_j^{(0)} A_j f(C_{ij})$:

For *i* = 1, *j* = 1: $T_{11} = 0.003260 \times 400 \times 1 \times 300 \times 1 = 391.2$
For *i* = 1, *j* = 2: $T_{12} = 0.003260 \times 400 \times 1 \times 500 \times 0.0100 = 6.52$
For *i* = 1, *j* = 3: $T_{13} = 0.003260 \times 400 \times 1 \times 400 \times 0.0044 = 2.30$

Row sum for zone 1: $391.2 + 6.52 + 2.30 = 400.0$ ≈ $P_1$ ✓

For *i* = 2, *j* = 1: $T_{21} = 0.001984 \times 300 \times 1 \times 300 \times 0.0100 = 1.79$
For *i* = 2, *j* = 2: $T_{22} = 0.001984 \times 300 \times 1 \times 500 \times 1 = 297.6$
For *i* = 2, *j* = 3: $T_{23} = 0.001984 \times 300 \times 1 \times 400 \times 0.0025 = 0.595$

Row sum for zone 2: $1.79 + 297.6 + 0.595 = 300.0$ ≈ $P_2$ ✓

For *i* = 3, *j* = 1: $T_{31} = 0.002484 \times 500 \times 1 \times 300 \times 0.0044 = 1.64$
For *i* = 3, *j* = 2: $T_{32} = 0.002484 \times 500 \times 1 \times 500 \times 0.0025 = 1.55$
For *i* = 3, *j* = 3: $T_{33} = 0.002484 \times 500 \times 1 \times 400 \times 1 = 496.8$

Row sum for zone 3: $1.64 + 1.55 + 496.8 = 500.0$ ≈ $P_3$ ✓

**Step 4 — Update $b_j$:** Compute column sums and balance.

Column 1 sum: $391.2 + 1.79 + 1.64 = 394.6$ (target $A_1 = 300$)
Column 2 sum: $6.52 + 297.6 + 1.55 = 305.7$ (target $A_2 = 500$)
Column 3 sum: $2.30 + 0.595 + 496.8 = 499.7$ (target $A_3 = 400$)

Compute new $b_j$:

$$
b_1^{(1)} = \dfrac{1}{\sum_i a_i^{(0)} P_i f(C_{i1})} = \dfrac{1}{0.003260 \times 400 \times 1 + 0.001984 \times 300 \times 0.0100 + 0.002484 \times 500 \times 0.0044}
$$

$$
= \dfrac{1}{1.304 + 0.00595 + 0.00547} = \dfrac{1}{1.315} = 0.760
$$

$$
b_2^{(1)} = \dfrac{1}{0.003260 \times 400 \times 0.0100 + 0.001984 \times 300 \times 1 + 0.002484 \times 500 \times 0.0025}
$$

$$
= \dfrac{1}{0.01304 + 0.5952 + 0.003105} = \dfrac{1}{0.6113} = 1.636
$$

$$
b_3^{(1)} = \dfrac{1}{0.003260 \times 400 \times 0.0044 + 0.001984 \times 300 \times 0.0025 + 0.002484 \times 500 \times 1}
$$

$$
= \dfrac{1}{0.00574 + 0.00149 + 1.242} = \dfrac{1}{1.249} = 0.801
$$

**Final T-matrix (after iteration 0→1):** The procedure is repeated until column sums match $A_j$ within tolerance. The **converged O-D matrix** represents the equilibrium trip distribution.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — BPR Function Behaviour

Starting from the Bureau of Public Roads volume-delay function:

$$
t = t_0 \left[1 + \alpha \left(\dfrac{v}{c}\right)^{\beta}\right]
$$

### 3.2.1 Limiting Case 1 — Free Flow

When $v \ll c$ (volume far below capacity):

$$
\dfrac{v}{c} \to 0 \implies t \to t_0 \left[1 + 0\right] = t_0
$$

Thus $t_0$ is the **free-flow travel time** — the lower bound.

### 3.2.2 Limiting Case 2 — At Capacity

When $v = c$:

$$
t = t_0 \left[1 + 0.15 \times 1^{4}\right] = t_0 \times 1.15
$$

Travel time is **15% higher** than free flow.

### 3.2.3 Limiting Case 3 — Over Capacity

When $v = 1.5c$:

$$
t = t_0 \left[1 + 0.15 \times (1.5)^{4}\right] = t_0 \left[1 + 0.15 \times 5.0625\right] = t_0 \times 1.759
$$

Travel time is **76% higher** — congestion becomes non-linear.

### 3.2.4 Practical Insight

The **fourth power** exponent means that a 50% overload increases travel time by ~76%, but a 100% overload ($v = 2c$) increases it by **250%**. This non-linearity is the **economic justification for capacity expansion**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Distribution Methods

| Criterion | Uniform Growth Factor | Fratar Method | Singly-Constrained Gravity | Doubly-Constrained Gravity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Data Requirement** | Low | Moderate | Moderate–High | High |
| **Accounts for Cost Impedance?** | No | No | **Yes** | **Yes** |
| **Preserves Row Totals?** | Yes (if applied) | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Preserves Column Totals?** | Yes (if applied) | Yes | No | **Yes** |
| **Convergence Speed** | Single step | 3–5 iterations | 5–10 iterations | 15–30 iterations |
| **Accuracy (Rural)** | High | High | Moderate | Moderate |
| **Accuracy (Urban)** | Low | Moderate | High | **Very High** |
| **Computational Cost** | Minimal | Low | Moderate | High |
| **KTU Exam Frequency** | Rare | Sometimes | Common | **Very Common** |
| **Real-World Use** | Quick estimates | Small networks | Strategic models | Metropolitan models |

The following block-level functional architecture illustrates the complete trip planning pipeline as specified in the KTU 2024 syllabus.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
```

**Reading the Diagram:** The architecture shows a *cyclic* process — outputs of trip assignment (link volumes, travel times) feed back to trip generation, refining future-year predictions. This is the basis of **iterative four-step modelling** used in KSRTC and NATPAC feasibility studies.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define the term "Trip Generation" in transportation planning. List any four factors affecting trip production in a residential zone.**

**Model Answer:**

Trip Generation is the first stage of the classical four-step transportation planning process that estimates the *number of trips originating from and terminating at* each Traffic Analysis Zone (TAZ) over a defined period (typically a 24-hour weekday).

It predicts two quantities:
- **Trip Production (Pᵢ):** Trips whose *origin* is in zone *i* (linked to households).
- **Trip Attraction (Aⱼ):** Trips whose *destination* is in zone *j* (linked to employment, retail, education).

**Four factors affecting trip production in a residential zone:**
1. **Household Income** — higher income → more discretionary trips.
2. **Car Ownership** — vehicles per household strongly correlate with daily trip rate.
3. **Family Size & Structure** — number of workers, children, and elderly members.
4. **Distance from City Centre (CBD)** — suburban zones typically produce 20–30% more trips than CBD zones due to longer journey-to-work distances.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Examiners award 1 mark for the definition and 0.5 mark per factor. Always pair a factor with its *direction of influence* (e.g., "income ↑ → trips ↑") to earn full credit.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is the Gravity Model in trip distribution? Write the formula for the singly-constrained gravity model and explain the role of the deterrence function.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Gravity Model** is a trip distribution technique that estimates the number of trips between two zones as directly proportional to the product of trip productions and attractions, and inversely proportional to a function of the travel cost (time, distance, or generalized cost) between them.

**Singly-Constrained Gravity Model Formula:**

$$
T_{ij} = P_i \cdot \dfrac{A_j \cdot f(C_{ij})}{\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{n} A_k \cdot f(C_{ik})}
$$

Where:
- $T_{ij}$ = trips between zones *i* and *j*
- $P_i$ = trip production at origin *i* (constrained)
- $A_j$ = trip attraction at destination *j*
- $f(C_{ij})$ = deterrence function of cost $C_{ij}$ between *i* and *j*

**Role of the Deterrence Function:**
The deterrence function $f(C_{ij})$ captures the empirical observation that **trip interchange decays with increasing separation**. It represents the *impedance* (friction) that discourages long trips. Common forms:
- **Inverse Power:** $f(C_{ij}) = C_{ij}^{-b}$ — simple, suited for intercity.
- **Exponential:** $f(C_{ij}) = e^{-\beta C_{ij}}$ — decays smoothly, suited for urban.
- **Tanner/Gamma:** combine both — most realistic for metropolitan areas.

The deterrence function must be **calibrated** using observed O-D data (typically a regression on $\ln f(C_{ij})$ vs. $\ln C_{ij}$).

---

## PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the four-step transportation planning model with a neat flowchart. Discuss the limitations of the gravity model in trip distribution.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A metropolitan area is divided into 3 zones. The future trip productions and attractions are given below:

| Zone | Production $P_i$ | Attraction $A_j$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 200 | 300 |
| 2 | 400 | 200 |
| 3 | 200 | 300 |

The inter-zonal travel times (in minutes) are:

| From \ To | 1 | 2 | 3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | 5 | 10 | 15 |
| **2** | 10 | 5 | 10 |
| **3** | 15 | 10 | 5 |

Using a singly-constrained gravity model with deterrence function $f(C_{ij}) = C_{ij}^{-2}$ and intrazonal deterrence $f(C_{ii}) = 1$, compute the future trip matrix $T_{ij}$.

---

### Model Solution A(a) — Four-Step Model & Limitations

**The Four-Step Transportation Planning Model (4STPM):**

| Step | Input | Output | Method |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Trip Generation** | Zonal land use, socio-economic data | $P_i$, $A_j$ | Regression, Cross-Classification |
| **2. Trip Distribution** | $P_i$, $A_j$, cost matrix | O-D matrix $T_{ij}$ | Gravity Model, Fratar, Growth Factor |
| **3. Modal Split** | O-D matrix, mode attributes | Modal O-D matrices | Logit, Probit, Nested Logit |
| **4. Trip Assignment** | Modal O-D, network | Link volumes $v_a$ | All-or-Nothing, Capacity Restraint, UE |

The process is **sequential but iterative** — assignment results feed back to modify cost matrices used in distribution.

**Limitations of the Gravity Model:**

1. **Empirical, Not Theoretical:** Parameters must be calibrated; the model has no behavioural foundation.
2. **Uniform Behaviour Assumption:** Treats all travellers identically within a zone — ignores individual heterogeneity.
3. **Static Cost:** Uses a single cost value, ignoring time-of-day variation.
4. **Treats Trips Independently:** Does not capture trip-chaining (linked trips).
5. **Calibration Difficulty:** Deterrence parameters may be unstable across sub-regions.
6. **Ignores Network Topology:** Two zones at equal cost may have very different connectivity in reality.

---

### Model Solution A(b) — Singly-Constrained Gravity Model

**Step 1 — Compute Deterrence Values** $f(C_{ij}) = C_{ij}^{-2}$:

| From \ To | 1 | 2 | 3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | $5^{-2} = 0.0400$ | $10^{-2} = 0.0100$ | $15^{-2} = 0.00444$ |
| **2** | $10^{-2} = 0.0100$ | $5^{-2} = 0.0400$ | $10^{-2} = 0.0100$ |
| **3** | $15^{-2} = 0.00444$ | $10^{-2} = 0.0100$ | $5^{-2} = 0.0400$ |

**Step 2 — Compute Row Denominators** $D_i = \sum_j A_j f(C_{ij})$:

For *i* = 1:
$$
D_1 = (300)(0.0400) + (200)(0.0100) + (300)(0.00444) = 12.0 + 2.0 + 1.333 = 15.333
$$

For *i* = 2:
$$
D_2 = (300)(0.0100) + (200)(0.0400) + (300)(0.0100) = 3.0 + 8.0 + 3.0 = 14.0
$$

For *i* = 3:
$$
D_3 = (300)(0.00444) + (200)(0.0100) + (300)(0.0400) = 1.333 + 2.0 + 12.0 = 15.333
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $T_{ij} = P_i \cdot A_j f(C_{ij}) / D_i$:**

For *i* = 1:
$$
T_{11} = 200 \times \dfrac{300 \times 0.0400}{15.333} = 200 \times 0.7826 = 156.5
$$

$$
T_{12} = 200 \times \dfrac{200 \times 0.0100}{15.333} = 200 \times 0.1304 = 26.1
$$

$$
T_{13} = 200 \times \dfrac{300 \times 0.00444}{15.333} = 200 \times 0.0869 = 17.4
$$

**Row 1 Check:** $156.5 + 26.1 + 17.4 = 200.0 = P_1$ ✓

For *i* = 2:
$$
T_{21} = 400 \times \dfrac{300 \times 0.0100}{14.0} = 400 \times 0.2143 = 85.7
$$

$$
T_{22} = 400 \times \dfrac{200 \times 0.0400}{14.0} = 400 \times 0.5714 = 228.6
$$

$$
T_{23} = 400 \times \dfrac{300 \times 0.0100}{14.0} = 400 \times 0.2143 = 85.7
$$

**Row 2 Check:** $85.7 + 228.6 + 85.7 = 400.0 = P_2$ ✓

For *i* = 3:
$$
T_{31} = 200 \times \dfrac{300 \times 0.00444}{15.333} = 200 \times 0.0869 = 17.4
$$

$$
T_{32} = 200 \times \dfrac{200 \times 0.0100}{15.333} = 200 \times 0.1304 = 26.1
$$

$$
T_{33} = 200 \times \dfrac{300 \times 0.0400}{15.333} = 200 \times 0.7826 = 156.5
$$

**Row 3 Check:** $17.4 + 26.1 + 156.5 = 200.0 = P_3$ ✓

**Final O-D Matrix:**

| From \ To | 1 | 2 | 3 | **Total** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | 156.5 | 26.1 | 17.4 | **200** |
| **2** | 85.7 | 228.6 | 85.7 | **400** |
| **3** | 17.4 | 26.1 | 156.5 | **200** |
| **Total** | **259.6** | **280.8** | **259.6** | **800** |

**Note:** Column totals are *not* constrained to equal $A_j$ in the singly-constrained model — this is the principal difference from the doubly-constrained variant.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is the Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP)? Explain the Capacitated VRP (CVRP) with a mathematical formulation. List three heuristic methods used to solve it.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A logistics company has a depot at node 0 and 4 customers at nodes 1, 2, 3, 4. The distance matrix (km) is:

| Node | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **0** | 0 | 10 | 15 | 20 | 25 |
| **1** | 10 | 0 | 12 | 18 | 22 |
| **2** | 15 | 12 | 0 | 10 | 15 |
| **3** | 20 | 18 | 10 | 0 | 8 |
| **4** | 25 | 22 | 15 | 8 | 0 |

Customer demands are 1: 8 units, 2: 10 units, 3: 7 units, 4: 12 units. Each vehicle has capacity 20 units. Find a feasible route plan and total distance.

---

### Model Solution B(a) — VRP & CVRP

**Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP):**
The VRP is a combinatorial optimization problem that determines the optimal set of routes for a fleet of vehicles to serve a set of customers, subject to constraints. It generalizes the **Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP)**.

**CVRP Mathematical Formulation:**

Decision Variables:
- $x_{ijk} = 1$ if vehicle *k* travels from *i* to *j*, else 0.
- $y_{ik} = 1$ if customer *i* is served by vehicle *k*, else 0.

Objective (Minimize Total Distance):

$$
\min Z = \sum_{k=1}^{m} \sum_{i=0}^{n} \sum_{j=0}^{n} d_{ij} \cdot x_{ijk}
$$

Subject to:
1. **Each customer visited once:** $\sum_{k=1}^{m} y_{ik} = 1 \quad \forall i = 1, \dots, n$
2. **Vehicle flow conservation:** $\sum_{j} x_{ijk} = \sum_{j} x_{jik} = y_{ik}$
3. **Capacity constraint:** $\sum_{i=1}^{n} q_i y_{ik} \le Q \quad \forall k$
4. **Depot start/end:** Each route begins and ends at node 0.
5. **Binary:** $x_{ijk}, y_{ik} \in \{0, 1\}$

**Three Heuristic Methods:**

1. **Clarke & Wright Savings Algorithm (1964):** Computes a "savings" $s_{ij} = d_{0i} + d_{0j} - d_{ij}$ for merging two routes; iteratively merges the highest-savings pair.
2. **Nearest Neighbour Heuristic:** Greedy approach — always go to the closest unvisited customer; simple but suboptimal.
3. **2-opt Local Search:** Iteratively removes two edges from a route and reconnects them in the opposite direction if it reduces total distance.

---

### Model Solution B(b) — Route Plan

**Step 1 — Total Demand:** $8 + 10 + 7 + 12 = 37$ units. With vehicle capacity 20, minimum vehicles required: $\lceil 37 / 20 \rceil = 2$ vehicles.

**Step 2 — Apply Clarke & Wright Savings:**

Compute savings $s_{ij} = d_{0i} + d_{0j} - d_{ij}$ for all customer pairs:

| Pair (i,j) | $d_{0i}$ | $d_{0j}$ | $d_{ij}$ | $s_{ij}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| (1,2) | 10 | 15 | 12 | **13** |
| (1,3) | 10 | 20 | 18 | **12** |
| (1,4) | 10 | 25 | 22 | **13** |
| (2,3) | 15 | 20 | 10 | **25** |
| (2,4) | 15 | 25 | 15 | **25** |
| (3,4) | 20 | 25 | 8 | **37** |

**Step 3 — Initial Routes (each customer as own route from depot):**
- Route 1: 0 → 1 → 0 (distance 20, demand 8)
- Route 2: 0 → 2 → 0 (distance 30, demand 10)
- Route 3: 0 → 3 → 0 (distance 40, demand 7)
- Route 4: 0 → 4 → 0 (distance 50, demand 12)
- Total initial distance: **140 km**

**Step 4 — Merge in Order of Decreasing Savings:**

**(a) Merge (3,4) — Savings 37:** Check capacity: $7 + 12 = 19 \le 20$ ✓
New route: 0 → 3 → 4 → 0 (or 0 → 4 → 3 → 0)
Distance: $20 + 8 + 25 = 53$ km (or $25 + 8 + 20 = 53$ km) — use 0 → 3 → 4 → 0

**(b) Merge (2,3) — Savings 25:** Check capacity: $10 + 19 = 29 > 20$ ✗ Reject.
Check (2,4) — Savings 25: $10 + 19 = 29 > 20$ ✗ Reject.

**(c) Merge (1,2) — Savings 13:** Check capacity: $8 + 10 = 18 \le 20$ ✓
New route: 0 → 1 → 2 → 0
Distance: $10 + 12 + 15 = 37$ km

**(d) Check remaining merges:** Route 1 (0-1-2-0) with Route (0-3-4-0) — would need to merge at the depot (1 with 3, or 2 with 3, etc.). The savings for (1,3) = 12 and (2,3) = 25. The pair (1,3) merge would yield 0 → 1 → 3 → 4 → 0. Capacity: $8 + 19 = 27 > 20$ ✗ Reject. The pair (2,3) merge yields 0 → 1 → 2 → 3 → 4 → 0. Capacity: $18 + 19 = 37 > 20$ ✗ Reject.

**Step 5 — Final Route Plan:**

- **Vehicle 1:** Depot → 1 → 2 → Depot (Distance = 37 km, Demand = 18 units)
- **Vehicle 2:** Depot → 3 → 4 → Depot (Distance = 53 km, Demand = 19 units)

**Total Distance:** $37 + 53 = \mathbf{90 \text{ km}}$

**Improvement over initial:** $\dfrac{140 - 90}{140} \times 100 = 35.7\%$ reduction in dead mileage.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Forgetting capacity check** before merging — a 3-mark deduction.
> 2. **Not enforcing depot return** — a route ending at a customer is *infeasible*.
> 3. **Confusing demand and capacity units** — always state them clearly.
> 4. **Single-route answers** — if a solution requires 2 vehicles but only one is provided, deduct 2 marks.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 60-Second Rapid Recap — Read this just before entering the exam hall.**

- ✅ **Trip Planning =** Generation → Distribution → Modal Split → Assignment (4STPM).
- ✅ **Trip Generation** uses trip rates $\tau = T / X$ or regression models.
- ✅ **Gravity Model** is the *default* trip distribution method; deterrence $f(C_{ij})$ must be calibrated.
- ✅ **Doubly-Constrained** gravity needs *iterative* balancing (Furness method).
- ✅ **Logit model** $P(m) = e^{U_m}/\sum e^{U_k}$ — the *only* modal split formula for KTU 2024.
- ✅ **BPR Function** $t = t_0 [1 + 0.15(v/c)^4]$ — parameters $\alpha=0.15$, $\beta=4$ are *fixed*.
- ✅ **Wardrop UE** = every used route has equal minimum time; **SO** = minimize total time.
- ✅ **VRP** generalizes TSP; CVRP adds capacity $Q$; VRPTW adds time windows.
- ✅ **Clarke & Wright Savings** is the most common VRP heuristic — always check capacity.
- ✅ **O-D Matrix must balance:** $\sum_j T_{ij} = P_i$ AND $\sum_i T_{ij} = A_j$ (for doubly-constrained).
- ✅ **Intrazonal trips** use $f(C_{ii}) = 1.0$ (or a finite value, never zero).
- ✅ **Design Hour = 30th highest hour**, not absolute peak.
- ✅ **AVO** ranges 1.2–1.5 for urban cars; **PHF** ranges 0.70–1.00.
- ✅ Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of `|` inside LaTeX-rendered KTU answer tables.
- ✅ Always present the **flowchart + numerical justification** for 14-mark answers.

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Trip Planning and Management**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Driver Management and Training
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT866 - VEHICLE TRANSPORT MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Driver Management and Training

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

**Driver Management** is the systematic process of planning, recruiting, selecting, training, deploying, motivating, evaluating, and retaining professional drivers to ensure safe, efficient, economical, and legally compliant operation of a transport fleet. It encompasses human resource practices, regulatory compliance, performance monitoring, and welfare administration.

**Driver Training** is the structured, pedagogically designed program of instruction (classroom, simulator-based, behind-the-wheel, and on-the-job) that equips drivers with the technical skills, safety knowledge, regulatory awareness, and attitudinal competencies required to operate commercial vehicles safely and productively.

Together, these two functions form the **human-centric pillar** of fleet operations management. In KTU's outcome-based framework, this topic directly addresses **CO2: Apply principles of manpower planning, training, and welfare in transport organizations**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note (2024 Scheme):** The human element — the driver — is statistically responsible for approximately **80%–90% of all road accidents** in commercial transport. Hence, KTU evaluators weight this module heavily as it bridges engineering knowledge (vehicle dynamics) with management science (HR practices).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **commercial truck as a guided missile** and the driver as the **pilot**. A missile without a skilled pilot is junk metal worth crores. A pilot without proper training is a danger to the mission, the crew, and the public.

- The **engine, brakes, and tyres** are the hardware of the missile.
- The **driver (pilot)** is the software executor who interprets signals, makes decisions, and controls the hardware in real-time traffic.
- **Driver Management** = the HR wing of an airline (recruiting pilots, checking licenses, medical fitness, rostering, paying salaries, handling grievances).
- **Driver Training** = the flight school + simulator sessions + recurrent training every 6 months.

Just as no airline would allow an unqualified pilot to fly passengers, no fleet owner should allow an untrained driver to operate a 16-tonne truck carrying 30 tonnes of cargo on Kerala's ghat roads.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Kerala Context (KTU-specific example):** On the **Wayanad Ghat Road** or **Kundara–Thenmala stretch**, a single mistake by an untrained driver can cause a multi-crore accident, landslides, and fatalities. KTU examiners appreciate regional context in answers.

### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Industry-Standard Terms)

- **Driver-to-Vehicle Ratio (DVR):** Typically **1.2 to 1.5 drivers per vehicle** (accounts for leave, off-days, and shift changes).
- **Annual Driver Turnover Rate:** **30%–60%** in the Indian trucking industry (one of the highest in any profession).
- **Cost of Driver Replacement:** Approximately **1.5× to 2× the driver's monthly salary** (recruitment + training + lost productivity).
- **Defensive Driving:** Proactive driving technique focused on preventing accidents despite adverse conditions or other drivers' mistakes.
- **Hours of Service (HoS):** Maximum driving hours legally permitted — in India, **8 hours of continuous driving followed by a mandatory 30-minute rest**, with a maximum of **9 hours total driving in a 12-hour shift** (per Motor Transport Workers Act, 1961).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Driver Management Lifecycle (DML)

Driver management is not a one-time event — it is a **continuous closed-loop cycle**. The six phases are:

1. **Manpower Planning & Demand Forecasting**
   - Determining *how many* drivers are needed based on fleet size, route distance, and shift patterns.
   - Formula-based calculation using trip frequency and turnaround time.

2. **Recruitment & Selection**
   - Sourcing candidates via drivers' associations, employment exchanges, referrals.
   - Document verification: Driving License (DL), Aadhaar, experience certificates.

3. **Licensing & Statutory Verification**
   - Mandatory validation of **Heavy Motor Vehicle (HMV) license** under the **Motor Vehicles Act, 1988**.
   - Endorsement checks: **Transport Vehicle (TV)** badge, hill endorsement, hazardous goods endorsement (if applicable).

4. **Training & Development**
   - Pre-service training (induction).
   - In-service training (refresher, defensive driving, eco-driving).
   - Specialized training (tanker, container, refrigerated trailer).

5. **Deployment & Roster Management**
   - Assigning vehicles, routes, and shifts.
   - Ensuring **Hours of Service** compliance.

6. **Performance Evaluation, Motivation & Welfare**
   - KPIs: km/litre, accident rate, on-time delivery, customer feedback.
   - Welfare: housing, medical, insurance, provident fund, bonus.
   - Disciplinary action: warnings, suspension, dismissal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Concept Linkage:** This DML cycle directly maps to **HRM (Human Resource Management)** theory by **Edwin Flippo** — the procurement → development → compensation → integration → maintenance loop.

### 2.2 Classification of Driver Training (High-Yield for KTU)

| Training Type | Mode | Duration | Purpose | KTU Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Pre-service (Induction) Training** | Classroom + Yard | 2–4 weeks | Familiarize new driver with company vehicles, routes, rules | High |
| **Behind-the-Wheel (BTW) Training** | On-road with instructor | 40–100 hours | Develop actual driving competence on diverse roads | Very High |
| **Defensive Driving Training (DDT)** | Classroom + Simulator + Road | 1–2 weeks | Accident prevention, hazard perception | Very High |
| **Eco-Driving / Fuel-Efficient Driving** | Classroom + Telematics feedback | 1 day–1 week | Reduce fuel consumption by 8%–15% | High |
| **Refresher Training** | Periodic | 2–4 days/year | Update on rules, refresh skills | Medium |
| **Specialized Vehicle Training** | Tanker/Container/Reefer/HCV | 1–4 weeks | Vehicle-specific handling (rollover, spill, cold chain) | High |
| **First Aid & Emergency Response** | Classroom + Practical | 1–2 days | Handle accidents, medical emergencies | Medium |
| **Customer Service Training** | Classroom | 0.5–1 day | Professional conduct, passenger interaction (buses) | Low–Medium |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Quantitative Driver Management)

> [!NOTE]
> Since this is a management topic, the "formulas" are operational, HR, and cost-based — not engineering math. KTU examiners love numericals in this module.

| # | Formula / Model | Expression | Variables | Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Driver-to-Vehicle Ratio (DVR)** | $DVR = \dfrac{N_d}{N_v}$ | $N_d$ = total drivers, $N_v$ = total vehicles | Ratio | Staffing |
| 2 | **Drivers Required per Day** | $D_{req} = \dfrac{T_t \times N_v}{H_{daily} \times U}$ | $T_t$ = avg trip time (hrs), $H_{daily}$ = driving hours/day (8), $U$ = utilization | Number | Shift planning |
| 3 | **Driver Turnover Rate** | $DTR = \dfrac{D_{left}}{D_{avg}} \times 100$ | $D_{left}$ = drivers who left, $D_{avg}$ = avg headcount | % | HR health metric |
| 4 | **Cost of Driver Replacement (CDR)** | $CDR = R + T + L \times M$ | $R$ = recruitment cost, $T$ = training cost, $L$ = lost productivity months, $M$ = monthly output | ₹ | Cost justification for training |
| 5 | **Training ROI** | $ROI = \dfrac{B - C}{C} \times 100$ | $B$ = training benefits (fuel saving + accident cost avoided), $C$ = training cost | % | Justification for management approval |
| 6 | **Accident Rate per Driver** | $AR = \dfrac{N_a}{D_{total} \times Y}$ | $N_a$ = accidents, $D_{total}$ = total drivers, $Y$ = years | Accidents/driver-yr | Safety KPI |
| 7 | **Fuel Efficiency Index (FEI)** | $FEI = \dfrac{km}{litre} \text{ (per driver)}$ | Standardized across vehicles | km/L | Eco-driving KPI |
| 8 | **Driver Productivity** | $P = \dfrac{R \times N_t}{D_{active}}$ | $R$ = revenue, $N_t$ = trips, $D_{active}$ = active drivers | ₹/driver | Performance rating |
| 9 | **Defensive Driving Effectiveness** | $DDE = 1 - \dfrac{A_{post}}{A_{pre}}$ | $A_{pre}$ = accidents before, $A_{post}$ = accidents after | Ratio | Training impact |
| 10 | **Hours of Service Compliance** | $HoS \leq 8$ hrs continuous, $\leq 9$ hrs total in 12-hr shift | Regulatory | Hrs | Legal compliance |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Never write the absolute value symbol with `|` in the above tables during an exam. Use the word "modulus" or simply substitute numeric values to avoid losing marks for syntax errors.

### 2.4 Functions of a Driver Training School (Per KTU/ASRTU/IRC Standards)

1. Imparting **theoretical knowledge** of road signs, traffic rules, vehicle systems.
2. Providing **practical driving instruction** in varied traffic and weather.
3. Training in **preventive maintenance checks** (daily walk-around).
4. Developing **psychological traits** — patience, alertness, responsibility.
5. **Certification and licensing assistance**.
6. Conducting **periodic assessment and recertification**.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Safety Engineering:** Driver training directly reduces accident-induced structural damage to vehicles, road infrastructure, and public property.
- **Vehicle Dynamics Application:** A well-trained driver understands load transfer during braking, rollover thresholds of high-CG vehicles (tankers), and stability control interventions.
- **Telematics & Industry 4.0:** Modern driver training uses **in-cab cameras, AI dashcams, and telematics-based feedback** to provide personalized coaching — relevant to KTU's automation theme.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Since Driver Management is a managerial/quantitative HR topic, the "derivations" below are **worked-out numerical problems** using the formulas from Section 2. KTU frequently asks these as **14-mark case-study numericals** in Part B.

### 3.1 Worked Example 1: Calculating Drivers Required for a Fleet

**Problem Statement (Typical KTU 14-Mark Style):**
> ABC Logistics Pvt. Ltd. operates **40 trucks** in Kerala. Each truck makes **2 trips per day**, and the average trip duration (including loading/unloading) is **7 hours per trip**. Each driver is legally allowed to drive a maximum of **8 hours per day**, and the driver utilization factor is **0.85** (accounts for leave, breaks, idle time). Calculate: (a) the total number of drivers required per day, and (b) the Driver-to-Vehicle Ratio.

**Given:**
- $N_v = 40$ vehicles
- Trips per vehicle per day = 2
- Trip time $T_t = 7$ hours per trip
- Daily legal driving limit $H_{daily} = 8$ hours
- Utilization factor $U = 0.85$

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Total trip-hours demanded per day by the fleet:**
$$
T_{total} = N_v \times N_{trips} \times T_t
$$

Substituting:
$$
T_{total} = 40 \times 2 \times 7 = 560 \text{ driver-hours/day}
$$

*(Text row: We multiply the number of vehicles by trips-per-day to get total trips, then multiply by hours-per-trip to convert trips into the total driving hours the fleet demands.)*

**Step 2 — Effective driving hours supplied by one driver per day:**
$$
H_{eff} = H_{daily} \times U
$$

Substituting:
$$
H_{eff} = 8 \times 0.85 = 6.8 \text{ hours/driver/day}
$$

*(Text row: Although a driver is allowed 8 hours legally, we account for idle time, breaks, and absences via the utilization factor of 0.85, yielding 6.8 productive driving hours per driver per day.)*

**Step 3 — Number of drivers required per day:**
$$
D_{req} = \dfrac{T_{total}}{H_{eff}}
$$

Substituting:
$$
D_{req} = \dfrac{560}{6.8} = 82.35 \approx 83 \text{ drivers}
$$

*(Text row: We divide the total demand (560 driver-hours) by the per-driver effective supply (6.8 hours) to get the minimum headcount needed. Rounding up is mandatory as we cannot have 0.35 of a driver.)*

**Step 4 — Driver-to-Vehicle Ratio:**
$$
DVR = \dfrac{D_{req}}{N_v} = \dfrac{83}{40} = 2.075
$$

*(Text row: Dividing 83 drivers by 40 vehicles gives a ratio of ~2.08, which is slightly above the industry benchmark of 1.5–1.8, indicating the company may have inefficient trip consolidation or a low utilization factor — a managerial insight that scores extra marks.)*

**Final Answer:**
- (a) **83 drivers required per day**
- (b) **DVR = 2.075 (≈ 2.08)** — slightly higher than ideal; management should investigate.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2: Training ROI Calculation

**Problem Statement:**
> A fleet operator spends **₹2,00,000** on a Defensive Driving Training program for 50 drivers. Post-training, fuel efficiency improves from **3.2 km/L to 3.6 km/L**. The fleet covers **50,000 km/month** with diesel costing **₹90/litre**. Accidents reduce from 12/year to 4/year, with average accident cost (insurance + downtime + repair) being **₹1,50,000/accident**. Calculate: (a) monthly fuel saving, (b) annual accident cost saving, and (c) Training ROI over 1 year.

**Given:**
- Training cost $C = ₹2,00,000$
- Fuel efficiency before $FE_{pre} = 3.2$ km/L, after $FE_{post} = 3.6$ km/L
- Monthly km = 50,000 km
- Diesel price = ₹90/L
- Accidents before = 12/yr, after = 4/yr
- Accident cost = ₹1,50,000 each

**Step 1 — Fuel saved per month (in litres):**
$$
F_{saved} = \left(\dfrac{1}{FE_{pre}} - \dfrac{1}{FE_{post}}\right) \times km_{monthly}
$$

Substituting:
$$
F_{saved} = \left(\dfrac{1}{3.2} - \dfrac{1}{3.6}\right) \times 50{,}000
$$

$$
F_{saved} = (0.3125 - 0.2778) \times 50{,}000 = 0.0347 \times 50{,}000 = 1{,}736.11 \text{ litres/month}
$$

*(Text row: Since fuel efficiency is in km per litre, its reciprocal gives litres per km. The difference in reciprocals, multiplied by distance, gives the litres saved per month. This is the standard engineering approach for fuel-economy comparisons.)*

**Step 2 — Monthly fuel cost saving:**
$$
S_{fuel} = F_{saved} \times P_{diesel} = 1{,}736.11 \times 90 = ₹1{,}56{,}250/\text{month}
$$

**Step 3 — Annual fuel saving:**
$$
S_{fuel,annual} = 1{,}56{,}250 \times 12 = ₹18{,}75{,}000
$$

**Step 4 — Annual accident cost saving:**
$$
S_{acc} = (A_{pre} - A_{post}) \times C_{acc} = (12 - 4) \times 1{,}50{,}000 = 8 \times 1{,}50{,}000 = ₹12{,}00{,}000
$$

*(Text row: Eight fewer accidents per year at ₹1.5 lakh each saves ₹12 lakh annually — a major benefit not always captured in simplistic ROI calculations.)*

**Step 5 — Total annual benefit:**
$$
B = S_{fuel,annual} + S_{acc} = 18{,}75{,}000 + 12{,}00{,}000 = ₹30{,}75{,}000
$$

**Step 6 — Training ROI:**
$$
ROI = \dfrac{B - C}{C} \times 100 = \dfrac{30{,}75{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000}{2{,}00{,}000} \times 100
$$

$$
ROI = \dfrac{28{,}75{,}000}{2{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 1437.5\%
$$

*(Text row: An ROI of 1437.5% is extraordinary, indicating that defensive driving training is one of the highest-yield investments a fleet operator can make. This kind of calculation is highly appreciated by KTU examiners.)*

**Final Answer:**
- (a) **₹1,56,250/month** fuel saving
- (b) **₹12,00,000/year** accident cost saving
- (c) **ROI ≈ 1437.5%** — extremely favourable

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Training Methods

| Criterion | Classroom Training | Simulator Training | On-Road (BTW) Training | Telematics-Based Coaching |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cost per Driver** | Low (₹2,000–5,000) | High (₹15,000–50,000) | Medium (₹8,000–20,000) | Low (after setup) |
| **Safety Risk** | Zero | Zero | High | Zero |
| **Realism** | Low | Medium-High | Very High | High |
| **Skill Transfer** | Theoretical only | Muscle memory | Fully real-world | Continuous improvement |
| **Scalability** | High | Medium | Low (1:1 ratio) | Very High |
| **KTU Examiner Preference** | Mention as base | Mention as modern | **Most expected answer** | Mention as future trend |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Synthesis Note for KTU 2024 Scheme:** The best practice in 2024+ is a **blended learning approach** — classroom → simulator → on-road → telematics-driven continuous feedback. Mentioning this in an answer demonstrates Bloom's Level 6 (*Create*).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1: The Driver Management Lifecycle (DML)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[stage1: Manpower Planning] --> B[stage2: Recruitment and Selection]
    B --> C[stage3: Licensing and Verification]
    C --> D[stage4: Induction Training]
    D --> E[stage5: Deployment and Roster]
    E --> F[stage6: Performance Evaluation]
    F --> G[stage7: Motivation and Welfare]
    G --> H[stage8: Refresher Training]
    H --> E
    F --> I[stage9: Exit Management]
    I --> J[stage10: Replacement Planning]
    J --> A
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Stages 4, 5, 6, 7, 8 form a continuous inner loop of daily operations. Stage 9–10 represent the exit and replacement phase, which feeds back into Stage 1 — closing the lifecycle.

### 4.2 Diagram 2: Defensive Driving Training (DDT) — The IPDE Method

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[step1: IDENTIFY Hazards] --> B[step2: PREDICT Risk]
    B --> C[step3: DECIDE Action]
    C --> D[step4: EXECUTE Maneuver]
    D --> A
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The IPDE cycle is the foundational cognitive model of defensive driving. A trained driver continuously cycles through Identify → Predict → Decide → Execute, with a total cycle time under 1.5 seconds. KTU examiners expect this term in any answer on defensive driving.

### 4.3 Diagram 3: Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Modern Driver Training System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUTS
        P1[inputA: Trainee Driver Profile]
        P2[inputB: Vehicle Telematics Data]
        P3[inputC: Route and Traffic Feed]
        P4[inputD: Regulatory Updates]
    end
    subgraph PROCESSING_CORE
        Q1[engine1: AI Behavior Analyzer]
        Q2[engine2: Personalized Coaching Module]
        Q3[engine3: Assessment and Scoring Engine]
        Q4[engine4: Compliance Monitor]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUTS
        R1[outputA: Driver Performance Scorecard]
        R2[outputB: Real-time Audio Coaching]
        R3[outputC: Training Curriculum Updates]
        R4[outputD: Statutory Compliance Report]
    end
    P1 --> Q1
    P2 --> Q1
    P3 --> Q2
    P4 --> Q4
    Q1 --> Q3
    Q2 --> Q3
    Q3 --> R1
    Q2 --> R2
    Q4 --> R4
    Q3 --> R3
```

> **Reading the diagram:** This is a **block-level functional architecture** showing how modern (2024+) fleet operators integrate telematics, AI, and regulatory inputs to deliver personalized driver training. The **AI Behavior Analyzer** is the brain that ingests profile + telematics data to detect patterns (harsh braking, over-speeding, idle time). The **Personalized Coaching Module** then delivers micro-coaching in real time. KTU 2024 Scheme values such Industry 4.0 representations.

### 4.4 Diagram 4: Cost of Driver Turnover — The Hidden Iceberg

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[costA: Visible Costs] --> A1[Recruitment Ads]
    A --> A2[Background Checks]
    A --> A3[License Verification]
    B[costB: Semi-Visible Costs] --> B1[Orientation and Induction]
    B --> B2[Initial Training and Mentoring]
    B --> B3[Supervisor Time]
    C[costC: Hidden Costs] --> C1[Lost Productivity in Probation]
    C --> C2[Customer Service Disruption]
    C --> C3[Team Morale Impact]
    C --> C4[Accidents by New Drivers Higher]
    C --> C5[Brand and Reputation Damage]
    A1 --> TOT[totalCost: Total Cost of Turnover]
    A2 --> TOT
    A3 --> TOT
    B1 --> TOT
    B2 --> TOT
    B3 --> TOT
    C1 --> TOT
    C2 --> TOT
    C3 --> TOT
    C4 --> TOT
    C5 --> TOT
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The classic **iceberg model** applied to driver turnover. **Visible costs** (recruitment ads, background checks) are only **~20% of the total cost**. The **hidden costs** (~80%) are the ones that bleed fleet profitability. KTU examiners often set a 14-mark question around this concept.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]`

**Q: Define "Defensive Driving" and list any four key principles followed during defensive driving training.**

**Model Answer:**
Defensive Driving is a set of proactive driving skills and attitudes that allow a driver to identify potential hazards in advance and take corrective action to prevent accidents, even when other road users make mistakes.

**Four Key Principles (any four, 0.5 mark each; definition 1 mark):**

1. **Visual Scanning** — Continuously scan 360° using mirrors and peripheral vision, looking 15–20 seconds ahead.
2. **Hazard Recognition and IPDE Process** — Identify, Predict, Decide, Execute the manoeuvre.
3. **Safe Following Distance** — Maintain at least a **3-second gap** (5 seconds in rain/fog) from the vehicle ahead.
4. **Speed Management** — Drive at speeds appropriate to road, weather, traffic, and load conditions, not just the posted limit.
5. **Avoiding Distractions** — No mobile phones, eating, or in-cab non-driving activities.
6. **Escape Route Awareness** — Always know where to steer if a sudden hazard appears.

---

#### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]`

**Q: List and briefly explain the four main categories of costs incurred in the recruitment and training of a transport driver.**

**Model Answer (1 mark per category, 0.5 for example):**

1. **Recruitment Costs** — Advertising, agency fees, license verification, medical tests. *Example: Newspaper ad costing ₹25,000.*
2. **Training Costs** — Instructor fees, classroom materials, simulator time, BTW (behind-the-wheel) sessions, certification. *Example: ₹15,000 per driver for a 2-week defensive driving course.*
3. **Lost Productivity Costs** — Output lost while the new driver is on probation (3–6 months). *Example: ₹50,000/month × 3 months.*
4. **Administrative and Overhead Costs** — HR staff time, paperwork, software, management oversight. *Example: ₹5,000 per hire.*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Part A):**
> - **Do not** write vague statements like "training is important" without specifying *what kind* of training and *how* it helps.
> - Always **bold the key term** in your opening line and follow with bullet points.
> - In 3-mark questions, students often write only 2 points and lose 1 mark. Always provide **at least 4 points** in case one is considered insufficient.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Part B questions carry 14 marks each and **MUST have internal choice**. Two alternative questions (Q-A and Q-B) are provided below. Either may appear in the exam.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Style, KTU 2024 Scheme]`

**Q: (a) Explain in detail the various types of training programs given to drivers in a commercial transport organization. Highlight the importance of Defensive Driving Training (DDT) and Eco-Driving Training. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A modern transport organization provides a **multi-tiered training architecture** to ensure drivers are technically competent, legally compliant, safety-conscious, and economically efficient.

**(i) Pre-Service / Induction Training (2 marks)**
Conducted at the time of joining, lasting 2–4 weeks. It covers:
- Company-specific rules, policies, and code of conduct.
- Vehicle familiarization (walk-around inspection, instrument panel, controls).
- Route training on the specific geography the driver will operate in.
- Statutory documentation: logbook, trip sheet, e-way bill, toll procedures.

**(ii) Behind-the-Wheel (BTW) Training (2 marks)**
The most critical component. Conducted in a real vehicle on real roads for **40–100 hours** under a certified instructor. Covers:
- Clutch control, gear shifting, steering accuracy.
- Hill driving, ghat road handling, gradient braking.
- Reversing, parking, narrow road negotiation.
- Night driving and adverse weather driving.

**(iii) Defensive Driving Training (DDT) (1.5 marks)**
- Based on the **IPDE method** (Identify, Predict, Decide, Execute).
- Focuses on hazard perception, anticipation, and avoidance.
- Reduces accidents by **40%–60%** as per industry data.
- Includes skid control, emergency braking, and evasive manoeuvres.

**(iv) Eco-Driving / Fuel-Efficient Driving (1.5 marks)**
- Smooth acceleration and deceleration.
- Optimal gear usage, engine braking, cruise control on highways.
- Reduces fuel consumption by **8%–15%**, saving crores in large fleets.
- Reduces vehicle wear, tyre life, and emissions — aligns with **BS-VI and ESG goals**.

> *(Conclusion Line for full marks:)* Hence, a blended training approach combining BTW, DDT, and Eco-Driving delivers the maximum ROI for a transport organization.

---

**Q: (b) "Drivers are the most valuable and the most vulnerable asset of a transport company." Discuss this statement with reference to (i) the high cost of driver turnover, and (ii) welfare measures that reduce turnover. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The statement holds universally true in commercial road transport, where the driver controls a multi-crore asset and is the single biggest factor in safety and profitability.

**(i) High Cost of Driver Turnover (3 marks)**
The cost of replacing a driver is **1.5× to 2× the driver's annual salary** and falls into three categories:
- **Direct Costs:** Recruitment ads (₹25,000), background verification (₹5,000), license check (₹2,000), medical test (₹3,000). Total ≈ ₹35,000.
- **Training Costs:** 2–4 weeks of paid training at ₹500–800/day = ₹10,000–₹20,000. Loss of vehicle utilization during this period.
- **Indirect / Hidden Costs (the iceberg below the waterline):**
  - Lower productivity in the first 3–6 months (probation).
  - Higher accident rate — new drivers cause **2× to 3× more accidents** than experienced ones.
  - Increased vehicle wear due to rough handling.
  - Customer dissatisfaction and order loss.
  - Disruption to the team and supervisor morale.

**The Indian trucking industry faces a turnover of 30%–60% annually**, making this a chronic issue.

**(ii) Welfare Measures to Reduce Turnover (3 marks)**
- **Financial:** Timely salary, performance-linked incentive, festival bonus, provident fund, gratuity, accident insurance cover of ₹5–10 lakh.
- **Health & Safety:** Free annual medical check-up, eyesight test, rest stops, clean toilets at depots, ergonomic seat, AC cabin where possible.
- **Social:** Family accommodation near depots, children's schooling support, marriage/gift advances.
- **Professional:** Career growth path (Driver → Senior Driver → Trainer → Depot Manager), recognition awards (Driver of the Month), grievance redressal cell.
- **Work-Life Balance:** Two-days-off per month, fixed shift timings, vehicle return home policy for short-haul drivers.

> *(Conclusion Line for full marks:)* Companies like **Tata Motors' Sampoorna Driver Programme** and **Ashok Leyland's iGen Platform** have demonstrated that structured welfare reduces turnover by 20%–30% and accident rates by 40%, validating that the driver is indeed the most valuable asset — and worth investing in.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Style, KTU 2024 Scheme]`

**Q: (a) A transport company operates 30 trucks. Each truck makes 3 trips per day. Average trip time is 5 hours. Legal daily driving limit is 8 hours and the driver utilization factor is 0.80. Calculate the number of drivers required per day and the Driver-to-Vehicle Ratio. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer (with full step-by-step solution):**

**Step 1 — Total trip-hours demanded per day:**
$$
T_{total} = 30 \times 3 \times 5 = 450 \text{ driver-hours/day}
$$

**Step 2 — Effective driving hours supplied by one driver per day:**
$$
H_{eff} = 8 \times 0.80 = 6.4 \text{ hours/driver/day}
$$

**Step 3 — Drivers required per day:**
$$
D_{req} = \dfrac{450}{6.4} = 70.31 \approx 71 \text{ drivers}
$$

**Step 4 — Driver-to-Vehicle Ratio:**
$$
DVR = \dfrac{71}{30} = 2.37
$$

**Final Answer:** **71 drivers per day**, with **DVR = 2.37** — higher than the ideal benchmark of 1.5–1.8, indicating that the high trip frequency (3/day) is stretching the human resource.

---

**Q: (b) A fleet owner spends ₹1,50,000 on a Defensive Driving Training program for 25 drivers. Post-training, accidents dropped from 10 per year to 4 per year, and average fuel efficiency improved from 3.0 km/L to 3.3 km/L. The fleet runs 40,000 km/month and diesel costs ₹95/litre. Average cost per accident (insurance + downtime) is ₹2,00,000. Calculate the Training ROI over 1 year. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer (with full step-by-step solution):**

**Step 1 — Fuel saved per month (in litres):**
$$
F_{saved} = \left(\dfrac{1}{3.0} - \dfrac{1}{3.3}\right) \times 40{,}000
$$

$$
F_{saved} = (0.3333 - 0.3030) \times 40{,}000 = 0.0303 \times 40{,}000 = 1{,}212.12 \text{ litres/month}
$$

**Step 2 — Annual fuel saving (₹):**
$$
S_{fuel} = 1{,}212.12 \times 95 \times 12 = ₹13{,}81{,}818
$$

**Step 3 — Annual accident cost saving (₹):**
$$
S_{acc} = (10 - 4) \times 2{,}00{,}000 = ₹12{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 4 — Total annual benefit:**
$$
B = 13{,}81{,}818 + 12{,}00{,}000 = ₹25{,}81{,}818
$$

**Step 5 — Training ROI:**
$$
ROI = \dfrac{25{,}81{,}818 - 1{,}50{,}000}{1{,}50{,}000} \times 100 = \dfrac{24{,}31{,}818}{1{,}50{,}000} \times 100 = 1621.2\%
$$

**Final Answer:** **ROI ≈ 1621%** — a spectacular return, proving that even small investments in driver training yield extraordinary long-term savings.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Part B — 14 Marks):**
> 1. **For numericals, ALWAYS show the formula first, then the substitution, then the final answer.** Marks are awarded step-wise, not just for the final number.
> 2. **For theory, structure your answer with sub-headings (i), (ii), (iii) and use bullet points.** Wall-of-text answers lose 2–3 marks even if content is correct.
> 3. **Always write a concluding sentence** linking back to industry practice or a real company (Tata, Ashok Leyland, Allcargo). This shows Bloom's Level 5 (Evaluate) thinking and adds 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Do not ignore units.** "₹" for money, "hrs" for time, "%" for percentages. Missing units = 0.5 mark deduction per occurrence.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Driver is responsible for ~80–90% of accidents** — training is non-negotiable.
- [x] **Driver-to-Vehicle Ratio (DVR):** ideal is **1.5–1.8**; above 2.0 signals over-stretched HR.
- [x] **Indian turnover rate:** **30–60%** — highest among professions; welfare is the antidote.
- [x] **Hours of Service (HoS):** **8 hrs continuous / 9 hrs total in a 12-hr shift** under the **Motor Transport Workers Act, 1961**.
- [x] **Defensive Driving = IPDE** (Identify, Predict, Decide, Execute) — must know acronym.
- [x] **Eco-Driving saves 8–15% fuel** — translates to crores for large fleets.
- [x] **BTW (Behind-the-Wheel) training** is the most important training type — mention in every answer.
- [x] **Cost of replacement = 1.5×–2× annual salary**; hidden costs dominate (iceberg model).
- [x] **Recruitment sources:** Employment exchanges, driver unions, referrals, online portals.
- [x] **Licensing:** HMV license with **Transport Vehicle (TV) endorsement** mandatory for commercial drivers.
- [x] **Training types to remember:** Pre-service, BTW, Defensive, Eco, Refresher, Specialized, First Aid, Customer Service.
- [x] **Training ROI is always positive** — show numerical proof in 14-mark answers for full marks.
- [x] **Blended training** (classroom + simulator + on-road + telematics) is the 2024+ best practice.
- [x] **Performance KPIs:** km/L, accident rate, on-time delivery, customer score, working days.
- [x] **Welfare = retention** — timely salary, insurance, medical, housing, recognition, career path.
- [x] **Modern tech:** AI dashcams, telematics, ADAS feedback, gamified training apps.
- [x] **Indian context:** National Highways, ghat roads, monsoon, night-time ban on certain vehicles, BS-VI compliance.
- [x] **Always end with a real-industry example** (Tata Sampoorna, Ashok Leyland iGen, Allcargo, VRL) — this clinches the 1–2 evaluation marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vehicle Maintenance Management - Preventive vs. Corrective Maintenance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT866 - VEHICLE TRANSPORT MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Vehicle Maintenance Management - Preventive vs. Corrective Maintenance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 — Vehicle Maintenance Management: Preventive vs. Corrective Maintenance

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vehicle Maintenance Management (VMM)** is the systematic, planned, and controlled execution of all activities — administrative, technical, and managerial — required to keep a vehicle or fleet in a state of operational readiness, or to restore it to that state, throughout its useful service life.

Within VMM, two fundamentally opposing (yet complementary) **maintenance philosophies** are examined in this module:

| # | Maintenance Type | KTU 2024 Definition |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Preventive Maintenance (PM)** | A scheduled, time- or usage-based intervention performed on a vehicle *before* a failure occurs, intended to reduce the probability of degradation or in-service breakdown. |
| 2 | **Corrective Maintenance (CM)** | An unscheduled, event-driven intervention performed *after* a failure has occurred, intended to restore the vehicle (or sub-system) to a defined operational state. |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU syllabus terminology, **Corrective Maintenance is often called "Breakdown Maintenance"** or **"Reactive Maintenance"**. The terms are used **interchangeably** in the KTU question papers.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of your own body and the difference between a **regular health check-up** vs. a **trip to the emergency room**:

- **Preventive Maintenance (PM)** = The **annual health check-up**. You go to the doctor *before* you fall sick — get blood tests, an ECG, maybe a vaccine booster. The visit is planned, scheduled, costs a known amount, and is meant to *prevent* a major illness.
- **Corrective Maintenance (CM)** = The **emergency room visit**. Something has already gone wrong — chest pain, a fracture, sudden fever. You rush in, pay an unpredictable (often high) bill, and the recovery is longer and more painful.

A smart vehicle fleet manager behaves like a smart patient — they do the **regular check-up** (PM) and only hope they never need the **emergency room** (CM). The economic, safety, and operational goal of a transport enterprise is therefore to **shift the maintenance curve as far left as possible toward prevention**.

### 1.3 Standard Industry Metrics Used in This Module

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following four metrics are the **core evaluation parameters** KTU expects students to use in every maintenance-related numerical. Memorize the symbols and units.
>
> - **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)** — expressed in **hours** or **km**.
> - **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair)** — expressed in **hours**.
> - **Availability (A)** — a **dimensionless ratio** between **0 and 1** (often expressed as a **percentage**).
> - **Failure Rate ($\lambda$)** — failures per unit of operating time, units = **failures/hour** or **failures/km**.

These four symbols are the alphabet of vehicle maintenance engineering — every formula in Section 2 will be written using them.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Preventive Maintenance (PM) — Deep Theoretical Breakdown

PM is **proactive**. The decision to intervene is based on a **trigger** (time or usage), not on observed damage. Its sub-classes, which examiners frequently test, are:

- **Scheduled / Time-Based PM:** Intervention at fixed calendar intervals (e.g., oil change every 6 months regardless of km).
- **Usage-Based / Odometer PM:** Intervention at fixed km-readings (e.g., every 10,000 km).
- **Predictive / Condition-Based PM:** Intervention triggered by *measured* condition data (vibration analysis, oil spectroscopy, brake-pad thickness sensor).
- **Prescriptive PM:** The newest form — uses AI/IoT to *prescribe* the exact next action and timing.

**Why PM works (the engineering logic):**
1. Components have a **wear curve** — degradation accelerates non-linearly as they age.
2. Catching degradation early converts a *catastrophic* failure (engine seizure) into a *minor* repair (piston-ring replacement).
3. **Economies of scheduling** — labour, spares, and bay time can be booked in advance at lower cost.
4. **Safety & compliance** — certain statutory checks (brakes, lights, PUC) are *legally* mandatory in India under the **Central Motor Vehicles Rules (CMVR), 1989**.

### 2.2 Corrective Maintenance (CM) — Deep Theoretical Breakdown

CM is **reactive**. Action begins **only after** a failure is reported. The internal logic is:

- The vehicle is operated **until it cannot perform** its intended function.
- A **failure event** is logged, a **work order** is opened, and **unscheduled** labour and spares are mobilised.
- Common KTU-tested subtypes:
  * **Emergency / Breakdown Repair:** Vehicle is immobilised; priority is restoration.
  * **Run-to-Failure (RTF):** Strategy applied deliberately to non-critical, low-cost items (e.g., a cabin bulb).
  * **Deferred CM:** Failure is noted, but repair is postponed to a planned window (e.g., a noisy wheel bearing repaired during the next PM).

**Why CM still exists in the real world (and why KTU marks you down if you ignore it):**

- Some failures are **stochastic** (e.g., a tyre blowout from a sharp object) and cannot be predicted.
- PM is **never economically optimal for 100% of components** — a balance must be struck.
- For very low-value items, the cost of PM *exceeds* the cost of replacement upon failure.

### 2.3 The Core Trade-Off: Cost Curve Logic

The foundational economic insight of this module is captured in a single graph (described here in text because it appears in every KTU paper as a 7-mark question):

- **PM Cost (per km)** *decreases* as the PM interval becomes **longer** (fewer interventions).
- **Failure / CM Cost (per km)** *increases* as the PM interval becomes **longer** (more breakdowns).
- The **Total Maintenance Cost (per km)** is the **U-shaped sum** of the two.
- The **optimal PM interval ($T^*$)** is the point where the **slopes of the two curves are equal in magnitude but opposite in sign** — i.e., the minimum of the total cost curve.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are the **only** formulas required for Module 2 numerics. Master the table — every KTU question is built from these.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Notes / Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Steady-State Availability | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | $A$ dimensionless; $MTBF$, $MTTR$ in hours | Assumes exponential time-to-failure distribution. $0 \le A \le 1$. |
| 2 | Inherent Availability | $A_i = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | Same as above | Excludes logistics & administrative delay. |
| 3 | Achieved Availability | $A_a = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR + MDT}$ | $MDT$ = Mean Down Time (hours) | Includes administrative & supply delay. |
| 4 | Mean Time Between Failures | $MTBF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | $\lambda$ = failure rate (failures/hour) | Valid for **constant failure rate** (useful life phase of the bathtub curve). |
| 5 | Cost per km (Total Maintenance) | $C_{total} = C_{PM} + C_{CM}$ | $C$ in ₹ per km | $C_{PM}$ decreases with interval; $C_{CM}$ increases. |
| 6 | Optimal PM Interval (First-Order) | $T^* \approx \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot C_p}{C_f \cdot \lambda^2}}$ | $C_p$ = cost of one PM; $C_f$ = cost of one failure | Derived by minimising $C_{total}(T)$. |
| 7 | Reliability Function (Exponential) | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | $R(t)$ dimensionless probability | Probability of survival up to time $t$. |
| 8 | Unreliability (Failure Probability) | $F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ | Dimensionless | Probability of failure by time $t$. |
| 9 | Cost-Effectiveness Ratio (PM) | $CER = \dfrac{C_{PM}}{MTBF_{PM} - MTBF_{no-PM}}$ | $CER$ in ₹ per hour of MTBF gain | Higher CER $\Rightarrow$ PM less justifiable. |
| 10 | Breakdown Frequency Ratio | $BFR = \dfrac{\text{No. of Breakdowns}}{\text{Total km operated}} \times 10^6$ | Per million km (stand. KTU unit) | Standard KTU fleet KPI. |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

These formulas are not academic toys — they are used daily by:

- **Fleet operators** (Ola, Uber, FedEx, India Post) to decide PM intervals.
- **Defence logistics** (Indian Army, Air Force) to calculate vehicle mission availability.
- **Public transport undertakings** (KSRTC, BMTC, DTC, BEST) for KPI dashboards.
- **OEMs** (Tata Motors, Ashok Leyland, Mahindra) to design service schedules.
- **Insurance companies** to compute commercial-vehicle premiums.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Steady-State Availability

**Engineering Statement:** Over a long operating period, the *fraction of time* the vehicle is in an operational (up) state is called its **steady-state availability**.

**Step 1 — Define the operating cycle.**

For a repairable system like a bus, one full cycle consists of an *Up Time* and a *Down Time*:

$$Uptime = MTBF \quad ; \quad Downtime = MTTR$$

**Step 2 — Total cycle length.**

$$T_{cycle} = MTBF + MTTR$$

**Step 3 — Definition of Availability.**

Availability is the proportion of the cycle during which the system is **Up**:

$$A = \frac{Uptime}{Total\ Cycle\ Time}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute.**

$$A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$$

**Step 5 — Express as percentage (KTU convention).**

$$A\ \% = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \times 100$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logically:** A vehicle with $MTBF = 800$ hours and $MTTR = 2$ hours has $A = 800/802 \approx 99.75\%$, i.e., it is *available* 99.75% of the time. Reducing $MTTR$ to 1 hour pushes $A$ to $99.875\%$ — a small *number* but a *massive* real-world operational gain.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Optimal PM Interval (Total Cost Minimisation)

**Engineering Statement:** Find the PM interval $T$ that minimises the *total cost per unit time* of operating a vehicle.

**Step 1 — Express the cost components per unit time.**

Let $C_p$ be the cost of a single PM action, $C_f$ the cost of a single failure, and $\lambda$ the (constant) failure rate. The PM is performed every $T$ time units.

The PM cost rate (₹ per unit time) is:

$$C_{PM}(T) = \frac{C_p}{T}$$

The expected number of failures per unit time, given a PM every $T$ hours, is approximately:

$$C_{CM}(T) = \lambda \cdot T \cdot \frac{C_f}{2}$$

(The factor of $\tfrac{1}{2}$ comes from averaging the failure probability uniformly over the interval — a KTU-standard simplification.)

**Step 2 — Total cost function.**

$$C_{total}(T) = C_{PM}(T) + C_{CM}(T) = \frac{C_p}{T} + \frac{\lambda \cdot T \cdot C_f}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate with respect to $T$ and set to zero.**

$$\frac{dC_{total}}{dT} = -\frac{C_p}{T^2} + \frac{\lambda \cdot C_f}{2} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $T^*$.**

$$\frac{C_p}{T^{*2}} = \frac{\lambda \cdot C_f}{2}$$

$$T^{*2} = \frac{2 \cdot C_p}{\lambda \cdot C_f}$$

$$\boxed{\,T^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot C_p}{\lambda \cdot C_f}}\,}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.**

- If $C_p$ (PM cost) **rises**, the optimal interval **lengthens** (do PM less often, since it is expensive).
- If $C_f$ (failure cost) **rises**, the optimal interval **shortens** (do PM more often, since failures are expensive).
- If $\lambda$ (failure rate) **rises**, the optimal interval **shortens** (the component is failing more often, intervene sooner).

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical — KTU Standard 14-Mark Style

> **Question (typical KTU style):** A delivery van records an average **4 breakdowns per 6 months**. Each breakdown keeps the van out of service for an average of **2 days**. Each PM action costs **₹8,000** and the average cost of a single breakdown repair is **₹35,000**. Calculate:
> (a) Steady-state availability
> (b) The optimal PM interval $T^*$.

**Solution (a) — Availability:**

Convert everything to a common unit. Use **hours**.

6 months $\approx 180$ days $\approx 180 \times 24 = 4320$ hours.

$MTBF = 4320 / 4 = 1080\ \text{hours}$.

$MTTR = 2\ \text{days} = 48\ \text{hours}$.

$$A = \frac{1080}{1080 + 48} = \frac{1080}{1128} = 0.9574 = 95.74\%$$

**Solution (b) — Optimal PM Interval:**

Failure rate:

$$\lambda = \frac{1}{MTBF} = \frac{1}{1080}\ \text{failures/hour}$$

$$T^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot C_p}{\lambda \cdot C_f}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot 8000}{(1/1080) \cdot 35000}}$$

$$T^* = \sqrt{\frac{16000 \cdot 1080}{35000}} = \sqrt{\frac{17{,}280{,}000}{35{,}000}} = \sqrt{493.71}$$

$$\boxed{\,T^* \approx 22.22\ \text{hours of operation}\,}$$

**Interpretation:** The van should receive a PM roughly every **22 hours of running** to minimise total maintenance cost.

### 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix — PM vs. CM

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix is the **single most exam-relevant summary** of Module 2. The KTU examiner often asks students to fill in such a table for **7 marks**.

| Dimension | Preventive Maintenance | Corrective Maintenance |
|---|---|---|
| Trigger | Time / usage / condition | Actual failure event |
| Planning horizon | Long / scheduled | Short / unscheduled |
| Cost per intervention | Low to moderate | Moderate to high |
| Total lifecycle cost | Lower (if well-designed) | Higher |
| Vehicle downtime | Low & predictable | High & unpredictable |
| Effect on safety | Strongly positive | Negative |
| Spare-parts inventory | Steady, JIT-friendly | Erratic, high buffer |
| Labour utilisation | Smooth, planned | Spikes, overtime-heavy |
| Customer impact | Minimal disruption | High disruption (cancellations) |
| Skill level needed | Standard technician | Diagnostic specialist |
| Data dependency | High (records & history) | Low |
| Suitable for | Safety-critical, high-utilisation vehicles | Low-criticality, low-cost components |
| Typical % of total maintenance cost in well-run fleet | 70 – 85 % | 15 – 30 % |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Flow — Maintenance Decision Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Vehicle in Service]) --> B{Monitoring Data Available?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Condition Threshold Crossed?}
    B -- No --> D{Scheduled PM Due?}
    B -- Yes --> C
    D -- Yes --> E[Trigger Preventive Maintenance]
    D -- No --> F[Continue Normal Operation]
    C -- Yes --> G[Trigger Predictive PM]
    C -- No --> F
    E --> H[Vehicle Out of Service]
    G --> H
    F --> A
    A2([Component Fails Unexpectedly]) --> I[Log Failure Event]
    I --> J[Open Unscheduled Work Order]
    J --> K[Source Spares & Labour]
    K --> L[Execute Corrective Maintenance]
    L --> M[Return to Service]
    M --> N[Update MTBF Database]
    N --> A
    H --> O[Replace / Inspect Components]
    O --> P[Update PM Interval Logic]
    P --> A

    style A2 fill:#C0392B,stroke:#641E16,color:#FFFFFF

    style G fill:#27AE60,stroke:#0E6251,color:#FFFFFF
    style L fill:#E67E22,stroke:#784212,color:#FFFFFF
```

### 4.2 Bathtub Curve — Why PM Timing Matters

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Infant Mortality Phase] -->|Early-life failures| B[Useful Life Phase]
    B -->|Wear-out begins| C[Wear-out Phase]
    A1[High Failure Rate] -->|Burn-in, debugging| A
    B1[Constant Low Failure Rate] -->|PM interval set here| B
    C1[Rising Failure Rate] -->|Overhaul, replacement| C
    style A fill:#E74C3C,stroke:#641E16,color:#FFFFFF
    style B fill:#27AE60,stroke:#0E6251,color:#FFFFFF
    style C fill:#E67E22,stroke:#784212,color:#FFFFFF
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** The *constant low failure rate* region of the bathtub curve is precisely where the **$MTBF = 1/\lambda$** and the **Availability** formulas are valid. The examiner often tests whether students know that PM intervals should be tuned to *end* the useful-life phase — i.e., schedule overhaul **just before** the wear-out phase begins.

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — PM vs. CM Systems

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PM["PREVENTIVE MAINTENANCE SYSTEM"]
        P1[PM Scheduler Module] --> P2[Work-Order Generator]
        P2 --> P3[Bay & Labour Allocator]

        P4 --> P5[Quality Inspection & Sign-off]
        P5 --> P6[PM History Database]
        P6 --> P1
    end
    subgraph CM["CORRECTIVE MAINTENANCE SYSTEM"]
        C1[Failure Reporting Portal] --> C2[Diagnostic Workstation]
        C2 --> C3[Spare-Part Lookup Module]
        C3 --> C4[Emergency Repair Bay]
        C4 --> C5[Root-Cause Analysis Rpt]
        C5 --> C6[CM History Database]
        C6 --> C2
    end
    P6 <-->|Continuous Feedback| C6
    C6 -->|Feeds PM Trigger Logic| P1
    style PM fill:#D5F5E3,stroke:#0E6251
    style CM fill:#FADBD8,stroke:#641E16
```
