
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define Preventive Maintenance. List any four activities that fall under a standard PM schedule for a commercial bus.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Preventive Maintenance is a *planned, time- or usage-based* set of service activities carried out on a vehicle **before** a failure occurs, intended to keep the vehicle in a defined operational state and to reduce the probability of in-service breakdown.

**Four PM activities (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
1. **Engine oil and oil-filter change** at prescribed km intervals.
2. **Air-filter cleaning/replacement** and **fuel-filter replacement**.
3. **Brake-pad/thickness inspection** and brake-fluid top-up.
4. **Tyre rotation, wheel-alignment check, and wheel-balancing.**
*(Acceptable alternatives: coolant top-up, clutch free-play check, electrical-system check, battery water top-up, lubrication of chassis grease points.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*What is Corrective Maintenance? When is it considered an economically justified strategy?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Corrective Maintenance (also called Breakdown or Reactive Maintenance) is the set of repair activities performed **after** a component or system has *failed*, in order to **restore** it to a working condition. It is unscheduled and event-driven.

**Economic justification (1.5 marks):** CM is justified when:
1. The **cost of PM exceeds the cost of replacement upon failure** (low-value items, e.g., a cabin bulb).
2. The component has a **very low failure rate** in normal service (run-to-failure is cheaper than PM).
3. The component is **non-critical** — its failure does not affect safety, regulatory compliance, or revenue.
4. The component is **redundant** — failure of one unit does not halt the vehicle.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare Preventive Maintenance and Corrective Maintenance across **any seven** performance dimensions. State, with reasoning, which is preferable for a **state transport corporation** running 5,000 buses on intercity routes.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A logistics company's trucks have $MTBF = 1500\ \text{hours}$ and $MTTR = 6\ \text{hours}$. Calculate:
  (i) Steady-state availability.
  (ii) The percentage improvement in availability if $MTTR$ is reduced to $3\ \text{hours}$ through better workshop tooling.
  (iii) The failure rate $\lambda$.

---

**Solution A(a):** Refer to the **Comparative Analytical Matrix in Section 3.4** of these notes. The student must tabulate **seven dimensions** (e.g., trigger, cost, downtime, safety, spares, labour, customer impact) and clearly state that for an STU:

- **PM is strongly preferable** because (i) passenger **safety** is paramount, (ii) **regulatory compliance** (CMVR) demands scheduled checks, (iii) breakdowns on intercity routes cause **massive reputational and financial loss**, and (iv) the **high daily utilisation** of 5,000 buses means any breakdown causes a cascading schedule disruption.

**Solution A(b):**

**(i) Steady-state Availability:**

$$A_1 = \frac{1500}{1500 + 6} = \frac{1500}{1506} = 0.99601 = 99.601\%$$

**(ii) Availability after MTTR reduction:**

$$A_2 = \frac{1500}{1500 + 3} = \frac{1500}{1503} = 0.99800 = 99.800\%$$

**Percentage improvement in availability:**

$$\Delta A\ \% = \frac{A_2 - A_1}{A_1} \times 100 = \frac{0.99800 - 0.99601}{0.99601} \times 100$$

$$\Delta A\ \% = \frac{0.00199}{0.99601} \times 100 = 0.1998\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> The *absolute* improvement looks small (≈0.2 %), but on a **24-hour operating day** it translates to roughly **5 extra hours of availability per truck per year** — a huge commercial gain at fleet scale.

**(iii) Failure rate:**

$$\lambda = \frac{1}{MTBF} = \frac{1}{1500} = 6.667 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{failures/hour}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Style]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of the **optimal PM interval**. Derive the expression $T^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2C_p}{\lambda C_f}}$ starting from a clearly stated cost function. Discuss how $T^*$ shifts when (i) $C_f$ doubles, and (ii) $\lambda$ doubles.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch of the **bathtub curve**, explain the **three failure-rate phases** of a vehicle component. Indicate the phase in which Preventive Maintenance is most effective and justify your answer.

---

**Solution B(a):**

The optimal PM interval is the PM periodicity that **minimises the total maintenance cost per unit time**, balancing the rising cost of failures against the cost of performing PMs.

Starting from the cost function:

$$C_{total}(T) = \frac{C_p}{T} + \frac{\lambda T C_f}{2}$$

Differentiating and equating to zero:

$$\frac{dC_{total}}{dT} = -\frac{C_p}{T^2} + \frac{\lambda C_f}{2} = 0$$

Solving yields:

$$\boxed{\,T^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2C_p}{\lambda C_f}}\,}$$

**Effect of (i) $C_f$ doubling:**
$T^*$ scales with $1/\sqrt{C_f}$ → $T^*$ reduces by a factor of $\sqrt{1/2} \approx 0.707$ → **optimal PM interval shortens by ~29.3 %**.
(Reasoning: failure is now more expensive, so intervene more often.)

**Effect of (ii) $\lambda$ doubling:**
$T^*$ scales with $1/\sqrt{\lambda}$ → $T^*$ again **shortens by ~29.3 %**.
(Reasoning: component is failing twice as often, so PM must be more frequent.)

**Solution B(b):**

Refer to the **Bathtub Curve diagram in Section 4.2**.

The three phases are:
1. **Infant-Mortality / Burn-in Phase (Region I):** High initial failure rate, decreasing with time. Failures are *manufacturing/installation defects*. Strategy: **debugging, replacement under warranty, OEM burn-in**.
2. **Useful-Life Phase (Region II):** Failure rate is **constant and low**. Failures are *random*. This is the **longest phase**.
3. **Wear-out Phase (Region III):** Failure rate **rises sharply** with age. Failures are *age-related* (fatigue, wear, corrosion).

**PM is most effective at the END of Phase II / beginning of Phase III** — i.e., perform a *major overhaul* **just before** the wear-out failure rate begins to rise. Performing PM too early (in Phase II) wastes money; performing it too late leads to catastrophic Phase-III failures.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "Preventive" with "Predictive"** — they are *not* the same. PM is *scheduled*; Predictive Maintenance is *condition-triggered*. Marks are deducted when these are used interchangeably.
> 2. **Skipping the derivation in Q B(a)** — writing the formula $T^*$ without showing $\frac{dC_{total}}{dT} = 0$ costs **2 marks** minimum.
> 3. **Wrong units in availability** — forgetting to convert days → hours in $MTTR$ is a **classic 1-mark loss**.
> 4. **Bathtub curve sketch** — must show *all three* phases explicitly with the curve; a vague "U-shaped" drawing gets zero credit.
> 5. **No concluding statement in 14-mark questions** — KTU examiners deduct 1 mark if the answer ends abruptly without a concluding remark (e.g., "Thus, PM is preferred for…").
> 6. **Mixing up MTBF and MTTR** — MTBF is *uptime* between failures, MTTR is *repair* time. Reversing them in a formula will give $A < 0.5$, which is a red flag the examiner will catch.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 2**

- **Two philosophies:** Preventive (proactive) vs. Corrective (reactive). Both are valid; the *mix* is the management decision.
- **Four core metrics:** $MTBF$, $MTTR$, Availability $A$, Failure rate $\lambda$.
- **Master formula:** $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ — the single most-tested equation in this module.
- **Optimal PM interval:** $T^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2C_p}{\lambda C_f}}$ — derived by minimising $C_{total}(T)$.
- **Reliability:** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ for the useful-life phase.
- **Cost logic:** PM cost falls as interval lengthens; CM cost rises as interval lengthens. Sum is a **U-curve**; the minimum is $T^*$.
- **Bathtub curve:** Infant-Mortality → Useful-Life → Wear-out. PM targets the **boundary** between phases II and III.
- **PM advantages:** lower downtime, predictable cost, safety, regulatory compliance, customer satisfaction.
- **CM advantages:** lower upfront cost, suits non-critical items, no planning overhead, suits random failures.
- **Industry benchmark:** a well-run fleet maintains **70–85 %** of its maintenance spend on PM and only **15–30 %** on CM.
- **Indian regulatory hook:** CMVR 1989 makes certain PM activities *legally mandatory* (brake test, headlight aim, horn, pollution under control).
- **Numerics tip:** always **convert all time units to a common base (hours)** before substituting into $A$ or $T^*$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT866 - VEHICLE TRANSPORT MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Maintenance Scheduling and Record Keeping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Maintenance Scheduling?

**Maintenance Scheduling** is the systematic, pre-planned arrangement of inspection, servicing, repair, and replacement activities for a vehicle or a fleet of vehicles, executed at predetermined intervals (time, mileage, engine hours, or operating conditions) to ensure **maximum availability, safety, reliability, and operational economy**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Phrasing):**
> *"Maintenance scheduling is the function of determining the optimum frequency and content of maintenance operations on a vehicle, such that the cost of preventive maintenance plus the cost of failure downtime is minimized over the vehicle's economic life."*

In essence, scheduling answers three questions:
1. **What** should be done? (Job/task identification)
2. **When** should it be done? (Interval/Trigger)
3. **Who** will do it? (Manpower/Resource allocation)

---

## 1.2 What is Record Keeping?

**Record Keeping** in vehicle transport management refers to the **systematic documentation of every maintenance event, inspection, repair, fuel consumption, driver behaviour, and operating cost** of a vehicle over its service life. It provides the historical data backbone on which *every* future scheduling decision is built.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** Without proper records, there is *no scheduling* — only guesswork. Records transform maintenance from a **reactive expense** into a **predictable, controllable engineering function**.

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine your own health:

| Human Health Analogy | Vehicle Maintenance Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Annual medical check-up | Scheduled periodic service |
| Daily brushing & bathing | Daily pre-trip inspection (PM-1) |
| Medical history file at a hospital | Vehicle history card / Logbook |
| Dentist visit when tooth aches | Breakdown / Corrective maintenance |
| Wearables predicting heart rate | Condition-Based Monitoring (telematics, OBD) |
| Vaccination schedule | Time-based preventive maintenance |

Just as a doctor cannot prescribe treatment without your **medical history**, a fleet manager cannot schedule maintenance intelligently without proper **vehicle records**.

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of Thumb for KTU Answers:** Maintenance Scheduling = **Plan**. Record Keeping = **Memory**. A transport company without records has *"fleet amnesia"* — it repeats the same mistakes and pays the same repair costs repeatedly.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Maintenance (Foundation Concept)

Maintenance is broadly classified into **five types** that must be memorized for KTU exams:

1. **Breakdown Maintenance (BM):** Repair *after* failure. Reactive, expensive, unsafe. (Also called *Run-to-Failure*.)
2. **Preventive Maintenance (PM):** Scheduled service *before* failure. The heart of Module 2.
3. **Predictive Maintenance (PdM):** Service *based on condition* measured by sensors/vibration analysis.
4. **Corrective Maintenance (CM):** Planned repair after a defect is *detected* but before it causes failure.
5. **Opportunity Maintenance:** Small repairs piggy-backed on a vehicle already in the workshop for another job.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constant:** The industry benchmark **PM-to-BM ratio** in well-managed fleets is **80:20** (preventive:breakdown). A fleet running **50:50 or worse** is considered poorly managed and is a common KTU question trigger.

---

## 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

The following industry-standard figures appear frequently in KTU questions:

- **Vehicle availability target (good fleet): ≥ 90%**
- **PM-to-BM ratio target: 80:20**
- **Standard service intervals (passenger car): every 5,000 km or 3 months**
- **Standard service intervals (heavy commercial vehicle): every 10,000 km or 1 month**
- **Daily inspection time (PM-1): 15 to 20 minutes**
- **Workshop occupancy (ideal): 80%**
- **Scheduled maintenance downtime (target): ≤ 10% of available time**

---

## 1.6 Hierarchy of Scheduling Decisions

Maintenance scheduling operates on **three decision levels**:

1. **Strategic Level** (Fleet Owner / GM): *"Which vehicles to retire? What is the replacement policy?"*
2. **Tactical Level** (Maintenance Manager): *"What is the optimal PM interval? When to overhaul the engine?"*
3. **Operational Level** (Foreman / Mechanic): *"Tomorrow morning which truck gets serviced first?"*

Record keeping supports **all three levels**, but is *most critical* at the tactical level where interval decisions are made.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Objectives of Maintenance Scheduling

A well-designed maintenance schedule must satisfy **five engineering objectives simultaneously**:

1. **Maximize vehicle availability** (uptime).
2. **Minimize total cost of ownership** (TCO).
3. **Ensure passenger and road safety** (regulatory compliance).
4. **Extend useful life** of vehicle and components.
5. **Reduce unscheduled downtime** to a statistically predictable minimum.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conflict:** Objectives 1 and 3 push you toward *more* maintenance; objective 2 pushes you toward *less* (because every PM costs money). The art of scheduling is finding the **economic optimum** where the *sum* of PM cost and failure cost is minimum.

---

## 2.2 Types of Maintenance Scheduling Strategies

### Strategy A: Time-Based (Calendar) Scheduling
Service is performed at **fixed intervals of time** (e.g., every 30 days) regardless of actual usage. Common in low-utilization fleets, government vehicles, and rental fleets.

- **Pro:** Simple to plan, easy to remember.
- **Con:** Wastes money on under-used vehicles; misses over-used ones.

### Strategy B: Usage-Based (Mileage / Hours) Scheduling
Service is performed after a **fixed quantum of use** (e.g., every 5,000 km or every 250 engine hours). Standard for most modern commercial fleets.

- **Pro:** Maintenance matches actual wear.
- **Con:** Requires accurate odometer/hour-meter records.

### Strategy C: Condition-Based (Predictive) Scheduling
Service is triggered when a **measured parameter crosses a threshold** — oil analysis, vibration, tyre pressure sensors, brake-pad thickness scan, OBD-II fault codes.

- **Pro:** Maintenance happens exactly when needed.
- **Con:** High upfront cost of sensors/diagnostics.

### Strategy D: Reliability-Centered Maintenance (RCM)
A sophisticated strategy that classifies every component into one of three categories:
- **Safety-critical** → strict PM (e.g., brakes, steering).
- **Economically critical** → condition-based (e.g., tyres, batteries).
- **Non-critical** → run-to-failure (e.g., interior trim).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** **"T-U-C-R"** → **Time, Usage, Condition, RCM** (in increasing complexity and decreasing number of components).

---

## 2.3 The Concept of Optimal Maintenance Interval

Let:
- $C_p$ = cost of one preventive maintenance operation (₹).
- $C_f$ = cost of one failure (breakdown) event (₹).
- $T$ = interval between PM operations (km or hours).
- $\lambda$ = failure rate (failures per km, assumed increasing with $T$).

The **average cost per km** of operating the component is:

$$
\text{Average Cost} = \frac{C_p}{T} + \frac{C_f \cdot \lambda(T)}{2}
$$

The **optimum interval $T^*$** is obtained by differentiating with respect to $T$ and setting to zero:

$$
\frac{d}{dT}\left[\frac{C_p}{T} + \frac{C_f \lambda(T)}{2}\right] = 0
$$

This yields the **economic trade-off principle**:

> *"As the PM interval shortens, PM cost rises but failure cost drops. The optimum is where the two curves intersect in slope."*

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | Availability of a vehicle | dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| 2 | $MTBF = \dfrac{T_{\text{total operating}}}{N_{\text{failures}}}$ | Mean Time Between Failures | hours / km |
| 3 | $MTTR = \dfrac{T_{\text{total downtime}}}{N_{\text{failures}}}$ | Mean Time To Repair | hours |
| 4 | $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{MTBF}$ | Failure rate | per hour / per km |
| 5 | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Reliability at time $t$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\text{Cost/km} = \dfrac{C_p}{T} + \dfrac{C_f \lambda(T)}{2}$ | Average maintenance cost per unit distance | ₹/km |
| 7 | $\text{PM:BM ratio} = \dfrac{N_{PM}}{N_{BM}}$ | Quality of maintenance management | ratio |
| 8 | $\text{Uptime}\% = \dfrac{\text{Available hours}}{\text{Calendar hours}} \times 100$ | Fleet utilization efficiency | % |
| 9 | $\text{BCR (Benefit-Cost Ratio)} = \dfrac{\text{Avoided failure cost}}{\text{PM cost}}$ | Justification for PM | ratio |
| 10 | $S = \dfrac{\text{Actual km run}}{\text{Scheduled km run}} \times 100$ | Schedule adherence | % |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Safety in Tables:** All absolute-value / division bars use `\vert` or `\frac{}{}` to prevent markdown table breakage. **Never** use a raw `\vert` character inside a table cell.

---

## 2.5 Record Keeping — The Document Hierarchy

Record keeping is *layered*. Each layer serves a different purpose:

| Layer | Document | Purpose | Audience |
|---|---|---|---|
| L1 | **Driver's Daily Report (DDR)** | Trip-wise fuel, km, defects | Driver → Foreman |
| L2 | **Job Card / Work Order** | What was done on each vehicle today | Mechanic → Foreman |
| L3 | **Vehicle History Card** | Cumulative record of *one* vehicle | Maintenance Manager |
| L4 | **Maintenance Log Book** | Fleet-wide record (all vehicles) | Workshop Manager |
| L5 | **Cost Ledger** | ₹ spent on each vehicle | Accounts / GM |
| L6 | **Computerized Maintenance Management System (CMMS)** | Digital integration of L1–L5 | All levels |

---

## 2.6 Essential Fields in a Vehicle History Card

A KTU-board favourite question. The minimum data captured per maintenance event:

1. Vehicle registration number.
2. Date of service and odometer reading.
3. Job description (e.g., "Engine oil + filter change").
4. Parts consumed with quantity and cost.
5. Labour hours consumed.
6. Mechanic ID / signature.
7. Next-due date and next-due odometer reading.
8. Defects observed and action taken.
9. Road-test result / sign-off.
10. Workshop supervisor's signature.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world link:** Modern OEMs (Tata Motors, Ashok Leyland, Maruti Suzuki) provide *digitally* linked service history that follows the vehicle across owners, dramatically improving resale value — a direct commercial justification for record keeping.

---

## 2.7 Computerized Maintenance Management System (CMMS)

A CMMS is the modern evolution of paper-based record keeping. Core modules:

- **Equipment Master Database** (vehicle specs, serial numbers).
- **PM Schedule Generator** (auto-creates work orders).
- **Spare Parts Inventory Linkage**.
- **Work Order Tracking** (open/closed/in-progress).
- **Cost Reporting and KPI Dashboard**.
- **Vendor / Warranty Management**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse **CMMS** with **ERP**. ERP is for *entire business* (HR, Finance, Sales); CMMS is *exclusively* for maintenance. They overlap but are not synonymous.

---

## 2.8 Real-World Utility in Transport Engineering

Maintenance scheduling and record keeping drive:

- **Insurance premium reduction** (insurers offer 10–20% discount for documented PM records).
- **Resale value uplift** (₹40,000 to ₹1,50,000 higher resale for a car with full service history vs. same-age car without).
- **Legal compliance** under Motor Vehicles Act, Factory Act, and OSH Code 2020.
- **Carbon-emission reporting** for ESG compliance.
- **Warranty claim processing** by OEMs.

---

## 2.9 Downtime Analysis — The Backbone of Scheduling

Downtime is classified into:

| Type | Definition | Typical % |
|---|---|---|
| **Active Repair Downtime** | Vehicle is being worked upon | 60–70% |
| **Waiting for Parts Downtime** | Idle awaiting spares | 15–25% |
| **Administrative Downtime** | Waiting for work-order approval | 5–10% |
| **Queuing Downtime** | Waiting for a free service bay | 5–10% |

Record keeping isolates which downtime dominates → scheduling fixes the root cause.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Optimum Preventive Maintenance Interval

**Problem Setup (KTU-typical):**

The cost per kilometre of operating a vehicle component consists of two parts:
1. Preventive maintenance cost spread over the interval.
2. Expected failure cost during the interval.

**Step 1 — Define the cost per km.**

Let:
- $C_p$ = cost of one PM operation (₹).
- $C_f$ = cost of one failure event (₹).
- $T$ = PM interval (km).
- $f(T)$ = expected number of failures in an interval of length $T$.

If failures occur randomly at rate $\lambda(T)$ (failures/km), then in an interval of length $T$ the expected number of failures is $\lambda(T) \cdot T$. The expected failure cost per interval is $C_f \cdot \lambda(T) \cdot T$.

**Step 2 — Total expected cost per interval.**

$$
C_{total}(T) = C_p + C_f \cdot \lambda(T) \cdot T
$$

**Step 3 — Average cost per km.**

$$
C_{\text{avg}}(T) = \frac{C_{total}(T)}{T} = \frac{C_p}{T} + C_f \cdot \lambda(T)
$$

> The first term **decreases** as $T$ grows (PM cost is spread over more km). The second term **increases** as $T$ grows (more failures accumulate).

**Step 4 — Differentiate and equate to zero.**

Assuming a linear failure-rate growth $\lambda(T) = a + bT$ (engineering approximation):

$$
\frac{dC_{\text{avg}}}{dT} = -\frac{C_p}{T^2} + C_f \cdot b = 0
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $T^*$.**

$$
\frac{C_p}{(T^*)^2} = C_f \cdot b
$$

$$
(T^*)^2 = \frac{C_p}{C_f \cdot b}
$$

$$
T^* = \sqrt{\frac{C_p}{C_f \cdot b}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The optimum interval $T^*$ grows with the **square root of PM cost** and shrinks with the **square root of failure cost growth rate**. Double the PM cost → increase interval by only ~41%, not 100%. This is the *diminishing returns* of frequent servicing.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Question:** A truck's brake system has a PM cost of ₹4,000 per service and a failure cost of ₹40,000 per breakdown. The failure rate grows linearly with interval length according to $\lambda(T) = 0.00005 \cdot T$ failures/km (where $T$ is in thousands of km). Find the **optimum PM interval**.

**Solution:**

Given:
- $C_p = 4000$ ₹.
- $C_f = 40000$ ₹.
- $b = 0.00005$ (per thousand km).

Applying the formula:

$$
T^* = \sqrt{\frac{C_p}{C_f \cdot b}} = \sqrt{\frac{4000}{40000 \times 0.00005}}
$$

**Step-by-step arithmetic:**

$$
C_f \cdot b = 40000 \times 0.00005 = 2
$$

$$
\frac{C_p}{C_f \cdot b} = \frac{4000}{2} = 2000
$$

$$
T^* = \sqrt{2000} \approx 44.72 \text{ thousand km}
$$

$$
\boxed{T^* \approx 44,720 \text{ km}}
$$

**Interpretation for the answer:** Service the brake system roughly every **45,000 km** to minimize total maintenance cost per km. Service more often (say every 10,000 km) → PM cost ₹400/km; service less often (say every 80,000 km) → failure cost ₹160/km. **45,000 km balances the two.**

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Maintenance Strategies

The following decision matrix is a KTU favourite and is best reproduced by heart:

| Criterion | Breakdown | Preventive (Time) | Preventive (Usage) | Predictive | RCM |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Initial Cost** | Lowest | Low | Medium | High | Highest |
| **Operating Cost** | Highest | Medium | Low | Lowest | Lowest |
| **Downtime** | Unplanned (worst) | Planned | Planned | Planned & minimal | Planned & minimal |
| **Safety** | Poor | Good | Good | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Data Required** | None | Calendar only | Odometer | Sensors + analytics | Full history + analytics |
| **Skill Required** | Reactive only | Medium | Medium | High | Very High |
| **Best Suited For** | Non-critical, low-cost parts | Government fleets | Commercial fleets | Premium / critical assets | Airlines, nuclear, large PSUs |
| **KTU Example** | Windshield wiper | Police van | City bus | Metro train brake | Aircraft engine |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic:** *"Broken Things Predict Reliability Correctly"* — **B**reakdown → **T**ime → **P**redictive → **R**eliability-Centered (in increasing order of intelligence).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Derivation: Availability Formula

**Derivation from first principles:**

Consider one vehicle observed for time $T_{calendar}$.

- Up-time (vehicle available for work) = $T_{up}$.
- Down-time (vehicle under repair) = $T_{down}$.
- $T_{calendar} = T_{up} + T_{down}$.

Number of failures observed during up-time = $N$.

Therefore:

$$
MTBF = \frac{T_{up}}{N}, \qquad MTTR = \frac{T_{down}}{N}
$$

Availability is defined as the **fraction of calendar time the vehicle is operational**:

$$
A = \frac{T_{up}}{T_{up} + T_{down}}
$$

Substituting $T_{up} = N \cdot MTBF$ and $T_{down} = N \cdot MTTR$:

$$
A = \frac{N \cdot MTBF}{N \cdot MTBF + N \cdot MTTR} = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}
$$

**Inherent Availability:**

$$
\boxed{A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}}
$$

**Achieved Availability** additionally includes administrative and logistic delays:

$$
A_{achieved} = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR + M_{delay}}
$$

where $M_{delay}$ is mean administrative/logistic delay.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical: Reliability Calculation

**Question:** A vehicle's headlamp bulb has an MTBF of **2,000 hours**. What is the probability that it will survive 500 hours of continuous operation?

**Solution:**

Failure rate:

$$
\lambda = \frac{1}{MTBF} = \frac{1}{2000} = 0.0005 \text{ per hour}
$$

Reliability:

$$
R(t) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-0.0005 \times 500} = e^{-0.25}
$$

$$
R(500) = 0.7788 \approx 77.9\%
$$

**Answer:** There is approximately a **77.9%** chance the bulb will survive 500 hours.

---

## 3.6 Trade-off Curve Sketch (KTU Board Drawing)

Always draw this curve in PM-vs-BM questions:

```
Cost per km
    │      ╱ Failure cost (C_f · λ · T)
    │     ╱
    │    ╱
    │   ╱        ╲ PM cost (C_p / T)
    │  ╱          ╲____
    │ ╱                ╲___
    │╱________________________╲________
    │              ↑           ↑
    │           Total cost    T*
    │           (U-curve)    (optimum)
    └──────────────────────────────── km interval (T)
```

The **U-shaped total cost curve** has its minimum at $T^*$. KTU examiners often ask students to **label the axes, identify $T^*$, and explain the trade-off** in 5–6 marks.

---

## 3.7 Reliability Function for Series System

A vehicle is a **series system** of components — if any one critical component fails, the vehicle is unavailable. If the vehicle has $n$ independent components with reliabilities $R_1, R_2, \ldots, R_n$, the vehicle reliability is:

$$
R_{vehicle} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i = R_1 \cdot R_2 \cdot R_3 \cdots R_n
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Implication:** A vehicle with 50 components each having 99% reliability has an overall reliability of only $0.99^{50} \approx 0.605$ i.e. **60.5%**. This is why *every* component matters in record keeping and scheduling.

---

## 3.8 Summary Table: KTU Calculation Templates

| Question Type | Key Formula | Plug-and-Play Steps |
|---|---|---|
| Compute Availability | $A = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)$ | Sum total up-time → divide by failures → get MTBF. Same for MTTR. |
| Optimum PM Interval | $T^* = \sqrt{C_p / (C_f \cdot b)}$ | Identify $C_p$, $C_f$, $b$ from the question stem; substitute. |
| Reliability | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Compute $\lambda = 1/MTBF$; substitute $t$. |
| Failure Rate | $\lambda = N / T_{op}$ | Failures ÷ operating time. |
| Cost per km | $(C_p/T) + (C_f \cdot \lambda)$ | Plug interval $T$ and known rates. |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Distinguish between Preventive Maintenance and Breakdown Maintenance. Give one advantage and one disadvantage of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Preventive Maintenance (PM) | Breakdown Maintenance (BM) |
|---|---|---|
| **Trigger** | Scheduled in advance | Failure has already occurred |
| **Cost** | Planned, lower per-event cost | Unplanned, much higher per-event cost (often 3–10× PM) |
| **Downtime** | Planned (during off-shift/night) | Unplanned, disrupts operations |
| **Safety** | Proactively managed | Risky — failure may occur on highway |
| **Advantage** | Higher availability, longer vehicle life | No upfront maintenance cost, simple to administer |
| **Disadvantage** | May service components that are still healthy | Causes cascading failures and customer dissatisfaction |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Modern transport companies aim for an **80:20 PM-to-BM ratio**; well-managed fleets approach **90:10**.

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**List any six essential fields that must be recorded in a Vehicle History Card.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — ½ mark per field, 6 fields):**

1. Vehicle registration number and chassis number.
2. Date of service and odometer reading at the time of service.
3. Description of job performed (e.g., engine oil change, brake pad replacement).
4. Spare parts consumed with part numbers, quantities and costs.
5. Labour hours consumed and mechanic ID.
6. Defects observed during inspection and corrective action taken.
7. Next-due date and next-due odometer reading.
8. Supervisor's signature and road-test sign-off.

> Any **six** of the above fetch full marks.

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Rule:** Answer **ONE full question** out of the two choices. Each choice has sub-parts **(a) for 7 marks** and **(b) for 7 marks**.

---

### **Question A** [KTU University Exam — May 2023 Model Paper]

**(a) Explain the concept of Preventive Maintenance Scheduling. Discuss in detail the various strategies used for PM scheduling with suitable examples.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Preventive Maintenance Scheduling is the pre-planned execution of inspection, servicing and replacement tasks on a vehicle at fixed intervals to prevent in-service failures and ensure operational readiness.

**Strategies (4 marks):**

1. **Time-Based Scheduling:** Service at fixed calendar intervals. Example: Police patrol vehicles serviced every 30 days irrespective of km run.
2. **Usage-Based Scheduling:** Service after fixed km or engine-hours. Example: City bus serviced every 10,000 km.
3. **Condition-Based (Predictive) Scheduling:** Service when a measured parameter crosses threshold. Example: Brake pad replaced when thickness sensor reads below 3 mm.
4. **Reliability-Centered Maintenance (RCM):** Each component classified by criticality. Example: Aircraft — engines are PM, cabin lights are run-to-failure.

**Selection Criteria (1 mark):**
Choice depends on **criticality, cost of failure, cost of monitoring, and data availability**.

**(b) The MTBF of a truck's tyre is 30,000 km and MTTR (replacement time) is 6 hours. If the truck runs an average of 12 hours per day, calculate:** **(7 marks)**
**(i)** Availability of the truck.
**(ii)** The reliability of the tyre to survive 10,000 km without failure.
**(iii)** Comment on the result.

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Availability (3 marks):**

$$
A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}
$$

Here $MTBF = 30{,}000$ km. To use the same units as MTTR (hours), convert via average speed.

Distance per day = $12 \text{ h/day} \times v$ km/h. Without explicit speed, take the ratio directly:

Assuming operating speed implicit in MTBF-to-MTTR comparison via **time dimension**: MTBF in hours = $30{,}000 / 60 = 500$ h (at 60 km/h average).

$$
A = \frac{500}{500 + 6} = \frac{500}{506} = 0.9881
$$

$$
\boxed{A \approx 98.81\%}
$$

**(ii) Reliability at 10,000 km (2 marks):**

$$
\lambda = \frac{1}{MTBF_{km}} = \frac{1}{30{,}000} = 3.33 \times 10^{-5} \text{ per km}
$$

$$
R(10{,}000) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-(3.33 \times 10^{-5}) \times 10{,}000} = e^{-0.333}
$$

$$
R(10{,}000) = 0.7165 \approx 71.65\%
$$

$$
\boxed{R(10{,}000 \text{ km}) \approx 71.65\%}
$$

**(iii) Comment (2 marks):**

The truck has **very high availability (~99%)** because the failure repair is fast (only 6 hours) relative to the long time between failures. However, by 10,000 km (one-third of MTBF), nearly **28% of tyres would have already failed**. Hence, scheduling a preventive replacement at around 20,000 km would significantly reduce road-side breakdowns. This shows how records + reliability math combine to design the optimum PM interval.

---

### **Question B** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]

**(a) What is record keeping in vehicle maintenance? Explain the various documents used in a transport company's maintenance record-keeping system.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Record keeping is the systematic documentation of all maintenance events, inspections, costs and operational data of vehicles over their service life, enabling informed decisions on scheduling, budgeting and replacement.

**Documents (5 marks — 1 mark each for 5 documents):**

1. **Driver's Daily Report (DDR):** Submitted by driver at end of shift — covers km run, fuel filled, defects noticed.
2. **Job Card / Work Order:** Issued by foreman to mechanic for each vehicle service — lists tasks, parts, labour.
3. **Vehicle History Card (VHC):** Permanent record of *one specific vehicle* across its life — all services, parts, costs.
4. **Maintenance Log Book:** Fleet-wide summary register — index of all jobs done in the workshop on a given day/week.
5. **Spare Parts Issue Register:** Records all parts drawn from stores against each job card.
6. **Cost Ledger:** Accounting record of ₹ spent on each vehicle per month/year.
7. **CMMS Reports:** Digital dashboards for KPI monitoring.

**(b) A city's bus fleet of 200 buses operates 16 hours/day. Last year the fleet recorded 800 tyre failures and total tyre-downtime of 2,400 hours. Compute the MTBF, MTTR, availability and the PM-to-BM ratio if planned PM jobs were 3,200.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- $N = 200$ buses × 16 h × 365 days = **1,168,000 bus-hours per year**.
- $N_f = 800$ failures.
- Total downtime $T_{down} = 2400$ hours.
- PM jobs = 3200.

**Step 1 — MTBF (2 marks):**

$$
MTBF = \frac{T_{total operating}}{N_f} = \frac{1{,}168{,}000}{800} = 1460 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 2 — MTTR (2 marks):**

$$
MTTR = \frac{T_{down}}{N_f} = \frac{2400}{800} = 3 \text{ hours per failure}
$$

**Step 3 — Availability (1 mark):**

$$
A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} = \frac{1460}{1460 + 3} = \frac{1460}{1463} = 0.998
$$

$$
\boxed{A \approx 99.8\%}
$$

**Step 4 — PM-to-BM Ratio (2 marks):**

$$
\text{PM:BM} = \frac{3200}{800} = 4:1 \quad \text{or} \quad 80:20
$$

**Conclusion (closing line):** The fleet shows **excellent availability (~99.8%)** and a **healthy 80:20 PM-to-BM ratio**, indicating that the record-keeping and scheduling system is operating at industry-best standards.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Unit mismatch in MTBF:** Students forget to convert km to hours (or vice versa) when mixing with MTTR. Always **standardize units** before substituting.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor ½ in cost-per-km formula:** The derivation assumes failures are *uniformly distributed* across the interval, so failure cost is multiplied by λ × T, then **divided by T** — yielding $C_f \cdot \lambda(T)$, *not* $C_f \cdot \lambda(T) \cdot T$ in the final average cost expression. Many students carry an extra $T$.
> 3. **PM-to-BM ratio inversion:** Always express as **PM jobs : BM jobs**, *not* the inverse. The examiner expects the larger preventive number first.
> 4. **Confusing availability with utilization:** **Availability** = (MTBF)/(MTBF+MTTR) — *technical* readiness. **Utilization** = (actual hours used)/(available hours) — *operational* usage. They are *different*.
> 5. **Skipping the "Comment/Conclusion" line:** KTU examiners award 1–2 marks for the engineering interpretation, not just the math. Always end with a 1-line comment.
> 6. **Mermaid confusion in answers:** If asked to draw a flowchart, **label every block** and **use arrows to show flow direction**. A diagram without arrows loses 50% of its marks.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Maintenance Scheduling = Plan; Record Keeping = Memory.** Both are inseparable.
- **Five types of maintenance:** Breakdown, Preventive, Predictive, Corrective, Opportunity.
- **Four scheduling strategies:** Time, Usage, Condition, RCM — in order of complexity.
- **Optimum PM Interval formula:** $T^* = \sqrt{C_p / (C_f \cdot b)}$ (under linear failure-rate assumption).
- **Availability formula:** $A = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)$.
- **Reliability formula:** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ where $\lambda = 1/MTBF$.
- **Target PM:BM ratio:** **80:20** (good fleet); **90:10** (best-in-class).
- **Target availability:** **≥ 90%** for commercial fleets; **≥ 95%** for public transport.
- **Six essential record documents:** DDR, Job Card, Vehicle History Card, Maintenance Log Book, Spare Parts Register, Cost Ledger.
- **CMMS** = digital system integrating all the above with auto PM-trigger generation.
- **Series-system reliability:** $R_{vehicle} = \prod R_i$ — small per-component unreliability multiplies up.
- **Cost curve is U-shaped:** PM cost ↓ with interval, Failure cost ↑ with interval; optimum at the trough.
- **RCM rule:** Safety-critical components → strict PM; non-critical → run-to-failure.
- **KTU favourite phrases to use in answers:** *"predictive maintenance"*, *"reliability-centered maintenance"*, *"optimum PM interval"*, *"PM-to-BM ratio"*, *"vehicle history card"*, *"CMMS"*.
- **Always end numerical answers with a 1-line engineering interpretation** — this fetches the "comment" marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Use of Telematics in Maintenance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT866 - VEHICLE TRANSPORT MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Use of Telematics in Maintenance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Telematics in Maintenance** is the integrated application of telecommunications, informatics, sensor technology, and data analytics to remotely monitor, diagnose, and manage the health and operational status of vehicles and transport fleets. It encompasses the real-time collection, transmission, and processing of vehicle data (location, engine parameters, fault codes, driver behaviour, and component condition) through wireless networks to a centralized fleet management platform.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Telematics in maintenance is a **cross-functional technology** that bridges **Mechanical Engineering** (vehicle subsystems), **Electronics & Communication** (sensors, GPS, GSM/4G/5G), and **Data Science** (analytics, ML-based prediction). In KTU 2024 Scheme, it directly maps to **CO2 (Apply IT tools in transport management)**.

The telematics architecture in a modern commercial vehicle integrates:

| Layer | Function | Key Hardware/Software |
|-------|----------|----------------------|
| **Perception Layer** | Data acquisition | OBD-II port, ECU, TPMS, accelerometers, gyroscopes |
| **Communication Layer** | Data transmission | GSM/4G/5G modules, satellite uplink, Bluetooth |
| **Processing Layer** | Analytics & decision | Cloud servers, AI/ML engines, dashboards |
| **Application Layer** | User interface | Fleet manager apps, driver apps, customer portals |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Fitness Tracker for Your Vehicle"

Imagine strapping a **smart fitness band** (like a Fitbit or Apple Watch) onto a human body. The band continuously measures heart rate, steps, sleep quality, and oxygen levels. It then *pushes* this data to your smartphone, which warns you: *"Your resting heart rate has risen 15% over the last week — you should consider seeing a doctor before a major cardiac event occurs."*

**Telematics in vehicle maintenance works exactly the same way.** The vehicle becomes the "body," the sensors and ECU become the "fitness band," the cellular network becomes the "sync to phone" pipeline, and the fleet management dashboard becomes the "smartphone app." Instead of warning about heart attacks, the system warns about **engine seizures, brake failures, transmission overheating, or coolant pump degradation — days before they actually happen.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:** Telematics transforms vehicle maintenance from a **reactive** ("fix it when it breaks") or **preventive** ("change oil every 5000 km") discipline into a **predictive** ("change oil NOW because sensor data shows degradation") and even **prescriptive** ("replace component X within 200 km to avoid cascade failure") discipline.

---

## 1.3 Why Telematics is a Game-Changer in Vehicle Maintenance

Traditional maintenance paradigms:

1. **Reactive (Breakdown) Maintenance** → Vehicle operates until failure. Cost: **High** (towing, downtime, secondary damage).
2. **Preventive (Scheduled) Maintenance** → Parts replaced at fixed intervals (km/month). Cost: **Medium** (over-maintenance, unused life).
3. **Predictive (Condition-Based) Maintenance** → Maintenance triggered by real sensor data. Cost: **Low** (only when needed).

Telematics enables the **4th paradigm**:
4. **Prescriptive Maintenance** → AI suggests *the exact optimal action* (what to repair, when, by whom, with what spare part) based on digital twin simulations. Cost: **Lowest**.

---

## 1.4 Core Telematics Parameters Monitored for Maintenance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Metrics for KTU:**

- **Engine Parameters:** Coolant temperature, oil pressure, RPM, fuel rail pressure, intake air temperature, exhaust gas temperature.
- **Transmission:** Gear position, clutch slip, ATF (Automatic Transmission Fluid) temperature, torque converter lock-up.
- **Braking System:** Pad wear sensor data, brake fluid pressure, ABS activation frequency, brake disc temperature.
- **Tires:** Pressure (TPMS), temperature, tread depth estimation, wheel speed variance.
- **Battery (EV/HEV):** State of Charge (SoC), State of Health (SoH), cell voltage imbalance, internal resistance.
- **Driver Behaviour:** Harsh braking, harsh acceleration, over-revving, idle time, overspeed incidents.
- **Location & Usage:** GPS route, mileage accumulation, load weight (via air suspension sensors), engine hours.

---

## 1.5 The OBD-II Interface — The Heart of Vehicle Telematics

The **On-Board Diagnostics (OBD-II) port** is the standardized interface (mandated in India under **AIS-004** for all petrol vehicles post-2010 and diesel vehicles post-2013, in line with **Bharat Stage VI** norms) that allows telematics devices to read:

- **Diagnostic Trouble Codes (DTCs):** Standardized P-codes (Powertrain), B-codes (Body), C-codes (Chassis), U-codes (Network).
- **Live Parameter IDs (PIDs):** Real-time sensor data streams.
- **Freeze Frame Data:** Snapshot of parameters at the moment of fault occurrence.
- **Emissions Readiness Monitors:** Catalyst, O2 sensor, EVAP system, EGR status.

**Example DTC:** `P0301` = Cylinder 1 Misfire Detected. A telematics unit can immediately push this code to the fleet manager's dashboard, triggering an automatic maintenance work order.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Telematics-Driven Maintenance

### **Pillar 1: Remote Diagnostics & Real-Time Fault Reporting**

When the ECU (Electronic Control Unit) detects an anomaly, it stores a **DTC** and illuminates the **Malfunction Indicator Lamp (MIL)** on the dashboard. With telematics, this DTC is:

1. Read by the telematics control unit (TCU) via OBD-II.
2. Encoded and transmitted via **GSM/4G** to the cloud server.
3. Parsed by the analytics engine.
4. Displayed on the fleet manager's dashboard with severity classification (Critical / Major / Minor).
5. Routed to the nearest service centre with the required spare part pre-staged.

**Severity Classification Logic (Standard KTU exam answer):**
- **Critical (P0):** Vehicle must stop immediately (e.g., engine overheating, brake failure, oil pressure drop).
- **Major (P1):** Schedule service within 24 hours (e.g., DPF clogged, AdBlue low, sensor malfunction).
- **Minor (P2):** Log and address during next scheduled service (e.g., minor emissions readiness flag).

---

### **Pillar 2: Condition-Based Monitoring (CBM)**

Instead of replacing parts at fixed intervals, sensors continuously monitor **actual wear condition**. Mathematical wear models use sensor data:

**General Wear Rate Equation:**
$$\text{Wear Rate } (W) = f(\text{Temperature}, \text{Pressure}, \text{Vibration}, \text{Usage Hours})$$

For brake pads, a simplified thermal-mechanical model:
$$W_{\text{brake}} = K \cdot \int_{0}^{t} \sqrt{T(\tau) \cdot v(\tau)} \, d\tau$$

Where:
- $K$ = pad material wear coefficient (mm·N^(-1/2)·m^(-1/2))
- $T(\tau)$ = instantaneous disc temperature at time $\tau$ (K)
- $v(\tau)$ = instantaneous vehicle speed at time $\tau$ (m/s)
- $t$ = total elapsed time

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** The wear is proportional to the **square root of the product of temperature and speed**, which is why brake pad wear is dramatically higher in **city stop-and-go traffic** (high temperatures from frequent braking) compared to highway driving.

---

### **Pillar 3: Predictive Maintenance using AI/ML**

Modern telematics platforms apply **machine learning algorithms** on historical failure data to predict Remaining Useful Life (RUL):

$$\text{RUL}(t) = T_{\text{failure}} - t$$

Where RUL is predicted by regression models such as:
- **LSTM (Long Short-Term Memory)** neural networks on time-series sensor data.
- **Random Forest** classifiers on feature-engineered DTC history.
- **Survival Analysis Models** (e.g., Weibull distribution) on component age.

**Hazard function (Weibull):**
$$h(t) = \frac{\beta}{\eta} \left( \frac{t}{\eta} \right)^{\beta - 1}$$

Where:
- $\beta$ = shape parameter (β > 1 indicates increasing failure rate — wear-out phase)
- $\eta$ = scale parameter (characteristic life)
- $t$ = age of component

---

### **Pillar 4: Fleet-Wide Maintenance Optimization**

Telematics aggregates data across the **entire fleet** to optimize:
- **Spare parts inventory** (just-in-time delivery to workshops).
- **Workshop load balancing** (route vehicle to least-loaded certified centre).
- **Driver coaching** (reduce harsh events that accelerate wear).
- **Warranty claim tracking** (auto-flag components within warranty).

---

### **Pillar 5: Integration with Enterprise Systems**

Telematics data feeds into:
- **ERP (Enterprise Resource Planning):** SAP, Oracle for procurement and invoicing.
- **TMS (Transport Management System):** For route replanning when a vehicle is in maintenance.
- **CRM (Customer Relationship Management):** To provide customers real-time ETA and delivery status.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Maintenance Telematics Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MANDATORY FORMULAS — Memorize for KTU University Exam**

| # | Metric | Formula | Units / Normal Range |
|---|--------|---------|----------------------|
| 1 | **Availability (A)** | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | Decimal (0 to 1); Target ≥ **0.95** |
| 2 | **Mean Time Between Failures** | $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{\text{Total Operating Time}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$ | Hours |
| 3 | **Mean Time To Repair** | $\text{MTTR} = \dfrac{\text{Total Downtime}}{\text{Number of Repairs}}$ | Hours |
| 4 | **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)** | $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$ | Decimal; World-class ≥ **0.85** |
| 5 | **Performance (P)** | $P = \dfrac{\text{Actual Throughput}}{\text{Theoretical Max Throughput}}$ | Decimal |
| 6 | **Quality (Q)** | $Q = \dfrac{\text{Good Units}}{\text{Total Units Produced}}$ | Decimal |
| 7 | **Cost per Kilometre (CPK)** | $\text{CPK} = \dfrac{\text{Total Maintenance Cost}}{\text{Total km Travelled}}$ | ₹/km |
| 8 | **Fleet Utilization Rate** | $U = \dfrac{\text{Active Operating Hours}}{\text{Total Available Hours}} \times 100$ | Percentage (%) |
| 9 | **Fuel Efficiency Index** | $\text{FEI} = \dfrac{\text{km / litre}}{\text{Standard Benchmark}}$ | Ratio |
| 10 | **Predictive Accuracy** | $\text{PA} = \dfrac{\text{True Positives}}{\text{True Positives} + \text{False Positives}} \times 100$ | Percentage (%) |
| 11 | **Telematics ROI** | $\text{ROI} = \dfrac{\text{Cost Savings} - \text{Telematics Investment}}{\text{Telematics Investment}} \times 100$ | Percentage (%) |
| 12 | **Schedule Compliance** | $\text{SC} = \dfrac{\text{Services Completed On Time}}{\text{Total Services Scheduled}} \times 100$ | Percentage (%) |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** When expressing absolute value or conditional notation in any table, use `\vert` instead of the pipe symbol `\vert` to avoid breaking markdown rendering.

In a typical Indian **State Transport Undertaking (STU)** like KSRTC or BEST, telematics-enabled maintenance has demonstrated:

- **15–25%** reduction in breakdown incidents.
- **10–20%** reduction in fuel consumption through driver behaviour monitoring.
- **30%** reduction in unscheduled workshop visits.
- **2× faster** mean time to repair due to pre-staged parts.

For **commercial logistics fleets** (Delhivery, BlueDart, Amazon India), telematics integration with TMS has enabled **predictive pre-positioning** of spare parts and **dynamic workshop routing**, reducing average vehicle downtime from 4 days to under 12 hours.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Availability from First Principles

The fundamental relationship between **reliability** and **maintainability** can be derived from a renewal-reward framework.

**Starting Premise:** A system alternates between two states — *operating* (Uptime) and *failed/under repair* (Downtime).

Let:
- $T_{\text{up}} =$ Total uptime of the vehicle over a long observation period
- $T_{\text{down}} =$ Total downtime (in maintenance) over the same period
- $N =$ Number of failure-repair cycles observed

**Step 1: Define Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)**

$$\text{MTBF} = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{T_{\text{up}}}{N}$$

**Step 2: Define Mean Time To Repair (MTTR)**

$$\text{MTTR} = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{T_{\text{down}}}{N}$$

**Step 3: Define Total Observation Time**

$$\text{Total Time} = T_{\text{up}} + T_{\text{down}}$$

Dividing throughout by $N$:

$$\frac{\text{Total Time}}{N} = \frac{T_{\text{up}}}{N} + \frac{T_{\text{down}}}{N}$$

**Step 4: Recognize the LHS as the Mean Cycle Time (MCT)**

$$\text{MCT} = \text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}$$

**Step 5: Availability is the fraction of time the system is operational**

$$\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{T_{\text{up}}}{T_{\text{up}} + T_{\text{down}}} \\
  &= \frac{T_{\text{up}}/N}{(T_{\text{up}} + T_{\text{down}})/N} \\
  &= \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Result:**

$$A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{1}{1 + \frac{\text{MTTR}}{\text{MTBF}}}$$

**Interpretation:** Availability approaches 1 (100%) when MTTR ≪ MTBF. Telematics reduces MTTR by providing **fault pre-information** to workshops before the vehicle arrives.

---

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example: OEE Calculation for a Delivery Fleet

> **Problem Statement (KTU-style):** A logistics company operates 50 vehicles. Last month:
> - Scheduled operating time per vehicle: **240 hours**
> - Actual operating time per vehicle (after downtime): **204 hours**
> - Theoretical max deliveries per hour: **8**
> - Actual deliveries per hour (when operating): **6.8**
> - Total deliveries attempted: **1386 per vehicle**
> - Total deliveries completed successfully: **1330 per vehicle**

**Calculate: (a) Availability, (b) Performance, (c) Quality, (d) OEE**

---

**Step 1: Calculate Availability (A)**

$$\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{\text{Actual Operating Time}}{\text{Scheduled Operating Time}} \\
  &= \frac{204}{240} \\
  &= 0.850
\end{aligned}$$

---

**Step 2: Calculate Performance (P)**

$$\begin{aligned}
P &= \frac{\text{Actual Throughput}}{\text{Theoretical Max Throughput}} \\
  &= \frac{6.8}{8.0} \\
  &= 0.850
\end{aligned}$$

---

**Step 3: Calculate Quality (Q)**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= \frac{\text{Good Units}}{\text{Total Units Produced}} \\
  &= \frac{1330}{1386} \\
  &= 0.9595 \approx 0.960
\end{aligned}$$

---

**Step 4: Calculate OEE**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{OEE} &= A \times P \times Q \\
           &= 0.850 \times 0.850 \times 0.960 \\
           &= 0.6936 \\
           &= 69.36\%
\end{aligned}$$

---

**Step 5: Interpret against World-Class Benchmark**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{OEE}_{\text{actual}} &= 69.36\% \\
\text{OEE}_{\text{world-class}} &= 85.0\% \\
\text{Gap} &= 85.0 - 69.36 = 15.64\% \text{ (below benchmark)}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** OEE of 69.36% is **typical for Indian commercial fleets** without telematics. A well-implemented telematics system can push this above 80% by:
> - Reducing Availability losses (predictive maintenance → less breakdown).
> - Reducing Performance losses (driver coaching via harsh-event alerts).
> - Reducing Quality losses (early fault detection → fewer damaged deliveries).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: Choosing Maintenance Strategy

The selection of the optimal maintenance strategy for a vehicle subsystem can be guided by the following **decision matrix**:

| Subsystem | Failure Cost | Failure Predictability | Data Availability | Optimal Telematics Strategy |
|-----------|--------------|-----------------------|-------------------|----------------------------|
| **Engine (Diesel)** | Very High | High (via OBD) | Excellent | **Predictive** (RUL modelling on oil quality + EGT) |
| **Brake Pads** | Medium | Very High (via wear sensors + temp) | Good | **Condition-Based** (replace at sensor threshold) |
| **Tires** | Medium | High (TPMS) | Excellent | **Condition-Based** + rotation scheduling |
| **Battery (12V)** | Low | Very High (voltage/temperature trend) | Excellent | **Predictive** (replacement before failure) |
| **Transmission** | Very High | Medium | Moderate | **Preventive + Diagnostic** (oil analysis + DTCs) |
| **AC Compressor** | Low | Low | Poor | **Run-to-Failure** (corrective replacement) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Strategic Insight:** Telematics data quality varies by subsystem. The most **data-rich** components (engine, brakes, tires, battery) yield the highest ROI when monitored via telematics. Low-data subsystems (AC, interior trim) are better left to traditional reactive maintenance.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Cost-Benefit Threshold for Telematics Adoption

A transport operator will adopt telematics when the **Net Present Value (NPV)** of the savings exceeds the installation cost.

$$\text{NPV} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{(\text{Savings}_t - \text{Operating Cost}_t)}{(1 + r)^t} - C_0$$

Where:
- $C_0$ = Initial telematics hardware + software investment (₹)
- $\text{Savings}_t$ = Annual maintenance + fuel + downtime savings in year $t$ (₹)
- $\text{Operating Cost}_t$ = Annual subscription + data charges (₹)
- $r$ = Discount rate (typically 10–12% in India)
- $n$ = Lifecycle in years (typically 5)

**Break-even year** is when cumulative NPV crosses zero:

$$\sum_{t=1}^{T_{\text{BE}}} \frac{(\text{Savings}_t - \text{Operating Cost}_t)}{(1 + r)^t} = C_0$$

> **Typical Indian Industry Values:** For a 50-vehicle fleet, $C_0$ ≈ ₹15–25 lakh, annual net savings ≈ ₹8–12 lakh, break-even typically in **18–24 months**.

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of a telematics-based vehicle maintenance system with a neat block diagram. Discuss the function of each layer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A transport company has 40 trucks. Data for one month:
- Total scheduled operating hours per truck: **250 hours**
- Total actual operating hours per truck: **210 hours**
- Theoretical deliveries per hour: **6**
- Actual deliveries per hour (when operating): **5.1**
- Good deliveries per truck: **1050**
- Total deliveries attempted per truck: **1092**

Calculate the **Availability, Performance, Quality, and OEE** of the fleet. Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

---

### **MODEL SOLUTION — Question A**

#### **Part (a): Architecture of Telematics-Based Maintenance System (7 marks)**

The telematics-based maintenance system has **four functional layers** (Reference Block Diagram in Section 4.1):

**Layer 1: Vehicle/Perspective Layer (1.5 marks)**
- Contains the **Engine Control Unit (ECU)**, **OBD-II port**, and various sensors (TPMS, brake wear, oil quality, battery monitor).
- Function: Continuously acquires real-time data from vehicle subsystems. The ECU executes self-diagnostic routines and generates **Diagnostic Trouble Codes (DTCs)** when parameters deviate from calibrated thresholds.
- The **Telematics Control Unit (TCU)** aggregates sensor data and prepares it for transmission.

**Layer 2: Communication Layer (1.5 marks)**
- Comprises **GSM/4G/5G modules**, optional **satellite uplink**, and **Bluetooth** for short-range driver-device pairing.
- Function: Securely transmits encoded data packets to the cloud gateway. Modern TCUs use **MQTT or HTTPs protocols** with AES-256 encryption.
- Supports **bidirectional communication** — fleet manager can send commands back (e.g., remote immobilization in case of theft, or over-the-air ECU parameter updates).

**Layer 3: Cloud Processing Layer (2 marks)**
- Contains **API gateway**, **time-series database**, **analytics/AI-ML engine**, **digital twin module**, and **alert routing engine**.
- Function: Ingests massive volumes of vehicle data (often millions of data points per day per fleet). The AI engine applies **anomaly detection** (e.g., Isolation Forest), **RUL prediction** (LSTM, Random Forest), and **failure mode classification**.
- The **digital twin** creates a virtual replica of each vehicle, simulating "what-if" scenarios for prescriptive maintenance.

**Layer 4: Application Layer (2 marks)**
- Includes **fleet manager dashboard**, **driver mobile app**, **workshop management module**, and **regulatory MIS reports**.
- Function: Converts raw analytics into actionable intelligence. Dashboards show real-time fleet health, predicted failures, and recommended actions. Workshop modules auto-generate work orders with required spare parts. MIS reports comply with **AIS-140** and other regulatory mandates in India.

---

#### **Part (b): OEE Calculation (7 marks)**

**Given Data (1 mark):**
- Scheduled time = 250 hr; Actual time = 210 hr
- Theoretical throughput = 6/hr; Actual throughput = 5.1/hr
- Good deliveries = 1050; Total deliveries = 1092

**Step 1: Availability (A) (1.5 marks)**
$$\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{\text{Actual Operating Time}}{\text{Scheduled Operating Time}} \\
  &= \frac{210}{250} \\
  &= 0.840 \quad \text{(84.0\%)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Performance (P) (1.5 marks)**
$$\begin{aligned}
P &= \frac{\text{Actual Throughput}}{\text{Theoretical Max Throughput}} \\
  &= \frac{5.1}{6.0} \\
  &= 0.850 \quad \text{(85.0\%)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Quality (Q) (1.5 marks)**
$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= \frac{\text{Good Deliveries}}{\text{Total Deliveries Attempted}} \\
  &= \frac{1050}{1092} \\
  &= 0.9615 \quad \text{(96.15\%)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: OEE (1 mark)**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{OEE} &= A \times P \times Q \\
           &= 0.840 \times 0.850 \times 0.9615 \\
           &= 0.6867 \\
           &= 68.67\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Comment (0.5 marks)**
OEE of 68.67% is **below the world-class benchmark of 85%**. The major loss is in **Availability** (16% downtime) and **Performance** (15% below theoretical). A telematics-driven predictive maintenance program is recommended to push OEE above 80%.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **predictive maintenance using telematics**. Discuss the role of AI/ML and digital twin in this context. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The maintenance cost of a fleet of 30 buses is analysed. With telematics:
- Annual maintenance cost = ₹18,00,000
- Annual fuel saving = ₹6,50,000
- Annual breakdown cost reduction = ₹4,20,000
- Annual telematics subscription + data charges = ₹3,00,000
- Initial investment = ₹12,00,000

Calculate the **annual net savings**, **first-year ROI**, and comment on the **break-even period**. Take discount rate as 0% for simplicity. **(7 marks)**

---

### **MODEL SOLUTION — Question B**

#### **Part (a): Predictive Maintenance using Telematics (7 marks)**

**Concept (2 marks):**
Predictive maintenance is a data-driven maintenance strategy that uses real-time sensor data, historical failure records, and machine learning algorithms to forecast when a vehicle component is likely to fail, enabling maintenance to be performed **just before failure** rather than on a fixed schedule or after breakdown.

**Role of AI/ML (3 marks):**
- **Time-Series Forecasting:** LSTM and Transformer models are trained on historical sensor streams (temperature, pressure, vibration) to predict future degradation trajectories.
- **Anomaly Detection:** Unsupervised models (Isolation Forest, Autoencoders) flag unusual sensor patterns that may indicate incipient failure.
- **Remaining Useful Life (RUL) Estimation:** Survival analysis (Weibull) and regression models output the expected time-to-failure for each component.
- **Failure Mode Classification:** Supervised classifiers (Random Forest, XGBoost) trained on labeled DTC data identify the specific fault type (e.g., fuel injector vs. spark plug misfire).
- **Continuous Learning:** Models retrain on new failure data, improving accuracy over time — achieving **predictive accuracy > 90%** in mature deployments.

**Role of Digital Twin (2 marks):**
- A **digital twin** is a virtual replica of each physical vehicle, integrating its real-time sensor data with a physics-based simulation model.
- It allows engineers to run **"what-if" scenarios** in silico — e.g., "What happens to battery temperature if the AC compressor fails?"
- Enables **prescriptive recommendations**: not just predicting failure, but also the **optimal repair action, spare part, and timing**.
- Facilitates **fleet-wide simulation** — testing the impact of a new maintenance policy on the entire fleet before deployment.

---

#### **Part (b): ROI Calculation (7 marks)**

**Given Data (1 mark):**
- Total annual savings (maintenance + fuel + breakdown) = ₹18,00,000 + ₹6,50,000 + ₹4,20,000 = **₹28,70,000**
- Annual telematics operating cost = ₹3,00,000
- Initial investment = ₹12,00,000

**Step 1: Annual Net Savings (2 marks)**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Net Savings} &= \text{Total Savings} - \text{Operating Cost} \\
                   &= ₹28,70,000 - ₹3,00,000 \\
                   &= ₹25,70,000
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: First-Year ROI (2 marks)**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{ROI}_{\text{Year 1}} &= \frac{\text{Net Savings} - \text{Initial Investment}}{\text{Initial Investment}} \times 100 \\
                           &= \frac{25,70,000 - 12,00,000}{12,00,000} \times 100 \\
                           &= \frac{13,70,000}{12,00,000} \times 100 \\
                           &= 114.17\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Break-Even Period (2 marks)**
$$\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{BE}} &= \frac{\text{Initial Investment}}{\text{Annual Net Savings}} \\
              &= \frac{12,00,000}{25,70,000} \\
              &= 0.467 \text{ years} \\
              &\approx 5.6 \text{ months}
\end{aligned}$$

**Comment (Bonus — optional in answer):**
A break-even of less than 6 months and Year-1 ROI of 114% indicates **highly economically viable** telematics adoption. The fleet recovers its initial investment within the first half-year and starts generating net positive returns thereafter.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing OEE formula:** Students often write OEE = (A + P + Q)/3. **Correct formula is product (A × P × Q), NOT average.** Losing 2 marks here.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert to percentage:** OEE is a ratio but is conventionally reported as a percentage. Always multiply final decimal by 100.
> 3. **In ROI calculation:** Students forget to subtract the **initial investment** from net savings in Year 1 (since Year 1 includes both savings and the upfront cost).
> 4. **In availability formula:** Writing Availability = MTTR/(MTBF + MTTR) — the numerator should be **MTBF**, not MTTR.
> 5. **DTC interpretation:** Know that P-codes = Powertrain, B = Body, C = Chassis, U = Network. Marks awarded for correct prefix classification.
> 6. **AIS-140 reference:** When asked about Indian regulations, mention **AIS-140** (Automotive Industry Standard 140) for intelligent transportation systems, mandated for public service vehicles.
