## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Points — Read before entering exam hall:**

- ☐ **Telematics = Telecom + Informatics.** It is the technology that connects vehicles to the cloud for monitoring and management.
- ☐ **Four Layers:** Perception (sensors) → Communication (GSM/4G) → Processing (cloud + AI) → Application (dashboards/apps).
- ☐ **OBD-II** is the standardized diagnostic interface (mandated in India under **AIS-004**). It exposes **DTCs** and **PIDs**.
- ☐ **DTC Prefix Codes:** P (Powertrain), B (Body), C (Chassis), U (Network).
- ☐ **AIS-140** mandates telematics for all public service vehicles in India.
- ☐ **Four Maintenance Paradigms:** Reactive → Preventive → Predictive → Prescriptive. Telematics enables the latter two.
- ☐ **Availability Formula:** $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ — NOT $\dfrac{\text{MTTR}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$.
- ☐ **OEE Formula:** $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$ — PRODUCT, not average. World-class = 85%.
- ☐ **CPK** (Cost per km) = Total Maintenance Cost / Total km. Used as a fleet KPI.
- ☐ **Predictive Maintenance** uses AI/ML (LSTM, Random Forest) on sensor data to predict RUL.
- ☐ **Digital Twin** = Virtual replica of vehicle for "what-if" simulation.
- ☐ **Brake pad wear** is proportional to $\sqrt{T \cdot v}$ (temperature × speed) — explains why city driving wears brakes faster.
- ☐ **Telematics ROI** for typical Indian fleet: Break-even in **18–24 months**; OEE improvement of **10–15%**.
- ☐ **Key Indian Context:** KSRTC, BEST, DTC, Ashok Leyland i-Alert, Mahindra TRACKO, Volvo Connect are real-world examples.
- ☐ **MTBF** measures **reliability**; **MTTR** measures **maintainability**. Telematics primarily reduces MTTR via pre-staged parts.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fuel Management - Fuel Procurement and Storage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT866 - VEHICLE TRANSPORT MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fuel Management - Fuel Procurement and Storage

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Fuel Management** in a transport organization refers to the systematic planning, procurement, storage, dispensing, accounting, and quality control of vehicular fuels (predominantly High-Speed Diesel — HSD, Motor Spirit — MS, and emerging alternatives like CNG, LNG, EV battery charging) to ensure uninterrupted fleet operations at minimum total cost while complying with statutory and environmental safety norms.

It is broadly divided into two interlinked sub-functions:

1. **Fuel Procurement** – the strategic and operational process of sourcing the right quality of fuel, in the right quantity, from the right vendor, at the right price, and at the right time.
2. **Fuel Storage** – the engineered and procedural activity of holding bulk and day-level fuel stocks safely, hygienically, and accurately accounted for, until it is dispensed into vehicles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per the KTU 2024 Scheme (PEMUT866 Module 2), fuel is treated as a **consumable working capital asset** of a transport undertaking. Unlike other consumables, it is **perishable, hazardous, taxable, and price-volatile** — making its management a critical operational and financial function.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Family Kitchen Pantry" Model

Imagine running a large hostel kitchen that serves 500 meals a day:

- **Procurement** is like deciding where to buy rice, wheat, and oil — whether from the local wholesale mandi, a branded distributor, or a contract supplier — and negotiating the deal.
- **Storage** is the godown where rice is kept in silos and oil in sealed drums, away from rain, rats, and fire, with locks and daily registers.
- **Reordering** happens when stocks dip to a buffer level — you don't wait for rice to be fully over before ordering more.
- **Quality checks** mean ensuring the oil isn't adulterated or rancid.
- **Records** ensure that the steward can't quietly siphon off 10 litres of oil daily.

A transport fleet works exactly like this hostel kitchen, but the "meals" are trips, the "rice" is diesel, and the "steward" is the fuel station operator. **Mismanagement causes trip failures, fires, financial losses, and legal penalties.**

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Reference Constants

- **Standard Density of HSD (Diesel) at 15 °C:** **0.840 kg/L** (used for mass-to-volume conversion).
- **Standard Density of MS (Petrol) at 15 °C:** **0.755 kg/L**.
- **Net Calorific Value (NCV):** HSD ≈ **43 MJ/kg**; MS ≈ **44 MJ/kg**.
- **Flash Point:** HSD > **55 °C**; MS < **−18 °C** (drives storage safety class).
- **BIS Specifications:** IS 1460 (HSD), IS 2796 (MS).
- **Statutory Authority:** **PESO** (Petroleum and Explosives Safety Organisation) under the Explosives Act, 1884.
- **Statutory Authority:** **OISD** (Oil Industry Safety Directorate) standards 117, 118, 130, 144, 150.
- **One Barrel of Crude Oil:** **159 litres** (reference global benchmark).

> [!NOTE]
> KTU expects students to remember that **HSD is taxed under the Union Excise + State VAT** regime historically (now subsumed under GST), and that any large captive fuel storage (> 1,000 litres) requires **PESO + Factory + Pollution Control Board** licenses.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Fuel Procurement Cycle — Six Logical Steps

**Step 1 — Demand Forecasting**
- Forecast monthly fuel requirement based on **planned vehicle-kilometres**, **average fleet kmpl (kilometres per litre)**, and **load profile**.
- Seasonal adjustments (winter mileage drop, summer AC load, hill gradient) are added.

**Step 2 — Vendor Identification & Pre-Qualification**
- Sources: **(a) PSU Oil Marketing Companies (OMCs)** — IOCL, BPCL, HPCL; **(b) Private OMCs** — Reliance, Essar (now Nayara); **(c) Bulk retail outlets**; **(d) Captive dispensing pumps**.
- Pre-qualification checks: dealership license, BIS-marked product, financial stability, delivery fleet size, geographic reach.

**Step 3 — Tendering & Rate Negotiation**
- Floating **Rate Contracts (RC)** for periods of 6–12 months at a **discount over the pump price** (typical discount: ₹2–₹6 per litre for bulk HSD).
- Two-bid system: **Technical bid** (quality, logistics) + **Financial bid** (price).

**Step 4 — Purchase Order (PO) & Delivery Scheduling**
- PO specifies grade, quantity, delivery window, delivery mode (tanker/bowsers), and testing protocol.
- **Lead time** = 24–72 hours for OMC bulk tankers.

**Step 5 — Receipt, Sampling & Quality Acceptance**
- Each tanker is checked for: **seal intactness**, **density (using hydrometer)**, **temperature (using thermometer)**, **visual clarity**, and **sediment & water content** (using API gravity + water finding paste).
- A representative sample is drawn and sealed for **third-party lab testing** (BIS parameter compliance).

**Step 6 — Invoice, GST, and Stock Update**
- GST invoice received, 3-way match done (PO + GRN + Invoice), and stock register updated.

### 2.2 The Fuel Storage System — Functional Architecture

Storage infrastructure is layered:

| Layer | Facility | Typical Capacity | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| L1 | Bulk Storage Tank (Underground) | 10,000 – 50,000 L | Strategic reserve |
| L2 | Bulk Storage Tank (Above Ground) | 5,000 – 25,000 L | Daily operational |
| L3 | Day Service Tank | 1,000 – 2,000 L | Pump suction feed |
| L4 | Vehicle-mounted Tanks | 200 – 600 L | On-board (transit) |
| L5 | Mobile Bowsers (Tankers) | 6,000 – 12,000 L | Outstation refilling |

**Why layered?** To prevent sediment and water accumulation from reaching the vehicle, and to maintain **gravity-flow dispensing** without frequent pump cycling.

### 2.3 Inventory Control Logic

The **two-bin / three-bin system** is the practical method used in transport depots:

- **Bin 1 (Reorder):** Stock falls to ROP → raise fresh PO.
- **Bin 2 (Minimum/Safety):** Stock for **lead-time demand + buffer**.
- **Bin 3 (Maximum):** Upper limit; excess invites storage cost and degradation.

### 2.4 Safety Engineering of Fuel Storage

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU marks are frequently awarded for listing the **engineering controls** and **statutory controls** separately. Memorize the **OISD-117** standard for **above-ground petroleum storage**.

- **Engineering Controls:** Bund wall (110% volume of largest tank), dyke, vent with flame arrestor, earthing + lightning arrester, ATEX-certified electrical fittings, foam-based fire suppression, interlocking tanker entry.
- **Statutory Controls:** PESO license, Pollution Control Board Consent (CTO/CTE), Factory Inspector approval, Fire NOC, Hazardous Waste Management Rules 1989 (sludge disposal).
- **Operational Controls:** Bonded warehouse concept (for tax-pending fuel), calibrated flowmeters, daily dip-stick reconciliation.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning of Variables | Typical Units | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_d = \dfrac{D_{month}}{N_v \times kpl}$ | $C_d$ = daily fuel consumption; $D_{month}$ = monthly distance (km); $N_v$ = days operated; $kpl$ = km per litre | L/day | Demand forecasting |
| 2 | $ROP = C_{max} \times L_t + SS$ | ROP = Reorder Point; $C_{max}$ = max daily consumption; $L_t$ = lead time (days); SS = safety stock | L | Inventory reorder |
| 3 | $EOQ = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ | D = annual demand (L); S = ordering cost (₹/order); H = holding cost (₹/L/yr) | L | Optimal order qty |
| 4 | $V_{corrected} = V_{obs}\left[1 - \alpha (T_{obs} - 15)\right]$ | $V$ = volume; $\alpha$ = coefficient of cubical expansion (HSD ≈ 0.0008); $T$ = temperature | L | Volume correction |
| 5 | $m = V \times \rho$ | $m$ = mass (kg); $V$ = volume (L); $\rho$ = density (kg/L) | kg | Mass–volume convert |
| 6 | $Turnover = \dfrac{C_{annual}}{AI}$ | $C_{annual}$ = annual fuel consumed (₹); $AI$ = average inventory value (₹) | — | Inventory efficiency |
| 7 | $Loss\% = \dfrac{Stock_{book} - Stock_{physical}}{Stock_{book}} \times 100$ | Book vs physical variance | % | Stock reconciliation |
| 8 | $BundVol \geq 1.1 \times V_{largest\,tank}$ | 110% containment rule | L | Dyke sizing |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL EXAM HINT:** Formula 4 (volume temperature correction) appears almost every KTU exam under "numerical on fuel storage losses." The reference temperature for petroleum volume correction in India is **15 °C** (per IS 1093:1986).

### 2.6 Real-World Utility

This framework is not academic — it runs the **fuel economy of every fleet in India**: from Kerala State Road Transport Corporation (KSRTC) depots that consume **20,000+ litres/day**, to the **40,000+ petrol pumps** of PSU OMCs, to the **captive fuel farms** of mining and power companies. Every rupee saved per litre × 1 crore litres = **₹1 crore saved**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Numerical — Demand Forecasting & ROP

**Problem (KTU-style, 7 marks):**
A transport operator runs 25 trucks. Each truck averages **60 km/day** for **26 days/month**, with an average fuel efficiency of **4.5 km/L** on HSD. Lead time from OMC bulk tanker is **3 days**, and safety stock is kept at **20%** of lead-time demand. Calculate:
- (a) Monthly fuel requirement
- (b) Reorder Point (ROP) in litres

**Solution (Exhaustive Step-by-Step):**

**Step 1 — Compute daily distance per truck**

$$
D_{truck} = 60 \text{ km/day}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute fleet-wide daily distance**

$$
D_{fleet} = N_v \times D_{truck} = 25 \times 60 = 1500 \text{ km/day}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute daily fuel consumption**

$$
C_d = \dfrac{D_{fleet}}{kpl} = \dfrac{1500}{4.5} = 333.33 \text{ L/day}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute monthly fuel requirement**

$$
C_{month} = C_d \times N_{working} = 333.33 \times 26 = 8666.67 \text{ L/month}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute lead-time demand**

$$
LD = C_d \times L_t = 333.33 \times 3 = 1000 \text{ L}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute safety stock**

$$
SS = 0.20 \times LD = 0.20 \times 1000 = 200 \text{ L}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute Reorder Point**

$$
ROP = LD + SS = 1000 + 200 = \boxed{1200 \text{ L}}
$$

**Interpretation:** Whenever the bulk tank dip reading shows **1,200 L remaining**, the depot manager must raise a fresh PO so that fuel arrives just as the tank approaches empty.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical — Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)

**Problem:**
Annual HSD demand of a depot = **3,00,000 L**. Ordering cost per PO = **₹2,500**. Holding cost = **₹4 per litre per year**. Find EOQ, number of orders, and order interval.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify parameters**

$$
D = 300000 \text{ L/yr}, \quad S = 2500 \text{ ₹/order}, \quad H = 4 \text{ ₹/L/yr}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply EOQ formula**

$$
EOQ = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 300000 \times 2500}{4}}
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify numerator**

$$
2 \times 300000 \times 2500 = 1{,}500{,}000{,}000
$$

**Step 4 — Divide by H**

$$
\frac{1{,}500{,}000{,}000}{4} = 375{,}000{,}000
$$

**Step 5 — Take square root**

$$
EOQ = \sqrt{375{,}000{,}000} = \boxed{19{,}364 \text{ L} \approx 19.4 \text{ kL}}
$$

**Step 6 — Number of orders per year**

$$
N = \frac{D}{EOQ} = \frac{300000}{19364} = 15.49 \approx 16 \text{ orders/yr}
$$

**Step 7 — Order interval**

$$
T = \frac{365}{16} = 22.8 \text{ days/order}
$$

**Step 8 — Total annual inventory cost (verification)**

$$
TC = \frac{D}{Q}S + \frac{Q}{2}H = \frac{300000}{19364}\times 2500 + \frac{19364}{2}\times 4
$$

$$
TC = 38729.6 + 38728 = \boxed{₹77{,}457.6 / \text{yr}}
$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical — Volume Temperature Correction

**Problem:**
A bulk tanker delivers **12,000 L** of HSD at a measured temperature of **32 °C**. The meter's indicated volume is **12,000 L**. Compute the volume corrected to **15 °C reference**, given α for HSD = **0.0008 /°C**.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — State correction formula**

$$
V_{15} = V_{T}\left[1 - \alpha (T - 15)\right]
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute**

$$
V_{15} = 12000 \times \left[1 - 0.0008 \times (32 - 15)\right]
$$

**Step 3 — Compute (T − 15)**

$$
T - 15 = 32 - 15 = 17
$$

**Step 4 — Compute α(T − 15)**

$$
0.0008 \times 17 = 0.0136
$$

**Step 5 — Compute (1 − α(T − 15))**

$$
1 - 0.0136 = 0.9864
$$

**Step 6 — Compute corrected volume**

$$
V_{15} = 12000 \times 0.9864 = \boxed{11{,}836.8 \text{ L}}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute apparent loss**

$$
Loss = 12000 - 11836.8 = 163.2 \text{ L}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **This 163.2 L is NOT a real loss** — it is a **physical expansion** of fuel at higher temperature. The depot is billed for the actual 15 °C mass/equivalent. This is why Indian summers cause "mysterious" book-vs-physical variances of 0.8–1.5%.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Storage Loss Causes & Controls

| Loss Type | Cause | Typical % | Control Measure |
|---|---|---|---|
| Evaporation | Vapour loss from vent | 0.2 – 0.5% | Vapour recovery system, pressurized tanks |
| Temperature expansion | Ambient heat | 0.5 – 1.5% | Volume correction, underground storage |
| Leakage | Tank/pipeline corrosion | 0.1 – 0.3% | Periodic hydrostatic test, cathodic protection |
| Handling spillage | Overfilling, transfer losses | 0.1 – 0.2% | Auto-shutoff nozzles, trained operators |
| Adulteration theft | Vendor malpractice | 0.5 – 2.0% | Sealed sampling, lab testing, third-party audit |
| Pilferage | Internal malpractice | 0.2 – 1.0% | CCTV, calibrated flowmeters, two-person rule |
| **Total tolerable loss** | — | **< 1.0%** | Tight inventory control |

---

### 3.5 Decision Tree — Selection of Storage Type

| Decision Criterion | Choose UST (Underground) | Choose AST (Above Ground) |
|---|---|---|
| Available land | Limited open area | Adequate open area |
| Climate | Hot (high evaporation) | Moderate / cold |
| Fire risk zone | Dense urban | Sparse industrial |
| Capex vs Opex | High capex, low evaporation loss | Lower capex, higher evaporation loss |
| Inspection ease | Difficult (shutdown) | Easy (visual, UT) |
| Regulatory preference | **Preferred in India (PESO)** | Allowed with bund wall |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End Fuel Management Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fleet Plan and Vehicle Kilometres] --> B[Demand Forecast]
    B --> C[Vendor Pre Qualification]
    C --> D[Tender and Rate Contract]
    D --> E[Purchase Order]
    E --> F[Bulk Tanker Dispatch from OMC]
    F --> G[Receipt and Seal Check]
    G --> H{Quality OK?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Tank Transfer via Pipeline]
    H -- No --> J[Reject and Claim]
    I --> K[Storage Tank UST or AST]
    K --> L[Day Service Tank]
    L --> M[Dispensing Pump and Flowmeter]
    M --> N[Fuel Slip Generation]
    N --> O[Vehicle Refuelling]
    O --> P[Daily Stock Reconciliation]
    P --> Q{Reorder Point Reached?}
    Q -- Yes --> E
    Q -- No --> R[Continue Operations]
    P --> S[Monthly MIS and Audit]
    S --> T[Variance Analysis and Loss Control]
```

### 4.2 Storage Architecture — Layered Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph L1[Layer 1 Strategic Reserve]
        T1[UST 1 20 kL]
        T2[UST 2 20 kL]
    end
    subgraph L2[Layer 2 Daily Operational]
        T3[AST 1 10 kL]
    end
    subgraph L3[Layer 3 Pump Feed]
        T4[Day Tank 1 kL]
    end
    subgraph L4[Layer 4 Vehicle Onboard]
        V1[Truck Tank 1 400 L]
        V2[Truck Tank 2 400 L]
        V3[Bus Tank 600 L]
    end
    T1 --> T3
    T2 --> T3
    T3 --> T4
    T4 --> V1
    T4 --> V2
    T4 --> V3
```

### 4.3 Procurement Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Procurement Cycle] --> B{Annual Volume Greater than 1 lakh L?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Float Rate Contract with OMC]
    B -- No --> D[Spot Purchase from Retail Outlet]
    C --> E{Single Vendor or Multi Vendor?}
    E -- Single --> F[Negotiate Volume Discount]
    E -- Multi --> G[Tender Two Bid System]
    F --> H[Sign 12 Month RC]
    G --> H
    D --> I[Cash and Carry Transaction]
    H --> J[Evaluate Quarterly Performance]
    J --> K{Renew or Re-tender?}
    K -- Renew --> H
    K -- Re-tender --> G
```

### 4.4 Quality Acceptance Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Tanker Arrives at Depot] --> B[Visual and Seal Check]
    B --> C{Seal Intact?}
    C -- No --> D[Reject and Lodge Complaint]
    C -- Yes --> E[Sample Drawing]
    E --> F[Density Measurement]
    E --> G[Temperature Measurement]
    E --> H[Water and Sediment Test]
    F --> I{Within BIS Range?}
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I -- All Yes --> J[Accept and Transfer to Tank]
    I -- Any No --> K[Quarantine and Lab Test]
    K --> L{Lab Passes?}
    L -- Yes --> J
    L -- No --> D
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*List any six essential parameters that must be checked during the receipt of a bulk fuel tanker at a transport depot.*

**Model Answer (6 points):**
1. Sealing — physical integrity of OMC/transport seal.
2. Density (kg/L) measured by hydrometer at observed temperature.
3. Temperature (°C) of fuel in tanker.
4. Visual appearance — clarity, colour, free water, sediment.
5. Quantity reconciliation between challan litres and dip measurement.
6. Quality sample drawn, sealed, and sent for lab testing.
7. Tax invoice and GST compliance verification.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between Underground Storage Tank (UST) and Above Ground Storage Tank (AST) in five points.*

**Model Answer (tabular, any 5):**
1. **Location** — UST: buried below ground; AST: surface-mounted.
2. **Evaporation Loss** — UST: low (cool soil); AST: high (sun exposure).
3. **Capex** — UST: higher; AST: lower.
4. **Fire Risk** — UST: lower; AST: higher.
5. **Inspection** — UST: difficult, requires shutdown; AST: easy visual + UT.
6. **Regulatory** — UST: preferred by PESO in residential zones; AST: requires 110% bund wall.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Internal Choice Pattern:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a) Explain the complete procedure for procurement of bulk High-Speed Diesel (HSD) for a transport fleet of 50 vehicles. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Demand Forecasting:** Compute monthly HSD demand using vehicle-kilometres and average kmpl. Add seasonal buffer (10–15%) and growth margin.
2. **Vendor Pre-qualification:** PSU OMCs (IOCL/BPCL/HPCL) preferred; check dealership validity, BIS certification, financial soundness, delivery capacity.
3. **Tendering:** Issue tender with two-bid system — technical (quality, safety, past performance) and financial (per-litre discount over pump price).
4. **Rate Contract:** Sign 6–12 month RC with discount of ₹3–₹6/L for bulk HSD. Define delivery SLAs (24–72 hr), minimum order quantity, payment terms.
5. **PO Generation:** Release Purchase Orders against RC, specifying quantity, grade, delivery window, and testing protocol.
6. **Receipt and Quality Check:** Verify seal, draw sample, test density, temperature, water content, BIS compliance; record dip reading.
7. **Invoice and Stock Update:** Match PO-GRN-Invoice, update stock register, claim GST input credit, transfer to bulk tank.

**(b) A depot's bulk HSD tank shows: opening stock 8,000 L, receipts 22,000 L, issues 24,500 L, book closing 5,500 L. Physical dip reading shows 5,380 L. Calculate the loss percentage and suggest two controls. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Loss in litres**

$$
Loss = Stock_{book} - Stock_{physical} = 5500 - 5380 = 120 \text{ L}
$$

**Step 2 — Loss percentage**

$$
Loss\% = \frac{120}{5500} \times 100 = \boxed{2.18\%}
$$

**Step 3 — Diagnosis**

- Acceptable industry benchmark is **< 1.0%**. Here loss is **2.18%**, which is **double the tolerance**, indicating either:
  - **Temperature expansion** (if measurement was done at 30 °C+ without correction).
  - **Evaporation loss** from vent.
  - **Calibration drift** of flowmeter.
  - **Pilferage** via unauthorised tapping.

**Step 4 — Controls**

- **Control 1:** Apply **volume temperature correction to 15 °C** during every physical dip measurement to eliminate false "loss" due to thermal expansion.
- **Control 2:** Install **calibrated flowmeters with periodic recalibration (every 6 months)** and **vapour recovery system** on vents to reduce both measurement error and evaporation.
- **Control 3 (bonus):** Enforce **two-person dip rule + CCTV** at the dip hatch to eliminate internal pilferage.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a) Discuss the engineering and statutory safety requirements for storage of 20,000 litres of HSD in an above-ground tank at a transport depot. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Engineering Requirements (4 marks):**
1. **Bund Wall / Dyke** — RCC bund wall of minimum 110% volume of largest tank (= 22,000 L capacity) to contain spill.
2. **Earthing and Lightning Arrester** — Resistance < 1 ohm; IS 3043 compliant; separate lightning mast.
3. **Vent with Flame Arrestor** — Properly sized to release vapour during filling/breathing; flame arrestor prevents external ignition.
4. **Fire Fighting** — Foam pourer system (AFFF), CO₂ for electrical, hydrant network, fire extinguishers (DCP) every 15 m.
5. **ATEX-rated Electricals** — All fittings flameproof; cables armoured.
6. **Anti-static Hose and Earthing of Tanker** — During unloading.

**Statutory Requirements (3 marks):**
1. **PESO License** — under Explosives Act 1884 and Petroleum Rules 2002.
2. **Pollution Control Board Consent** — CTE (Consent to Establish) before construction + CTO (Consent to Operate) before commissioning.
3. **Factory License** — if depot falls under Factories Act 1948.
4. **Fire NOC** — from local Fire Department (Kerala Fire & Rescue Services).
5. **OISD-117 / 118 Compliance** — for AST / UST design and operations.

**(b) Compute the Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) for a depot with annual demand 5,00,000 L of HSD. Ordering cost is ₹3,000 per order and holding cost is ₹5 per litre per year. Also find the number of orders per year and the order interval in days. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — List parameters**

$$
D = 500000 \text{ L}, \quad S = 3000 \text{ ₹}, \quad H = 5 \text{ ₹/L/yr}
$$

**Step 2 — EOQ formula**

$$
EOQ = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 500000 \times 3000}{5}}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerator**

$$
2 \times 500000 \times 3000 = 3{,}000{,}000{,}000
$$

**Step 4 — Division**

$$
\frac{3{,}000{,}000{,}000}{5} = 600{,}000{,}000
$$

**Step 5 — Square root**

$$
EOQ = \sqrt{600{,}000{,}000} = \boxed{24{,}495 \text{ L} \approx 24.5 \text{ kL}}
$$

**Step 6 — Number of orders per year**

$$
N = \frac{D}{EOQ} = \frac{500000}{24495} = \boxed{20.4 \approx 21 \text{ orders/yr}}
$$

**Step 7 — Order interval**

$$
T = \frac{365}{20.4} = \boxed{17.9 \text{ days per order}}
$$

**Step 8 — Verification of minimum total cost (optional)**

$$
TC_{min} = \sqrt{2DSH} = \sqrt{2 \times 500000 \times 3000 \times 5} = ₹1{,}22{,}474
$$

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in Fuel Procurement & Storage questions:**
> 1. **Confusing 15 °C reference with 20 °C** — Indian petroleum volume correction is **15 °C** (IS 1093:1986), NOT 20 °C (which is European/ASTM). Writing 20 °C = full mark loss.
> 2. **Forgetting "litres at 15 °C" notation** — Always specify the reference temperature in final answers.
> 3. **Wrong bund wall %** — Many students write 100% or 120%; the standard is **110%** (per OISD-117).
> 4. **Mixing up PESO and OISD** — PESO is the **legal/licensing** body; OISD issues **engineering standards**. They are not interchangeable.
> 5. **In EOQ, students forget the "√"** and write $EOQ = 2DS/H$ — instant zero.
> 6. **In ROP, ignoring safety stock** — Examiner specifically looks for "+ SS" term.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Fuel Management**

- **Fuel = working capital asset** — unique because it is **perishable, hazardous, taxable, and price-volatile**.
- **Procurement Cycle:** Forecast → Pre-qualify → Tender → RC → PO → Receipt → Quality Check → Invoice → Stock Update.
- **Vendor preference:** PSU OMCs (IOCL/BPCL/HPCL) for large fleets; spot retail for small fleets.
- **Volume correction reference temperature in India: 15 °C** — formula uses α ≈ 0.0008 for HSD.
- **Density (HSD): 0.840 kg/L; (MS): 0.755 kg/L** at 15 °C.
- **BIS standards:** IS 1460 (HSD), IS 2796 (MS).
- **Statutory bodies:** PESO (licensing), OISD (engineering standards), CPCB/SPCB (pollution).
- **Safety:** Bund wall = **110%** of largest tank; vent with flame arrestor; ATEX fittings; earthing < 1 Ω.
- **ROP = max daily consumption × lead time + safety stock.**
- **EOQ = √(2DS/H)**; cycle time = 365/N orders.
- **Stock turnover** = annual fuel cost / average inventory value (higher = better).
- **Tolerable storage loss < 1%**; anything above must be investigated.
- **FIFO method** preferred for fuel to avoid sediment build-up at bottom.
- **Two-bin / three-bin system** is the practical inventory method used in depots.
- **GST Input Tax Credit** is claimable on fuel for commercial vehicles (Transport & Logistics), making proper invoicing critical.
- **EV charging as future fuel** — depot design must provision **transformer capacity, charging bays, and renewable energy tie-up** for next 5–10 years.
- **Documentation:** Daily dip log, issue register, fuel slip, monthly reconciliation, vendor performance scorecard.
- **CCTV + two-person rule + calibrated flowmeters** = the **3 pillars of anti-pilferage**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fuel Consumption Monitoring
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT866 - VEHICLE TRANSPORT MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fuel Consumption Monitoring

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fuel Consumption Monitoring (FCM)** is the systematic, continuous, and recorded process of measuring, recording, analyzing, and controlling the volume of fuel utilized by a vehicle or a fleet of vehicles over a defined operational period, expressed through standardized engineering metrics such as **kilometers per litre (km/l)**, **litres per 100 kilometres (L/100 km)**, or **cost per kilometer (₹/km)**, in order to optimize operational expenditure, ensure regulatory compliance, detect pilferage, and benchmark vehicle efficiency.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework for **PEMUT866 – Vehicle Transport Management**, Fuel Consumption Monitoring is positioned as a sub-component of **Fleet Operational Cost Control** and **Logistics Resource Management**. It bridges three functional domains:

1. **Mechanical Engineering Domain** – the physical measurement of fuel (volumetric, mass-based, or electronic sensing).
2. **Management Science Domain** – the accounting and control functions related to cost reduction.
3. **Information Technology Domain** – the use of telematics, GPS, and fleet management software (FMS).

## 1.2 Standard Metrics Used in Industry

The following metrics are universally accepted and appear in KTU question papers:

| Metric Symbol | Unit | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| **FC** | Litres/hour (L/h) or kg/h | Fuel Consumption rate (instantaneous) |
| **FE** | km/litre | Fuel Economy / Efficiency |
| **SFC** | g/kWh | Specific Fuel Consumption (engine testing) |
| **CO₂** | g/km | Carbon dioxide emission per unit distance |
| **FCR** | % | Fuel Cost Ratio = (Fuel Cost / Total Operating Cost) × 100 |
| **DPI** | km | Distance Per Litre Indicator |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Bodies to Remember (Very High KTU Yield):**
> - **ARAI** – Automotive Research Association of India (testing authority for Indian vehicles under CMVR)
> - **BIS** – Bureau of Indian Standards (IS 14654:1999 for fuel measurement)
> - **ISO 9001:2015** – Quality management in fuel logging
> - **MoRTH** – Ministry of Road Transport and Highways (policy)

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Think of Fuel Consumption Monitoring like a Household Electricity Bill (KSEB).**
>
> Just as KSEB installs a **meter** in your home to measure units (kWh) consumed every month — and then bills you based on a tariff — a transport company installs a **fuel meter or sensor** in each vehicle to track litres consumed per trip. Just as you compare your electricity bill to last month to find savings, the transport manager compares vehicle-wise **km/litre** across months to identify which vehicle is *thirsty* (low km/l) and which is *efficient* (high km/l). Without the meter (monitoring), there is no bill (cost), no comparison (analysis), and no reason to switch off the fan (reduce consumption).

## 1.4 Why Fuel Consumption Monitoring is Critical in Transport Management

1. **Fuel is the largest variable cost** in road transport – typically **40% to 60%** of total operating cost.
2. **Fuel prices fluctuate** daily under dynamic pricing, requiring tight control.
3. **Fuel pilferage and theft** are rampant; monitoring provides audit trails.
4. **Environmental compliance** – monitoring CO₂ helps meet **BS-VI** and future emission norms.
5. **Performance benchmarking** – driver behaviour, route selection, and vehicle condition can be correlated with fuel data.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Fuel Consumption Monitoring System (FCMS) – Architecture

A complete FCMS consists of **four functional layers**:

1. **Data Acquisition Layer (DAL)**
   - Volumetric flow meters
   - Electronic Control Unit (ECU) reading injector pulse width
   - Dipstick / manual log entries
   - OBD-II port data extraction

2. **Data Transmission Layer (DTL)**
   - GSM / GPRS modules
   - GPS synchronization for location-tagging
   - RFID at fuel stations (auto-logging)

3. **Data Storage & Processing Layer (DSPL)**
   - Cloud-based Fleet Management Software (FMS)
   - On-board diagnostics storage
   - Spreadsheet / ERP modules (SAP, Tally integration)

4. **Reporting & Control Layer (RCL)**
   - Dashboards (km/l trends, driver scorecards)
   - Alerts (anomalous consumption, fuel theft detection)
   - Policy enforcement (fuel cards with PIN)

## 2.2 Why and How Behind Each Step – Bulleted Logic

- **Why measure volumetric fuel?**
  * Because the *physical quantity* (litres) is independent of price fluctuations; only then can fair comparisons be made across months.
- **Why use km/litre instead of L/100 km?**
  * In India, **km/litre** is the standard reporting unit in OEM brochures and ARAI test certificates. KTU questions are also framed around km/l.
- **Why integrate with GPS?**
  * Without GPS, a driver could claim 50 km on a 5 L refill (10 km/l), but if GPS shows only 30 km, theft/incorrect logging is detected.
- **Why electronic over manual?**
  * Manual logs suffer from **rounding error, bias, and human manipulation** – a known KTU critical-thinking point.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units | Boundary / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $FE = \dfrac{D}{V}$ | Fuel Economy | km/L | $D$ = Distance run, $V$ = Volume of fuel used |
| 2 | $SFC = \dfrac{m_{f}}{W \cdot t}$ | Specific Fuel Consumption | g/kWh | Engine testing metric |
| 3 | $FCR\,(\%) = \dfrac{C_{fuel}}{C_{total}} \times 100$ | Fuel Cost Ratio | % | $C_{total}$ includes driver wage, tyres, depreciation |
| 4 | $CO_{2} = 2.68 \times V$ | CO₂ Emission per vehicle | kg | For petrol; for diesel multiply by **2.68** as well (mass-based) |
| 5 | $FC_{trip} = \dfrac{V_{refilled} - V_{reserve}}{n_{trips}}$ | Average Trip Fuel | L/trip | Excludes initial/final tank residue |
| 6 | $FC_{hourly} = \dfrac{V_{engaged}}{t_{engaged}}$ | Idle Fuel Wastage | L/h | $t_{engaged}$ = engine ON time |
| 7 | $\eta_{thermal} = \dfrac{W_{out}}{m_{f} \times CV}$ | Thermal Efficiency | % | $CV$ = Calorific Value (≈ 43 MJ/kg for diesel) |
| 8 | $\Delta FE = \dfrac{FE_{actual} - FE_{standard}}{FE_{standard}} \times 100$ | Deviation Index | % | Used to flag underperforming vehicles |
| 9 | $C_{per\,km} = \dfrac{P_{fuel} \times V}{D}$ | Cost per km | ₹/km | $P_{fuel}$ = price per litre |
| 10 | $U_{tank} = \dfrac{V_{tank} - V_{refill}}{V_{tank}} \times 100$ | Tank Utilization | % | Indicates trip planning efficiency |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always write units in your final answer. Marks are specifically deducted for missing units in numerical problems on this topic.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Logistics Companies** (e.g., DHL, Blue Dart) use FCMS to compute driver incentives.
- **Public Transport** (KSRTC) uses it to optimize depot-wise refuelling.
- **Mining & Construction Fleets** use SFC to evaluate engine overhauls.
- **Electric Vehicle (EV) Transition** uses the same architecture but with **kWh/km** instead of L/km – the monitoring framework remains identical, a point emphasized in the 2024 NEP-aligned syllabus.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Demonstrations

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Fuel Economy (km/litre) from Refill Data

### Problem Statement (Model)
A truck refills **250 L** of diesel at the start of a trip. After running **1,800 km**, the driver refills **260 L** to bring the tank back to the original level. Compute the fuel economy in **km/litre** and the fuel cost per km if diesel price is **₹ 92.50/L**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

Let us define the variables first.

- $D$ = Total distance run = $1800 \text{ km}$
- $V$ = Volume of fuel actually consumed during the trip (litres)

The first refill of 250 L is for the *previous* trip, not the current one. We must therefore use the **second refill volume** to determine fuel used, assuming the tank was returned to the same level (full-tank method).

$$
V = 260 \text{ L}
$$

Now, the Fuel Economy formula from the cheat sheet:

$$
FE = \frac{D}{V}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
FE = \frac{1800 \text{ km}}{260 \text{ L}}
$$

Performing the division:

$$
FE = 6.923 \text{ km/L}
$$

Rounding to two decimal places (standard in KTU answers):

$$
\boxed{FE \approx 6.92 \text{ km/L}}
$$

### Computation of Fuel Cost per km

We use Formula 9 from the cheat sheet:

$$
C_{per\,km} = \frac{P_{fuel} \times V}{D}
$$

Substituting $P_{fuel} = 92.50$ ₹/L, $V = 260$ L, $D = 1800$ km:

$$
C_{per\,km} = \frac{92.50 \times 260}{1800}
$$

Multiplying the numerator:

$$
92.50 \times 260 = 24050
$$

Therefore:

$$
C_{per\,km} = \frac{24050}{1800}
$$

Performing the division:

$$
C_{per\,km} = 13.361 \text{ ₹/km}
$$

Rounded:

$$
\boxed{C_{per\,km} \approx ₹ 13.36 \text{ per km}}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Deviation Index of a Vehicle

### Problem Statement (Model)
ARAI certified mileage of a bus is **5.5 km/L**. In actual operation, the depot recorded an average mileage of **4.8 km/L**. Compute the **Deviation Index (ΔFE)** and comment on whether the vehicle requires engine tuning.

### Step-by-Step Solution

From Formula 8:

$$
\Delta FE = \frac{FE_{actual} - FE_{standard}}{FE_{standard}} \times 100
$$

Substituting $FE_{actual} = 4.8$ km/L, $FE_{standard} = 5.5$ km/L:

$$
\Delta FE = \frac{4.8 - 5.5}{5.5} \times 100
$$

Simplifying the numerator:

$$
4.8 - 5.5 = -0.7
$$

Therefore:

$$
\Delta FE = \frac{-0.7}{5.5} \times 100
$$

Performing the division:

$$
\frac{-0.7}{5.5} = -0.12727
$$

Multiplying by 100:

$$
\Delta FE = -12.727\,\%
$$

Rounded:

$$
\boxed{\Delta FE \approx -12.73\,\%}
$$

### Engineering Interpretation

A **negative deviation of more than 10%** is a red flag. The probable causes are:
- Air filter clogging
- Tyre pressure below specification
- Injector malfunction
- Excessive idling
- Aggressive driving style

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Rule of Thumb:** If $\vert \Delta FE \vert > 10\%$, the vehicle must be sent for **preventive maintenance** and the driver must be **retrained on eco-driving**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Thermal Efficiency and Fuel Mass

### Problem Statement (Model)
A diesel engine delivers **80 kW** of brake power for **2 hours**, consuming **40 L** of diesel. Density of diesel = **0.85 kg/L**, Calorific Value (CV) = **43 MJ/kg**. Compute the **brake specific fuel consumption (BSFC)** and **thermal efficiency**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 3.3.1 – Compute Fuel Mass**

$$
m_{f} = V \times \rho = 40 \times 0.85 = 34 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 3.3.2 – Compute Energy Input**

$$
E_{in} = m_{f} \times CV = 34 \times 43 = 1462 \text{ MJ}
$$

Converting to kJ:

$$
E_{in} = 1462 \times 1000 = 1{,}462{,}000 \text{ kJ}
$$

**Step 3.3.3 – Compute Energy Output**

$$
W_{out} = P \times t = 80 \times 2 = 160 \text{ kWh}
$$

Converting kWh to kJ (1 kWh = 3600 kJ):

$$
W_{out} = 160 \times 3600 = 576{,}000 \text{ kJ}
$$

**Step 3.3.4 – Compute Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC)**

$$
BSFC = \frac{m_{f}}{W_{out}} = \frac{34 \text{ kg}}{160 \text{ kWh}} = 0.2125 \text{ kg/kWh}
$$

Converting to g/kWh:

$$
\boxed{BSFC = 212.5 \text{ g/kWh}}
$$

**Step 3.3.5 – Compute Thermal Efficiency**

Using Formula 7:

$$
\eta_{thermal} = \frac{W_{out}}{m_{f} \times CV}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\eta_{thermal} = \frac{160 \times 3600}{1462 \times 1000}
$$

Calculating:

$$
\eta_{thermal} = \frac{576{,}000}{1{,}462{,}000} = 0.3940
$$

Converting to percentage:

$$
\boxed{\eta_{thermal} \approx 39.40\,\%}
$$

> [!TIP]
> A modern diesel engine has thermal efficiency between **35% and 42%**. The computed 39.4% is therefore within healthy range.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix – Common Numerical Scenarios in KTU Exams

| Scenario | Given Data | Required Output | Formula Used | Common Mistake |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Refill method | Distance + refilled volume | km/L | $FE = D/V$ | Forgetting that first refill is for *previous* trip |
| Top-up method | Initial + final dipstick | Litres consumed | $V = V_i - V_f + V_{refilled}$ | Sign errors |
| Cost control | Mileage + fuel price | ₹/km | $C_{per\,km} = P \times V / D$ | Using 100 km as base instead of D |
| Carbon accounting | Litres of diesel | kg CO₂ | $CO_2 = 2.68 \times V$ | Using petrol factor for diesel |
| Deviation analysis | ARAI vs actual | ΔFE% | $\Delta FE = (FE_{act} - FE_{std})/FE_{std} \times 100$ | Forgetting the ×100 multiplier |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram – Fuel Consumption Monitoring System (FCMS) Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block renders the **Block-Level Functional Architecture** of an FCMS, showing how data flows from the fuel tank to the manager's dashboard. This is the standard diagram students are expected to reproduce in KTU 14-mark questions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[TankFuelTank] --> B[SensorFlowMeter]
    B --> C[ECUVehicleECU]
    B --> D[GPSPositioningModule]
    C --> E[TelematicsUnitOBDBoard]
    D --> E
    E --> F[CloudServerFMS]
    F --> G[DatabaseTripLog]
    G --> H[AnalyticsEngine]
    H --> I[DashboardManagerView]
    H --> J[AlertsAnomalyDetection]
    F --> K[DriverFeedbackApp]
    H --> L[ReportsCostKPI]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**

- `TankFuelTank` is the physical source.
- `SensorFlowMeter` measures volumetric flow in real-time.
- `ECUVehicleECU` reads injector pulse width and correlates with sensor data.
- `GPSPositioningModule` stamps every fuel reading with location.
- `TelematicsUnitOBDBoard` consolidates the data and transmits it.
- `CloudServerFMS` stores the data securely.
- `DatabaseTripLog` archives each trip's fuel-distance-cost record.
- `AnalyticsEngine` computes km/l, ΔFE, cost/km.
- `DashboardManagerView` provides a visual interface.
- `AlertsAnomalyDetection` triggers theft/idling alarms.
- `DriverFeedbackApp` sends eco-driving scores to drivers.
- `ReportsCostKPI` generates monthly MIS for management.

---

## 4.2 Process Flow – Refill Event Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Start Trip] --> P2[Record Odometer Start]
    P2 --> P3[Vehicle in Operation]
    P3 --> P4{Pilot Decision}
    P4 -- Refuel Required --> P5[Stop at Fuel Station]
    P5 --> P6[Capture Litres Refilled]
    P6 --> P7[Capture Cost and Price per Litre]
    P7 --> P8[Record Odometer End]
    P8 --> P9[Compute Trip FE and Cost per km]
    P9 --> P10[Log Entry in Trip Ledger]
    P10 --> P11{Anomaly Detected?}
    P11 -- Yes --> P12[Generate Alert and Audit]
    P11 -- No --> P13[Update Vehicle Average]
    P13 --> P1
    P12 --> P1
```

**Process Description:**

- A trip is **initiated** with a full-tank or pre-measured fuel state.
- The driver records the **starting odometer reading** (manual or automatic).
- The vehicle runs until a **refuel decision** is triggered (low fuel indicator).
- At the fuel station, the **volume filled, cost, and price** are captured.
- The **ending odometer** is recorded, and the system computes **Trip FE** and **Cost per km**.
- The entry is **logged** in the trip ledger.
- An **anomaly detection engine** checks whether the trip's km/l is within 1.5 standard deviations of the vehicle's historical average.
- If an anomaly is detected, an **audit alert** is generated.
- If normal, the **vehicle running average** is updated, and the cycle repeats.

---

## 4.3 Comparative Block – Manual vs Electronic Monitoring

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Trad[Manual Monitoring]
        M1[Driver Logbook] --> M2[Supervisor Review]
        M2 --> M3[Spreadsheet Entry]
    end
    subgraph Auto[Electronic Monitoring]
        E1[OBD Sensor] --> E2[Telematics Gateway]
        E2 --> E3[Cloud Dashboard]
    end
    Trad -- Lacks Accuracy --> Risk1[Pilferage Risk High]
    Auto -- Real Time --> Benefit1[Anomaly Detection Live]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define *Fuel Consumption Monitoring* and list any **two** standardized metrics used to report fuel efficiency in Indian road transport.

#### Model Answer

Fuel Consumption Monitoring is the systematic measurement, recording, and analysis of fuel used by a vehicle over a defined period, with the objective of cost control, performance benchmarking, and pilferage detection.

The two standardized metrics are:

1. **Kilometers per Litre (km/L)** – distance covered per unit volume of fuel; widely used in India.
2. **Litres per 100 Kilometres (L/100 km)** – fuel volume needed to cover 100 km; preferred in European and BS-VI emission labelling.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
State the role of **ARAI** in fuel consumption testing.

#### Model Answer

The **Automotive Research Association of India (ARAI)**, headquartered in Pune, is the authorized testing agency under the Central Motor Vehicle Rules (CMVR). Its role in fuel consumption testing is to:

1. Certify the **standard fuel economy** of every vehicle sold in India under **Bharat Stage (BS)** emission norms.
2. Conduct tests as per **AIS-008** and **IS 14654:1999** standards.
3. Issue the **manufacturer-claimed mileage figure** that appears on the vehicle's sales brochure and on the windshield label.
4. Provide the **baseline (FE_standard)** against which real-world fleet deviations are measured.

---

## 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) – Internal Choice

### Question A

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 – Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the **functional architecture** of a Fuel Consumption Monitoring System (FCMS) with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the **Telematics Unit** and the **Analytics Engine** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A logistics company operates 50 trucks. The average fuel refill data for one month is as follows:
- Total distance run by all trucks combined: **3,50,000 km**
- Total diesel refilled: **65,000 L**
- Average diesel price: **₹ 91.20/L**

Compute the (i) average fuel economy of the fleet, (ii) the total fuel cost for the month, and (iii) the cost per kilometre. Comment on the cost-effectiveness if the industry benchmark is **5.5 km/L**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

### Part (a) – FCMS Architecture (7 marks)

A complete FCMS has **four functional layers** as described in Section 2.1. The block diagram is reproduced below in textual form for the answer sheet:

**Block Diagram (to be drawn on answer sheet):**

```
[Tank] → [Flow Sensor] → [ECU] ─┐
                                 ├→ [Telematics Unit] → [Cloud Server]
[GPS Module] ────────────────────┘                          ↓
                                                       [Analytics Engine]
                                                              ↓
                          ┌────────────────┬─────────────────┐
                          ↓                ↓                 ↓
                   [Dashboard]      [Anomaly Alerts]   [Driver App]
```

**Role of Telematics Unit:**

- It is the **data aggregator** mounted on the vehicle.
- It collects fuel data from the **flow meter / ECU** and location data from **GPS**.
- It transmits consolidated packets via **GPRS/4G** to the cloud.
- It can also store data locally when network is unavailable (store-and-forward).
- It enables **real-time monitoring** rather than after-the-fact analysis.

**Role of Analytics Engine:**

- It is the **computational brain** of the FCMS, hosted in the cloud.
- It computes **km/L, cost/km, ΔFE, idle fuel wastage, route-wise consumption** for every trip.
- It applies **statistical models** (e.g., moving average, Z-score) to detect anomalies.
- It generates **KPI dashboards** for the fleet manager and **driver scorecards** for performance evaluation.
- It enables **predictive analytics** – for example, forecasting next month's fuel cost using time-series models.

---

### Part (b) – Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $D_{total} = 350000$ km
- $V_{total} = 65000$ L
- $P_{fuel} = 91.20$ ₹/L
- Industry benchmark $FE_{std} = 5.5$ km/L

**Step 1 – Average Fuel Economy of the Fleet**

Using Formula 1:

$$
FE_{fleet} = \frac{D_{total}}{V_{total}} = \frac{350000}{65000}
$$

$$
\boxed{FE_{fleet} \approx 5.38 \text{ km/L}}
$$

**Step 2 – Total Fuel Cost for the Month**

$$
C_{total} = V_{total} \times P_{fuel} = 65000 \times 91.20
$$

$$
C_{total} = 5{,}928{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

$$
\boxed{C_{total} = ₹ 59.28 \text{ Lakhs}}
$$

**Step 3 – Cost per Kilometre**

Using Formula 9:

$$
C_{per\,km} = \frac{C_{total}}{D_{total}} = \frac{5928000}{350000}
$$

$$
\boxed{C_{per\,km} \approx ₹ 16.94 \text{ per km}}
$$

**Step 4 – Cost-Effectiveness Comment**

Compute the Deviation Index using Formula 8:

$$
\Delta FE = \frac{5.38 - 5.5}{5.5} \times 100 = \frac{-0.12}{5.5} \times 100 = -2.18\,\%
$$

Since the deviation is only **2.18% below benchmark**, the fleet is **reasonably cost-effective**, but there is a small **scope for improvement of 0.12 km/L** per vehicle. A 0.12 km/L improvement across 3,50,000 km would save:

$$
\Delta V = \frac{350000}{5.5} - \frac{350000}{5.62} \approx 63636 - 62278 = 1358 \text{ L/month}
$$

At ₹ 91.20/L, this is a saving of **₹ 1,23,856 per month** – justifying investments in driver training and tyre-pressure monitoring.

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice for Internal Option)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 – Module 2]**

**(a)** With the help of a flow diagram, explain the **steps involved in monitoring fuel consumption using the full-tank refill method**. List two advantages and two limitations of this method. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A city bus refilled **120 L** of diesel at the depot on Day 1, ran **540 km** over 3 days, and on Day 4 the tank was topped up with **135 L** to bring it back to full. Compute the fuel economy in km/L. If ARAI certified mileage is **6.0 km/L**, calculate the deviation index and state whether corrective action is required (threshold: ±10%). **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

### Part (a) – Full-Tank Refill Method (7 marks)

**Flow Diagram (textual form for answer sheet):**

```
Step 1 → Start of trip: tank filled FULL. Record odometer reading O₁.
Step 2 → Vehicle in operation.
Step 3 → End of trip / low-fuel event: refill to FULL at same station.
Step 4 → Record volume refilled (V) and ending odometer (O₂).
Step 5 → Distance = O₂ − O₁.
Step 6 → FE = Distance / V.
Step 7 → Log entry.
Step 8 → Repeat for each trip.
```

**Two Advantages:**

1. **High accuracy** – since the tank is always full, the volume of fuel refilled equals the volume consumed (no dipstick error).
2. **Standardized** – recommended by ARAI and BIS for fleet audit purposes.

**Two Limitations:**

1. **Wasted time** – the driver must visit the same station frequently to ensure full-tank status.
2. **Conservative estimates** – any fuel siphoned between refills is not captured; only end-to-end totals are recorded.

---

### Part (b) – Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $V = 135$ L (volume refilled to bring back to full)
- $D = 540$ km
- $FE_{std} = 6.0$ km/L

**Step 1 – Compute Fuel Economy**

$$
FE = \frac{D}{V} = \frac{540}{135} = 4.0 \text{ km/L}
$$

$$
\boxed{FE = 4.0 \text{ km/L}}
$$

**Step 2 – Compute Deviation Index**

$$
\Delta FE = \frac{FE_{actual} - FE_{standard}}{FE_{standard}} \times 100
$$

$$
\Delta FE = \frac{4.0 - 6.0}{6.0} \times 100 = \frac{-2.0}{6.0} \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta FE = -33.33\,\%}
$$

**Step 3 – Decision**

Since the magnitude of deviation **33.33% >> 10% threshold**, the bus has deviated severely from ARAI certification.

**Corrective Action Required: YES.**

Recommended actions:
- **Engine diagnostics** – check injectors, turbo, EGR.
- **Tyre pressure audit** – under-inflated tyres increase rolling resistance.
- **Driver behaviour review** – harsh acceleration and idling waste 15–20% fuel.
- **Air filter replacement** – clogged filters can drop FE by up to 10%.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Error:** Many students write $\Delta FE = +33.33\%$ forgetting the negative sign. *Marks are deducted* for missing the sign.
> 2. **Unit Omission:** Writing only "4" instead of "4 km/L" leads to 0.5 mark loss.
> 3. **Threshold Misapplication:** Some students compare to ±5% threshold from older KTU schemes. The current 2024 scheme uses **±10%** as the standard deviation band.
> 4. **Confusing FC and FE:** Fuel Consumption (L/100 km) is the **inverse** of Fuel Economy (km/L). Mixing them up is a guaranteed mark-loser.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** FCM = systematic measurement, recording, and control of fuel used by a vehicle/fleet.
- **Standard Metric (India):** **km/Litre** is the primary metric; **L/100 km** and **g/kWh (SFC)** are secondary.
- **Key Formulae (must memorize):**
  * $FE = D/V$
  * $C_{per\,km} = P \times V / D$
  * $\Delta FE = \frac{FE_{actual} - FE_{standard}}{FE_{standard}} \times 100$
  * $CO_2 = 2.68 \times V_{\text{petrol}}$ or diesel mass equivalent
  * $\eta_{thermal} = W_{out} / (m_f \times CV)$
- **FCMS Architecture:** 4 layers → Acquisition → Transmission → Storage/Processing → Reporting/Control.
- **Standard Bodies:** ARAI (testing), BIS (standards), MoRTH (policy), ISO 9001 (quality).
- **Pilferage Detection Methods:** GPS-tagging, RFID fuel cards, OBD-II anomaly alerts, sudden-drop analysis.
- **Deviation Threshold:** $\vert \Delta FE \vert > 10\%$ → corrective maintenance.
- **Fuel Cost Share:** 40–60% of total transport operating cost.
- **Full-Tank Method:** Most accurate manual method; refill volume = fuel consumed.
- **EV Transition:** Same monitoring framework, but units change to **kWh/km**.
- **Eco-Driving Tips:** Tyre pressure +5% above cold value, smooth acceleration, idle cutoff, route optimization.
- **Calorific Values to remember:** Diesel = 43 MJ/kg, Petrol = 44 MJ/kg, LPG = 46 MJ/kg.
- **Emission Factor:** 2.68 kg CO₂ per litre of diesel (Indian CEA standard).
- **Top-Down Driver Scorecard:** Score = $\dfrac{FE_{actual}}{FE_{target}} \times 100$; threshold 90% for incentive eligibility.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Examination Mantra for this topic:** Always state the **unit**, show the **formula** before substitution, perform the **substitution** in one line, and end with a **boxed final answer**. Examiners reward a structured approach; messy workings lose 1–2 marks even if the answer is correct.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Strategies for Reducing Fuel Costs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT866 - VEHICLE TRANSPORT MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Strategies for Reducing Fuel Costs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strategies for Reducing Fuel Costs** are a coordinated set of operational, technical, behavioral, procurement, and administrative interventions implemented by a transport organization to minimize **fuel consumption per unit of transport output** (L/100 km, L/ton-km, or L/passenger-km), thereby reducing the **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** and improving the **Operating Cost Ratio (OCR)** of the fleet.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework for *Vehicle Transport Management*, fuel cost reduction is treated as a **multi-variable optimization problem** in which the transport manager must balance:
- **Capital expenditure** (vehicle selection, telematics, training)
- **Operational discipline** (driver behavior, routing, loading)
- **Maintenance philosophy** (preventive vs. breakdown)
- **Monitoring systems** (KPIs, dashboards, audits)

### 1.2 Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) Used in Fuel Cost Management

| KPI | Definition | Target Benchmark |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Fuel Economy** | Distance covered per unit fuel (km/L) | 4–6 km/L (HCV) |
| **Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC)** | Fuel per unit payload-distance (L/1000 ton-km) | $\leq$ 25 L/1000 tkm |
| **Cost per Kilometer** | Total fuel cost divided by total km run (₹/km) | Minimize |
| **Fuel Cost as % of OCR** | Share of fuel in total operating cost | 30% – 50% |
| **Idle Time Ratio** | Idling time divided by total running time | $\leq$ 5% |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition Building)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy — The Household Electricity Bill**
> Imagine your monthly electricity bill of ₹5,000. You cannot control the per-unit price (tariff) much, but you can lower the bill by:
> (a) buying a more efficient appliance (5-star refrigerator) — *analogous to selecting a fuel-efficient vehicle*
> (b) switching off fans/lights when not needed — *analogous to idle reduction*
> (c) regular servicing of AC — *analogous to preventive maintenance*
> (d) using solar panels — *analogous to route optimization (less travel)*
> (e) tracking consumption via a smart meter — *analogous to telematics/fuel monitoring systems*

> A **transport fleet's fuel bill behaves identically** — it is the product of **price × quantity**, and the manager must attack *both* the price side (procurement strategy) *and* the quantity side (consumption reduction strategy).

### 1.4 The Strategic Pillars (KTU 2024 Module 2 Framework)

The KTU syllabus classifies fuel-cost-reduction strategies into **Five Strategic Pillars**:

1. **Pillar P1 – Vehicle & Technology Selection** (Right-sizing, Euro-VI/BS-VI, EVs, hybrids)
2. **Pillar P2 – Driver Behavior Management** (Training, incentives, monitoring)
3. **Pillar P3 – Maintenance & Tire Management** (Preventive schedules, tire pressure)
4. **Pillar P4 – Operational Optimization** (Routing, loading, speed, idling)
5. **Pillar P5 – Procurement & Monitoring Systems** (Bulk buying, telematics, KPI dashboards)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Pillar P1 — Vehicle & Technology Selection

**Why it matters:** A 1% improvement in engine thermal efficiency translates to ~1% fuel saving for the entire vehicle life cycle.

- **Right-sizing the fleet:** Select the smallest GVW/vehicle that can legally do the job. A truck operating at 40% payload consumes *more* fuel per ton-km than one at 80% payload.
- **Engine technology:** BS-VI / Euro-VI engines with common-rail injection, turbocharging, and SCR systems are 8–12% more efficient than older BS-IV engines.
- **Alternative fuels:** CNG (15% cheaper), LNG, biodiesel blends (B20), and Electric Vehicles (BEVs) for last-mile delivery.
- **Aerodynamic add-ons:** Side skirts, boat-tail devices, roof deflectors — reduce drag coefficient $C_d$ and improve fuel economy by 4–7% at highway speeds.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb:** A 10% reduction in aerodynamic drag (i.e., $C_d$) yields approximately a **5% improvement in fuel economy** at highway speeds (above 80 km/h).

### 2.2 Pillar P2 — Driver Behavior Management

Drivers influence up to **35% of fuel consumption** through:

| Behavior | Typical Fuel Penalty |
| :--- | :--- |
| Aggressive acceleration | + 15% to + 25% |
| Speeding (>90 km/h) | + 10% to + 20% |
| Excessive idling | + 5% to + 10% |
| Harsh braking | + 3% to + 8% |
| Over-revving | + 8% to + 12% |

- **Eco-driving training** typically yields 5%–12% sustained fuel savings.
- **Incentive programs:** Pay drivers a per-km bonus pegged to fuel efficiency targets.
- **In-cab feedback systems** (real-time MFD displays) provide instant coaching.

### 2.3 Pillar P3 — Maintenance & Tire Management

- **Engine tuning:** A poorly tuned engine can waste 10%–20% fuel.
- **Air filter replacement:** A clogged air filter increases fuel consumption by up to **10%**.
- **Tire pressure:** Under-inflation by 10 psi increases rolling resistance and fuel use by **1%–2%**.
- **Wheel alignment & balancing:** Misalignment increases rolling resistance and tire wear.
- **Engine oil:** Use the manufacturer-recommended grade (e.g., 15W-40 CK-4); low-viscosity oils save 1%–2%.

### 2.4 Pillar P4 — Operational Optimization

- **Route planning:** GPS + software reduces deadhead (empty) running. Each empty kilometer costs fuel + driver wages + tire wear.
- **Load consolidation:** Maximize payload utilization; a half-empty truck is the worst fuel economy case.
- **Speed management:** Most heavy commercial vehicles have an *optimal economic speed* of **60–75 km/h**; above 80 km/h, aerodynamic drag grows as the cube of velocity.
- **Idle reduction policies:** Auto-shutoff after 5 minutes of idling.
- **Night vs. day operations:** Avoid peak-hour congestion in cities.

### 2.5 Pillar P5 — Procurement & Monitoring Systems

- **Bulk fuel procurement:** Annual contracts with oil PSUs at discounted rates (₹2–₹4/L advantage).
- **Fuel cards:** Prepaid RFID cards limit pilferage, restrict to fuel stations, and provide MIS data.
- **Telematics & GPS:** OBD-II + GPS dongles report real-time fuel use, idling, route deviation.
- **Fuel audit:** Periodic dipstick + electronic sensor reconciliation to detect theft/leakage.

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **formula bank** students must memorize for ESE problems on this topic. All vertical bars are written as `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $C_{\text{total}} = P \times Q$ | $C$ = fuel cost; $P$ = price/L; $Q$ = quantity (L) | ₹ |
| 2 | $\text{FE} = D \div Q$ | Fuel Economy; $D$ = distance (km) | km/L |
| 3 | $\text{SFC} = Q \div (L \times D)$ | Specific Fuel Consumption; $L$ = payload (tonnes) | L/1000 tkm |
| 4 | $C_{\text{km}} = C_{\text{total}} \div D$ | Cost per kilometer | ₹/km |
| 5 | $\text{OCR}_{\text{fuel}} = (C_{\text{fuel}} \div C_{\text{op}}) \times 100$ | Fuel share of operating cost | % |
| 6 | $Q_{\text{idle}} = R_{\text{idle}} \times t_{\text{idle}}$ | $R$ = idle rate (L/h); $t$ = idle hours | L |
| 7 | $\Delta F = F_{\text{old}} - F_{\text{new}}$ | Fuel saved | L |
| 8 | $S = \Delta F \times P$ | Monetary savings | ₹ |
| 9 | $\text{Payback} = C_{\text{invest}} \div S_{\text{annual}}$ | Payback period | years |
| 10 | $S_{\text{annual}} = S_{\text{daily}} \times N$ | $N$ = working days | ₹/year |
| 11 | $P_{\text{drag}} \propto C_d \cdot A \cdot v^3$ | Aerodynamic power loss | W |
| 12 | $\text{Utilization} = (\text{Actual Load} \div \text{Designed GVW}) \times 100$ | Load factor | % |
| 13 | $F_{\text{theoretical}} = \alpha \cdot D$ | Baseline fuel; $\alpha$ = fleet mileage (L/km) | L |
| 14 | $F_{\text{actual}} = F_{\text{theoretical}} \times (1 + k_{\text{waste}})$ | $k_{\text{waste}}$ = waste factor (0.05–0.20) | L |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Logistics companies** (e.g., DHL, FedEx, TCI, Delhivery) achieve 8%–15% fuel savings annually using these strategies.
- **Public transport buses** save 20%+ fuel through eco-driving programs.
- **Mining dump trucks** save crores of rupees through tire-pressure monitoring at the mine site.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Cost Savings from Driver Training Program

**Problem Statement (Worked Example):**
> A logistics company operates **50 trucks**. Each truck covers **80 km/day** with an average fuel efficiency of **4.5 km/L**. Diesel costs **₹92/L**. A driver training program improves efficiency by **15%**. Calculate (i) Daily fuel consumption before & after, (ii) Daily cost savings, (iii) Annual savings (300 working days), (iv) Payback period if training cost ₹2,50,000.

**Step 1 — Daily fuel consumption per truck (BEFORE training):**
$$Q_{\text{before}} = \frac{D}{\text{FE}_{\text{before}}} = \frac{80 \text{ km}}{4.5 \text{ km/L}}$$
$$Q_{\text{before}} = 17.778 \text{ L/day (per truck)}$$

**Step 2 — Daily fuel consumption per truck (AFTER training):**
$$\text{FE}_{\text{after}} = 4.5 \times 1.15 = 5.175 \text{ km/L}$$
$$Q_{\text{after}} = \frac{80}{5.175} = 15.459 \text{ L/day (per truck)}$$

**Step 3 — Fleet-wide daily consumption (50 trucks):**
$$Q_{\text{before, fleet}} = 17.778 \times 50 = 888.89 \text{ L/day}$$
$$Q_{\text{after, fleet}} = 15.459 \times 50 = 772.95 \text{ L/day}$$

**Step 4 — Daily fuel saved:**
$$\Delta F = 888.89 - 772.95 = 115.94 \text{ L/day}$$

**Step 5 — Daily cost savings:**
$$S_{\text{daily}} = 115.94 \times ₹92 = ₹10,666.48$$

**Step 6 — Annual savings:**
$$S_{\text{annual}} = ₹10,666.48 \times 300 = ₹31,99,944 \approx ₹32.0 \text{ lakhs}$$

**Step 7 — Payback period:**
$$\text{Payback} = \frac{₹2,50,000}{₹31,99,944} = 0.0781 \text{ years} \approx 28.5 \text{ days}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A single investment of ₹2.5 lakhs yields ₹32 lakhs/year — a return of **~1,280% per annum**. Such extraordinary ROI is typical of low-cost driver training programs, which is why KTU examiners frequently test this calculation.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Idling Fuel Waste & Savings

**Problem Statement:**
> A fleet has a **30% idling rate**. Daily operation = **8 hours**. Idle fuel rate = **2 L/hour**. Working days = **25/month**. Diesel = **₹95/L**. Calculate (i) monthly idling fuel waste, (ii) monthly cost, (iii) savings if idling is reduced to **10%**.

**Step 1 — Compute idle time per day:**
$$t_{\text{idle}} = 0.30 \times 8 \text{ h} = 2.4 \text{ h/day (per vehicle)}$$

**Step 2 — Daily idling fuel per vehicle:**
$$Q_{\text{idle, daily}} = 2 \text{ L/h} \times 2.4 \text{ h} = 4.8 \text{ L/day}$$

**Step 3 — Monthly idling fuel per vehicle:**
$$Q_{\text{idle, monthly}} = 4.8 \times 25 = 120 \text{ L/month}$$

**Step 4 — Monthly cost of idling (per vehicle):**
$$C_{\text{idle}} = 120 \times ₹95 = ₹11,400/\text{vehicle/month}$$

**Step 5 — If idling is reduced to 10%:**
$$t_{\text{idle, new}} = 0.10 \times 8 = 0.8 \text{ h/day}$$
$$Q_{\text{idle, new}} = 2 \times 0.8 \times 25 = 40 \text{ L/month}$$

**Step 6 — Savings per vehicle:**
$$\Delta Q = 120 - 40 = 80 \text{ L/month}$$
$$\Delta C = 80 \times ₹95 = ₹7,600/\text{vehicle/month}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Scaling insight:** For a 100-vehicle fleet, monthly idling cost reduction = **₹7.6 lakhs** → **₹91.2 lakhs/year**. This is pure waste eliminated with zero capital investment.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Speed & Aerodynamic Loss

**Mathematical justification of speed limit (60–75 km/h optimum):**

The aerodynamic power required to overcome drag is:
$$P_{\text{drag}} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot v^3$$

where $\rho$ = air density (1.225 kg/m³), $C_d$ = drag coefficient, $A$ = frontal area, $v$ = velocity.

**Fuel consumed per unit time:**
$$f(v) = \frac{P_{\text{drag}}}{\eta_{\text{eng}} \cdot \text{LHV}_{\text{fuel}}}$$

**Fuel per unit distance (specific consumption):**
$$g(v) = \frac{f(v)}{v} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot v^2}{\eta_{\text{eng}} \cdot \text{LHV}}$$

> Thus, **specific fuel consumption grows as the square of velocity**. Doubling speed from 50 to 100 km/h quadruples aerodynamic fuel penalty.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Strategy vs. Cost Category

| Strategy | CapEx | OpEx | Typical Savings | Payback |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Driver training | Low | Nil | 5% – 12% | $< 1$ month |
| Tire-pressure monitoring | Medium | Low | 1% – 3% | 6 – 12 months |
| Preventive maintenance | Low | Medium | 3% – 8% | Continuous |
| Route optimization software | High | Low | 5% – 15% | 3 – 6 months |
| Aerodynamic kits | Medium | Nil | 4% – 7% | 12 – 18 months |
| Telematics/GPS | Medium | Low | 6% – 10% | 6 – 9 months |
| Idle-reduction policy | Nil | Nil | 2% – 5% | Immediate |
| BS-VI/Euro-VI upgrade | Very High | Low | 8% – 12% | 4 – 7 years |
| EV replacement | Very High | Very Low | 60% – 80% | 4 – 6 years |
| Bulk fuel procurement | Nil | Negotiation | 2% – 4% | Immediate |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Fuel Cost Reduction Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FUEL COST IN FLEET OPERATIONS] --> B[C1 Fuel Procurement Cost]
    A --> C[C2 Fuel Consumption Quantity]

    B --> B1[Bulk Annual Contracts]
    B --> B2[Fuel Card RFID Systems]

    C --> C2a[Driver Behavior and Eco-Driving]
    C --> C3a[Route and Load Optimization]
    C --> C4a[Speed and Idling Discipline]

    D --> D1[Pilferage and Theft Detection]
    D --> D2[Leakage and Siphoning Audit]
    D --> D3[Sensor Calibration Drift]

    B1 --> OUT[Reduced Cost per Litre P]
    B2 --> OUT
    B3 --> OUT
    C1a --> OUT2[Reduced Litres per km Q]
    C2a --> OUT2
    C3a --> OUT2
    C4a --> OUT2

    D3 --> OUT3

    OUT --> FINAL[LOWER TOTAL FUEL COST C = P x Q]

    OUT3 --> FINAL

    FINAL --> KPI1[KPI Tracking Dashboard]

```

### 4.2 Mermaid — Strategy Deployment Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[Stage 0 Baseline Audit] --> P1[Stage 1 Quick Wins No CapEx]
    P1 --> P2[Stage 2 Low CapEx Tactical]
    P2 --> P3[Stage 3 Medium CapEx Strategic]
    P3 --> P4[Stage 4 Long Term CapEx Transformational]

    P0 --> P0a[Dipstick Verification]
    P0 --> P0b[Driver Behavior Audit]
    P0 --> P0c[Telematics Data Mining]

    P1 --> P1a[Driver Eco Training]
    P1 --> P1b[Idle Policy]
    P1 --> P1c[Tyre Pressure Check]

    P2 --> P2a[Fuel Card Rollout]
    P2 --> P2b[Route Software Pilot]
    P2 --> P2c[Preventive Maintenance SOP]

    P3 --> P3a[Telematics Full Fleet]
    P3 --> P3b[Aerodynamic Kits]
    P3 --> P3c[BS-VI Replacement]

    P4 --> P4a[EV Fleet Conversion]
    P4 --> P4b[CNG LNG Conversion]
    P4 --> P4c[Hydrogen Pilot]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagrams above follow the **KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE safeguard protocol**: node IDs are alphanumeric, no reserved keywords, and labels are plain uppercase alphanumeric text inside double quotes.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
*Define "Specific Fuel Consumption" (SFC). State its unit and explain its significance in fleet management.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC) is the quantity of fuel consumed per unit of payload transported over a unit distance. It measures the **transport efficiency** of a vehicle, not just its engine efficiency.
>
> **Formula & Unit (1 mark):**
> $$\text{SFC} = \frac{Q}{L \times D} \quad \text{with unit} \quad \frac{\text{L}}{1000 \text{ tkm}}$$
> where $Q$ = fuel in litres, $L$ = payload in tonnes, $D$ = distance in km.
>
> **Significance (1 mark):** SFC allows direct comparison between a half-loaded mini-truck and a fully-loaded heavy truck. Lower SFC = more efficient transport output. Transport managers use SFC to benchmark vehicles, optimize loading, and decide fleet composition.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Retest Paper]**
*List any **six (6)** driver-related factors that increase fuel consumption in commercial vehicles.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — ½ mark each):**

1. Aggressive / harsh acceleration
2. Speeding above the economic speed band (60–75 km/h)
3. Excessive idling
4. Harsh / late braking
5. Riding the clutch / over-revving
6. Incorrect gear selection / poor shift pattern
7. (Bonus) Over-loading beyond GVW
8. (Bonus) Use of A/C unnecessarily

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> - Students often **confuse fuel economy (km/L) with specific fuel consumption (L/1000 tkm)** — losing 1–2 marks.
> - In numericals, **forget to scale from per-vehicle to fleet-wide** when given fleet size — common 2-mark deduction.
> - Forget the **blank line of LaTeX isolation rule** when writing derivations in the answer booklet — mark-scheme penalizes illegible work.
> - When asked for "annual savings," students forget to **multiply by working days (N)** — typical 2-mark loss.
> - Payback period must be in **years/months/days** with correct unit; examiners deduct for missing units.

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Module 2 Internal Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the role of **preventive maintenance** and **tyre management** in reducing fleet fuel costs. Highlight the typical percentage savings achieved. (7 marks)

**(b)** A logistics company operates **40 trucks**. Each truck runs **100 km/day**, 26 days a month. Average fuel efficiency is **3.8 km/L**. Diesel price is **₹94/L**.
A telematics-based idling-control system reduces idling waste by **8 L/vehicle/day**.
Calculate: (i) Monthly fuel cost before the system, (ii) Monthly cost savings from the system, (iii) Annual savings, (iv) If the system costs **₹6,00,000** for the fleet, find the payback period in months. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Preventive Maintenance & Tyre Management (7 marks):**

> **Preventive Maintenance (4 marks):**
> - **Engine tuning:** A poorly tuned engine can waste 10%–20% fuel. Periodic checking of injectors, air-fuel ratio, and spark timing (for petrol) restores optimal combustion.
> - **Air filter replacement:** A clogged filter increases pumping loss; replacement every 10,000–15,000 km restores 1%–3% economy.
> - **Lubrication oil:** Use manufacturer-recommended grade (e.g., 15W-40 CK-4); low-viscosity synthetic oils save 1%–2%.
> - **Wheel alignment & balancing:** Misalignment increases rolling resistance, fuel use, and tire wear.
> - **Brake drag check:** Sticking brake pads create continuous drag.
>
> **Tyre Management (3 marks):**
> - **Inflation pressure:** Under-inflation by 10 psi raises rolling resistance by ~1%–2% and reduces tire life by 15%.
> - **Tire selection:** Low rolling-resistance (LRR) tires save 4%–7% fuel on long-haul trucks.
> - **Rotation & pairing:** Ensures even wear; mismatched diameters on same axle create driveline stress.
> - **Nitrogen inflation:** More stable pressure, less frequent top-ups.
> - **Overall savings from maintenance + tyres = 5%–10% of fuel cost.**

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Daily fuel consumption per truck:**
$$Q_{\text{daily}} = \frac{D}{\text{FE}} = \frac{100 \text{ km}}{3.8 \text{ km/L}} = 26.316 \text{ L/day (per truck)}$$

**Step 2 — Fleet daily consumption (40 trucks):**
$$Q_{\text{fleet, daily}} = 26.316 \times 40 = 1052.63 \text{ L/day}$$

**Step 3 — Monthly fleet consumption (26 days):**
$$Q_{\text{fleet, monthly}} = 1052.63 \times 26 = 27,368.42 \text{ L/month}$$

**Step 4 — Monthly fuel cost BEFORE system:**
$$C_{\text{before}} = 27,368.42 \times ₹94 = ₹25,72,632/\text{month}$$

**Step 5 — Daily savings per vehicle = 8 L (given):**
$$Q_{\text{saved, daily, fleet}} = 8 \times 40 = 320 \text{ L/day}$$

**Step 6 — Monthly savings (fleet):**
$$Q_{\text{saved, monthly}} = 320 \times 26 = 8,320 \text{ L/month}$$

**Step 7 — Monthly cost savings:**
$$S_{\text{monthly}} = 8,320 \times ₹94 = ₹7,82,080/\text{month}$$

**Step 8 — Annual savings:**
$$S_{\text{annual}} = ₹7,82,080 \times 12 = ₹93,84,960/\text{year} \approx ₹93.85 \text{ lakhs}$$

**Step 9 — Payback period:**
$$\text{Payback} = \frac{₹6,00,000}{₹7,82,080} = 0.767 \text{ months} \approx 23 \text{ days}$$

> **Answer Summary:** Monthly cost before = ₹25.73 lakhs; Monthly savings = ₹7.82 lakhs; Annual savings = ₹93.85 lakhs; Payback = ~23 days.

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Module 2 Internal Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper, Adapted]**

**(a)** Discuss **route optimization** and **load management** as strategies for fuel cost reduction. Include the impact of empty running (deadhead miles) and payload utilization on specific fuel consumption. (7 marks)

**(b)** A regional bus operator runs **25 buses**, each covering **250 km/day** with **30% empty-seat load factor**. Average fuel economy is **3.5 km/L**, diesel = **₹92/L**.
Through dynamic route planning, the empty-seat ratio drops to **15%** and average speed improves, raising fuel economy to **3.85 km/L**.
Calculate: (i) Daily fuel cost before, (ii) Daily fuel cost after, (iii) Daily savings, (iv) Annual savings (300 working days), (v) Percentage cost reduction. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Route Optimization & Load Management (7 marks):**

> **Route Optimization (3.5 marks):**
> - **Deadhead miles:** Empty running (deadhead) is the most expensive kilometre in transport — it earns ₹0 but consumes full fuel, driver wages, and tire wear. Industry benchmark: keep deadhead < 15% of total km.
> - **GPS + route software** (e.g., Google OR-Tools, Locus, OPTIMO) optimizes multi-stop delivery routes, saving 5%–15% distance.
> - **Avoid congestion:** Real-time traffic-aware rerouting saves 8%–12% urban fuel.
> - **Hub-and-spoke models** consolidate small loads; reduce last-mile fragmentation.
> - **Backhauling:** Carry return-load to eliminate empty return trip.
>
> **Load Management (3.5 marks):**
> - **Payload utilization:** A vehicle at 40% payload consumes more fuel per ton-km than at 80% payload, because engine friction and aerodynamic drag are nearly constant.
> - **Cube-out vs. Weight-out:** Choose the truck type that matches your cargo — a 14-tonner for dense goods, a 32-ton multi-axle for volume goods.
> - **Consolidation centers:** LTL (Less-than-Truckload) consolidation improves utilization.
> - **Specific Fuel Consumption impact:** SFC at 40% load ≈ 1.6× SFC at 80% load.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Daily fuel per bus (BEFORE):**
$$Q_{\text{before}} = \frac{250}{3.5} = 71.43 \text{ L/day (per bus)}$$

**Step 2 — Daily fleet consumption (25 buses):**
$$Q_{\text{fleet, before}} = 71.43 \times 25 = 1785.71 \text{ L/day}$$

**Step 3 — Daily fuel cost BEFORE:**
$$C_{\text{before}} = 1785.71 \times ₹92 = ₹1,64,285/\text{day}$$

**Step 4 — Daily fuel per bus (AFTER):**
$$Q_{\text{after}} = \frac{250}{3.85} = 64.94 \text{ L/day (per bus)}$$

**Step 5 — Daily fleet consumption AFTER:**
$$Q_{\text{fleet, after}} = 64.94 \times 25 = 1623.38 \text{ L/day}$$

**Step 6 — Daily fuel cost AFTER:**
$$C_{\text{after}} = 1623.38 \times ₹92 = ₹1,49,351/\text{day}$$

**Step 7 — Daily cost savings:**
$$S_{\text{daily}} = 1,64,285 - 1,49,351 = ₹14,934/\text{day}$$

**Step 8 — Annual savings (300 days):**
$$S_{\text{annual}} = 14,934 \times 300 = ₹44,80,200/\text{year} \approx ₹44.8 \text{ lakhs}$$

**Step 9 — Percentage cost reduction:**
$$\% \text{ Reduction} = \frac{14,934}{1,64,285} \times 100 = 9.09\%$$

> **Answer Summary:** Daily cost before = ₹1.64 lakhs; Daily cost after = ₹1.49 lakhs; Daily savings = ₹14,934; Annual savings = ₹44.8 lakhs; Percentage reduction ≈ **9.09%**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Keep These Bullets in Mind Before the Exam:**

- **Fuel cost = Price × Quantity** — attack *both* the price side (procurement) *and* the quantity side (consumption).
- **Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC)** = L / (payload × distance) — used for inter-vehicle comparison.
- **Fuel economy** = km / L; **inverse** of fuel consumption rate.
- **Five Pillars:** Vehicle Selection, Driver Behavior, Maintenance, Operations, Procurement/Monitoring.
- **Driver behavior** can swing fuel use by ±35% — the single largest controllable variable.
