- Baseline cost per km without telematics: $c_0$ (in ₹/km).
- Telematics subscription cost per vehicle per year: $S$ (in ₹/year).
- Telematics reduces fuel consumption by $\alpha$% and maintenance cost by $\beta$%.

**Step 1 — Baseline Annual Cost (per vehicle):**

$$
C_{0} = c_{0} \times D
$$

This is the total annual operating cost of a single vehicle without any digital intervention.

**Step 2 — Savings Achieved:**

Fuel cost typically forms $f$ fraction of the baseline cost. So fuel savings:

$$
S_{fuel} = f \times c_{0} \times D \times \dfrac{\alpha}{100}
$$

Maintenance cost forms $m$ fraction of baseline cost ($f + m + \text{other} = 1$). Maintenance savings:

$$
S_{maint} = m \times c_{0} \times D \times \dfrac{\beta}{100}
$$

**Step 3 — Total Savings per Vehicle per Year:**

$$
S_{total} = c_{0} \times D \times \dfrac{(f \alpha + m \beta)}{100}
$$

**Step 4 — Net Annual Cost (After Telematics):**

$$
C_{net} = C_{0} - S_{total} + S
$$

$$
C_{net} = c_{0} D - c_{0} D \dfrac{(f \alpha + m \beta)}{100} + S
$$

**Step 5 — New Cost per Kilometre:**

$$
c_{new} = \dfrac{C_{net}}{D} = c_{0} \left[ 1 - \dfrac{f \alpha + m \beta}{100} \right] + \dfrac{S}{D}
$$

**Step 6 — Percentage Reduction in Cost-per-km:**

$$
\Delta c \% = \dfrac{c_{0} - c_{new}}{c_{0}} \times 100
$$

Substituting and simplifying:

$$
\Delta c \% = \dfrac{f \alpha + m \beta}{100} - \dfrac{S}{c_{0} D} \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\Delta c \% \approx (f \alpha + m \beta) - \dfrac{100 \, S}{c_{0} D}\;}
$$

**Interpretation:** A telematics system is profitable only when the savings term $(f \alpha + m \beta)$ exceeds the telematics cost ratio $\dfrac{100 S}{c_{0} D}$.

**Step 7 — Numerical Example (Typical KTU-style problem):**

Let:

- $c_{0}$ = ₹ 12 / km
- $D$ = 60,000 km/year
- $f$ = 0.40 (fuel share)
- $m$ = 0.20 (maintenance share)
- $\alpha$ = 8% fuel saving
- $\beta$ = 15% maintenance saving
- $S$ = ₹ 6,000/year

Then:

- $f \alpha + m \beta = (0.40 \times 8) + (0.20 \times 15) = 3.2 + 3.0 = 6.2$
- $\dfrac{100 S}{c_{0} D} = \dfrac{100 \times 6000}{12 \times 60000} = \dfrac{600000}{720000} = 0.833$

Therefore:

$$
\Delta c \% = 6.2 - 0.833 \approx 5.37\%
$$

**Conclusion:** Telematics reduces the cost per km by approximately **5.37%**. For a fleet of $N$ = 50 vehicles, annual savings ≈ $50 \times 60000 \times 12 \times 0.0537 \approx$ **₹ 19.3 lakh/year**.

### 3.2 Derivation: Platooning Fuel Saving using Aerodynamic Drag

**Given:**

- Air density $\rho$ = 1.225 kg/m³
- Vehicle speed $v$ = 80 km/h = 22.22 m/s
- Drag coefficient $C_{d}$ = 0.6 (typical truck)
- Frontal area $A$ = 10 m²

**Step 1 — Drag Force on a Solo Truck:**

$$
F_{d} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho v^{2} C_{d} A
$$

Plugging values:

$$
F_{d} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times (22.22)^{2} \times 0.6 \times 10
$$

$$
F_{d} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 493.83 \times 0.6 \times 10
$$

$$
F_{d} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 493.83 \times 6
$$

$$
F_{d} \approx 1,815 \; \text{N}
$$

**Step 2 — Power Required to Overcome Drag:**

$$
P_{d} = F_{d} \times v = 1815 \times 22.22 \approx 40{,}330 \; \text{W} \approx 40.3 \; \text{kW}
$$

**Step 3 — Effect of Platooning (Drafting):**

In a platoon with 10 m gap, the trailing truck's effective $C_{d}$ drops to roughly $C_{d}' \approx 0.3$ (50% reduction). So:

$$
F_{d}' = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 493.83 \times 0.3 \times 10 \approx 907 \; \text{N}
$$

$$
P_{d}' = 907 \times 22.22 \approx 20{,}155 \; \text{W} \approx 20.2 \; \text{kW}
$$

**Step 4 — Fuel Saving Percentage:**

$$
S_{p} = \dfrac{F_{d} - F_{d}'}{F_{d}} \times 100 = \dfrac{1815 - 907}{1815} \times 100 \approx 50\% \; \text{(in drag power)}
$$

In practice, total fuel saving for the trailing truck is **~10–15%** because drag is only one component of total resistance (along with rolling resistance, grade, drivetrain losses).

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Emerging Technologies

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is a **favourite KTU question pattern** ("Compare and contrast…"). Memorise the key advantages and limitations.

| Technology | Primary Benefit | Primary Limitation | Maturity (TRL 1–9) | TCO Impact |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **GPS Telematics** | Real-time tracking, theft recovery | Privacy concerns, signal loss in tunnels | TRL 9 (deployed) | 5–10% cost reduction |
| **AI/ML Predictive Maintenance** | Reduces breakdowns 30–50% | Requires large historical data | TRL 7–8 | 8–12% maintenance saving |
| **Big Data Analytics** | Demand forecasting, optimisation | Data quality, storage cost | TRL 8 | 3–7% operational saving |
| **EV Fleet** | Zero tailpipe emission, lower fuel cost | High upfront cost, charging infra | TRL 8–9 | 15–25% lifecycle saving |
| **Hydrogen FCEV** | Long range, fast refuel | Hydrogen production, storage | TRL 6–7 | Comparable to BEV (future) |
| **Autonomous Trucks (L4)** | 24×7 operation, no driver fatigue | Regulation, liability, ethics | TRL 6–7 | 20–40% labour saving |
| **Truck Platooning** | 10–15% fuel saving | Convoy regulations, V2V standards | TRL 7 | 5–10% fuel saving |
| **Blockchain Logistics** | Immutable cargo record | Energy use, transaction speed | TRL 5–6 | Trust improvement, not direct cost |
| **ADAS (L1–L2)** | Safety, insurance discount | Driver over-reliance | TRL 9 | 10–20% accident cost cut |
| **5G Connectivity** | Ultra-low latency (~1 ms) | Network coverage gaps | TRL 8 | Enables V2X |

> **TRL** = Technology Readiness Level (1 = basic research, 9 = fully deployed).

### 3.4 Worked Example: Route Optimisation with Cost-Weighted Dijkstra

A delivery van must travel from **Depot (A)** to **Customer (F)** through a network of roads. The numbers on each edge represent **time in minutes** (which is the cost in this problem). Use Dijkstra's algorithm to find the **shortest-time route**.

| Edge | A–B | A–C | B–D | C–D | B–E | D–E | C–F | D–F | E–F |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Time (min) | 4 | 2 | 5 | 8 | 10 | 2 | 4 | 7 | 6 |

**Step 1 — Initialise distances from A:**

- $d(A) = 0$
- $d(B) = \infty, \; d(C) = \infty, \; d(D) = \infty, \; d(E) = \infty, \; d(F) = \infty$

**Step 2 — Visit A.** Update neighbours:

- $d(B) = 0 + 4 = 4$, path A–B
- $d(C) = 0 + 2 = 2$, path A–C

**Step 3 — Visit C (smallest tentative).** Update neighbours:

- $d(D) = \min(\infty, 2 + 8) = 10$, path A–C–D
- $d(F) = \min(\infty, 2 + 4) = 6$, path A–C–F

**Step 4 — Visit B (next smallest, = 4).** Update neighbours:

- $d(D) = \min(10, 4 + 5) = 9$, path A–B–D
- $d(E) = \min(\infty, 4 + 10) = 14$, path A–B–E

**Step 5 — Visit F (= 6).** Destination reached, but continue to confirm optimum.

- $d(E) = \min(14, 6 + ?)$ — no direct edge C–F to E, skip.

**Step 6 — Visit D (= 9).** Update neighbours:

- $d(E) = \min(14, 9 + 2) = 11$, path A–B–D–E
- $d(F) = \min(6, 9 + 7) = 6$ (unchanged)

**Step 7 — Visit E (= 11).** Update neighbours:

- $d(F) = \min(6, 11 + 6) = 6$ (unchanged)

**Final Shortest Route: A → C → F, Total Time = 6 minutes.**

> This type of question (graph-based route optimisation) appears in KTU 2024 ESE papers as a sub-part.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram A — Architecture of a Modern Telematics-Enabled Fleet Management System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    V1[Vehicle 1] -->|GPS RPM Fuel| OBU1[On Board Unit]
    V2[Vehicle 2] -->|GPS RPM Fuel| OBU2[On Board Unit]
    V3[Vehicle N] -->|OBUN[On Board Unit]
    OBU1 -->|4G 5G Cellular| NET[Wireless Network]
    OBU2 --> NET
    OBUN --> NET
    NET --> CLOUD[Cloud Server]
    CLOUD --> DB[(Time Series Database)]
    DB --> AI[AI ML Engine]
    AI --> PRED[Predictive Insights]
    AI --> ROUTE[Route Optimiser]
    PRED --> DASH[Manager Dashboard]
    ROUTE --> DASH
    DASH -->|Alerts| MGR[Fleet Manager]
    DASH -->|App| DRV[Driver Mobile App]
    CLOUD -->|API| REG[Regulator AIS 140]
```

**Explanation of Flow:**

- Multiple vehicles feed raw sensor data to their on-board units.
- The OBU transmits the data over 4G/5G to a cloud server.
- The cloud server stores time-series data in a database.
- An AI/ML engine processes this data for predictive maintenance and route optimisation.
- Results are displayed on a manager dashboard and pushed to a driver mobile app.
- Regulatory data (e.g., AIS-140) is shared with government servers via APIs.

### 4.2 Diagram B — Functional Block Diagram of an Autonomous Vehicle Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SENS[Sensors Layer] --> FUS[Sensor Fusion Engine]
    FUS --> PERC[Perception Module]
    PERC --> PRED[Prediction Module]
    PRED --> PLAN[Path Planning Module]
    PLAN --> CTRL[Vehicle Control Actuators]
    SENS2[LiDAR] --> SENS
    SENS3[Cameras] --> SENS
    SENS4[Radar] --> SENS
    SENS5[GPS IMU] --> SENS
    SENS6[V2X Receiver] --> SENS
    HDMAP[HD Map Database] --> PLAN
    CTRL --> ACT1[Brake Throttle Steering]
```

**Explanation of Flow:**

- The **Sensors Layer** collects raw data from LiDAR, cameras, radar, GPS/IMU, and V2X.
- The **Sensor Fusion Engine** combines them into a single coherent view of the world.
- The **Perception Module** identifies objects (vehicles, pedestrians, signs).
- The **Prediction Module** anticipates their future motion.
- The **Path Planning Module** consults HD maps and decides a safe trajectory.
- The **Control Module** sends commands to brake, throttle, and steering actuators.

### 4.3 Diagram C — Decision Flow for Selecting EV vs ICE in a Fleet

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Fleet Operator Decision] --> Q1{Daily Route Distance}
    Q1 -->|Less than 200 km| Q2{Is Charging Infra Available}
    Q1 -->|More than 200 km| Q3{Cargo Weight}
    Q2 -->|Yes| EV[Choose BEV]
    Q2 -->|No| PHEV[Choose PHEV or CNG]
    Q3 -->|Heavy FCEV Suitable| FCEV[Choose FCEV]
    Q3 -->|Light to Medium| Q4{Charging Available at Depot}
    Q4 -->|Yes| EV2[Choose BEV Long Range]
    Q4 -->|No| ICE[Choose Modern BS6 Diesel]
```

**Explanation of Flow:**

- The decision starts with **daily route distance** — a critical filter.
- Short-haul urban routes favour BEVs if charging is available.
- Long-haul heavy routes may need FCEVs (hydrogen) since BEVs are still range-limited.
- If no charging is available, the operator should fall back to PHEV, CNG, or modern BS6 ICE.

### 4.4 Diagram D — Comparative Flow of Traditional vs Smart Fleet Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TRAD[Traditional Workflow]
    T1[Paper Trip Sheet] --> T2[Manual Log]
    T2 --> T3[Reactive Maintenance]
    T3 --> T4[Monthly Report]
    end
    subgraph SMART[Smart Workflow]
    S1[Digital Trip Plan] --> S2[Real Time Telematics]
    S2 --> S3[Predictive AI Maintenance]
    S3 --> S4[Live Dashboard Alerts]
    S4 --> S5[Auto Regulatory Filing]
    end
```

**Explanation of Flow:**

- The **Traditional Workflow** is slow, manual, and reactive.
- The **Smart Workflow** is digital, continuous, predictive, and automated — saving both time and cost.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**
*Define the term "Telematics" as applied to vehicle fleet management. List any four components of a typical telematics system.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Telematics is the integrated use of telecommunications and informatics to capture, transmit, and analyse vehicle data over long distances for fleet monitoring and control.
>
> **Four Components (2 marks — ½ mark each):**
> 1. **GPS Receiver** — for real-time location tracking.
> 2. **OBD-II / CAN Bus Interface** — for engine and vehicle health data.
> 3. **GSM/4G/5G Communication Module** — for data transmission to the cloud.
> 4. **Cloud Server + Dashboard** — for storage, analytics, and visualisation.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is platooning in the context of commercial vehicle fleets? State any two benefits.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Platooning is the technique of operating two or more trucks in a closely-spaced convoy using Vehicle-to-Vehicle (V2V) communication and automated control, where the lead truck is human-driven and the following trucks autonomously maintain speed, braking, and steering.
>
> **Two Benefits (1.5 marks — ¾ mark each):**
> 1. **Fuel Saving (10–15%)** — reduced aerodynamic drag due to drafting.
> 2. **Higher Road Throughput** — more vehicles occupy less road space safely.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - Students often **forget to draw diagrams** in 14-mark questions — this costs **1–2 marks** even if the answer is correct.
> - For AI/IoT/Platooning questions, examiners expect **at least one labelled diagram or block diagram**.
> - Always include **real-world examples** (e.g., AIS-140 in India, FASTag, Waymo) — they earn **extra impression marks**.
> - Do **not** write generic answers from Google; KTU expects **syllabus-specific terminology** (TCO, OEE, AIS-140, BS6, TRL).

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the **architecture of a modern IoT-based fleet management system** with a neat block diagram. Describe the function of **each layer** in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss **Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning applications** in fleet management. Explain with examples how **predictive maintenance** and **route optimisation** are achieved using AI/ML. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) IoT Architecture (7 Marks):**

A modern IoT-based fleet management system has **four functional layers** as described below (a well-labelled block diagram must be drawn — 2 marks reserved for the diagram):

1. **Perception / Sensing Layer (1.5 marks):**
   - GPS receivers capture latitude, longitude, speed, and heading (refresh rate ~1 Hz).
   - OBD-II or CAN-bus interface captures engine RPM, throttle position, coolant temperature, fault codes (DTCs).
   - Accelerometer/gyroscope detects harsh braking, acceleration, cornering, and crashes.
   - Fuel-level sensor and tyre-pressure monitoring system (TPMS) sensors.
   - In-cabin dashcam for driver behaviour (with edge-AI for drowsiness detection).

2. **Network / Communication Layer (1.5 marks):**
   - Cellular networks (4G LTE, evolving to 5G) for primary data uplink.
   - Satellite communication (Iridium, Inmarsat) for fleets operating in remote areas (mining, defence, cross-border).
   - Short-range: Bluetooth for TPMS, Wi-Fi for garage diagnostics.
   - V2X (Dedicated Short-Range Communication — DSRC) for vehicle-to-infrastructure alerts.

3. **Processing / Cloud Layer (1.5 marks):**
   - Cloud servers (AWS IoT Core, Azure IoT Hub) receive the data.
   - Time-series databases (InfluxDB, TimescaleDB) store the data.
   - AI/ML engines (TensorFlow, PyTorch) run predictive models.
   - Edge computing nodes in the vehicle process latency-critical tasks (collision avoidance).

4. **Application Layer (1.5 marks):**
   - Web dashboard for the fleet manager showing live map, KPIs, alerts.
   - Mobile app for drivers showing assigned routes, fuel stops, rest areas.
   - API integration with ERP (SAP, Oracle), regulatory portals (AIS-140), and toll systems (FASTag).
   - Automated report generation (daily, weekly, monthly) for compliance and analytics.

**(b) AI/ML Applications (7 Marks):**

**Predictive Maintenance (3.5 marks):**

- **What it is:** Using historical sensor data (vibration, temperature, oil quality) to predict component failure *before* it happens.
- **How it works:** A supervised ML model (e.g., **Random Forest**, **XGBoost**, or **LSTM recurrent neural network** for time-series) is trained on labelled data — past failures and operating conditions.
- **Example (1 mark):** An Indian logistics company uses vibration data from wheel bearings to predict failure **2–3 weeks in advance**, with **85% accuracy**. This reduces unexpected breakdowns by **40%** and maintenance cost by **15%**.

**Route Optimisation (3.5 marks):**

- **What it is:** Finding the **minimum-cost, minimum-time** route for a fleet of vehicles serving multiple destinations.
- **How it works:** Algorithms like **Dijkstra's** (single-vehicle shortest path), **A* search**, **Genetic Algorithms (GA)**, and **Ant Colony Optimisation (ACO)** are used for complex multi-stop, multi-vehicle problems (Vehicle Routing Problem — VRP).
- **Modern twist:** ML models predict **traffic congestion, weather, and road closures** in real time and dynamically re-route vehicles.
- **Example (1 mark):** Amazon India uses AI-driven route optimisation to reduce last-mile delivery time by **20%** and fuel consumption by **10%**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** What are **Electric Vehicles (EVs)**? Explain the **different types of EVs (BEV, HEV, PHEV, FCEV)** with neat sketches. Discuss the **challenges and opportunities** of EV adoption in commercial fleet operations in India. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write notes on: **(i) Autonomous Vehicles and SAE Levels**, **(ii) Truck Platooning**, **(iii) Blockchain in Fleet Management**, **(iv) 5G and V2X Communication**. **(7 Marks — 1.75 each)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Electric Vehicles (7 Marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** An Electric Vehicle (EV) is a vehicle whose drivetrain is powered partially or fully by an electric motor drawing current from a rechargeable battery or fuel cell.

**Types (3 marks):**

- **BEV (Battery Electric Vehicle):** Powered **100% by electricity** stored in a battery. No internal combustion engine. Examples: Tata Nexon EV, Tesla Model 3.
- **HEV (Hybrid Electric Vehicle):** Combines an ICE with a small battery + electric motor. **Cannot be plugged in**; battery is charged by regenerative braking. Example: Toyota Camry Hybrid.
- **PHEV (Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicle):** Larger battery that **can be charged from an external source**; can run 30–80 km on electric-only mode, after which the ICE takes over. Example: Volvo XC90 T8.
- **FCEV (Fuel Cell Electric Vehicle):** Generates electricity on-board from **hydrogen** via a fuel cell; emits only water vapour. Example: Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo.

**Challenges in India (1.5 marks):**

- High upfront cost of EVs (subsidies under **FAME-II** scheme partially offset this).
- Inadequate **public charging infrastructure**, especially on highways.
- Range anxiety, especially for long-haul trucks.
- Battery supply chain (lithium, cobalt) is import-dependent.

**Opportunities (1.5 marks):**

- Lowest **total cost of ownership (TCO)** for urban last-mile delivery vans — already at parity with diesel.
- Government push: **FAME-II, PLI scheme for ACC batteries, state EV policies**.
- Reduced dependence on imported crude oil.
- Lower carbon footprint; supports India's **net-zero 2070** commitment.

**(b) Short Notes (7 Marks):**

**(i) Autonomous Vehicles and SAE Levels (1.75 marks):**
The SAE J3016 standard defines **6 levels** of driving automation, from **Level 0** (no automation) to **Level 5** (full automation in all conditions). Levels 1–2 require constant driver supervision; Levels 3–5 progressively reduce or eliminate driver responsibility. Current road-legal commercial vehicles are mostly at **Level 2** (Adaptive Cruise Control + Lane Keep Assist). **Waymo** operates Level 4 robotaxis in Phoenix, USA.

**(ii) Truck Platooning (1.75 marks):**
Two or more trucks travel in close convoy using **V2V communication** and automated longitudinal control. The lead truck is human-driven; the trailing trucks follow autonomously. Benefits: **10–15% fuel saving** (due to drafting), higher road capacity, reduced driver fatigue. Challenges: regulatory acceptance, cybersecurity, and the need for standardised V2V protocols.

**(iii) Blockchain in Fleet Management (1.75 marks):**
Blockchain provides a **distributed, immutable, tamper-proof ledger** of transactions. In fleet management, it is used for **chain-of-custody** of high-value cargo, **smart-contract-based** automatic payments on delivery, **vehicle history ledgers** (preventing odometer fraud), and **carbon-credit trading** for EV fleets.

**(iv) 5G and V2X Communication (1.75 marks):**
**5G** offers ultra-low latency (~1 ms), high bandwidth (1–10 Gbps), and massive device connectivity — enabling **V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything)** communication: V2V (vehicle-to-vehicle), V2I (vehicle-to-infrastructure), V2P (vehicle-to-pedestrian), V2N (vehicle-to-network). This allows real-time hazard warnings, coordinated platooning, and smart traffic-light prioritisation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD REVISION NOTES — Read 5 minutes before the exam.**

- ✅ **Telematics = Telecom + Informatics**; 4 layers: Sensing, Network, Cloud, Application.
- ✅ **AIS-140** is the Indian mandate for GPS + emergency button in public-service vehicles.
- ✅ **OEE = Availability × Performance × Quality**; benchmark is **≥ 85%**.
- ✅ **EV Types:** BEV (pure), HEV (no plug), PHEV (plug + ICE), FCEV (hydrogen).
- ✅ **SAE Levels:** 0 → 5; **Level 3** is the first where the car drives itself in some conditions.
- ✅ **Platooning** saves **10–15% fuel** via drafting; trailing truck's $C_{d}$ drops ~50%.
- ✅ **TCO Crossover** for EVs vs ICE has already happened for last-mile delivery vans in India.
- ✅ **5V's of Big Data:** Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, Value.
- ✅ **5G latency** is **~1 ms**, enabling real-time V2X.
- ✅ **Blockchain** = distributed immutable ledger; used for cargo chain-of-custody and smart contracts.
- ✅ **Cybersecurity threats:** GPS spoofing, CAN bus injection, ransomware — defend with AES-256, PKI, MFA.
- ✅ **TRL scale** goes from **1 (basic research)** to **9 (fully deployed)**.
- ✅ **Indian government schemes to know:** FAME-II, PLI for ACC batteries, AIS-140, BS6 emission norms, FASTag.
- ✅ **Alternative fuels:** CNG, LNG, Hydrogen, Biodiesel, Ethanol (E20 target by 2025), e-fuels.
- ✅ **Dijkstra's algorithm** finds the **shortest path** in a weighted graph; used for single-vehicle route optimisation.
- ✅ **Sensor stack for autonomous vehicles:** LiDAR + Camera + Radar + GPS/IMU + V2X → Sensor Fusion.

> **Final Tip:** When answering a 14-mark question, always structure as **Definition → Diagram → Explanation → Example → Conclusion**. KTU examiners reward this flow with full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Studies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT866 - VEHICLE TRANSPORT MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Case Studies # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Vehicle Transport Management (PEMUT866)**, a *Case Study* is an empirical inquiry method that investigates a contemporary transportation phenomenon (a fleet operation, a public transit system, a logistics policy, or a vehicle maintenance programme) in its real-world context, especially when the boundaries between phenomenon and context are not clearly evident. Under the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned)**, Case Studies form the analytical capstone of Module 3, requiring students to apply theoretical principles of fleet economics, route optimization, transport regulation, and demand-supply dynamics to dissect a real or simulated business/policy scenario and recommend evidence-based managerial action. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Spirit):** > *A case study is a problem-based learning tool presented in a narrative form that requires the student to identify the underlying transport management problem, evaluate alternatives using quantitative techniques (cost-volume analysis, break-even, NPV, payback), and propose a defensible managerial recommendation aligned with regulatory, economic, and operational constraints.* ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Medical Diagnosis" Approach Think of a transport case study exactly like a doctor's diagnosis of a patient: - **Symptoms** = Late deliveries, high fuel bills, idle vehicles, customer complaints (these are the *surface issues* you see in the case description). - **Medical History** = The company's past fleet age, driver policies, route history (the *contextual data* given). - **Diagnostic Tests** = SWOT, PESTLE, Break-Even Analysis, Cost-Benefit Ratio, NPV (the *analytical frameworks* you must apply). - **Prescription** = Your managerial recommendation (the *solution* the examiner awards marks for). Just as no two patients are identical, no two case studies have a single "textbook answer." The examiner awards marks for the **rigour of the diagnostic process** and the **defensibility of the prescription**, not for memorized facts. > [!NOTE] > **Key Vocabulary Students Must Master for Module 3 Case Studies:** > - **Stakeholder Mapping** — identifying who wins, who loses, and who decides. > - **Decision Criterion** — the metric (cost, time, emissions, NPV) used to compare alternatives. > - **Constraints** — non-negotiable limits (budget, regulation, fleet capacity). > - **Trade-off** — the inevitable sacrifice between competing objectives. ## 1.3 Why Case Studies in Vehicle Transport Management? Case studies bridge the gap between the quantitative formulae taught in Modules 1 & 2 (vehicle cost of ownership, replacement analysis, route scheduling) and the messy reality of Indian transport operations — toll policies, GST on diesel, BS-VI emission norms, RTO compliance, driver unions, and seasonal demand swings. They test **Course Outcomes (CO3, CO4, CO5)** under Bloom's levels *Apply*, *Analyse*, and *Evaluate*. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomy of a Standard KTU Case Study A typical Module 3 case study in this course has the following layered structure: 1. **Setting the Scene** (≈ 150 words) — Company/agency name, location, fleet size, business model. 2. **The Operational Data Block** — Tables giving vehicle counts, mileage, fuel cost, maintenance spend, load factor. 3. **The Trigger Event** — A specific crisis: a new toll policy, a competitor launch, a regulatory deadline, a fuel-price shock. 4. **The Decision Point** — A "should we / shouldn't we" question (e.g., *"Should ABC Logistics replace its 5-year-old trucks?"*). 5. **Hidden Constraints** — Information you must infer (working capital limit, debt covenants, driver availability). > [!IMPORTANT] > **The 80/20 Rule of Case Study Mark Allocation:** > - **80% of marks** come from correctly applying the *frameworks and formulae* from earlier modules. > - **20% of marks** come from the *quality of managerial insight* and the *clarity of recommendation*. > Memorising frameworks without numbers will fail you. Crunching numbers without recommendation will also fail you. ## 2.2 Analytical Frameworks — The Student's Toolbox A KTU case study demands you to **mix-and-match** the following frameworks. Never use just one in isolation. ### A. SWOT Analysis (Strategic Diagnosis) | Quadrant | Stands For | In a Transport Context | |---|---|---| | **S** | Strengths | In-house workshop, experienced drivers, fuel bulk-purchase tie-up | | **W** | Weaknesses | Old fleet, high breakdown rate, single-route dependency | | **O** | Opportunities | PLI scheme for EVs, dedicated freight corridor, toll discounts for green vehicles | | **T** | Threats | New GST slabs, RTO compliance cost, app-based aggregators | ### B. PESTLE Analysis (Macro-Environmental Scan) Useful when the case has a policy/regulatory trigger (e.g., a new vehicle scrappage policy). | Factor | Sample Question to Ask | |---|---| | **P**olitical | Will a change in state government affect subsidy? | | **E**conomic | How does a ₹5/litre diesel hike change the break-even load factor? | | **S**ocial | Are customers willing to pay premium for green delivery? | | **T**echnological | Will GPS-based load matching reduce empty runs? | | **L**egal | Is BS-VI Phase 2 compliance mandatory before FY27? | | **E**nvironmental | Does the carbon-tax proposal affect total cost? | ### C. Quantitative Tools (Reused from Modules 1 & 2) These are the *engines* of any numerical case study answer. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > The table below consolidates every quantitative tool you may legitimately be asked to *re-apply* inside a case study. You do **not** derive these here — you **deploy** them. | # | Tool / Formula | Symbolic Form | When Used in a Case | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Equivalent Annual Cost (EAC)** | $EAC = \dfrac{P \cdot CRF + A}{1-T}$ where $CRF = \dfrac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ | *"Should we replace this vehicle now or run it for 2 more years?"* | | 2 | **Vehicle Cost per km** | $C_{km} = \dfrac{FC + VC + OC + LC}{Annual\ km}$ | Comparing two vehicle options (diesel vs CNG vs EV) | | 3 | **Break-Even Load Factor** | $L_{BE} = \dfrac{Fixed\ Cost\ per\ Trip}{Variable\ Revenue\ per\ Unit\ Load}$ | *"At what occupancy does the bus route become profitable?"* | | 4 | **Net Present Value (NPV)** | $NPV = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} - C_0$ | Capital decisions: depot construction, fleet expansion | | 5 | **Payback Period** | $Payback = \dfrac{Initial\ Investment}{Annual\ Net\ Cash\ Inflow}$ | Quick-cut decision when cash is the constraint | | 6 | **Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR)** | $BCR = \dfrac{PV(Benefits)}{PV(Costs)}$ | Public-transport cases: metro vs BRTS vs buses | | 7 | **Cost-Volume-Profit (CVP)** | $Profit = (P-v)Q - F$ | "How many extra trips cover the new toll?" | | 8 | **Empty Running Ratio** | $Empty\ Ratio = \dfrac{km\ without\ load}{Total\ km}$ | Logistics cases: return-load optimisation | | 9 | **Fleet Utilisation Index** | $FUI = \dfrac{Total\ productive\ hours}{Total\ available\ hours} \times 100$ | Diagnosing idle-capacity in a fleet | | 10 | **Truck Turnaround Time** | $TAT = T_{loading} + T_{transit} + T_{unloading} + T_{waiting}$ | Port/dock-yard logistics case studies | ### Symbol Legend (used throughout this module) - $P$ = Principal / Purchase price - $A$ = Annual maintenance cost - $i$ = Discount rate (or interest rate) - $n$ = Life in years - $T$ = Tax rate (depreciation shield) - $FC, VC, OC, LC$ = Fixed, Variable, Overhead, Labour costs - $CF_t$ = Cash flow in year $t$ - $r$ = Discount rate - $Q$ = Quantity / trips - $v$ = Variable cost per unit - $F$ = Fixed cost ## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields These frameworks are not academic toys. Transport engineers at **NHAI, BMTC, Delhi Metro Rail Corporation, Amazon Logistics, and Rivigo** use the identical tools daily when deciding lane construction, fleet procurement, depot sizing, and route rationalisation. Mastering them in Module 3 directly mirrors what a working Transport Manager does in Week 1 of the job. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exhaustive Mandate:** Every algebraic step and every numerical value is shown. No skipped steps. ## 3.1 Master Worked-Out Case Study: *"Kerala State Road Transport Corporation (KSRTC) vs Private Operators — Should KSRTC Retain Premium Air-Conditioned Buses on the Bengaluru–Kochi Route?"* ### Step 1: Decode the Case Statement (The "Read-Me-Twice" Step) **Given Data (extracted verbatim from a typical KTU case):** - Route length: **560 km** one-way. - KSRTC Premium AC bus fare: ₹1,400 per seat. - Seating capacity: **45 seats** (single-deck AC). - Average load factor: **70%** (i.e., 31.5 passengers, rounded to 32). - Daily fuel cost per bus (round trip): **₹8,500** (diesel). - Crew, toll, depreciation, insurance cost per bus per day: **₹4,200**. - Daily total operating cost: ₹8,500 + ₹4,200 = **₹12,700**. - Idle return-trip load factor: **20%** (10 passengers). - New competitive threat: a private multi-axle sleeper at ₹900/seat, 36 seats. **Decision Question:** Should KSRTC (a) *retain and price-cut* the AC bus, (b) *replace it with a non-AC semi-sleeper*, or (c) *withdraw the service*? ### Step 2: Calculate Revenue Under Current (Premium AC) Option Revenue per one-way trip at full load (45 seats × ₹1,400) = ₹63,000. But the load factor is **70%**, so expected occupied seats = 45 × 0.70 = 31.5 ≈ **32 seats**. $$ \text{One-way Revenue} = 32 \times 1400 = 44800\ \text{INR} $$ For the return trip, load factor is only **20%**, so occupied seats = 45 × 0.20 = **9 seats**. $$ \text{Return-trip Revenue} = 9 \times 1400 = 12600\ \text{INR} $$ $$ \text{Daily Total Revenue (Premium AC)} = 44800 + 12600 = 57400\ \text{INR} $$ ### Step 3: Compute Profit / Loss Under Current Option $$ \text{Daily Profit} = \text{Total Revenue} - \text{Total Operating Cost} $$ $$ \text{Daily Profit}_{AC} = 57400 - 12700 = 44700\ \text{INR} $$ ✅ **The Premium AC bus is profitable even at 70% load factor on the forward leg.** This is a counter-intuitive finding because students often wrongly assume AC buses always lose money. ### Step 4: Now Stress-Test Under the Private Competitor's Entry If KSRTC cuts the fare to ₹1,100 to retain passengers, but load factor improves only marginally to 75%: Forward trip: 45 × 0.75 = 33.75 ≈ **34 seats** × ₹1,100 = ₹37,400. Return trip load factor (a reasonable assumption) drops to **15%** due to private competition: 45 × 0.15 = 6.75 ≈ **7 seats** × ₹1,100 = ₹7,700. $$ \text{Daily Revenue}_{Price-Cut} = 37400 + 7700 = 45100\ \text{INR} $$ $$ \text{Daily Profit}_{Price-Cut} = 45100 - 12700 = 32400\ \text{INR} $$ Profit drops from ₹44,700 to ₹32,400, a **27.5% decline** — still positive, but the cushion is thinner. ### Step 5: Test Option (b) — Replace with Non-AC Semi-Sleeper **New Operating Data:** - Fare: ₹700/seat, 50 seats (higher capacity). - Load factor (non-AC is more elastic): **80%** forward, **25%** return. - Fuel cost per day (non-AC, lighter vehicle): ₹6,800. - Crew, toll, depreciation: ₹3,500. - Total operating cost per day: ₹6,800 + ₹3,500 = **₹10,300**. Forward revenue: 50 × 0.80 = **40 seats** × ₹700 = ₹28,000. Return revenue: 50 × 0.25 = **12.5 ≈ 13 seats** × ₹700 = ₹9,100. $$ \text{Daily Revenue}_{Non-AC} = 28000 + 9100 = 37100\ \text{INR} $$ $$ \text{Daily Profit}_{Non-AC} = 37100 - 10300 = 26800\ \text{INR} $$ ### Step 6: Compare the Three Options in a Decision Matrix | Decision Variable | Option (a) Retain & Price-Cut | Option (b) Replace with Non-AC | Option (c) Withdraw | |---|---|---|---| | Daily Revenue (₹) | 45,100 | 37,100 | 0 | | Daily Operating Cost (₹) | 12,700 | 10,300 | 0 | | Daily Profit (₹) | **32,400** | 26,800 | 0 | | Annualised Profit (₹, × 365) | **1,18,26,000** | 97,82,000 | 0 | | Brand Equity Impact | Slight loss | Major loss (premium image) | Massive loss | | Strategic Optionality | High | Medium | None | ### Step 7: The Managerial Recommendation (The "Prescription") $$ \boxed{\text{Recommendation: Option (a) — Retain Premium AC, with marginal price-cut and load-factor boost via online booking.}} $$ **Justification Logic:** 1. The AC bus yields the **highest annual profit** (₹1.18 crore vs ₹0.98 crore for non-AC). 2. Withdrawal (Option c) discards a profitable contribution-margin asset. 3. Non-AC replacement sacrifices brand equity for only an ₹18 lakh/year profit differential — not worth it. 4. *Trade-off accepted:* lower yield per seat (₹1,100 vs ₹1,400) in exchange for volume protection against the private sleeper. ### Step 8: Sensitivity Check — What if Diesel Hikes by ₹5/Litre? Assume a ₹5/litre hike adds ₹1,700/day to fuel cost. New daily operating cost for AC bus = ₹14,400. Daily profit under Option (a) drops to ₹45,100 − 14,400 = **₹30,700** — still positive. The decision remains robust. --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Case Triggers to Frameworks > [!NOTE] > Save this matrix. When you read a case study in the exam, identify the *trigger event* first, then reach for the corresponding framework. | Trigger Event in the Case | Primary Framework to Deploy | Secondary Framework | |---|---|---| | New toll policy / fuel hike | CVP, Cost per km | Sensitivity Analysis | | Vehicle replacement due | EAC, NPV | Payback | | Loss-making route | Break-Even Load Factor | Empty Running Ratio | | Competitor entry | SWOT + Pricing | Demand elasticity | | Public transport project | BCR, NPV | PESTLE | | EV transition | Total Cost of Ownership | Government subsidy (PLI) | | Accident / Safety crisis | Fishbone (Ishikawa) | Stakeholder analysis | | Driver shortage | HR-driven frameworks | Productivity per driver | | Warehouse / depot location | Centre-of-Gravity, NPV | Land cost + rent | ## 3.3 Common Student Errors in Case Study Solutions (and How to Avoid Them) 1. **Mistake: Using irrelevant formulae.** A "routing problem" is not solved with NPV. Match the *problem* to the *tool*. 2. **Mistake: Ignoring constraints.** If the case states a working-capital limit of ₹2 crore, an option requiring ₹3 crore capex is automatically infeasible — regardless of NPV. 3. **Mistake: Recommending without justification.** A bare "Option B is best" gets zero marks. Always end with a 2–3 line defensible justification. 4. **Mistake: Confusing accounting profit with cash flow.** Capital cases use cash flow, not P&L profit. 5. **Mistake: Forgetting tax / depreciation shields.** EAC without the (1−T) factor loses 2 marks. # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The KTU Case Study Solver Pipeline The diagram below captures the **end-to-end mental workflow** a student should follow when opening any Module 3 case study in the exam hall. Treat it as a checklist. ```mermaid flowchart TD node1[Read the Case Twice] --> node2[Highlight Operational Data] node2 --> node3[Identify the Decision Question] node3 --> node4[Spot the Hidden Constraints] node4 --> node5[Match Trigger to Framework Matrix] node5 --> node6a{Numerical Case?} node5 --> node6b{Strategic Case?} node6a --> node7[Compute EAC or NPV or Break-Even] node6b --> node8[Build SWOT or PESTLE] node7 --> node9[Build Decision Matrix 2x3] node8 --> node9 node9 --> node10[Sensitivity Check] node10 --> node11[Write Managerial Recommendation] node11 --> node12[Justify Trade-offs] node12 --> node13[Final Answer Box] ``` ## 4.2 Framework Selection Logic This block diagram helps you decide *which* framework to deploy based on the keywords hidden in the case study text. ```mermaid flowchart LR caseInput[Case Study Text] --> kw1{Contains words like
replace, life, new vehicle?} caseInput --> kw2{Contains words like
toll, fuel hike, fare?} caseInput --> kw3{Contains words like
subsidy, metro, public project?} caseInput --> kw4{Contains words like
competitor, market share?} kw1 -- Yes --> f1[Use EAC and NPV] kw2 -- Yes --> f2[Use CVP and Cost per km] kw3 -- Yes --> f3[Use BCR and NPV with Social Discount Rate] kw4 -- Yes --> f4[Use SWOT plus Pricing Elasticity] f1 --> out[Decision Matrix] f2 --> out f3 --> out f4 --> out out --> rec[Recommendation] ``` ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Indian Public Transport System (Used as Case Context) When a case study refers to an Indian city (Kochi, Bengaluru, Delhi), use this architecture map to anchor your analysis. ```mermaid graph TD govt[State Government] --> regulator[Transport Commissioner RTO] regulator --> policy[Policy Instruments
Toll Tax Subsidy BS VI Norms] policy --> operator1[KSRTC Government Operator] policy --> operator2[Private Stage Carriage Operators] policy --> operator3[App Aggregators Ola Uber Rapido] operator1 --> fleet1[Owned AC Non AC Bus Fleet] operator2 --> fleet2[Mini Bus Contract Carriage] operator3 --> fleet3[Cabs Bikes Autos Aggregated] fleet1 --> commuter[Daily Commuter Choice] fleet2 --> commuter fleet3 --> commuter commuter --> outcome[Outcome: Load Factor Revenue Profitability] outcome -.Feedback.-> policy ``` ## 4.4 Ishikawa Fishbone — Generic Template for "Why is this transport business losing money?" When the case describes a *symptom* (loss, breakdown, delays), the fishbone forces you to look at all root causes. ```mermaid fishbone %%{init: {"theme":"base","themeVariables":{"fontSize":"14px"}}}%% %% This is a comment - actual fishbone structure below classDef rootCause fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333; L[Loss Making Transport Business]:::rootCause C1[Vehicle Age]:::rootCause C2[Fuel Inefficiency]:::rootCause C3[Route Planning]:::rootCause C4[Driver Skill]:::rootCause C5[Regulatory Cost]:::rootCause C6[Demand Drop]:::rootCause L --> C1 L --> C2 L --> C3 L --> C4 L --> C5 L --> C6 ``` *(In a written exam, simply draw six labelled arrows converging on the central problem — the examiner will recognise the rigour.)* # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]` **Q: Define a "Case Study" in the context of Vehicle Transport Management. State any two analytical frameworks commonly used to dissect transport case studies.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A case study in Vehicle Transport Management is an empirical investigation of a real or simulated transport operation — a fleet, a route, a policy, or a depot — where the student is required to identify the managerial problem, apply analytical tools, and recommend a defensible solution grounded in operational and regulatory data. *(1 mark)* Two analytical frameworks commonly used are: 1. **SWOT Analysis** — to map the internal Strengths/Weaknesses and external Opportunities/Threats of the transport operation. *(1 mark)* 2. **Cost-Volume-Profit (CVP) Analysis** — to determine the break-even load factor or trip volume required for profitability after a cost shock such as a fuel hike or toll revision. *(1 mark)* > [!WARNING] > **Pitfall:** Students often write textbook definitions of "case study" without tying it to a *transport* context. Always anchor your definition to the *course domain*. --- ### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]` **Q: Differentiate between a "Strategic Case Study" and a "Numerical Case Study" with one example of each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **Strategic Case Study** focuses on qualitative issues — competition, regulation, brand, stakeholder interests — and is typically solved using SWOT, PESTLE, or Porter's Five Forces. *Example: "Should Maruti Suzuki enter the EV fleet market for corporate taxi operators?"* *(1.5 marks)* A **Numerical Case Study** provides operational data (vehicle count, fuel cost, load factor) and demands computation using EAC, NPV, CVP, or BCR. *Example: "A logistics firm must choose between a ₹25-lakh diesel truck and a ₹38-lakh electric truck over a 7-year horizon."* *(1.5 marks)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks) > Per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE rules, the student answers **ONE** of the two questions below. Each question has two sub-parts worth 7 marks each. ### Question A (14 Marks) — `[Numerical Replacement Case]` > *"Southern Star Logistics (SSL) operates a fleet of 80 diesel trucks on the Chennai–Bengaluru corridor. Each truck covers 1,20,000 km/year. The current cost of ownership is ₹18/km. Diesel cost is ₹92/litre and the trucks average 4 km/litre. A new EV truck is available at a 60% higher purchase price but offers a 70% reduction in fuel cost per km and 40% lower maintenance cost per km. The corporate tax rate is 25%, and SSL uses an 8% discount rate.* > > *(a) Compute the Equivalent Annual Cost (EAC) for both options over a 7-year life and recommend the more economical choice.* > > *(b) SSL's finance director insists on a 4-year payback period as a hard constraint. Does the EV truck satisfy this? Perform a sensitivity check if diesel rises by 10%."* #### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks **Step 1 — Current Diesel Truck Annual Cost Data (1 mark)** - Fuel cost per km = ₹92 / 4 = **₹23/km**. - Maintenance + tyre + repair = ₹18 − ₹23 (fuel) = negative — inconsistent; we re-read: *Total ₹18/km* is given as "current cost of ownership", which typically includes all costs *except* fuel in some textbooks, OR fuel-inclusive. To make the case consistent with a realistic Indian fleet, **assume ₹18/km = total operating cost excluding fuel, and fuel is additional**. - Therefore, fuel cost = 23 × 1,20,000 = **₹27,60,000/year**. - Other costs (depreciation, driver, tyres, insurance, maintenance, toll) = 18 × 1,20,000 = **₹21,60,000/year**. - **Total Annual Operating Cost (Diesel) = ₹27,60,000 + ₹21,60,000 = ₹49,20,000/year.** **Step 2 — Diesel Truck EAC (3 marks)** Let purchase price $P_{D}$ = ₹25,00,000 (assume realistic figure for a heavy truck; if the case omits it, state the assumption). Life $n$ = 7 years, rate $i$ = 8%. $$ CRF = \dfrac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1} = \dfrac{0.08 \cdot (1.08)^7}{(1.08)^7 - 1} $$ $$ (1.08)^7 = 1.7138 \quad \Rightarrow \quad CRF = \dfrac{0.08 \cdot 1.7138}{0.7138} = \dfrac{0.1371}{0.7138} = 0.1921 $$ $$ \text{Capital Recovery} = 2500000 \times 0.1921 = 480250\ \text{INR} $$ Apply depreciation tax shield: assume SLM depreciation over 7 years = ₹3,57,143/year. Tax shield = 3,57,143 × 0.25 = ₹89,286. $$ EAC_{Diesel} = \text{Capital Recovery} + \text{Annual Operating Cost} - \text{Tax Shield} $$ $$ EAC_{Diesel} = 480250 + 4920000 - 89286 = 5310964\ \text{INR/year} $$ **Step 3 — EV Truck Annual Cost Data (1.5 marks)** - Purchase price $P_{E}$ = 1.6 × ₹25,00,000 = **₹40,00,000**. - Fuel (electricity) cost reduction 70%: 23 × 0.30 = **₹6.90/km**. - Fuel cost per year = 6.90 × 1,20,000 = **₹8,28,000**. - Maintenance 40% lower: 18 × 0.60 = ₹10.80/km → annual = 10.80 × 1,20,000 = **₹12,96,000**. - **Total Annual Operating Cost (EV) = ₹8,28,000 + ₹12,96,000 = ₹21,24,000/year.** **Step 4 — EV Truck EAC (1 mark)** Depreciation over 7 years = ₹40,00,000 / 7 = ₹5,71,429. Tax shield = ₹5,71,429 × 0.25 = ₹1,42,857. $$ EAC_{EV} = (4000000 \times 0.1921) + 2124000 - 142857 $$ $$ EAC_{EV} = 768400 + 2124000 - 142857 = 2749543\ \text{INR/year} $$ **Step 5 — Recommendation (0.5 mark)** $$ \boxed{EAC_{EV} = ₹27.5\text{ lakh/year} \quad < \quad EAC_{Diesel} = ₹53.1\text{ lakh/year}} $$ **Recommendation:** The EV truck is overwhelmingly more economical on an annualised basis — saving nearly ₹25.6 lakh per truck per year. SSL should migrate to EV. **However**, the recommendation must be qualified by the *upfront capital constraint* (₹40 lakh vs ₹25 lakh = ₹15 lakh extra per truck × 80 trucks = ₹12 crore additional capex). #### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks **Step 1 — Compute Payback Period (3 marks)** - Incremental capex = ₹40,00,000 − ₹25,00,000 = **₹15,00,000**. - Annual cash-flow saving = (₹49,20,000 − ₹21,24,000) × (1 − 0.25) [after tax] = ₹27,96,000 × 0.75 = **₹20,97,000/year**. - (The tax shield from extra depreciation is already embedded in the EAC; for payback we use after-tax operating savings.) $$ \text{Payback} = \dfrac{1500000}{2097000} = 0.715\ \text{years} \approx 8.6\ \text{months} $$ ✅ **The EV truck pays back in under 9 months** — easily clearing the 4-year hurdle. **Step 2 — Sensitivity Check: Diesel +10% (3 marks)** - New diesel price = ₹92 × 1.10 = ₹101.20/litre. - New fuel cost per km = 101.20 / 4 = **₹25.30/km**. - New annual fuel cost (diesel) = 25.30 × 1,20,000 = ₹30,36,000. - New annual operating cost (diesel) = ₹30,36,000 + ₹21,60,000 = ₹51,96,000. - New saving = (₹51,96,000 − ₹21,24,000) = ₹30,72,000; after tax = ₹30,72,000 × 0.75 = **₹23,04,000/year**. - New payback = ₹15,00,000 / ₹23,04,000 = **0.651 years ≈ 7.8 months**. The decision is **highly robust** — even with a 10% diesel hike, payback falls further, reinforcing the EV recommendation. **However**, if the EV electricity tariff rises by 30%, recompute — the answer may flip. **Step 3 — Final Verdict (1 mark)** $$ \boxed{\text{Both payback and sensitivity favour the EV truck. SSL should migrate, financed via GOI PLI subsidy for EV trucks.}} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** The most common mistake students make is forgetting the **tax shield on depreciation** in the EAC calculation. This alone can flip the answer. The second common mistake is treating "cost of ownership ₹18/km" as fuel-inclusive when the case clearly intends otherwise — always re-read the case footnote. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — `[Strategic Policy Case]` *[Internal Choice Alternative]* > *"The State Government of Kerala proposes to introduce a **Uniform City Congestion Levy of ₹50 per entry** for all private vehicles entering the Ernakulam city centre between 8 AM and 11 AM on weekdays. The objective is to reduce congestion, fund public transport, and reduce emissions. App-based aggregators (Ola, Uber, Rapido) argue this will hurt last-mile connectivity. Resident Welfare Associations argue it will help. Bus operators (KSRTC, private) see an opportunity.* > > *(a) Perform a PESTLE analysis of this policy intervention and identify the two most critical risks.* > > *(b) Using a break-even analysis, evaluate whether KSRTC should add 20 new air-conditioned mini-buses on the Ernakulam–Aluva corridor in response to expected modal shift."* #### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks **PESTLE Analysis (5 marks):** | Factor | Analysis | |---|---| | **P**olitical | Strong political will for revenue generation; risk of being labelled "anti-middle-class"; coalition dynamics could reverse the levy. | | **E**conomic | ₹50/entry may be regressive; low elasticity among high-income car users; potential short-term dip in retail sales in the city centre by 5–10%. | | **S**ocial | Public opinion split; social-equity concerns for daily-wage workers commuting into the city centre. | | **T**echnological | ANPR cameras make enforcement feasible; RFID-based FASTag-style systems can automate deduction. | | **L**egal | Must align with MV Act 1988 amendments and Motor Vehicle Taxation statutes; risk of High Court stay if not framed as a "user fee" not a "tax". | | **E**nvironmental | Expected PM2.5 reduction of 8–12% in the city centre; aligns with NCAP targets. | **Two Most Critical Risks (2 marks):** 1. **Equity Risk:** The flat ₹50 levy is regressive — it burdens a daily-wage helper commuting at 8 AM as much as a luxury-car owner. A sliding-scale or time-of-day differential is preferable. 2. **Enforcement Risk:** Without ANPR + FASTag interoperability, leakage (non-payment) could exceed 30%, collapsing revenue projections and political support. > [!WARNING] > **Pitfall:** Students often write a PESTLE table but **fail to rank the risks**. The instruction says "identify the *two most critical* risks" — without ranking, you lose 1.5 marks. #### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks **Given (assume realistic values):** - AC mini-bus purchase cost: ₹35,00,000 each. - Seating capacity: **25 seats**. - Expected modal shift due to congestion levy: 15% of current car commuters shift → add **10 passengers per trip** on average. - Ticket price: ₹40. - Daily trips per bus: **8 round-trips** on the Ernakulam–Aluva corridor. - Fixed cost per bus per day: depreciation + driver + insurance + permit = ₹4,500. - Variable cost per bus per km (40 km round trip × 8 = 320 km): ₹12/km × 320 = **₹3,840/day**. **Step 1 — Compute Daily Fixed + Variable Cost (1 mark)** $$ \text{Total Daily Cost} = 4500 + 3840 = 8340\ \text{INR/bus} $$ **Step 2 — Compute Daily Revenue (2 marks)** Assume average load factor with modal shift: 70% of 25 = **17.5 ≈ 18 seats** per trip. $$ \text{Daily Revenue per Bus} = 18\ \text{seats} \times 40\ \text{INR} \times 8\ \text{trips} = 5760\ \text{INR} $$ **Step 3 — Break-Even Analysis (2 marks)** The daily revenue (₹5,760) is **less than** the daily cost (₹8,340) — the bus is making a daily loss of ₹2,580. Break-even load factor: $$ \text{BE Seats per Trip} = \dfrac{\text{Total Daily Cost}}{\text{Ticket Price} \times \text{Trips per Day}} = \dfrac{8340}{40 \times 8} = 26.06 $$ But the bus only has **25 seats**. So mathematically, **break-even is unattainable at the current ticket price**. KSRTC must either: 1. Raise ticket price to ₹42 (₹8,340 / (25 × 8) = ₹41.70), or 2. Increase trips to 10/day (₹8,340 / (25 × 10) = ₹33.36 — lower breakeven ticket). **Step 4 — Recommendation (2 marks)** $$ \boxed{\text{At ₹40 fare and 18-seat average load, the new mini-buses run at a loss. KSRTC should add only 10 buses (not 20), pair them with feeder e-rickshaws, and request a Viability Gap Funding grant under the Centre's Urban Transport scheme.}} $$ **Justification:** With 10 buses, KSRTC tests the demand without committing ₹7 crore upfront; e-rickshaw feeders solve the last-mile problem that aggregators currently monopolise. > [!WARNING] > **Pitfall:** Students often compute only the **revenue** and stop. They forget the **fixed cost** in CVP. Break-even *requires* the full cost equation: $Q_{BE} = F / (P - v)$. Half-completed CVP loses 3 of 7 marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 3: Case Studies** - ✅ A case study answer **must contain**: (1) problem identification, (2) data extraction, (3) framework deployment, (4) quantitative computation if numerical, (5) decision matrix, (6) sensitivity check, (7) defensible recommendation. - ✅ **Always re-state assumptions** explicitly. Case studies rarely give every number — bridge the gap transparently. - ✅ The **three golden formulae** you cannot skip: EAC, NPV, and Break-Even Load Factor. - ✅ **SWOT** is for diagnosis; **CVP / EAC / NPV** is for prescription; **PESTLE** is for context. Never substitute one for the other. - ✅ **Sensitivity analysis** is what separates an A-grade answer from a B-grade one. Always perturb one variable (diesel price, load factor, discount rate) by ±10% and re-state the decision. - ✅ **Tax shield** = Depreciation × Tax Rate. Forgetting this flips EAC answers. - ✅ **Indian context is non-negotiable.** A KTU examiner expects references to PLI scheme, GST on diesel, BS-VI norms, FASTag tolls, RTO compliance, or state-specific subsidies. - ✅ **Decision matrix** with 4–5 rows (Profit, Cost, Risk, Brand, Strategic Fit) earns full structural marks. - ✅ **Conclusion box** at the very end with the recommendation inside `\boxed{}` is examiner-friendly and improves legibility. - ✅ **Avoid generic fluff** like "good for the company" or "beneficial in the long run." Always quantify. - ✅ **Practice at least three full case solutions under timed conditions** before the ESE — Module 3 case studies are the *highest-weightage, highest-discrimination* portion of PEMUT866.

topics.title: Fleet Planning and Optimization - Strategic Fleet Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT866 - VEHICLE TRANSPORT MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fleet Planning and Optimization - Strategic Fleet Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strategic Fleet Planning (SFP)** is the long-horizon, top-level decision-making process that determines the **optimal size, composition, age profile, and procurement strategy** of a transport organization's vehicle fleet, aligned with projected demand, capital availability, regulatory constraints, and the firm's competitive/service-level objectives.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, Strategic Fleet Planning sits at the apex of the *Fleet Management Pyramid*. It is a **tactical-strategic** function that bridges corporate strategy with day-to-day operations management. Decisions made here have lock-in periods of **5–15 years** and directly influence capital expenditure (CAPEX), operating expenditure (OPEX), service reliability, and environmental compliance.

The four pillars of Strategic Fleet Planning are:

| Pillar | Decision Focus | Typical Time Horizon |
|---|---|---|
| **Fleet Sizing** | How many vehicles? | 1–5 years |
| **Fleet Composition** | Which vehicle types/sizes? | 3–7 years |
| **Fleet Replacement** | When to retire & replace? | 5–15 years asset life |
| **Fleet Ownership** | Buy, lease, or outsource? | 1–10 years contract |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping:** This topic maps directly to **Course Outcome CO4** – *Apply fleet planning, scheduling, and optimization techniques to transport operations.* The cognitive levels targeted are **L4 (Analyze)** and **L5 (Evaluate)** under Revised Bloom's Taxonomy.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are the **CEO of a regional logistics company** in Kerala handling perishable goods (fish from Kollam, spices from Idukki) destined for supermarkets across South India. You cannot simply buy *more trucks* every time demand spikes — that would bankrupt you. Nor can you run a *single truck* and pray it doesn't break down.

**Strategic Fleet Planning is like planning a wedding banquet:**
- You estimate how many guests (demand forecast)
- Choose between a sit-down dinner vs buffet (vehicle type — reefer vs dry van)
- Decide whether to rent the hall or build your own (own vs lease vs outsource)
- Reserve a replacement caterer in case the main one falls sick (redundancy/standby)

The decisions are **expensive, irreversible, and long-term**. A wrong choice locks you in for years.

### 1.3 Physical & Financial Constants (Industry Benchmarks)

- **Vehicle Utilization Rate (target):** **80–85%** for commercial fleets (lower = inefficient; higher = maintenance risk).
- **Optimal Replacement Age (heavy trucks):** **8–12 years** or **800,000–1,200,000 km**.
- **Annual Depreciation (straight-line):** typically **10–15%** of purchase cost.
- **TCO multiplier rule of thumb:** *Every ₹1 saved on purchase price = ₹3 spent in maintenance over asset life.*
- **Fleet Right-Sizing benchmark:** Replace 1 old vehicle with **0.8–1.0** new vehicles of higher capacity when demand is growing.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six-Stage Strategic Fleet Planning Process

Strategic Fleet Planning is **not a single decision** — it is a structured sequence of six analytical stages:

**Stage 1 — Demand Forecasting**
- Project tonnage / passenger volume for the planning horizon (5–10 years).
- Methods: Time-series (ARIMA), regression on GDP, scenario analysis.
- Output: Annual tonne-kilometers (TKm) or passenger-kilometers (PKm).

**Stage 2 — Capacity Requirement Calculation**
- Convert demand into vehicle-hours using available capacity per vehicle.
- Account for empty running, deadhead miles, and average trip length.

**Stage 3 — Fleet Sizing**
- Determine the *minimum* number of vehicles to meet service level.
- Formula: Required Fleet Size = Peak Demand ÷ Per-Vehicle Capacity × Service Factor.

**Stage 4 — Vehicle Specification (Composition)**
- Choose vehicle class: light commercial (LCV), heavy commercial (HCV), trailers, EVs.
- Trade-off: Capital cost vs fuel efficiency vs payload vs regulatory compliance.

**Stage 5 — Ownership Strategy**
- Three options: **Own (Buy)**, **Lease (Financial/Operating)**, **Outsource (3PL/Contract)**.
- Evaluated through **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** and **Net Present Value (NPV)**.

**Stage 6 — Replacement & Renewal Schedule**
- Build a multi-year replacement matrix.
- Use Equivalent Uniform Annual Cost (EUAC) method to find optimal replacement age.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is **exam-critical**. Every KTU Board valuation key expects these formulas in symbolic form. **Use `\vert` instead of `|` for absolute values.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Fleet Size (Peak Demand Method)** | $N = \dfrac{D_{peak} \times (1 + R)}{C_v \times U}$ | $D_{peak}$ = peak daily demand (tonnes); $R$ = reserve %; $C_v$ = vehicle capacity; $U$ = utilization |
| 2 | **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** | $TCO = C_{cap} + C_{fuel} + C_{maint} + C_{crew} + C_{admin} + C_{disposal}$ | All in ₹ over asset life |
| 3 | **Net Present Value of Fleet** | $NPV = -C_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{(B_t - C_t)}{(1+i)^t}$ | $C_0$ = initial cost; $B_t$ = benefits; $C_t$ = operating cost; $i$ = discount rate |
| 4 | **Equivalent Uniform Annual Cost** | $EUAC = (P - S) \cdot \dfrac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1} + S \cdot i + A$ | $P$ = purchase price; $S$ = salvage; $n$ = years; $A$ = annual operating cost |
| 5 | **Capital Recovery Factor (CRF)** | $CRF = \dfrac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ | $i$ = interest rate; $n$ = life in years |
| 6 | **Annual Depreciation (Straight Line)** | $D = \dfrac{P - S}{n}$ | $P$ = price; $S$ = salvage; $n$ = life |
| 7 | **Vehicle Utilization Rate** | $U = \dfrac{\text{Actual Operating Hours}}{\text{Available Hours}} \times 100$ | Percentage |
| 8 | **Break-Even Outsourcing Cost** | $C_{outsource} = TCO_{own} - \text{Risk Premium}$ | ₹/vehicle-year |
| 9 | **Productivity Index** | $PI = \dfrac{\text{Tonne-km delivered}}{\text{Tonne-km capacity}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 10 | **Right-Sizing Adjustment** | $N_{new} = N_{old} \times \dfrac{Q_{new}}{Q_{old}} \times \dfrac{U_{old}}{U_{new}}$ | $Q$ = avg capacity; $U$ = utilization |

### 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

Strategic Fleet Planning is indispensable in:

- **Public Transport (KSRTC, BMTC):** Determines bus procurement every 5 years.
- **E-commerce Logistics (Flipkart, Amazon):** Decides EV vs diesel mix for last-mile.
- **Construction Industry:** Right-sizing tipper fleet to project pipeline.
- **Municipal Solid Waste:** Optimizing compactor fleet by ward.
- **Cold Chain (Amul, Suguna):** Strategic placement of reefer fleet across India.

The discipline prevents two classic failure modes: **over-capitalization** (idle trucks burning cash) and **under-capacity** (lost orders and SLA penalties).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Optimal Fleet Size

Let us derive the peak-demand fleet sizing formula rigorously.

**Given:**
- $D_{peak}$ = peak daily freight demand (tonnes)
- $C_v$ = rated payload per vehicle (tonnes)
- $U$ = effective utilization factor (0 < U ≤ 1)
- $R$ = reserve / spare ratio (typically 0.10–0.15)

**Step 1 — Compute the *gross* number of vehicles needed on the busiest day.**

The number of trips required per day is:

$$
N_{trips} = \frac{D_{peak}}{C_v}
$$

**Step 2 — Adjust for the fact that a vehicle cannot run 24 hours straight. Apply the utilization factor.**

A vehicle working only a fraction $U$ of its time covers only $U \times C_v$ effective tonnes per day. So the *effective* trips required become:

$$
N_{trips}^{eff} = \frac{D_{peak}}{C_v \cdot U}
$$

This is the **actual fleet size on the road** at peak.

**Step 3 — Add the reserve (standby) factor for breakdowns, maintenance, and leave.**

$$
N_{fleet} = N_{trips}^{eff} \cdot (1 + R)
$$

**Step 4 — Final boxed formula.**

$$
\boxed{\; N = \frac{D_{peak} \cdot (1 + R)}{C_v \cdot U} \;}
$$

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU exam-style):**

A Kerala-based logistics firm expects peak demand of **240 tonnes/day**. Each truck carries **8 tonnes**. Utilization is **0.85**. Reserve is **12%**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{trips} &= \frac{240}{8 \times 0.85} \\
&= \frac{240}{6.8} \\
&= 35.29 \text{ trips/day} \\
N_{fleet} &= 35.29 \times (1 + 0.12) \\
&= 35.29 \times 1.12 \\
&= 39.53 \\
\Rightarrow N &\approx 40 \text{ trucks}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Always round UP** for fleet size — under-sizing causes service failure, over-sizing by 1 unit is acceptable.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Equivalent Uniform Annual Cost (EUAC)

EUAC converts a multi-year irregular cash flow into a single comparable annual figure — the basis for replacement decisions.

**Setup:**
- A vehicle is bought at price $P$ today.
- It is sold (salvaged) at price $S$ after $n$ years.
- Annual operating cost is $A$ (constant).
- Discount rate is $i$.

**Step 1 — Capital Recovery (the "loan payment" for the asset).**

The capital recovery factor (CRF) amortizes $(P - S)$ over $n$ years at rate $i$:

$$
A_{cap} = (P - S) \cdot \frac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}
$$

**Step 2 — Opportunity Cost of Salvage.**

Even after $n$ years, the salvage $S$ could have been earning interest. So we charge an *interest-on-salvage* term:

$$
A_{sal} = S \cdot i
$$

**Step 3 — Add Annual Operating Cost.**

$$
A_{total} = A_{cap} + A_{sal} + A
$$

**Step 4 — Boxed Master Formula.**

$$
\boxed{\; EUAC(n) = (P - S)\cdot\frac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1} + S \cdot i + A(n) \;}
$$

**Interpretation:** The optimal replacement age $n^*$ is the value of $n$ that **minimizes** $EUAC(n)$.

**Numerical Illustration:**

A truck costs ₹25,00,000 ($P$), salvage after 6 years is ₹7,00,000 ($S$), discount rate 10% ($i = 0.10$), annual operating cost increases with age: $A(1)=3L, A(2)=3.3L, A(3)=3.7L, A(4)=4.2L, A(5)=4.8L, A(6)=5.5L$ (₹ Lakhs).

Compute CRF for $n=6$, $i=0.10$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
CRF &= \frac{0.10(1.10)^6}{(1.10)^6 - 1} \\
&= \frac{0.10 \times 1.771561}{0.771561} \\
&= \frac{0.177156}{0.771561} \\
&\approx 0.22961
\end{aligned}
$$

Now compute $A_{cap}$:

$$
A_{cap} = (25{,}00{,}000 - 7{,}00{,}000) \times 0.22961 = 18{,}00{,}000 \times 0.22961 = ₹4{,}13{,}298
$$

And $A_{sal}$:

$$
A_{sal} = 7{,}00{,}000 \times 0.10 = ₹70{,}000
$$

Total EUAC at $n=6$:

$$
EUAC(6) = 4{,}13{,}298 + 70{,}000 + 5{,}50{,}000 = ₹10{,}33{,}298 \text{ per year}
$$

Repeat this for $n=3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8$ and the **minimum EUAC** gives the **economic replacement age**.

---

### 3.3 TCO Decomposition — Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Examiners award **2 marks** for showing the TCO components in any fleet-related question. Memorize this structure.

$$
TCO = \underbrace{C_{cap}}_{\text{Capital}} + \underbrace{C_{fuel}}_{\text{Fuel}} + \underbrace{C_{maint}}_{\text{Maintenance}} + \underbrace{C_{crew}}_{\text{Drivers}} + \underbrace{C_{admin}}_{\text{Insurance, taxes}} + \underbrace{C_{disposal}}_{\text{End-of-life cost}}
$$

| Cost Bucket | % of TCO (Heavy Truck) | Controllable? |
|---|---|---|
| Capital Depreciation | 25–30% | Yes (buy vs lease) |
| Fuel | 30–35% | Yes (route, driver) |
| Maintenance & Tyres | 15–20% | Yes (PM schedule) |
| Crew (Driver + Helper) | 15–20% | Partially |
| Insurance, Tax, Toll | 8–10% | No |
| Disposal / Salvage Loss | 2–5% | No |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Strategic Fleet Planning Decision Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Corporate Strategy] --> B[Demand Forecast 5-10 yrs]
    B --> C[Capacity Requirement]
    C --> D[Fleet Size Decision]
    D --> E[Fleet Composition Decision]
    E --> F[Ownership Strategy]
    F --> G{Buy / Lease / Outsource}
    G -->|Buy| H[Procurement Specs]
    G -->|Lease| I[Lease Contract Terms]
    G -->|Outsource| J[3PL SLAs]
    H --> K[Replacement Schedule]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Annual Fleet Plan]
    L --> M[Operations & Monitoring]
    M --> N[Feedback to Step B]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Start at the top: corporate strategy defines demand.
- The flow proceeds top-down; feedback loops are critical (note N → B).
- Decisions become **increasingly operational** as we descend.

### 4.2 TCO Component Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TCO[TOTAL COST OF OWNERSHIP] --> A[Capital Cost]
    TCO --> B[Fuel Cost]
    TCO --> C[Maintenance Cost]
    TCO --> D[Crew Cost]
    TCO --> E[Admin Cost]
    TCO --> F[Disposal Cost]

    A --> A1[Purchase Price]
    A --> A2[Financing Interest]
    A --> A3[Depreciation]

    B --> B1[Diesel or Electric kWh]
    B --> B2[Route Efficiency]

    C --> C1[Preventive Maintenance]
    C --> C2[Corrective Repairs]
    C --> C3[Tyres and Spares]

    D --> D1[Driver Salary]
    D --> D2[Helper Wage]
    D --> D3[Overtime]

    E --> E1[Insurance]
    E --> E2[Road Tax and Toll]
    E --> E3[Permits]

    F --> F1[Salvage Recovery]
    F --> F2[Decommission Cost]
```

### 4.3 Make-or-Buy (Own vs Outsource) Decision Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Fleet Decision Point] --> Q1{Is core competency?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Capital available?}
    Q1 -->|No| Out[Outsource to 3PL]
    Q2 -->|Yes| Own1[OWN FLEET]
    Q2 -->|No| Lease1[LEASE FLEET]

    Own1 --> Check1[Check TCO over 5 yrs]
    Lease1 --> Check2[Check lease rate vs TCO]
    Out --> Check3[Check per-km rate vs TCO]

    Check1 --> Dec[Lowest Cost Wins]
    Check2 --> Dec
    Check3 --> Dec
```

### 4.4 Multi-Year Replacement Matrix (Schematic)

```mermaid
gantt
    title Fleet Replacement Schedule (Illustrative)
    dateFormat YYYY
    axisFormat %Y
    section Heavy Trucks
    Truck H1 (2020-2028) :a1, 2020, 2028
    Truck H2 (2021-2029) :a2, 2021, 2029
    Truck H3 (2022-2030) :a3, 2022, 2030
    section Light Commercial Vehicles
    LCV L1 (2019-2024) :b1, 2019, 2024
    LCV L2 (2020-2025) :b2, 2020, 2025
    LCV L3 (2024-2029) :b3, 2024, 2029
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Define Strategic Fleet Planning. List its four key decision areas.*

**Model Answer:**
Strategic Fleet Planning is the long-term, top-level process of determining the optimal size, composition, replacement, and ownership strategy of a vehicle fleet to meet forecasted demand at minimum total cost.

Four key decision areas:
1. **Fleet Sizing** – how many vehicles
2. **Fleet Composition** – what types/classes
3. **Replacement Policy** – when to retire
4. **Ownership Strategy** – own vs lease vs outsource

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*What is the Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) of a vehicle? Name any five components.*

**Model Answer:**
TCO is the comprehensive sum of all costs incurred over the entire life of a vehicle — from acquisition to disposal — expressed in present-value terms.

Five components:
1. Capital cost (purchase + financing)
2. Fuel cost
3. Maintenance and tyre cost
4. Crew cost (driver + helper)
5. Administrative cost (insurance, tax, tolls)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> • Students frequently forget to **convert units** (₹ vs ₹ Lakhs, days vs years) — lose 1–2 marks.
> • In EUAC problems, **forgetting the salvage interest term $S \cdot i$** is the most common error — costs 2 marks.
> • Always **state assumptions** at the start of a numerical (constant demand, straight-line depreciation, etc.) — adds 1 mark.
> • **Rounding:** Do not round intermediate steps; round only the final answer.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Sizing and Composition**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]**

*(a)* A logistics company expects an annual freight demand of **1,20,000 tonnes** with peak season being **1.4× the average**. Each truck carries **10 tonnes** and operates with **85% utilization**. A reserve of **10%** must be maintained. Calculate the optimal fleet size. (7 marks)

*(b)* Discuss the strategic considerations involved in deciding the **fleet composition** for a long-distance freight operator in India. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Peak demand: $D_{peak} = 1{,}20{,}000 \times 1.4 = 1{,}68{,}000$ tonnes/year
Daily peak (assuming 300 working days): $D_{peak}^{day} = 1{,}68{,}000 / 300 = 560$ tonnes/day

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{560 \times (1 + 0.10)}{10 \times 0.85} \\
&= \frac{560 \times 1.10}{8.5} \\
&= \frac{616}{8.5} \\
&= 72.47 \\
\Rightarrow N &\approx 73 \text{ trucks}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer: 73 trucks** (rounded up).

**(b)** Strategic considerations for fleet composition:
1. **Payload requirements** – mix of LCV and HCV to match varied consignment sizes.
2. **Route terrain** – flat vs hilly (Kerala requires more powerful engines).
3. **Fuel efficiency vs cost** – balance between diesel, CNG, and EVs.
4. **Regulatory compliance** – BS-VI norms, axle load limits, National Permit rules.
5. **Operating cost spread** – diversity reduces risk if one segment underperforms.
6. **Maintenance infrastructure** – standardizing on fewer makes simplifies spares.
7. **Resale value** – some brands retain higher residual value, affecting TCO.
8. **Driver availability** – automatic vs manual transmission preferences.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Replacement Analysis**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]**

*(a)* A truck costs **₹20,00,000** new and has a salvage value that declines linearly to **₹4,00,000 after 8 years**. Annual operating cost is **₹3,00,000 in year 1** and rises by **₹30,000 every subsequent year**. Using **EUAC analysis at 12% discount rate**, determine the **economic replacement age**. (7 marks)

*(b)* Explain the strategic importance of **fleet ownership decisions** (own vs lease vs outsource) with reference to Total Cost of Ownership. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Setup the EUAC formula:

$$
EUAC(n) = (P - S) \cdot CRF(i,n) + S \cdot i + A(n)
$$

Where $S(n)$ for linear decline: $S(n) = 20L - \dfrac{16L}{8} \cdot n = 20L - 2L \cdot n$

And $A(n) = 3L + 0.30L \cdot (n-1) = 3L + 0.30L \cdot n - 0.30L = 2.7L + 0.30L \cdot n$

Compute $CRF$ for each candidate year $n$ at $i = 0.12$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
CRF(n) &= \frac{0.12 \times (1.12)^n}{(1.12)^n - 1}
\end{aligned}
$$

| n | $1.12^n$ | CRF | $(P-S)$ | $A_{cap}$ | $S \cdot i$ | $A(n)$ | EUAC |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 4 | 1.5735 | 0.32923 | 12.0 L | 3.95 L | 2.88 L | 3.90 L | 10.73 L |
| 5 | 1.7623 | 0.29741 | 10.0 L | 2.97 L | 2.40 L | 4.20 L | 9.57 L |
| 6 | 1.9738 | 0.27623 | 8.0 L | 2.21 L | 1.92 L | 4.50 L | 8.63 L |
| 7 | 2.2107 | 0.25988 | 6.0 L | 1.56 L | 1.44 L | 4.80 L | 7.80 L |
| **8** | **2.4760** | **0.24731** | **4.0 L** | **0.99 L** | **0.96 L** | **5.10 L** | **7.05 L** |

> $EUAC$ continues to fall, indicating the truck is still economically productive at year 8. **Optimal replacement age = 8 years or beyond** (in practice, extend the table until EUAC begins to rise).

**(b)** Ownership decision framework:

- **Own (Buy):** Best when utilization is **>80%**, capital is available, and the operation is core to business. TCO includes full capital cost.
- **Lease (Financial/Operating):** Best when utilization is **40–70%** or when newer technology is needed every 3–4 years. Shifts asset risk to lessor.
- **Outsource (3PL):** Best for **non-core routes, seasonal peaks, or one-way flows**. Pay per km; no capital lock-in.

The **TCO benchmark** for "make-vs-buy" is:

$$
C_{own}^{annual} \lessgtr C_{outsource}^{annual}
$$

If $TCO_{own} < TCO_{outsource} \times (1 - \text{risk premium})$, **own**.

Strategic benefits:
- Owning builds operational capability and brand reliability.
- Leasing preserves balance sheet and adds flexibility.
- Outsourcing converts fixed cost to variable cost and de-risks demand uncertainty.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Strategic Fleet Planning**

- ✅ **Strategic Fleet Planning = 4 decisions:** Size, Composition, Replacement, Ownership.
- ✅ **Formula to memorize:** $N = \dfrac{D_{peak}(1+R)}{C_v \cdot U}$ — fleet sizing.
- ✅ **EUAC formula has 3 components:** capital recovery + salvage interest + operating cost.
- ✅ **TCO has 6 buckets** — Capital, Fuel, Maintenance, Crew, Admin, Disposal.
- ✅ **Utilization target:** 80–85% for commercial fleets.
- ✅ **Optimal replacement age** = the year that **minimizes EUAC**.
- ✅ **Right-sizing rule:** Replace 1 old truck with 0.8–1.0 higher-capacity new trucks.
- ✅ **Own vs Lease vs Outsource:** Driven by utilization rate and core-competency test.
- ✅ **Always state assumptions** before numericals (linear salvage, constant demand, single discount rate).
- ✅ **Round UP** fleet size; **never round DOWN** for capacity planning.
- ✅ **Discount rate $i$** must be expressed as a **decimal** (12% = 0.12).
- ✅ **KTU expects symbolic formula + numeric substitution + boxed final answer.**

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fleet Optimization Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT866 - VEHICLE TRANSPORT MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fleet Optimization Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fleet Optimization** is the systematic process of determining the *optimal size, composition, deployment, and routing* of a vehicle fleet so as to **minimize total transportation cost** (or maximize service level) subject to demand, capacity, regulatory, and operational constraints.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, fleet optimization sits at the intersection of **Operations Research (OR)**, **Logistics Management**, and **Transport Economics**. It is a decision-making framework that converts qualitative transport goals (e.g., "deliver faster," "use fewer trucks") into **quantitative, mathematically tractable models** that yield an actionable plan.

The four classical pillars of fleet optimization are:

1. **Fleet Sizing** – *How many vehicles do we actually need?*
2. **Vehicle Composition / Mix** – *What types and capacities?*
3. **Vehicle Routing** – *Which vehicle serves which customer in which order?*
4. **Vehicle Scheduling** – *At what time, and how frequently?*

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PEMUT866 – Module 4):**
> Fleet Optimization is examined as a *decision-support framework* drawing on Linear Programming (LP), Integer/Linear-Mixed Programming (ILP/LMP), Heuristics, and Simulation. The examiner typically tests the **objective function**, the **constraints**, and the **managerial interpretation** of the solution.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Tea Stall Delivery Van" Intuition

Imagine you run a tea-stall supply business in Ernakulam. You have **5 delivery vans** of different sizes parked outside your godown every morning. Every day you must decide:

- *Do I really need all 5 vans, or can 3 do the job?*
- *Should the small van go to the nearby stalls, and the big van to the far ones?*
- *Can I combine the morning and evening milk runs into one trip?*
- *If van #2 breaks down, can I re-route van #1 to cover its customers without overloading it?*

That entire mental juggling act — deciding the **right number of vehicles, the right type, the right route, and the right time** — is **Fleet Optimization**. In a small firm the manager does it by gut-feel. In a large transport company (Amazon, DHL, KSRTC, BPCL tankers) the same decision is made by a **computer model** because the variables run into thousands.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Anchor:** Fleet Optimization = *"Right vehicle, right load, right route, right time — minimum total cost."*

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Engineering Units)

The following standard metrics govern every fleet optimization study:

| Metric | Symbol | Definition | Engineering Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Load Factor** | $\beta$ | Payload carried / Rated payload capacity | **dimensionless (0–1)** |
| **Vehicle Utilization** | $U_v$ | Time vehicle is productive / Total available time | **dimensionless (0–1)** |
| **Deadhead Miles** | $D_m$ | Miles run empty (no payload) | **kilometres** |
| **Total Fleet Cost** | $C_T$ | Sum of fixed + variable + operating costs | **₹ / ₹ per km** |
| **Ton-Kilometres Utilised** | $TKU$ | Payload (tonnes) × distance (km) actually loaded | **tonne-km** |
| **Available Ton-Kilometres** | $ATK$ | Capacity (tonnes) × distance (km) of every vehicle | **tonne-km** |
| **Cost per Tonne-km** | $C_{tk}$ | $C_T / TKU$ | **₹ / tonne-km** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Layers of Fleet Optimization

Think of fleet optimization as a **three-layer cake** — each layer adds more realism and more mathematics:
