In fleet management, the "family income" is the freight revenue, the "groceries" are fuel and tyres, the "doctor visits" are unplanned repairs, and the "future car" is the next vehicle replacement. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Benchmarks (Industry Standards) The following are universally accepted **Key Performance Indicators (KPIs)** in fleet financial management: | Metric | Standard Unit | Typical Benchmark | |---|---|---| | **Cost per kilometre (CPKM)** | ₹/km or $/km | ₹8–₹15 (commercial trucks) | | **Cost per ton-km** | ₹/tonne-km | ₹2.5–₹5.0 | | **Fleet utilization rate** | % | 70–85% | | **Fuel cost as % of total cost** | % | 35–50% | | **Maintenance cost as % of total cost** | % | 15–25% | | **Depreciation (Straight Line)** | % of vehicle cost per year | 10–15% | | **Working capital ratio** | Ratio | ≥ 1.5 | | **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** | % | ≥ 15% (transport projects) | | **Net Present Value (NPV)** | ₹ Lakhs | > 0 | | **Payback Period** | Years | ≤ 5 years | > [!NOTE] > **Key Constant:** Standard working days in a transport fleet = **300 days/year** (after accounting for 52 Sundays + ~13 holidays + maintenance downtime). ## 1.4 Objectives of Fleet Budgeting (As per KTU Module 4 Outcomes) 1. **Cost Control:** Identify, measure, and limit expenditures to approved limits. 2. **Resource Allocation:** Distribute scarce capital among competing fleet expansion/replacement needs. 3. **Performance Evaluation:** Compare *actual* vs. *budgeted* figures to find variances. 4. **Cash Flow Management:** Ensure liquidity — money is available when fuel bills, salaries, and EMIs are due. 5. **Strategic Decision Support:** Provide data for buy-vs-lease, replace-vs-rebuild, expand-vs-contract decisions. 6. **Accountability & Coordination:** Define departmental financial responsibilities. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Fleet Costs Understanding cost behaviour is the foundation of budgeting. Costs are classified into three primary categories based on how they respond to changes in fleet activity (kilometres operated or ton-km hauled). ### 2.1.1 Fixed Costs (FC) - These costs **do NOT vary** with the level of fleet utilization (km run) in the short term. - They are incurred even if the vehicle is idle. - **Examples:** Vehicle loan EMI, insurance premium, road tax, depreciation, garage rent, permanent driver salary, depreciation. - **Key Insight:** Fixed costs are *time-based*, not *use-based*. ### 2.1.2 Variable Costs (VC) - These costs **vary directly and proportionately** with the kilometres operated. - If the truck runs double the distance, variable costs double. - **Examples:** Fuel/diesel, tyres, lubricants, trip allowance, wear-and-tear parts, tolls (per km). - **Key Insight:** Variable costs are *activity-based*. ### 2.1.3 Semi-Variable (Mixed) Costs - Contain both a fixed component and a variable component. - **Examples:** Driver wages (fixed basic + variable per-km allowance), maintenance (fixed routine servicing + variable breakdown repairs), telephone/communication. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked to compute "total cost per km", the correct approach is: > $$ \text{Cost per km} = \frac{\text{Total Fixed Cost} + \text{Total Variable Cost}}{\text{Total Kilometres Operated}} $$ > Students often forget to spread fixed costs over the *expected* km, not actual km. ## 2.2 Types of Fleet Budgets ### 2.2.1 Capital Budget (Long-Term) - Covers **vehicle acquisition** (purchase or lease) and **major infrastructure** (depot, workshop). - Planning horizon: 3–10 years. - Evaluated using **engineering economy methods** (NPV, IRR, BCR, Payback). - Funded by equity, debt (loans), or leasing. ### 2.2.2 Operating Budget (Short-Term) - Covers **day-to-day expenses** for one fiscal year. - Sub-divided into: - **Direct costs:** Attributable to a specific vehicle (fuel, driver, tyres). - **Indirect costs (Overheads):** Shared across the fleet (administration, depot rent, head office allocation). ### 2.2.3 Maintenance Budget - A subset of operating budget focused on preventive and breakdown maintenance. - Often expressed as **₹/km** or **% of replacement value**. ### 2.2.4 Replacement Budget - Plans the disposal of old vehicles and acquisition of new ones. - Uses **sinking fund concept** to accumulate replacement capital. ## 2.3 Budgeting Process — Step-by-Step Logic The KTU 2024 syllabus demands that students describe the **budgeting cycle**: 1. **Establish Objectives:** Define service levels (e.g., 95% availability, on-time delivery ≥ 90%). 2. **Collect Historical Data:** Analyze past 2–3 years of actual costs, utilization, and revenue. 3. **Forecast Demand:** Estimate future freight volume, route distances, seasonal variations. 4. **Estimate Revenues:** Multiply forecasted volume by tariff rates. 5. **Estimate Costs:** Apply cost behaviour classification to each line item. 6. **Compute Net Cash Flow:** Revenue − Costs. 7. **Decision & Approval:** Senior management reviews and approves the budget. 8. **Implementation & Control:** Monitor actuals vs. budget, compute variances. 9. **Variance Analysis & Corrective Action:** Investigate unfavourable variances > 5–10%. 10. **Feedback & Revision:** Update next year's budget using the variance learnings. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is a **must-memorize** reference. KTU 14-mark questions frequently require application of these formulas to numerical problems. | # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Total Cost** | $TC = FC + (VC_{\text{per-km}} \times \text{Kilometres})$ | TC, FC in ₹; VC in ₹/km | | 2 | **Cost per km (CPKM)** | $CPKM = \dfrac{TC}{K}$ | ₹/km | | 3 | **Cost per ton-km** | $CTKM = \dfrac{TC}{Q \times K}$ | Q = payload (tonnes) | | 4 | **Straight Line Depreciation** | $D = \dfrac{C - S}{N}$ | C = cost, S = salvage, N = life (years) | | 5 | **Written Down Value Depreciation** | $WDV_n = C \times (1 - r)^n$ | r = WDV rate (e.g., 0.15) | | 6 | **Sinking Fund Factor (SFF)** | $SFF = \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ | i = interest rate, n = years | | 7 | **Annual Sinking Fund Deposit** | $A = S \times SFF$ | S = replacement value | | 8 | **Simple Payback Period** | $PP = \dfrac{\text{Initial Investment}}{\text{Annual Net Cash Inflow}}$ | Years | | 9 | **Net Present Value (NPV)** | $NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+i)^t}$ | CF = cash flow, i = discount rate | | 10 | **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** | $\sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+IRR)^t} = 0$ | IRR solved by trial/interpolation | | 11 | **Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR)** | $BCR = \dfrac{PV(\text{Benefits})}{PV(\text{Costs})}$ | Accept if BCR > 1 | | 12 | **Break-Even Kilometres** | $K_{BE} = \dfrac{FC}{R_{\text{per-km}} - VC_{\text{per-km}}}$ | FC, R, VC in consistent units | | 13 | **Break-Even Load Factor** | $LF_{BE} = \dfrac{FC}{R_{\text{total}} - VC_{\text{total}}}$ | Dimensionless (0 to 1) | | 14 | **Equivalent Annual Cost (EAC)** | $EAC = \dfrac{NPV \times i}{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}$ | For comparing alternatives | | 15 | **Present Worth of Annuity** | $P = A \times \dfrac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i}$ | A = annual amount | **Boundary Conditions & Constraints:** - All cash flows must be in the **same base year** (constant currency) for NPV/IRR. - Discount rate **i** must be ≥ cost of capital (typically 10–15% in India for transport projects). - **Decision Rule:** Accept the project if NPV > 0, IRR > MARR (Minimum Attractive Rate of Return), BCR > 1, or Payback < target. ## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering Budgeting and financial planning is the **lingua franca** of transport managers. Without it: - A transport company cannot secure bank loans (banks require cash-flow projections). - It cannot win government contracts (tenders require financial capability proof). - It cannot decide between buying 10 new trucks vs. leasing them vs. hiring transporters. - It cannot survive fuel price shocks without a contingency reserve. The principles taught here are directly applicable to **public transport corporations (KSRTC, BMTC)**, **private logistics firms (Delhivery, BlueDart, DHL)**, and **in-house corporate fleets** (sales fleets, school bus operators). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Break-Even Kilometres (Algebraic) **Problem Setup:** A fleet operator has a fixed monthly cost of ₹5,00,000 (depreciation, insurance, EMI, permanent staff). The revenue per km is ₹45, and the variable cost per km is ₹25 (fuel + tyres + trip allowance). How many kilometres per month must the fleet run to break even? **Step 1: Define the equations** - Total Revenue: $TR = R_{\text{per-km}} \times K = 45K$ - Total Cost: $TC = FC + VC_{\text{per-km}} \times K = 5{,}00{,}000 + 25K$ - Profit: $\pi = TR - TC$ **Step 2: Apply the break-even condition** (Profit = 0) $$ \begin{aligned} \pi &= 0 \\ TR - TC &= 0 \\ 45K - (5{,}00{,}000 + 25K) &= 0 \\ 45K - 5{,}00{,}000 - 25K &= 0 \\ 20K &= 5{,}00{,}000 \\ K_{BE} &= \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{20} = 25{,}000 \text{ km/month} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Interpretation** The fleet must run **25,000 km per month** (or 300,000 km/year) to just cover costs. Below this, the operator loses money; above this, profit is ₹20 per additional km (the contribution margin). **Step 4: Sanity Check** - Total Revenue at 25,000 km = $45 \times 25{,}000$ = ₹11,25,000 - Total Cost at 25,000 km = $5{,}00{,}000 + 25 \times 25{,}000$ = ₹5,00,000 + ₹6,25,000 = ₹11,25,000 ✓ - Profit = ₹0 ✓ ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Sinking Fund Method for Vehicle Replacement **Problem:** A truck costs ₹30,00,000. It must be replaced every 8 years with a salvage value of ₹6,00,000. The transport company can invest at 10% per annum compounded annually. How much must be deposited each year into a sinking fund to accumulate the replacement cost? **Step 1: Calculate the net replacement amount needed after 8 years** The company must have, at the end of year 8, the cost of the new truck (₹30,00,000) plus the depreciation benefit, OR alternatively, must accumulate a fund equal to the difference. Using the **sinking fund concept**, we accumulate the depreciation amount: $D = C - S = 30{,}00{,}000 - 6{,}00{,}000 = 24{,}00{,}000$. **Step 2: Apply Sinking Fund Factor formula** Sinking Fund Factor (SFF) at i = 10%, n = 8: $$ \begin{aligned} SFF &= \frac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1} \\ SFF &= \frac{0.10}{(1.10)^8 - 1} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Compute (1.10)^8** $$ \begin{aligned} (1.10)^2 &= 1.21 \\ (1.10)^4 &= (1.21)^2 = 1.4641 \\ (1.10)^8 &= (1.4641)^2 = 2.1436 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Substitute and solve** $$ \begin{aligned} SFF &= \frac{0.10}{2.1436 - 1} = \frac{0.10}{1.1436} = 0.08744 \\ A &= S \times SFF = 24{,}00{,}000 \times 0.08744 = ₹2{,}09{,}866 \text{ per year} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5: Interpretation** The company must set aside **₹2,09,866 every year** in a dedicated sinking fund earning 10% interest, so that at the end of year 8, the accumulated amount (principal + compound interest) equals ₹24,00,000, which combined with the ₹6,00,000 salvage value from selling the old truck, fully finances the new ₹30,00,000 truck. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: NPV of a Fleet Acquisition Project **Problem:** A logistics company is considering buying a truck for ₹25,00,000. The truck is expected to generate net cash inflows of ₹7,00,000 per year for 5 years. At the end of year 5, the salvage value is ₹5,00,000. The company's cost of capital is 12%. Should the company buy the truck? **Step 1: Tabulate the cash flows** | Year (t) | Cash Flow (₹) | Type | |---|---|---| | 0 | −25,00,000 | Initial Investment (Outflow) | | 1 | +7,00,000 | Operating Inflow | | 2 | +7,00,000 | Operating Inflow | | 3 | +7,00,000 | Operating Inflow | | 4 | +7,00,000 | Operating Inflow | | 5 | +7,00,000 + 5,00,000 = +12,00,000 | Operating + Salvage Inflow | **Step 2: Compute the discount factor for each year** Discount factor (DF) = $\dfrac{1}{(1+i)^t} = \dfrac{1}{(1.12)^t}$ $$ \begin{aligned} DF_0 &= 1.0000 \\ DF_1 &= \frac{1}{1.12} = 0.8929 \\ DF_2 &= \frac{1}{1.2544} = 0.7972 \\ DF_3 &= \frac{1}{1.4049} = 0.7118 \\ DF_4 &= \frac{1}{1.5735} = 0.6355 \\ DF_5 &= \frac{1}{1.7623} = 0.5674 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Multiply each cash flow by its discount factor (Present Value)** $$ \begin{aligned} PV_0 &= -25{,}00{,}000 \times 1.0000 = -25{,}00{,}000 \\ PV_1 &= 7{,}00{,}000 \times 0.8929 = 6{,}25{,}030 \\ PV_2 &= 7{,}00{,}000 \times 0.7972 = 5{,}58{,}040 \\ PV_3 &= 7{,}00{,}000 \times 0.7118 = 4{,}98{,}260 \\ PV_4 &= 7{,}00{,}000 \times 0.6355 = 4{,}44{,}850 \\ PV_5 &= 12{,}00{,}000 \times 0.5674 = 6{,}80{,}880 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Sum to get NPV** $$ \begin{aligned} NPV &= -25{,}00{,}000 + 6{,}25{,}030 + 5{,}58{,}040 + 4{,}98{,}260 + 4{,}44{,}850 + 6{,}80{,}880 \\ NPV &= -25{,}00{,}000 + 28{,}07{,}060 \\ NPV &= +3{,}07{,}060 \text{ ₹} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5: Decision** Since **NPV = ₹3,07,060 > 0**, the project is financially viable. The company should purchase the truck because it generates ₹3.07 lakhs of value above the 12% required return. ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Equivalent Annual Cost (EAC) — Buy vs. Lease Decision **Problem:** A company can either: - **Option A (Buy):** Purchase a truck for ₹20,00,000, life = 6 years, salvage = ₹4,00,000, annual maintenance = ₹1,50,000, annual fuel = ₹3,00,000. - **Option B (Lease):** Pay annual lease of ₹5,00,000 (includes maintenance, but fuel is separate at ₹3,00,000/year). - Cost of capital: 10%. **Step 1: Compute EAC for Option A (Buy)** Capital Recovery Cost (CRC): $$ \begin{aligned} CRC &= (P - S) \times \frac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1} + S \times i \\ &= (20{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}00{,}000) \times CRF + 4{,}00{,}000 \times 0.10 \end{aligned} $$ Capital Recovery Factor (CRF) at 10%, n = 6: $$ \begin{aligned} CRF &= \frac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1} = \frac{0.10 \times (1.10)^6}{(1.10)^6 - 1} \\ (1.10)^6 &= 1.7716 \\ CRF &= \frac{0.10 \times 1.7716}{0.7716} = \frac{0.17716}{0.7716} = 0.2296 \\ CRC &= 16{,}00{,}000 \times 0.2296 + 4{,}00{,}000 \times 0.10 \\ CRC &= 3{,}67{,}360 + 40{,}000 = 4{,}07{,}360 \\ EAC_A &= CRC + \text{Maintenance} + \text{Fuel} \\ EAC_A &= 4{,}07{,}360 + 1{,}50{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 = 8{,}57{,}360 \text{ ₹/year} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Compute EAC for Option B (Lease)** $ EAC_B = \text{Lease} + \text{Fuel} = 5{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 = 8{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹/year} $ **Step 3: Decision** $ EAC_B (₹8,00,000) < EAC_A (₹8,57,360) $ Therefore, **leasing is cheaper by ₹57,360 per year** over the 6-year horizon. The company should lease. ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Capital Budgeting Techniques | Criterion | NPV | IRR | BCR | Payback | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Considers Time Value of Money** | Yes ✓ | Yes ✓ | Yes ✓ | No ✗ | | **Considers Total Project Life** | Yes ✓ | Yes ✓ | Yes ✓ | No ✗ | | **Single Decision Metric** | Yes (NPV > 0) | Yes (IRR > MARR) | Yes (BCR > 1) | Yes (PP < target) | | **Handling of Multiple Sign Changes** | Robust | Problematic | Problematic | Inapplicable | | **Use of Discount Rate** | Required | Implicit | Required | Not used | | **KTU Recommended Preference** | **Most preferred** | Second | Third | Supplementary only | | **Common Pitfall** | Scale bias with large projects | Multiple IRRs with unconventional cash flows | Ambiguous when BCR is computed differently | Ignores post-payback returns | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Fleet Budgeting Process Flow (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD node1[Step 1: Define Objectives
Service Levels and Targets] --> node2[Step 2: Collect Historical Data
Past 2 to 3 Years of Costs] node2 --> node3[Step 3: Forecast Demand
Freight Volume and Kilometres] node3 --> node4[Step 4: Estimate Revenue
Volume multiplied by Tariff] node4 --> node5[Step 5: Classify Costs
Fixed, Variable, Semi-Variable] node5 --> node6[Step 6: Compute Net Cash Flow
Revenue minus Total Cost] node6 --> node7[Step 7: Decision and Approval
Senior Management Review] node7 --> node8[Step 8: Implementation
Allocate Budget to Departments] node8 --> node9[Step 9: Monitor and Compare
Actuals versus Budget] node9 --> node10[Step 10: Variance Analysis
Investigate Deviations] node10 --> node11[Step 11: Corrective Action
Cost Control Measures] node11 --> node12[Step 12: Feedback
Update Database for Next Cycle] node12 --> node1 ``` > **Figure 1:** The continuous budgeting cycle for fleet operations. The feedback loop (node 12 → node 1) signifies that budgeting is a *rolling* process, not a one-time activity. ## 4.2 Cost Classification Architecture ```mermaid graph TD rootNode[Fleet Total Cost TC] --> fcNode[Fixed Costs FC] rootNode --> vcNode[Variable Costs VC] rootNode --> svcNode[Semi-Variable Costs SVC] fcNode --> fcA[Depreciation] fcNode --> fcB[Insurance Premium] fcNode --> fcC[Road Tax and Permits] fcNode --> fcD[Loan EMI] fcNode --> fcE[Permanent Driver Salary] fcNode --> fcF[Depot Rent] vcNode --> vcA[Diesel and Fuel] vcNode --> vcB[Tyre Wear] vcNode --> vcC[Lubricants] vcNode --> vcD[Trip Allowance] vcNode --> vcE[Toll Charges] svcNode --> svcA[Maintenance and Repairs] svcNode --> svcB[Driver Wages Basic plus Variable] svcNode --> svcC[Communication Expenses] ``` > **Figure 2:** Hierarchical decomposition of Total Fleet Cost. This architecture is essential for building the operating budget. ## 4.3 Capital Budgeting Decision Flow (NPV/IRR Method) ```mermaid flowchart TD startNode[Start: Identify Fleet Investment Opportunity] --> dataNode[Gather Cash Flow Estimates
Initial Cost, Annual Inflows, Salvage] dataNode --> lifeNode[Determine Project Life n Years] lifeNode --> rateNode[Select Discount Rate i equal to MARR] rateNode --> calcNPV[Compute NPV using Formula] calcNPV --> decisionNPV{NPV greater than Zero?} decisionNPV -->|Yes| accNode[Accept Project] decisionNPV -->|No| calcIRR[Compute IRR by Interpolation] calcIRR --> decisionIRR{IRR greater than MARR?} decisionIRR -->|Yes| accNode decisionIRR -->|No| rejNode[Reject Project] accNode --> sensitivity[Conduct Sensitivity Analysis] rejNode --> alternative[Consider Lower Cost Alternative] ``` > **Figure 3:** Decision tree for fleet capital investments. Sensitivity analysis is mandatory to account for fuel price volatility. ## 4.4 Break-Even Concept Visualization ```mermaid graph LR axis[Axis: Kilometres K on X Axis
Rupees on Y Axis] --> trLine[Total Revenue Line
Slope = Revenue per km] axis --> tcLine[Total Cost Line
Slope = Variable Cost per km
Intercept = Fixed Cost] trLine --> bePoint[Break Even Point BE] tcLine --> bePoint bePoint --> lossZone[Loss Zone: K less than KBE
TR less than TC] bePoint --> profitZone[Profit Zone: K greater than KBE
TR greater than TC] ``` > **Figure 4:** The graphical break-even model. The slope difference (Rₚₖ − VCₚₖ) is the **contribution margin** — the profit earned per additional kilometre operated beyond the break-even point. ## 4.5 Vehicle Replacement Decision Framework ```mermaid flowchart TD q1{Vehicle Age and Condition} -->|High Mileage| q2{Market Value of Old Vehicle} q1 -->|Low Mileage| q3{Operating Cost per km} q2 -->|High| q4{Sunk Fund Available} q2 -->|Low| q5[Consider Major Overhaul] q3 -->|Greater than Threshold| q6{New Vehicle Cost} q3 -->|Less than Threshold| q7[Continue Operations] q4 -->|Yes| q8[Buy New Vehicle] q4 -->|No| q9[Arrange Finance or Lease] q5 --> q10[Evaluate NPV of Overhaul versus Replacement] q6 -->|Affordable| q8 q6 -->|Expensive| q9 q8 --> result[Replace Old Vehicle] q9 --> result q10 --> result q7 --> result result --> monitor[Update Financial Plan and Budget] ``` > **Figure 5:** The replacement decision tree integrates both engineering (operating cost per km) and financial (sinking fund availability) criteria. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Define fleet budgeting. List any four objectives of preparing a fleet budget.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** Fleet budgeting is the quantitative financial plan prepared in advance, estimating the revenues, costs (both capital and operating), and cash flows associated with the acquisition, operation, maintenance, and replacement of vehicles in a fleet over a specified planning period. **Four Objectives (½ mark each, total 2 marks):** 1. **Cost Control** – To identify, monitor, and limit expenditures to approved limits, preventing cost overruns. 2. **Resource Allocation** – To distribute scarce capital efficiently among competing fleet needs (new vehicles, replacement, infrastructure). 3. **Performance Evaluation** – To provide a benchmark (budget) against which actual performance can be compared (variance analysis). 4. **Cash Flow Management** – To ensure sufficient liquidity for routine payments (fuel bills, salaries, EMI) and prevent insolvency. *(Any other valid objective: Strategic decision support, Coordination, Accountability.)* ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Distinguish between fixed costs and variable costs in fleet operations with two examples each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Basis | Fixed Cost | Variable Cost | |---|---|---| | **Behaviour with km** | Does NOT change with km operated in short term | Changes directly and proportionately with km operated | | **Nature** | Time-based (per month/year) | Activity-based (per km) | | **Incurred when idle?** | Yes, still incurred | No, not incurred | | **Example 1** | Insurance premium (₹50,000/year) | Diesel cost (₹5/km) | | **Example 2** | Vehicle loan EMI (₹80,000/month) | Tyre wear cost (₹0.50/km) | *(1 mark for distinction + 1 mark for examples + 0.5 mark each for two examples = 3 marks)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** A transport company is evaluating the purchase of a new heavy commercial vehicle. The details are as follows: | Parameter | Value | |---|---| | Purchase Cost (P) | ₹30,00,000 | | Useful Life (n) | 6 years | | Salvage Value (S) | ₹6,00,000 | | Annual Net Cash Inflow | ₹7,50,000 per year | | Cost of Capital (i) | 12% per annum | **(a) Compute the Net Present Value (NPV) of the project and state whether the investment is financially viable. (7 marks)** **(b) Calculate the Internal Rate of Return (IRR) of the project using the trial-and-error / interpolation method. (7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution ### Part (a): NPV Calculation (7 marks) **Step 1: Tabulate the cash flows (1 mark)** | Year (t) | Cash Flow (₹) | Type | |---|---|---| | 0 | −30,00,000 | Investment | | 1 to 6 | +7,50,000 each | Operating Inflow | | 6 (additional) | +6,00,000 | Salvage | **Step 2: Compute discount factors at 12% (2 marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} DF_0 &= 1.0000 \\ DF_1 &= 1 / 1.12 = 0.8929 \\ DF_2 &= 1 / 1.2544 = 0.7972 \\ DF_3 &= 1 / 1.4049 = 0.7118 \\ DF_4 &= 1 / 1.5735 = 0.6355 \\ DF_5 &= 1 / 1.7623 = 0.5674 \\ DF_6 &= 1 / 1.9738 = 0.5066 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Compute Present Value of each year's cash flow (2 marks)** | Year | CF (₹) | DF | PV (₹) | |---|---|---|---| | 0 | −30,00,000 | 1.0000 | −30,00,000 | | 1 | 7,50,000 | 0.8929 | 6,69,675 | | 2 | 7,50,000 | 0.7972 | 5,97,900 | | 3 | 7,50,000 | 0.7118 | 5,33,850 | | 4 | 7,50,000 | 0.6355 | 4,76,625 | | 5 | 7,50,000 | 0.5674 | 4,25,550 | | 6 | 7,50,000 | 0.5066 | 3,79,950 | | 6 (Salvage) | 6,00,000 | 0.5066 | 3,03,960 | **Step 4: Sum to get NPV (1 mark)** $$ \begin{aligned} NPV &= -30{,}00{,}000 + 6{,}69{,}675 + 5{,}97{,}900 + 5{,}33{,}850 + 4{,}76{,}625 + 4{,}25{,}550 + 3{,}79{,}950 + 3{,}03{,}960 \\ NPV &= -30{,}00{,}000 + 33{,}87{,}510 \\ NPV &= +3{,}87{,}510 \text{ ₹} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5: Decision (1 mark)** Since NPV = +₹3,87,510 > 0, the project is **financially viable** and the company should purchase the vehicle. --- ### Part (b): IRR Calculation (7 marks) **Step 1: Set up the IRR equation (1 mark)** We need to find the rate r such that NPV = 0: $$ NPV = -30{,}00{,}000 + 7{,}50{,}000 \times \left[\frac{1 - (1+r)^{-6}}{r}\right] + \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{(1+r)^6} = 0 $$ **Step 2: Trial 1 at r = 15% (2 marks)** Discount factors at 15%: $DF_1 = 0.8696, DF_2 = 0.7561, DF_3 = 0.6575, DF_4 = 0.5718, DF_5 = 0.4972, DF_6 = 0.4323$ PV of inflows (years 1–6) = $7{,}50{,}000 \times (0.8696+0.7561+0.6575+0.5718+0.4972+0.4323) + 6{,}00{,}000 \times 0.4323$ Sum of annuity factors = $3.7845$ PV of operating inflows = $7{,}50{,}000 \times 3.7845 = 28{,}38{,}375$ PV of salvage = $6{,}00{,}000 \times 0.4323 = 2{,}59{,}380$ Total PV of inflows = $28{,}38{,}375 + 2{,}59{,}380 = 30{,}97{,}755$ NPV at 15% = $30{,}97{,}755 - 30{,}00{,}000 = +97{,}755$ (Positive) **Step 3: Trial 2 at r = 16% (2 marks)** Discount factors at 16%: $DF_1 = 0.8621, DF_2 = 0.7432, DF_3 = 0.6407, DF_4 = 0.5523, DF_5 = 0.4761, DF_6 = 0.4104$ Sum of annuity factors = $3.6848$ PV of operating inflows = $7{,}50{,}000 \times 3.6848 = 27{,}63{,}600$ PV of salvage = $6{,}00{,}000 \times 0.4104 = 2{,}46{,}240$ Total PV of inflows = $27{,}63{,}600 + 2{,}46{,}240 = 30{,}09{,}840$ NPV at 16% = $30{,}09{,}840 - 30{,}00{,}000 = +9{,}840$ (Positive, but small) **Step 4: Trial 3 at r = 17% (1 mark)** Sum of annuity factors at 17% = $3.5892$ PV of operating inflows = $7{,}50{,}000 \times 3.5892 = 26{,}91{,}900$ PV of salvage at 17% = $6{,}00{,}000 \times 0.3338 = 2{,}00{,}280$ Total PV of inflows = $28{,}92{,}180$ NPV at 17% = $28{,}92{,}180 - 30{,}00{,}000 = -1{,}07{,}820$ (Negative) **Step 5: Linear Interpolation (1 mark)** $$ \begin{aligned} IRR &= r_1 + \frac{NPV_1}{NPV_1 - NPV_2} \times (r_2 - r_1) \\ IRR &= 16\% + \frac{9{,}840}{9{,}840 - (-1{,}07{,}820)} \times (17\% - 16\%) \\ IRR &= 16\% + \frac{9{,}840}{1{,}17{,}660} \times 1\% \\ IRR &= 16\% + 0.0839\% = 16.08\% \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion:** IRR ≈ **16.08%** > MARR (12%), so the project is **acceptable** ✓ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** A fleet operator has 10 trucks. The following are the annual financial details: | Item | Amount (₹) | |---|---| | Depreciation | 25,00,000 | | Insurance | 5,00,000 | | Road Tax | 2,00,000 | | Driver Salary (Permanent) | 18,00,000 | | Depot Rent | 4,00,000 | | Diesel | 60,00,000 | | Tyres | 8,00,000 | | Trip Allowance | 12,00,000 | | Maintenance (Variable) | 10,00,000 | | Total Kilometres Operated (all trucks) | 6,00,000 km | | Total Payload carried (tonne-km) | 30,00,000 ton-km | | Revenue earned | 1,20,00,000 | **(a) Classify each cost item as Fixed or Variable. Compute the Total Fixed Cost, Total Variable Cost, and Total Cost. Hence determine the Cost per Kilometre (CPKM) and Cost per Tonne-km. (7 marks)** **(b) Compute the Break-Even Kilometres per year, assuming the same revenue per km and variable cost per km applies uniformly. Also calculate the current operating profit/loss. (7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution ### Part (a): Cost Classification and CPKM (7 marks) **Step 1: Classification (3 marks)** | Cost Item | Amount (₹) | Classification | Reason | |---|---|---|---| | Depreciation | 25,00,000 | **Fixed** | Time-based, independent of km | | Insurance | 5,00,000 | **Fixed** | Annual premium | | Road Tax | 2,00,000 | **Fixed** | Annual statutory payment | | Driver Salary (Permanent) | 18,00,000 | **Fixed** | Monthly salary, not per-km | | Depot Rent | 4,00,000 | **Fixed** | Contractual annual rent | | Diesel | 60,00,000 | **Variable** | Directly proportional to km | | Tyres | 8,00,000 | **Variable** | Wear proportional to km | | Trip Allowance | 12,00,000 | **Variable** | Per-trip / per-km allowance | | Maintenance (Variable) | 10,00,000 | **Variable** | Wear-and-tear based | **Step 2: Compute Totals (2 marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Total Fixed Cost (FC)} &= 25{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 + 18{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}00{,}000 = 54{,}00{,}000 \\ \text{Total Variable Cost (VC)} &= 60{,}00{,}000 + 8{,}00{,}000 + 12{,}00{,}000 + 10{,}00{,}000 = 90{,}00{,}000 \\ \text{Total Cost (TC)} &= FC + VC = 54{,}00{,}000 + 90{,}00{,}000 = 1{,}44{,}00{,}000 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Compute CPKM and Cost per Tonne-km (2 marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} CPKM &= \frac{TC}{\text{Total km}} = \frac{1{,}44{,}00{,}000}{6{,}00{,}000} = ₹24 \text{ per km} \\ \text{Cost per Tonne-km} &= \frac{TC}{\text{Total tonne-km}} = \frac{1{,}44{,}00{,}000}{30{,}00{,}000} = ₹4.80 \text{ per tonne-km} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Presentation (marginal marks for table clarity and units)** --- ### Part (b): Break-Even and Profit/Loss (7 marks) **Step 1: Compute per-km metrics (2 marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Variable Cost per km} &= \frac{90{,}00{,}000}{6{,}00{,}000} = ₹15 \text{ per km} \\ \text{Revenue per km} &= \frac{1{,}20{,}00{,}000}{6{,}00{,}000} = ₹20 \text{ per km} \\ \text{Contribution per km} &= 20 - 15 = ₹5 \text{ per km} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Compute Break-Even Kilometres (3 marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} K_{BE} &= \frac{FC}{\text{Revenue per km} - \text{VC per km}} \\ K_{BE} &= \frac{54{,}00{,}000}{20 - 15} = \frac{54{,}00{,}000}{5} = 10{,}80{,}000 \text{ km/year} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Compute Current Profit/Loss (2 marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Total Revenue} &= ₹1{,}20{,}00{,}000 \\ \text{Total Cost} &= ₹1{,}44{,}00{,}000 \\ \text{Profit / (Loss)} &= 1{,}20{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}44{,}00{,}000 = -₹24{,}00{,}000 \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion:** The fleet is incurring a **loss of ₹24,00,000 per year** because the current utilization of 6,00,000 km is far below the break-even utilization of 10,80,000 km. The operator must either **increase utilization by 80%** or **increase tariffs** or **reduce fixed costs** to break even. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > > **1. Misclassification of Semi-Variable Costs:** A frequent error is treating *all* of driver wages as fixed. If the question specifies "₹10,000 basic + ₹2/km variable allowance", you **must** split it: ₹10,000 × 12 × 10 drivers = ₹12,00,000 is fixed; ₹2 × 6,00,000 = ₹12,00,000 is variable. (Lose 1 mark if not split.) > > **2. Discount Factor Misuse in NPV:** Year 0 has DF = 1.0000. Year 1 DF = 1/(1.12). Students often mistakenly use DF₁ = 1.12 instead of 0.8929, inflating NPV. Always recheck the exponent. > > **3. Break-Even Formula Confusion:** Many students write $K_{BE} = FC / R$ instead of $K_{BE} = FC / (R_{pk} - VC_{pk})$. Forgetting to subtract variable cost from revenue in the denominator is a 2-mark error. > > **4. Sinking Fund vs. Capital Recovery:** Sinking Fund deposits an *amount A* every year to *accumulate* S. Capital Recovery *withdraws* an amount A every year to *recover* P over n years. Do not interchange. > > **5. IRR Interpolation Sign:** Ensure you interpolate between the rate giving **positive NPV** and the next higher rate giving **negative NPV**. If both give positive NPV, your trial rates are too low. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Budgeting and Financial Planning for Fleets** ### Core Definitions - ☐ **Fleet Budget** = Pre-prepared quantitative plan of revenues & costs over a defined period. - ☐ **Financial Plan** = Long-term (3–10 yr) capital allocation strategy. - ☐ **Fixed Cost** = Time-based; does not vary with km (depreciation, EMI, insurance). - ☐ **Variable Cost** = Activity-based; varies with km (fuel, tyres, lubricants). - ☐ **Semi-Variable Cost** = Mixed (driver wages, maintenance). ### Critical Formulas (Memorize) - ☐ $TC = FC + (VC \times K)$ - ☐ $CPKM = TC / K$ - ☐ $K_{BE} = FC / (R - VC)$ (per km basis) - ☐ $D_{SL} = (C - S) / n$ - ☐ $SFF = i / [(1+i)^n - 1]$ - ☐ $NPV = \sum CF_t / (1+i)^t$ - ☐ $CRF = i(1+i)^n / [(1+i)^n - 1]$ - ☐ $EAC = NPV \times CRF$ ### Decision Rules - ☐ **Accept** if NPV > 0 - ☐ **Accept** if IRR > MARR (cost of capital) - ☐ **Accept** if BCR > 1 - ☐ **Accept** if Payback < target years ### Process Steps (12-Step Budgeting Cycle) - ☐ Objectives → Historical Data → Demand Forecast → Revenue Estimate → Cost Classification → Net Cash Flow → Approval → Implementation → Monitoring → Variance Analysis → Corrective Action → Feedback. ### Numerical Values / Standards - ☐ Working days in fleet = **300/year**. - ☐ Typical fuel share = **35–50%** of total cost. - ☐ Maintenance share = **15–25%** of total cost. - ☐ IRR threshold for transport projects = **≥ 12–15%**. - ☐ NPV currency unit = **Lakhs of ₹** in Indian context. ### Common Pitfalls to Avoid - ☐ Do not confuse depreciation (non-cash) with cash outflow for EMI. - ☐ Do not treat salvage value as same as book value at end of life. - ☐ Always check the **base year** for inflation consistency in NPV. - ☐ Do not use **Payback Period alone** — it ignores time value of money. - ☐ In buy-vs-lease, always compare on **EAC basis**, not just first-year cost. ### Examiner's Top 3 Expected Questions - ☐ Compute Break-Even Kilometres given fixed, variable cost, and revenue per km. - ☐ Compute NPV / IRR for a vehicle purchase proposal. - ☐ Distinguish between fixed, variable, and semi-variable costs with examples.

topics.title: Environmental Considerations - Environmental Impact of Fleets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT866 - VEHICLE TRANSPORT MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Environmental Considerations - Environmental Impact of Fleets

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Environmental Impact of Fleets** refers to the aggregate set of direct and indirect effects produced by a group of commercial vehicles (cars, trucks, buses, or a mixed fleet) on the natural environment across its entire **Life Cycle** — spanning raw-material extraction, vehicle production, fuel/energy generation, operation (use phase), maintenance, and end-of-life disposal/recycling.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework for *Vehicle Transport Management*, the environmental impact of a fleet is quantified using a **Triple Bottom Line (TBL)** evaluation:
1. **Ecological Footprint** — emissions, resource depletion, waste
2. **Energy Intensity** — fuel/electricity consumption per unit of transport work
3. **External Cost (Social Cost of Carbon)** — monetized environmental damage

Key performance indicators tracked at KTU level include:
- **CO₂ equivalent (CO₂e)** emissions per vehicle-km or per ton-km
- **Particulate Matter (PM₂.₅ / PM₁₀)** mass emitted
- **NOₓ, CO, HC, SOₓ** mass emissions
- **Noise Level (dB(A))**
- **Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC)** in g/kWh or L/100 km

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Lung of the City" Analogy**
> Imagine a city is a living organism, and the vehicle fleet is its respiratory system. A small fleet of electric buses is like deep, slow, clean breaths — taking in energy and exhaling minimal pollutants. A large, ageing diesel-truck fleet is like rapid, shallow, smoky breathing — the organism survives, but it accumulates "carbon soot" in its lungs (the atmosphere), gradually causing chronic illness (climate change, smog, respiratory disease). The **Environmental Impact of Fleets** is the medical chart that tells us how sick the city is getting — and how to prescribe a healthier breathing pattern.

Think of it this way: **Every litre of diesel burned releases ~2.68 kg of CO₂** into the atmosphere. A single long-haul truck doing 150,000 km/year consumes ~30,000 L of diesel → ~**80 tonnes of CO₂/year**. Multiply by 1,000 trucks in a logistics fleet, and you have a small city's worth of emissions from one company's operations.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Constant / Metric | Symbol | Value |
|---|---|---|
| CO₂ emission factor — Diesel | $EF_D$ | **2.68 kg CO₂ / L** |
| CO₂ emission factor — Petrol/Gasoline | $EF_P$ | **2.31 kg CO₂ / L** |
| CO₂ emission factor — CNG | $EF_{CNG}$ | **2.75 kg CO₂ / kg** |
| Density of Diesel | $\rho_D$ | **0.832 kg/L** |
| Density of Petrol | $\rho_P$ | **0.745 kg/L** |
| Global Warming Potential of CH₄ (100-yr) | $GWP_{CH_4}$ | **28** |
| Global Warming Potential of N₂O (100-yr) | $GWP_{N_2O}$ | **265** |
| Lower Heating Value — Diesel | $LHV_D$ | **42.5 MJ/kg** |
| 1 barrel of crude oil equivalent | — | **~6.1 GJ** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of Fleet Environmental Impact

A KTU examiner expects students to classify fleet impacts into **four structured pillars**:

### Pillar 1 — Air Pollution (Tailpipe Emissions)
The internal combustion engine emits:
- **CO₂** (greenhouse gas, primary climate forcer)
- **CO** (carbon monoxide — toxic, from incomplete combustion)
- **NOₓ** (nitrogen oxides — smog, acid rain, respiratory irritants)
- **HC** (unburnt hydrocarbons — VOC, ground-level ozone precursor)
- **PM** (particulate matter — PM₁₀ and PM₂.₅, carcinogenic)
- **SOₓ** (from sulphur in diesel fuel — reduced by ultra-low-sulphur diesel)

> [!NOTE]
> **Tailpipe emissions** are regulated by **Bharat Stage (BS) norms** in India (currently **BS-VI**, equivalent to **Euro 6**).

### Pillar 2 — Greenhouse Gas (GHG) & Climate Change
Fleet contribution is measured as **CO₂-equivalent**:

$$CO_{2e} = m_{CO_2} + 28 \cdot m_{CH_4} + 265 \cdot m_{N_2O}$$

A logistics company must report Scope 1, 2, and 3 emissions under the **GHG Protocol**.

### Pillar 3 — Noise Pollution
Measured in **dB(A)**. Heavy trucks produce **80–95 dB(A)** at 7.5 m. EU directive **2002/49/EC** mandates noise mapping; India follows CPCB norms.

### Pillar 4 — Resource Depletion & Waste
- End-of-life vehicles (ELV): tyres, batteries, oil, coolants, metals
- Tyre wear: ~**1.5 kg per 10,000 km** per vehicle
- Engine oil: ~**1 L per 1,000 km** in heavy commercial vehicles

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning / Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{CO_2} = V_f \times EF$ | CO₂ mass = Fuel volume × Emission factor | kg CO₂ |
| 2 | $SFC = \dfrac{m_f}{P_b}$ | Specific Fuel Consumption (brake) | g/kWh |
| 3 | $BSFC = \dfrac{m_f \times 3600}{P_b \times t}$ | Brake Specific Fuel Consumption over time | g/kWh |
| 4 | $FE = \dfrac{D}{V_f}$ | Fuel Economy (distance per fuel volume) | km/L |
| 5 | $\eta_b = \dfrac{P_b}{\dot{m}_f \times LHV}$ | Brake Thermal Efficiency | dimensionless |
| 6 | $CO_{2e} = \sum_i m_i \times GWP_i$ | CO₂-equivalent of mixed GHG | kg CO₂e |
| 7 | $E_{intensity} = \dfrac{E_{total}}{W_{transport}}$ | Energy intensity of transport (per ton-km) | MJ/ton-km |
| 8 | $EI_x = \dfrac{m_x}{\dot{m}_f}$ | Emission Index of pollutant $x$ | g/kg fuel |
| 9 | $W_t = \dfrac{P_b}{\dot{m}_f}$ | Brake Specific Work | kJ/kg |
| 10 | $P_b = \dfrac{T \cdot 2\pi N}{60}$ | Brake Power from torque & rpm | kW |
| 11 | $LCA_{impact} = \sum_{j} E_j \times CF_j$ | Life-Cycle Assessment total impact | kg CO₂e |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tip for KTU exams:** The most frequently tested formulas are **#1 (CO₂ from fuel)**, **#6 (CO₂-equivalent)**, and **#4 (Fuel Economy)**. Memorize the emission factors in **bold** above.

---

## 2.3 The Why & How Behind Each Formula

### Why Formula #1 works
$1\text{ kg}$ of diesel, when fully combusted, produces $\sim 3.16$ kg of CO₂ due to the **stoichiometric ratio**:
$$C_{12}H_{23} + 17.75\,O_2 \rightarrow 12\,CO_2 + 11.5\,H_2O$$
The carbon mass fraction of diesel is $\sim 0.864$; therefore each kg of carbon yields $\frac{44}{12}=3.67$ kg CO₂. Combined with density, this gives **2.68 kg CO₂ per litre**.

### Why Formula #2 matters
**BSFC** is the single metric that tells a fleet manager whether a vehicle is efficient. Modern diesel engines achieve **200–220 g/kWh**, while petrol engines achieve **240–280 g/kWh**.

### Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Logistics companies** (e.g., DHL, FedEx) use these formulas for **carbon accounting** and **sustainability reporting (ESG)**.
- **Urban planners** use emission intensity (Formula #7) to design Low Emission Zones (LEZ).
- **KTU Industry Connect** projects often involve calculating fleet CO₂ for a small transport company — this is the formula you must know.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation #1: CO₂ Emission from Fuel Consumption

**Problem Statement (KTU-type):**
> A logistics fleet of 25 trucks, each consuming 12 L/100 km, runs an average of 80,000 km/year. Calculate the annual CO₂ emissions. Diesel emission factor = 2.68 kg/L.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Fuel consumed per truck per year**
$$\text{Fuel per truck} = \dfrac{\text{Distance}}{\text{Fuel Economy}} = \dfrac{80{,}000 \text{ km}}{100 \text{ km}} \times 12 \text{ L}$$
$$= 800 \times 12 = 9{,}600 \text{ L/year per truck}$$

**Step 2: CO₂ per truck per year**
$$E_{CO_2, \text{truck}} = V_f \times EF_D = 9{,}600 \text{ L} \times 2.68 \text{ kg/L}$$
$$= 25{,}728 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{year} \approx 25.73 \text{ tonnes CO}_2$$

**Step 3: Fleet total (25 trucks)**
$$E_{CO_2, \text{fleet}} = 25 \times 25{,}728 = 643{,}200 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{year}$$
$$\boxed{E_{CO_2, \text{fleet}} \approx 643.2 \text{ tonnes CO}_2/\text{year}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Equivalent context:** This equals the CO₂ absorbed by ~**10,700 tree seedlings grown for 10 years** (US EPA equivalence: 1 tree ≈ 60 kg CO₂/yr).

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation #2: CO₂-Equivalent (Mixed GHG)

**Problem Statement:**
> A CNG bus fleet emits the following per day: 850 kg CO₂, 0.45 kg CH₄, 0.08 kg N₂O. Compute the daily CO₂-equivalent.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Apply the GWP weighting formula**
$$CO_{2e} = m_{CO_2} + 28 \cdot m_{CH_4} + 265 \cdot m_{N_2O}$$

**Step 2: Substitute values**
$$CO_{2e} = 850 + (28 \times 0.45) + (265 \times 0.08)$$

$$CO_{2e} = 850 + 12.6 + 21.2$$

$$\boxed{CO_{2e} = 884.8 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e/day}}$$

**Step 3: Interpret**
Although CO₂ dominates (96%), the trace gases **CH₄ + N₂O contribute 3.92%** — small in mass, **large in warming impact**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Derivation #3: Brake Thermal Efficiency & BSFC

**Problem Statement:**
> A diesel engine consumes 18 kg/hr of fuel at full load. Brake power = 75 kW. LHV of diesel = 42.5 MJ/kg. Compute BSFC and brake thermal efficiency.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Convert fuel flow to per-second units**
$$\dot{m}_f = \dfrac{18}{3600} = 0.005 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 2: Energy input rate (chemical)**
$$\dot{Q}_{in} = \dot{m}_f \times LHV = 0.005 \times 42{,}500 = 212.5 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3: Brake Thermal Efficiency**
$$\eta_b = \dfrac{P_b}{\dot{Q}_{in}} = \dfrac{75}{212.5} = 0.3529$$
$$\boxed{\eta_b \approx 35.3\%}$$

**Step 4: Brake Specific Fuel Consumption**
$$BSFC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{P_b} = \dfrac{18{,}000 \text{ g/hr}}{75 \text{ kW}} = 240 \text{ g/kWh}$$

$$\boxed{BSFC = 240 \text{ g/kWh}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> A modern BS-VI diesel engine should achieve BSFC in the range **200–220 g/kWh** at optimal load. The 240 g/kWh here indicates either off-design operation or an older unit.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Fleet Powertrain Comparison

| Powertrain | CO₂ (g/km) | NOₓ (g/km) | PM (mg/km) | Noise dB(A) | Efficiency |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Petrol (BS-VI) | 120–160 | 0.06 | 4.5 | 70–74 | 25–35% |
| Diesel (BS-VI) | 100–140 | 0.08 | 4.5 | 73–78 | 35–42% |
| CNG | 90–120 | 0.04 | 1.0 | 68–72 | 30–38% |
| Battery Electric (BEV) | 0 (tailpipe) | 0 | 0 | 60–65 | 85–95% (motor) |
| Hybrid Electric (HEV) | 70–100 | 0.03 | 2.0 | 65–70 | 40–50% |
| Hydrogen FCEV | 0 (tailpipe) | 0 | 0 | 62–68 | 50–60% |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Well-to-Wheel (WTW) analysis** is the KTU-recommended approach. A BEV charged by India's coal-heavy grid still has WTW CO₂ of ~150–200 g/km — better than petrol, but **not zero**. True zero requires renewable electricity.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Fleet Environmental Impact Assessment (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    INPUT[INPUT: Fleet Activity Data]
    INPUT --> A1[Vehicle Count and Type]
    INPUT --> A2[Distance Travelled km]
    INPUT --> A3[Fuel Type and Quantity L]
    INPUT --> A4[Load Factor ton]

    PROC[PROCESSING MODULES]
    A1 --> P1[Fuel Consumption Calculator]
    A2 --> P1
    A3 --> P1
    A4 --> P2[Emission Module CO2 NOx PM]
    P1 --> P2
    P1 --> P3[GHG Conversion CO2e]
    P3 --> P4[Carbon Footprint kg CO2e]
    P2 --> P5[Air Quality Impact Index]
    P4 --> P6[LCA Aggregation]
    P5 --> P6
    A4 --> P7[Energy Intensity MJ per ton km]
    P1 --> P7

    OUTPUT[OUTPUT: Environmental KPIs]
    P6 --> OUT1[Total CO2e tonnes per year]
    P5 --> OUT2[NOx PM mass grams per km]
    P7 --> OUT3[Specific Energy Intensity]

    P5 --> OUT5[Compliance Status BS VI Euro 6]

    MIT[MITIGATION LAYER]
    OUT1 --> M1[Route Optimisation AI]
    OUT2 --> M2[Emission Control SCR DPF]

    OUT4 --> M4[Fleet Renewal Policy]

    M1 --> REPORT[Sustainability ESG Report]
    M2 --> REPORT
    M3 --> REPORT
    M4 --> REPORT
    M5 --> REPORT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each box is a functional module. Data flows from raw fleet operations (left) through computational engines (centre) into KPIs (right) and finally into mitigation strategies (bottom). This is the architecture used by fleet management software such as **Geotab**, **Samsara**, and **Trimble**.

---

## 4.2 Well-to-Wheel vs Tank-to-Wheel Emission Boundary

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph WTW[WELL TO WHEEL BOUNDARY]
        W[Well: Crude Oil Extraction] --> R[Refining]
        R --> D[Distribution to Pump]
        D --> T[Tank of Vehicle]
        T --> WHL[Wheel: Vehicle Operation]
    end

    subgraph TTW[TANK TO WHEEL BOUNDARY]
        T2[Tank] --> WHL2[Wheel]
    end

    W -.->|Scope 3 Upstream| E1[Pre combustion CO2]
    WHL -.->|Scope 1 Tailpipe| E2[Tailpipe CO2]
    E1 --> TOTAL[Total WTW Emissions]
    E2 --> TOTAL

    style TOTAL fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

---

## 4.3 Fleet Decarbonisation Strategy Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L0[Zero Emission Target] --> L1[Level 1: Avoid Trips]
    L1 --> L2[Level 2: Modal Shift Rail or Sea]
    L2 --> L3[Level 3: Optimise Existing ICE Fleet]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Avoid → Shift → Improve framework (ASI)** is the gold-standard for transport decarbonisation, adopted by the **ITF (International Transport Forum)** and recommended by KTU for sustainable fleet design.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]
**Define the term "Environmental Impact of Fleets" and list the four major categories of environmental impacts.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Environmental Impact of Fleets is the cumulative effect of a vehicle fleet on the environment across its life cycle, encompassing resource extraction, operation, and disposal.

**Four categories (1 mark each for any three):**
1. **Air pollution** — tailpipe emissions (CO₂, CO, NOₓ, HC, PM, SOₓ)
2. **Greenhouse gas emissions & climate change** — CO₂-equivalent contribution
3. **Noise pollution** — measured in dB(A)
4. **Resource depletion & solid waste** — tyres, oils, batteries, end-of-life vehicles

*(Award full 3 marks for any 3 correctly listed categories with brief description.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**The CO₂ emission factor for diesel is 2.68 kg/L. State its physical significance and calculate the CO₂ emitted by a truck consuming 25 L of diesel.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Significance (1 mark):** The factor **2.68 kg CO₂/L** means that for every litre of diesel completely combusted, **2.68 kg of CO₂** is released into the atmosphere. It arises from the carbon content of diesel (~86.4% by mass) and the stoichiometric C→CO₂ conversion ratio (44/12).

- **Calculation (2 marks):**
$$E_{CO_2} = V_f \times EF_D = 25 \times 2.68 = \boxed{67.0 \text{ kg CO}_2}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Format)

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]

**A logistics company operates a fleet of 40 diesel trucks. Each truck runs 75,000 km/year at a fuel economy of 4.5 km/L. The fleet also generates 0.35 kg CH₄ per truck per year and 0.12 kg N₂O per truck per year from minor leaks.**

#### **(a)** Calculate the **total annual CO₂ emissions** of the fleet. **(7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Fuel consumed per truck per year (1 mark)**
$$V_f = \dfrac{75{,}000}{4.5} = 16{,}666.67 \text{ L/year}$$

**Step 2 — CO₂ per truck per year (1 mark)**
$$E_{CO_2, \text{truck}} = 16{,}666.67 \times 2.68 = 44{,}666.67 \text{ kg} \approx 44.67 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Step 3 — CO₂ for 40 trucks (1 mark)**
$$E_{CO_2, \text{fleet}} = 40 \times 44.67 = 1{,}786.67 \text{ tonnes CO}_2/\text{year}$$

**Step 4 — Verification (1 mark)**
Per-truck km intensity: $\dfrac{44{,}666.67}{75{,}000} = 0.5955$ kg CO₂/km → matches typical heavy-duty BS-VI diesel range of **0.55–0.65 kg CO₂/km**. ✔

**Result:** $\boxed{E_{CO_2, \text{fleet}} \approx 1{,}786.67 \text{ tonnes CO}_2/\text{year}}$

---

#### **(b)** Calculate the **total CO₂-equivalent emissions** including CH₄ and N₂O, and discuss **two fleet-level mitigation strategies** to reduce this footprint by at least 30%. **(7 marks)**

**Step 1 — CH₄ contribution per fleet (1 mark)**
$$m_{CH_4, \text{fleet}} = 0.35 \times 40 = 14 \text{ kg/yr}$$

**Step 2 — N₂O contribution per fleet (1 mark)**
$$m_{N_2O, \text{fleet}} = 0.12 \times 40 = 4.8 \text{ kg/yr}$$

**Step 3 — CO₂-equivalent (1 mark)**
$$CO_{2e} = 1{,}786{,}670 + (28 \times 14) + (265 \times 4.8)$$
$$= 1{,}786{,}670 + 392 + 1{,}272 = 1{,}788{,}334 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e}$$
$$\approx 1{,}788.33 \text{ tonnes CO}_2\text{e/year}$$

**Step 4 — Mitigation strategies (3 marks)**

> **Strategy 1 — Eco-Driver Training + Telematics**
> Professional driver training can reduce fuel consumption by **8–12%** through smoother acceleration, optimal gear shifting, and speed control (ideal: 50–70 km/h). Implemented across 40 trucks → saves ~215 tonnes CO₂/yr (~12%).

> **Strategy 2 — Fleet Electrification (Phase-wise)**
> Replace 30% of the fleet (12 trucks) with **Battery Electric Trucks** for urban/suburban routes. Each BEV avoids ~44.67 tonnes CO₂/yr. Savings = 12 × 44.67 ≈ **536 tonnes CO₂/yr (~30%)**.

**Combined impact** easily exceeds the 30% reduction target. ✔

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{CO_{2e} \approx 1{,}788.33 \text{ tonnes CO}_2\text{e/year}}$

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**A city bus operator wants to assess the environmental impact of replacing its 20 diesel buses (BS-IV) with BS-VI CNG buses. Each bus runs 60,000 km/year; diesel fleet FE = 3.5 km/L; CNG fleet FE = 2.8 km/kg.**

#### **(a)** Compute the **annual CO₂ reduction** achieved by this switch. Use $EF_D = 2.68$ kg/L and $EF_{CNG} = 2.75$ kg/kg. **(7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Diesel bus fuel & CO₂ (1.5 marks)**
$$V_D = \dfrac{60{,}000}{3.5} = 17{,}142.86 \text{ L/bus}$$
$$E_{CO_2, D} = 17{,}142.86 \times 2.68 = 45{,}942.86 \text{ kg/bus}$$

**Step 2 — CNG bus fuel & CO₂ (1.5 marks)**
$$m_{CNG} = \dfrac{60{,}000}{2.8} = 21{,}428.57 \text{ kg/bus}$$
$$E_{CO_2, CNG} = 21{,}428.57 \times 2.75 = 58{,}928.57 \text{ kg/bus}$$

**Step 3 — Per-bus comparison (1 mark)**
$$E_{CO_2, D} - E_{CO_2, CNG} = 45{,}942.86 - 58{,}928.57 = -12{,}985.71 \text{ kg/bus}$$

> ⚠ **The CNG switch INCREASES tailpipe CO₂ by ~13 tonnes/bus/yr!**

**Step 4 — Interpretation (2 marks)**
This is a classic KTU pitfall. **Tailpipe CO₂ of CNG is higher because methane (CH₄) has a lower C:H ratio**, but per unit of energy, CNG emits ~25% LESS CO₂. Also, **CNG drastically reduces NOₓ (~40%), PM (~90%), and HC**.

If we measure by **per km basis:**
$$CO_2^D/km = \dfrac{45{,}942.86}{60{,}000} = 0.766 \text{ kg/km}$$
$$CO_2^{CNG}/km = \dfrac{58{,}928.57}{60{,}000} = 0.982 \text{ kg/km}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall #1:**
> Students commonly conclude that CNG is "always greener". The correct KTU answer must state: **CNG reduces local pollutants (NOₓ, PM) dramatically but may slightly increase tailpipe CO₂ in mass terms — the real benefit is in per-MJ energy basis (~25% lower) and avoided particulate harm.** This question tests whether the student understands **mass vs energy vs harm metrics**.

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{\Delta E_{CO_2} = -12{,}985.71 \text{ kg/bus/year (CNG emits MORE tailpipe CO}_2\text{)}}$

---

#### **(b)** Recommend a **Well-to-Wheel (WTW) emission strategy** and discuss the role of **BS-VI emission norms** in regulating this fleet. **(7 marks)**

**Step 1 — WTW framework (2 marks)**

The WTW analysis has two segments:
- **Well-to-Tank (WTT)** — fuel extraction, processing, transport to pump
- **Tank-to-Wheel (TTW)** — vehicle operation

Typical WTT factors:
- Diesel: ~0.55 kg CO₂/L
- CNG (Indian grid mix): ~0.40 kg CO₂/kg

**Step 2 — WTW CO₂ calculation (2 marks)**

Diesel:
$$CO_{2, WTW}^{D} = (V_D \times EF_{WTT,D}) + E_{CO_2, TTW}^{D} = (17{,}142.86 \times 0.55) + 45{,}942.86$$
$$= 9{,}428.57 + 45{,}942.86 = 55{,}371.43 \text{ kg/bus}$$

CNG:
$$CO_{2, WTW}^{CNG} = (21{,}428.57 \times 0.40) + 58{,}928.57$$
$$= 8{,}571.43 + 58{,}928.57 = 67{,}500 \text{ kg/bus}$$

> Per km: Diesel = 0.923 kg/km; CNG = 1.125 kg/km. CNG still higher in pure CO₂ mass, but lower in regulated pollutants.

**Step 3 — Role of BS-VI norms (3 marks)**

BS-VI norms (effective April 2020) are **equivalent to Euro 6** and introduce:

| Pollutant | BS-IV Limit | BS-VI Limit | Reduction |
|---|---|---|---|
| NOₓ (diesel) | 5.0 g/kWh | 0.46 g/kWh | **~91%** |
| PM (diesel) | 0.025 g/kWh | 0.01 g/kWh | **~80%** |
| HC + NOₓ | 1.3 g/km | 0.17 g/km | **~87%** |

Mandatory **on-board diagnostics (OBD-II)**, **SCR (Selective Catalytic Reduction)**, and **DPF (Diesel Particulate Filter)** technologies are required. Compliance is enforced through **type approval** (testing at ARAI, Pune) and **in-service conformity** checks.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall #2:**
> Many students write only "BS-VI reduces emissions". The KTU board expects you to **quote specific numerical limits** (e.g., NOₓ dropped from 5.0 to 0.46 g/kWh) and name the **enabling technologies** (SCR, DPF, EGR). Failure to do so caps marks at 50%.

**Final WTW Comparison:**
$$\boxed{CO_{2, WTW}^D \approx 55{,}371 \text{ kg/bus} \quad vs \quad CO_{2, WTW}^{CNG} \approx 67{,}500 \text{ kg/bus}}$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day**

- ✅ **CO₂ emission factor for diesel = 2.68 kg/L** (memorize this!)
- ✅ **CO₂ emission factor for petrol = 2.31 kg/L**
- ✅ **GWP of CH₄ = 28** and **N₂O = 265** (over 100 years)
- ✅ Formula: $E_{CO_2} = V_f \times EF$ — the single most tested equation
- ✅ **BS-VI = Euro 6**, effective in India from **April 2020**
- ✅ **BSFC** unit is **g/kWh**; modern diesel target = **200–220 g/kWh**
- ✅ **Well-to-Wheel (WTW)** vs **Tank-to-Wheel (TTW)** — always clarify the boundary
- ✅ Four impact pillars: **Air, GHG, Noise, Waste**
- ✅ **CNG reduces NOₓ/PM drastically** but may slightly raise tailpipe CO₂ mass — note the nuance
- ✅ **Avoid → Shift → Improve** is the recommended decarbonisation hierarchy
- ✅ Electric vehicles have **zero tailpipe** emissions but **non-zero WTW** emissions if grid is coal-based
- ✅ Average diesel truck emits ~**1 kg CO₂ per km**; per year ~**80–100 tonnes CO₂** for long-haul
- ✅ For Part A: focus on **definitions + single-number calculations**
- ✅ For Part B: show **all working steps**, **state assumptions clearly**, **quote numerical standards**, and **mention enabling technologies**

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Environmental Considerations: Environmental Impact of Fleets**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT866 - VEHICLE TRANSPORT MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Strategies for Reducing Environmental Footprint

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🌍 Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Environmental Footprint (in Transport Context)** is defined as the total quantitative measure of the direct and indirect environmental impact attributable to a vehicle, fleet, or transport system across its full life cycle, expressed conventionally in terms of **grams of CO₂-equivalent emissions per kilometre (g CO₂e/km)** or **kilograms of CO₂-equivalent per tonne-kilometre (kg CO₂e/t-km)** for freight operations.

The transport sector accounts for approximately **14–16 % of global greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions**, with road vehicles alone contributing nearly **75 %** of that share. Hence, *reducing the environmental footprint* is a multi-dimensional engineering, managerial, and policy challenge.

> [!NOTE]  
> **KTU 2024 Concept Box:** The footprint is not just tailpipe emissions. It encompasses the **Well-to-Wheel (WTW)** chain — i.e., fuel extraction → fuel processing → distribution → vehicle operation → end-of-life recycling.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Leaking Bucket"

Imagine your transport fleet is a **bucket of water** representing the planet's clean atmosphere. Every time a truck rolls down the road, a small **leak** occurs (CO₂, NOₓ, PM). If the leaks are faster than the bucket can be refilled (regenerated via forests, carbon sinks), the bucket eventually runs dry. The **strategies** are simply the engineering methods we use to:

| Leak Type | Real-World Equivalent | Bucket Plug |
|---|---|---|
| Carbon Dioxide (CO₂) | Tailpipe + upstream emissions | Electric propulsion |
| Particulate Matter (PM) | Diesel soot | DPF filters |
| Nitrogen Oxides (NOₓ) | High combustion T° | SCR, EGR |
| Noise | Tyre/engine sound | EV near-silent operation |

The **goal**: reduce the leak rate faster than consumption, so the bucket never empties.

---

## 1.3 Core Metrics Used in KTU Valuation

> [!NOTE]  
> **Key Standardized Metrics:**
> - **g CO₂/km** — passenger car (light-duty vehicles, per **WLTP** protocol).  
> - **g CO₂/t-km** — freight efficiency (per **EN 16258** standard).  
> - **MJ/100 km** — primary energy demand (per **ISO 14040 LCA**).  
> - **kWh/100 km** — battery electric vehicles (BEVs).  
> - **Well-to-Wheel (WTW)** efficiency — system boundary for full impact.

---

## 1.4 Categories of Footprint-Reduction Strategies

The KTU 2024 syllabus organises strategies into **three concentric layers**:

1. **Vehicle-Level (Technology)** — propulsion, lightweighting, aerodynamics.
2. **Operation-Level (Human & Procedural)** — eco-driving, route optimisation.
3. **System-Level (Policy & Modal)** — modal shift, public transport, sharing economy.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Strategy Hierarchy — A 3-Tier Analytical Framework

### 🔹 Tier 1: Vehicle Technology Strategies

**A. Alternative Powertrains**
- **Battery Electric Vehicles (BEV):** Zero tailpipe emissions; WTW depends on electricity grid mix.
- **Hybrid Electric Vehicles (HEV):** Combines ICE + electric motor; typical CO₂ reduction **20–35 %**.
- **Plug-in Hybrid (PHEV):** Larger battery; **40–60 %** reduction depending on grid.
- **Fuel Cell Electric Vehicles (FCEV):** Hydrogen-powered; only water as exhaust.
- **CNG / LNG / Biodiesel / Ethanol:** Substituting petroleum-derived fuels.

**B. Vehicle Design Optimisation**
- **Lightweighting:** Use of aluminium, carbon-fibre, high-strength steel → ~**6–8 %** fuel saving per **10 %** mass reduction.
- **Aerodynamic Drag Reduction:** Lower Cd (drag coefficient). Savings ≈ **0.04 × ΔCd** at highway speeds.
- **Low Rolling Resistance Tyres:** Reduces tyre-induced losses by **5–10 %**.
- **Engine Downsizing + Turbocharging:** Maintains power with smaller displacement.

**C. Emission After-Treatment Technologies**
- **Three-Way Catalytic Converter (TWC):** Reduces CO, HC, NOₓ.
- **Diesel Particulate Filter (DPF):** Traps soot (PM).
- **Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR):** Converts NOₓ to N₂ + H₂O using AdBlue.
- **Exhaust Gas Recirculation (EGR):** Lowers combustion peak temperature.

---

### 🔹 Tier 2: Operational Strategies

| Strategy | Mechanism | Typical Savings |
|---|---|---|
| **Eco-Driving Training** | Smooth throttle, anticipation, gear optimisation | **5–15 %** fuel |
| **Predictive Cruise Control** | Topography-aware speed | **3–7 %** |
| **Route Optimisation (GIS)** | Shortest path + traffic avoidance | **8–12 %** |
| **Tyre Pressure Monitoring (TPMS)** | Maintains optimal inflation | **2–3 %** |
| **Platooning (Trucks)** | Aerodynamic drafting | **10–15 %** at highway |
| **Telematics & Fleet Management** | Real-time monitoring | **10–20 %** overall |

---

### 🔹 Tier 3: System & Policy Strategies

- **Modal Shift:** Movement of passengers/freight from road to rail, water, or public transit.
- **Urban Consolidation Centres (UCC):** Last-mile logistics with EVs.
- **Shared Mobility:** Carpooling, ride-hailing increases occupancy.
- **Low Emission Zones (LEZ):** Restricts high-emission vehicles in city centres.
- **Carbon Pricing / Fuel Taxes:** Demand-side lever.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU 2024 — High-Yield Equations for Exam Valuation**

| # | Formula / Relationship | Symbol Meaning | Typical Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{CO_2} = F_c \times \rho_f \times C_f$ | $F_c$ = fuel consumed, $\rho_f$ = fuel density, $C_f$ = carbon factor | kg CO₂ | Total emissions from fuel use |
| 2 | $\eta_{wtw} = \dfrac{E_{mech}}{E_{primary}}$ | WTW efficiency | dimensionless | Energy audit |
| 3 | $F_{spec} = \dfrac{F_c}{d}$ | Specific fuel consumption | L/100 km | Vehicle rating |
| 4 | $m_{CO_2} = \dfrac{d \times F_{spec} \times \rho_f \times C_f}{100}$ | Per-trip CO₂ | g CO₂/km | Fleet footprint |
| 5 | $P_{drag} = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho_a C_d A v^3$ | Aerodynamic loss | W | Highway savings |
| 6 | $F_{rr} = C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g$ | Rolling resistance | N | Tyre savings |
| 7 | $\eta_{payload} = \dfrac{m_{payload}}{m_{payload} + m_{vehicle}}$ | Load efficiency | dimensionless | Freight optimisation |
| 8 | $\Delta F = k \cdot \Delta m_{vehicle}$ | Lightweight savings (k ≈ 0.6–0.8 L/100km per 100 kg) | L/100 km | Mass reduction |
| 9 | $E_{total} = \sum_i (Q_i \times GWP_i)$ | Global Warming Potential | kg CO₂e | LCA footprint |
| 10 | $\eta_{brake} = \dfrac{P_{brake}}{P_{fuel}}$ | Brake thermal efficiency | dimensionless | Engine tuning |

> **Standard Carbon Factors (KTU-accepted):**
> - Petrol: **2.31 kg CO₂/L**
> - Diesel: **2.68 kg CO₂/L**
> - CNG: **2.75 kg CO₂/kg**
> - Grid electricity (India avg.): **0.72 kg CO₂/kWh**

---

## 2.3 Real-World Utility

These formulas underpin:
- **Fleet Sustainability Audits** in logistics companies (Amazon, DHL, Flipkart).
- **Vehicle Homologation** under **BS-VI / Euro 6** norms.
- **Corporate ESG Reporting** (Scope 1, 2, 3 emissions).
- **Government Policy Modelling** (FAME-II, PLI scheme for EVs in India).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Per-Trip CO₂ Emission from a Diesel Truck

**Problem Setup:** A logistics company operates a delivery truck with the following specifications:

- Distance covered per trip: **d = 150 km**
- Specific fuel consumption: **F_spec = 18 L / 100 km**
- Diesel density: **ρ_f = 0.835 kg/L**
- Carbon factor (diesel): **C_f = 3.17 kg CO₂/kg of fuel**
- Load carried: **2.5 tonnes**
- Vehicle kerb weight: **6.5 tonnes**

**Find:**
1. Total fuel used per trip.
2. Total CO₂ emitted per trip.
3. Specific footprint per tonne-km.

---

### Step 1 — Total Fuel Consumption

We start from the definition of specific fuel consumption:

$$
F_{spec} = \dfrac{F_c}{d} \times 100
$$

Rearranging for $F_c$:

$$
F_c = \dfrac{F_{spec} \times d}{100}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
F_c = \dfrac{18 \times 150}{100}
$$

The multiplication 18 × 150 equals 2700, divided by 100 gives:

$$
F_c = 27 \text{ L per trip}
$$

---

### Step 2 — Convert Fuel Volume to Fuel Mass

Using the fuel density relationship:

$$
m_f = F_c \times \rho_f
$$

$$
m_f = 27 \times 0.835
$$

Performing the multiplication:

$$
m_f = 22.545 \text{ kg of diesel per trip}
$$

---

### Step 3 — Convert Fuel Mass to CO₂ Mass

Apply the emission factor formula:

$$
m_{CO_2} = m_f \times C_f
$$

$$
m_{CO_2} = 22.545 \times 3.17
$$

Multiplying:

$$
m_{CO_2} \approx 71.47 \text{ kg CO}_2 \text{ per trip}
$$

---

### Step 4 — Compute Footprint per Tonne-Kilometre

The transport work performed per trip is:

$$
W_{t\text{-}km} = m_{payload} \times d
$$

$$
W_{t\text{-}km} = 2.5 \times 150 = 375 \text{ tonne-km}
$$

Therefore the specific intensity is:

$$
F_{intensity} = \dfrac{m_{CO_2}}{W_{t\text{-}km}}
$$

$$
F_{intensity} = \dfrac{71.47}{375}
$$

$$
F_{intensity} \approx 0.1906 \text{ kg CO}_2 \text{/tonne-km}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Result: The truck emits ≈ 190.6 g CO₂ per tonne-km.**  
> Comparable benchmarks: rail freight ≈ **22–30 g CO₂/t-km**; inland waterways ≈ **15–25 g CO₂/t-km**. This shows the *modal shift potential*.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Quantifying Eco-Driving Savings

**Given:** A taxi fleet (1000 vehicles), each running **50,000 km/year**, with current fuel consumption **6.5 L/100 km** (petrol).

**Eco-driving programme reduces consumption by 12 %.**

### Step 1 — Original Annual Fuel Use

$$
F_{orig} = N \times \dfrac{d_{annual}}{100} \times F_{spec}
$$

$$
F_{orig} = 1000 \times \dfrac{50000}{100} \times 6.5
$$

$$
F_{orig} = 1000 \times 500 \times 6.5
$$

$$
F_{orig} = 3{,}250{,}000 \text{ L/year}
$$

---

### Step 2 — Fuel Saved

$$
F_{saved} = F_{orig} \times 0.12
$$

$$
F_{saved} = 3{,}250{,}000 \times 0.12 = 390{,}000 \text{ L/year}
$$

---

### Step 3 — CO₂ Avoided

Using petrol's carbon factor **2.31 kg CO₂/L**:

$$
CO_{2, avoided} = 390{,}000 \times 2.31
$$

$$
CO_{2, avoided} = 900{,}900 \text{ kg/year}
$$

$$
CO_{2, avoided} = 900.9 \text{ tonnes CO}_2 \text{/year}
$$

> [!NOTE]  
> **Equivalent to taking ~196 cars off the road for a year** (using an average passenger car emitting ~4.6 t CO₂/year).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Strategies

| Strategy | Capital Cost | Payback Period | CO₂ Reduction | Maturity (TRL) | KTU Exam Priority |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| BEV (urban fleet) | High | 3–5 yrs | 60–100 % | 9 | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| HEV | Medium | 4–6 yrs | 20–35 % | 9 | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| Lightweighting | Medium | 5–7 yrs | 6–10 % | 8 | ⭐⭐ |
| Aerodynamics | Low–Medium | 2–3 yrs | 3–7 % | 9 | ⭐⭐ |
| Eco-driving | Very Low | < 1 yr | 5–15 % | 9 | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| Modal Shift | High (infra) | Long-term | 50–80 % | 7 | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| Tyre Upgrade | Low | Immediate | 2–5 % | 9 | ⭐⭐ |
| Platooning | Medium | 3–5 yrs | 10–15 % | 6 | ⭐ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
